content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὧν_3">, since </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρόθυμος_ἦσθ_3">=<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπεθύμεις</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Ἄργος_4"> in prose usu. means the town only, the territory being <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡ Ἀργεία</quote> or <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡ Ἀργολίς</quote>. But poetry retained the larger sense which Homer had made familiar. Thus in <title>I. T.</title> 508 Orestes says, <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ κλεινὸν Ἄργος πατρίδ’ ἐμὴν ἐπεύχομαι</quote>, adding that he comes <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκ τῶν Μυκηνῶν</quote>. Cp. Eur. fr. 228. 6 (Danaüs) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐλθὼν ἐς Ἄργος ᾤκισ’ Ἰνάχου πόλιν</quote> (came to <title>Argolis</title>, and settled in the town of Argos). Indeed Thuc. can say <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 6.105">(6. 105),</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">Λακεδαιμόνιοι ἐς τὸ Ἄργος ἐσέβαλον</quote> </cit>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παλαιὸν_4"> refers not merely to the town, but to the associations of the land. The oldest legends of intercourse between Greece and Asia belonged to the shores of the Argive Gulf (cp. <ref n="Hdt. 1. 1">Her. 1. 1</ref>). Cp. Aristeides <title>Panath.</title> p. 188 <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἀργεῖοι παλαιότατοι τῶν Ἑλλήνων ἀξιοῦσιν εἶναι</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῆς_κόρης_5">. The Inachus (now the <title>Bonitza</title>) rises in the highlands between Arcadia and Argolis; flows N.E. , and then S.E. , through the Argive plain; and enters the Gulf on the east side of the town. This river-god figured as the earliest king of Argos. Cp. the <title>Inachus</title> of Sophocles, fr. 248 <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἴναχε νᾶτορ, παῖ τοῦ κρηνῶν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">πατρὸς</quote> <quote xml:lang="grc">Ὠκεανοῦ, μέγα πρεσβεύων</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἄργους τε γύαις Ἥρας τε πάγοις</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ Τυρσηνοῖσι Πελασγοῖς</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄλσος_5">, the whole region, regarded as ground which her story has made sacred: <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔλεγον γὰρ πᾶν χωρίον ἀφιερωμένον θεῷ, κἂν ψιλὸν φυτῶν ᾖ, ἄλσος</quote> (schol. <ref n="Pind. O. 3">Pind. <title>O.</title> 3. 31</ref>). So <ref n="Soph. Ant. 845">Soph. <title>Ant.</title> 845</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">Θήβας τ’ εὐαρμάτου ἄλσος</quote> (n.): <ref n="Pind. N. 10">Pind. <title>N.</title> 10. 19</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἀργεῖον..τέμενος</quote> (‘the sacred Argive land’). In <title>Suppl.</title> 538 ff. the Danaides at Argos say:—‘We have come hither, into the ancient footsteps of our mother (Io), to the flowery meads of the watcher (Argus), where the cow was pastured, and whence, vexed by the gad-fly, she fled in frenzy.’</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀγορὰ_Λύκειος_6"> in Argos lay at the eastern foot of the Larisa, or citadel; as Livy (32. 25) describes it, <quote xml:lang="lat">subiectum arci forum.</quote> The temple of Apollo <quote xml:lang="grc">Λύκειος</quote> was probably on the north side of the agora, opposite to a temple of Zeus <quote xml:lang="grc">Νεμεαῖος</quote>. Before its eastern front stood a monument representing a wolf slaying a bull, in memory of the omen which had given the sovereignty to Danaüs ( <ref n="Paus. 2. 19. 3">Paus. 2. 19. 3</ref>).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Λύκειος_6"> must be ultimately traced to the root <quote xml:lang="grc">λυκ</quote>, <foreign xml:lang="lat">lux</foreign>, as designating the god of light. But it was popularly connected with <quote xml:lang="grc">λύκος</quote>. Sophocles here explains it by </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λυκοκτόνος_6">, an attribute suitable to Apollo as protector of flocks and herds (<quote xml:lang="grc">νόμιος</quote>, <ref n="Soph. OT 1103"> <title>O. T.</title> 1103</ref> n.). The <quote xml:lang="grc">Λύκειος</quote> is invoked especially as a destroyer of foes ( <ref n="Soph. OT 203"> <title>O. T.</title> 203</ref> n.: <ref n="Aesch. Seven 145">Aesch. <title>Theb.</title> 145</ref>). See Appendix. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Ἥρας_ναός_8">. The site of the Heraeum, discovered by General Gordon in 1831, is about a mile and three quarters S. E. of Mycenae, and about five miles N. E. of Argos. E. Itcan be seen from Argos, but is hidden from Mycenae by a projecting spur of the hills. The temple stood on a rocky eminence under Mount Euboea, one of the heights which bound the Argive plain on the east. The streams <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἐλευθερίων</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἀρτερίων</quote> flowed on either side of it. Beneath it was a grassy tract known as <quote xml:lang="grc">Πρόσυμνα</quote> (Statius 3. 325 <quote xml:lang="lat">viridis devexa Prosymnae</quote>); whence the goddess was sometimes styled <quote xml:lang="grc">Προσυμναία</quote> ( <title>Fluv.</title> 18. 3).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φάσκειν_9"> (infin. as imperat.),=‘deem,’ ‘believe’: <ref n="Soph. OT 462"> <title>O. T.</title> 462</ref> n. For the expression cp. Milton, <title>Paradise Regained</title>, 4. 44 f.: ‘The city which thou seest no other <emph>deem</emph> | Than great and glorious Rome.’</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Μυκήνας_9">. This plural form (the prevalent one) occurs in <ref n="Hom. Il. 2.569"> <title>Il.</title> 2. 569</ref>, <ref n="Hom. Il. 4.376"> Il. 4. 376</ref>; but elsewhere metrical convenience led the Homeric poet to prefer the sing. <quote xml:lang="grc">Μυκήνη</quote>, which allowed him to prefix <quote xml:lang="grc">εὐρυάγυια</quote> ( <ref n="Hom. Il. 4.52"> <title>Il.</title> 4. 52</ref>), and <quote xml:lang="grc">πολυχρύσοιο</quote> ( <ref n="Hom. Il. 7.180"> <title>Il.</title> 7. 180</ref>, <ref n="Hom. Il. 11.46"> Il. 11. 46</ref>: <ref n="Hom. Od. 3.305"> <title>Od.</title> 3. 305</ref>).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὰς_πολυχρύσους_9">: the Homeric epithet (see above). It is illustrated by the number of golden cups, cylinders, diadems and other objects found in the graves at Mycenae by Schliemann; who estimated the amount of gold thus discovered at ‘about 100 lbs troy’ (<title>Mycenae</title>, p. 379).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πολύφθορον_10">, desolated by many deaths; so <ref n="Soph. Trach. 477"> <title>Tr.</title> 477</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡ πολύφθορος</quote> | ... <quote xml:lang="grc">Οἰχαλία</quote>. Atreus and Thyestes slew their brother Chrysippus; Atreus slew his own son Pleisthenes, and then two sons of Thyestes; Aegisthus, son of Thyestes, slew Atreus and Agamemnon. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πατρὸς_φόνων_11">. For the plur. of <quote xml:lang="grc">φόνος</quote>, referring to one deed, <ref n="Soph. El. 779">cp. 779</ref>, <ref n="Soph. OC 990"> <title>O. C.</title> 990</ref>: so <quote xml:lang="grc">θανάτους</quote>, <ref n="Soph. OC 206."> O. C. 206.</ref>In <ref n="Soph. Trach. 558"> <title>Tr.</title> 558</ref> the MS. <quote xml:lang="grc">φόνων</quote> is well corrected to <quote xml:lang="grc">φονῶν</quote>, which denotes a bleeding wound. But here, where the reference is to the act of murder, <quote xml:lang="grc">φόνων</quote> should be kept.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκ_11"> might be ‘after’: but is perhaps better taken as ‘away from’ the scene of slaughter. The boy's life, too, was in peril. <ref n="Soph. El. 601">Cp. 601</ref>: <ref n="Pind. P. 11">Pind. <title>P.</title> 11. 17</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν δὴ</quote> (Orestes) <quote xml:lang="grc">φονευομένου πατρὸς Ἀρσινόα Κλυταιμνήστρας</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">χειρῶν ὑπὸ κρατερᾶν κἀκ δόλου τροφὸς ἄνελε δυσπενθέος</quote>. <cit> <bibl n="Eur. El. 16">Eur. <title>El.</title> 16</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν μὲν πατρὸς γεραιὸς ἐκκλέπτει τροφεὺς | μέλλοντ’ Ὀρέστην χερὸς ὕπ’ Αἰγίσθου θανεῖν</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

data.quote:
τὸν μὲν πατρὸς γεραιὸς ἐκκλέπτει τροφεὺς |
                                        μέλλοντ’ Ὀρέστην χερὸς ὕπ’ Αἰγίσθου θανεῖν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὁμαίμου_12">, ‘kinswoman,’ is here defined by </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κασιγνήτης_12">: though Sophocles never uses <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅμαιμος</quote> or <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁμαίμων</quote> except of the fraternal tie ( <ref n="Soph. OC 330"> <title>O. C.</title> 330</ref> n.). The emphasis is like that of <quote xml:lang="grc">κοινὸν αὐτάδελφον</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1"> <title>Ant.</title> 1</ref>), or the Homeric <quote xml:lang="grc">κασίγνητος καὶ ὄπατρος</quote> ( <ref n="Hom. Il. 12.371"> <title>Il.</title> 12. 371</ref>). <ref n="Soph. El. 156">Cp. 156</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἤνεγκα_13"> is taken by the schol. to imply that Orestes was then a child in arms (<quote xml:lang="grc">οὔ τι βαδίσαι δυναμένου</quote>); but this is to press it overmuch. Orestes was born before his father went to Troy, and so must have been more than ten years old at the time of the murder.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κἀξεθρεψάμην_13">: this aorist occurs also in fr. 355, and <quote xml:lang="grc">θρεψαίμην</quote> in <ref n="Soph. OT 1143."> <title>O. T.</title> 1143.</ref>In poetry the midd. of <quote xml:lang="grc">τρέφω</quote> differs from the act. only as marking the interest felt by the <quote xml:lang="grc">τροφός</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοσόνδ_ἥβης_13">: <ref n="Soph. El. 961">cp. 961</ref>: <ref n="Soph. OC 1138"> <title>O. C.</title> 1138</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐς τόδ’ ἡμέρας</quote>: for the place of the prep., <ref n="Soph. OT 178"> <title>O. T.</title> 178</ref> n.: for <quote xml:lang="grc">τοσόνδ’</quote>, <ref n="Hom. Il. 9.485"> <title>Il.</title> 9. 485</ref>(Phoenix to Achilles) <quote xml:lang="grc">καί σε τοσοῦτον ἔθηκα</quote>. Aegisthus reigned seven years, and was slain in the eighth ( <ref n="Hom. Od. 3.303"> <title>Od.</title> 3. 303</ref> ff.). Orestes, then, is about nineteen or twenty. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σὺ_15"> is no argument for the conjecture </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βουλεύετον_15">.—Pylades was the son of that Strophius, king of Phocis, in whose house the young Orestes had found a refuge. Thus Pindar speaks of a Pythian victory as won <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν ἀφνεαῖς ἀρούραισι Πυλάδα</quote> (<title>P.</title> 11. 15). Euripides notices the legend that Orestes bestowed the hand of Electra upon his friend (<ref n="Eur. El. 1350"> <title>El.</title> 1350</ref>: <ref n="Paus. 2. 16. 7">Paus. 2. 16. 7</ref>). His name recurs at <ref n="Soph. El. 1373">v. 1373.</ref> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἡμὶν_17">: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 25"> <title>O. C.</title> 25</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἑῷα_17">. The sights and sounds of early morning are in unison with the spirit of this play, in which the <quote xml:lang="grc">παννυχίδες</quote> (v. 92) of Electra's sorrow are turned to joy, and the god of light prevails. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κινεῖ_σαφῆ_17"> (proleptic): <ref n="Soph. El. 1366">cp. 1366</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ταῦτα...δείξουσιν σαφῆ</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 475"> <title>Ant.</title> 475</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄστρων_εὐφρόνη_19">=<quote xml:lang="grc">εὐφρόνη ἀστερόεσσα</quote>, the gen. of material or quality, like <quote xml:lang="grc">σῶμα...σποδοῦ</quote> (758), <quote xml:lang="grc">τόλμης πρόσωπον</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. OT 533"> <title>O. T.</title> 533</ref>), <quote xml:lang="grc">χιόνος πτέρυγι</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. Ant. 114"> <title>Ant.</title> 114</ref>, where see n.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκλέλοιπεν_19">, intrans. and absol., ‘has failed’: <ref n="Soph. El. 985">cp. 985</ref>, <ref n="Soph. El. 1149">1149</ref>.— Not, ‘the dark night has lost its stars,’ as one schol. construes, followed by Ellendt and others. In classical Greek <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκλείπω</quote> never takes a gen., as <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐλλείπω</quote> does. (Plutarch, indeed, has <quote xml:lang="grc">θεραπείας σώματος ἐξέλειπε</quote>, <ref n="Plut. Marc. 17"> <title>Marc.</title> 17</ref>: but that may well be acc. plur. rather than gen. sing.) The sense also is against this, since it would imply that night itself had not yet wholly past away. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐξοδοιπορεῖν_20"> (a compound which occurs only here) has been needlessly suspected. <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁδοιπορεῖν</quote> in poetry is sometimes no more than <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔρχεσθαι</quote> or <quote xml:lang="grc">χωρεῖν</quote>: <foreign xml:lang="lat">e.g.</foreign>, <ref n="Soph. OC 1251"> <title>O. C.</title> 1251</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὧδ’ ὁδοιπορεῖ</quote>: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 1230"> <title>Ai.</title> 1230</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">κἀπ’ ἄκρων ὡδοιπόρεις</quote> </cit>.—They must concert their plans while there is yet no risk of their conversation being interrupted. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ξυνάπτετον_21">, intrans., ‘join,’ as <ref n="Soph. Phil. 702">Soph. <title>Ph.</title> 702</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς ἐς λόγους ξυνῆψα Πολυνείκει</quote>. Here the modal dat. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λόγοισιν_21"> takes the place of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐς λόγους</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡς_ἐνταῦθ_21"> <foreign xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</foreign> See Appendix, where the proposed emendations are classified. The main points are, I think, these.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐμὲν_21"> as=<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐσμέν</quote>, found only in Callim. fr. 294, is undoubtedly corrupt. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἴμεν</quote>, the easiest correction, is excluded by its sense. It could not mean, ‘we <emph>are moving in</emph> a place where...,’ ‘we are thereabouts’ (Campbell). It would mean, ‘we <emph>are going to</emph> a place where....’ But he speaks of the present.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐνταῦθ_21"> spurious? That is the crucial question. If it is spurious, then <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμὲν</quote> may be a vestige of a longer 1st pers. plur., such as <quote xml:lang="grc">ἕσταμεν</quote>: but, if <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐνταῦθ’</quote> is genuine, all such conjectures are barred. Now, when the usage of Sophocles is scrutinised, two points favour the genuineness of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐνταῦθ’</quote>. (<emph>a</emph>) It stands as antecedent to <quote xml:lang="grc">ἵνα</quote> in <ref n="Soph. Phil. 429"> <title>Ph.</title> 429</ref>: to <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔνθα</quote> below, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 380"> Ph. 380</ref>: and to <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅπου</quote> in <ref n="Soph. Trach. 800."> <title>Tr.</title> 800.</ref>(<emph>b</emph>) Sophocles is peculiarly fond of using it in that <emph>figurative</emph> sense which it would have here, as=‘under these circumstances,’ ‘in that situation’: see, <foreign xml:lang="lat">e.g.</foreign>, <ref n="Soph. OT 582"> <title>O. T.</title> 582</ref>, <ref n="Soph. OT 598"> O. T. 598</ref>, <ref n="Soph. OT 720"> O. T. 720</ref>: <ref n="Soph. OC 585"> <title>O. C.</title> 585</ref>: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 429"> <title>Ph.</title> 429</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 433"> Ph. 433</ref>: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 37"> <title>Tr.</title> 37</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Trach. 772"> Tr. 772</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Trach. 936."> Tr. 936.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔργων_ἀκμή_21">: <ref n="Soph. El. 1338">cp. 1338</ref>: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 12"> <title>Ph.</title> 12</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀκμὴ γὰρ οὐ μακρῶν ἡμῖν λόγων</quote>: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 811"> <title>Ai.</title> 811</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐχ ἕδρας ἀκμή</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὦ_ἀνδρῶν_23"> <quote xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</quote> Cp. <ref n="Shak. AYL 2.3">Shakesp. <title>As You Like It</title>, act 2, sc. 3. 56</ref> (Orlando to Adam): ‘O good old man, how well in thee appears | The constant service of the antique world.’</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σημεῖα_φαίνεις_23"> (=<quote xml:lang="grc">δῆλος εἶ</quote>)...</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γεγώς_23">: cp. Lycurg. § 50 <quote xml:lang="grc">φανερὸν πᾶσιν ἐποίησαν οὐκ ἰδίᾳ πολεμοῦντες</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὥσπερ_εὐγενὴς_25">: <title>Vit. Sophist.</title> 2. 23. 4 <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ εἶδον ἄνδρα παραπλήσιον τῷ Σοφοκλείῳ ἵππῳ, νωθρὸς γὰρ ὑφ’ ἡλικίας δοκῶν νεάζουσαν ὁρμὴν ἐν ταῖς σπουδαῖς ἀνεκτᾶτο</quote>. Equally famous was the <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἰβύκειος ἵππος</quote> (<ref n="Plat. Parm. 137a">Plat. <title>Parm.</title> 137 A</ref>); Ibycus fr. 2. 3 (Bergk), <quote xml:lang="grc">ἦ μὰν τρομέω νιν</quote> (the Love-god) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπερχόμενον</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">ὥστε φερέζυγος ἵππος ἀεθλοφόρος ποτὶ γήραϊ ἀέκων</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">σὺν οχεσφι θοοις ἐς ἅμιλλαν ἔβα</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐν_δεινοῖς_26">, in dangers: <ref n="Thuc. 1. 70">Thuc. 1. 70</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπὶ τοῖς δεινοῖς εὐέλπιδες</quote>; <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 84 <quote xml:lang="grc">τῶν...ἐξοτρυνόντων ἡμᾶς ἐπὶ τὰ δεινά,</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀπώλεσεν_26">, gnomic aor., combined with pres. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἵστησιν_26">: <ref n="Soph. Aj. 674"> <title>Ai.</title> 674</ref> ff. <quote xml:lang="grc">δεινῶν τ’ ἄημα πνευμάτων ἐκοίμισε</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">στένοντα πόντον· ἐν δ’ ὁ παγκρατὴς ὕπνος</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">λύει πεδήσας</quote>: <ref n="Hom. Il. 17.177"> <title>Il.</title> 17. 177</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅς τε καὶ ἄλκιμον ἄνδρα φοβεῖ καὶ ἀφείλετο νίκην</quote>: <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Rep. 566d">Plat. <title>Rep.</title> 566 D, E</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀσπάζεται ... ὑπισχνεῖται ... <*>λευθέρωσε ... διένειμε...προσποιεῖται</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Dem. 2.9">Dem. or. 2 § 9</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐθέλουσιν...ἀνεχαίτισε καὶ διέλυσεν</quote> </cit>.—Distinguish those instances in which the ordinary (and not gnomic) aor. is combined with the <emph>historic</emph> pres., as <ref n="Soph. Ant. 406"> <title>Ant.</title> 406</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁρᾶται...ᾑρέθη</quote> (n.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡσαύτως_δὲ_26">: here <quote xml:lang="grc">δὲ</quote> introduces the apodosis; so <quote xml:lang="grc">οὕτω δέ</quote>, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 426"> <title>Ant.</title> 426</ref>(n.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐν_ἕπει_28">. The image is from the case of leaders in battle, whose men follow them in several ranks; this old man is in the front rank. Cp. <ref n="Hom. Il. 8.337"> <title>Il.</title> 8. 337</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἕκτωρ δ’ ἐν πρώτοισι κίε</quote>. The military sense of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἕπομαι</quote> is frequent (<foreign xml:lang="lat">e.g.</foreign> <ref n="Hom. Il. 11.795"> <title>Il.</title> 11. 795</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλὰ σέ περ προέτω, ἅμα δ’ ἄλλος λαὸς ἑπέσθω</quote>). In prose <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν πρώτοις</quote> is sometimes little more than <foreign xml:lang="lat">imprimis</foreign> ( <ref n="Plat. Rep. 522C">Plat. <title>Rep.</title> 522C</ref>): but this would be tame here. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰ_τι_31">. This adverbial <quote xml:lang="grc">τι</quote> is frequent in such expressions of diffidence: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1279"> <title>Ph.</title> 1279</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ μἡ τι πρὸς καιρὸν λέγων</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">κυρῶ</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 586"> <title>Tr.</title> 586</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἴ τι μὴ δοκῶ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">πράσσειν μάταιον</quote>: <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 712 <quote xml:lang="grc">εἴ τι μὴ ψευσθήσομαι</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">γνώμης.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καιροῦ_τυγχάνω_31">: cp. <title>Legg.</title> 687 A <quote xml:lang="grc">πῶς...τοῦ καιροῦ ἂν...ἔτυχον</quote>; <ref n="Pind. N. 8">Pind. <title>N.</title> 8. 4</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">καιροῦ μὴ πλαναθέντα.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μεθάρμοσον_31">: <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπανόρθωσον</quote> schol. <!-- *** --> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γὰρ_32">, merely prefatory ( <ref n="Soph. OT 277"> <title>O. T.</title> 277</ref> n.).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἱκόμην_32">, followed by the historic pres. <quote xml:lang="grc">χρῇ</quote>: <ref n="Soph. El. 425">cp. 425</ref>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μάθοιμ_32">: the elision gives quasi-caesura, as in <ref n="Soph. Ant. 44"> <title>Ant.</title> 44</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 276"> <title>Ph.</title> 276</ref>, etc.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀροίμην_32">, opt. of 2nd aor. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἠρόμην</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. Ant. 907"> <title>Ant.</title> 907</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Aj. 247"> <title>Ai.</title> 247</ref>). The direct deliberative form is <quote xml:lang="grc">τίνι τρόπῳ ἄρωμαι</quote>; The indirect, (<emph>a</emph>) after a primary tense, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἱκνοῦμαι ὡς ἂν μάθω ὅτῳ ἄρωμαι</quote>: (<emph>b</emph>) after a secondary tense, as above. Cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 71"> <title>O. T.</title> 71</ref> f. <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς πύθοιθ’ ὅ τι</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">δρῶν...ῥυσαίμην πόλιν</quote> (n.). It is also possible to take <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀροίμην</quote> as opt. of fut. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀροῦμαι</quote>: but it may be objected that, in such clauses, the fut. indic. was commonly retained, even after a secondary tense (cp. Goodwin, <title>Moods and Tenses</title>, new ed., §§ 130, 340). —For the sense, ‘win,’ ‘achieve,’ cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 193"> <title>Ai.</title> 193</ref>, <ref n="Soph. OC 460."> <title>O. C.</title> 460.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χρῇ_35">: the pres. of this <quote xml:lang="grc">χράω</quote> occurs also in Her. (1. 55 <quote xml:lang="grc">χρᾷ</quote>, etc.). In <ref n="Soph. OC 87">Soph. <title>O. C.</title> 87</ref> we have the impf. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξέχρη</quote>: and the aor. pass. in <ref n="Soph. OT 604">Soph. <title>O. T.</title> 604</ref>, <ref n="Soph. OC 355.">Soph. <title>O. C.</title> 355.</ref>— </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοιαῦθ_35"> (with <quote xml:lang="grc">οι^</quote>, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 691">Soph. <title>Ant.</title> 691</ref>)...</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὧν_35"> (instead of <quote xml:lang="grc">οἵων</quote>): cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1353">Soph. <title>O. C.</title> 1353</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὁ_Φοῖβος_35">. Blomfield wished to omit the art., which Sophocles has not elsewhere prefixed to this name; but <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ Φοῖβος</quote> occurs (<foreign xml:lang="lat">e.g.</foreign>) in <ref n="Aristoph. Kn. 1024">Ar. <title>Eq.</title> 1024</ref>, <ref n="Aristoph. Kn. 1084">1084</ref>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πεύσει_35">. The gen. after this verb usu. denotes the informant (as <ref n="Soph. OT 333"> <title>O. T.</title> 333</ref>): but the thing heard is sometimes put in the gen., instead of the regular acc.: thus <ref n="Hom. Od. 2.255"> <title>Od.</title> 2. 255</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀγγελιάων</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">πεύσεται</quote>. So, too, with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀκούω</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. OC 485"> <title>O. C.</title> 485</ref>, etc.), and <quote xml:lang="grc">κλύω</quote> (<ref n="Soph. OC 1174">ib. 1174</ref>).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τάχα_35"> marks a momentary pause. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὐτὸν_36">, alone; cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 221"> <title>O. T.</title> 221</ref>, <ref n="Soph. OC 1650."> <title>O. C.</title> 1650.</ref> <emph>—<quote xml:lang="grc">ἄσκευον</quote> </emph>...</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀσπίδων_36"> <foreign xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</foreign>, ‘unfurnished with’ them: for the gen., <ref n="Soph. El. 1002">cp. 1002</ref>: <ref n="Soph. OT 191"> <title>O. T.</title> 191</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄχαλκος ἀσπίδων</quote>: <ref n="Soph. OC 677"> <title>O. C.</title> 677</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνήνεμον...χειμώνων</quote> (n.). The adj. occurs also in <ref n="Soph. OC 1029"> <title>O. C.</title> 1029</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ ψιλὸν οὐδ’ ἄσκευον</quote>, ‘not without accomplice or resource.’</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀσπίδων_στρατοῦ_36"> = <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡπλισμένου στρατοῦ</quote>, a rhetorical hendiadys, like ‘without arms or <emph>numbers.</emph>’— </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δόλοισι_36">, in requital of the <quote xml:lang="grc">δόλος</quote> on the side of the murderers (v. 197).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χειρὸς_36"> goes with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σφαγάς_36"> (476 <quote xml:lang="grc">δίκαια χειροῖν κράτη</quote>): the vengeance is to be won by his own right hand, not by means of allies.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κλέψαι_36">, to effect by stealth: <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1137"> <title>Ai.</title> 1137</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κλέψειας κακά</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅτε_38"> causal,=<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπειδή</quote>: <ref n="Soph. El. 1318">cp. 1318</ref>: <ref n="Soph. OT 918"> <title>O. T.</title> 918</ref>(n.): <ref n="Soph. Ant. 170."> <title>Ant.</title> 170.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σὺ_μολὼν_38"> <foreign xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</foreign>: for the absence of caesura, cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 101"> <title>Ph.</title> 101</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1369"> Ph. 1369</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1021."> <title>Ant.</title> 1021.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καιρὸς_38"> is almost personified here: <ref n="Soph. El. 75">cp. 75</ref>: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 466"> <title>Ph.</title> 466</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">καιρὸς γὰρ καλεῖ</quote>: <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 837 <quote xml:lang="grc">καιρὸς...πάντων γνώμαν ἴσχων</quote> (n.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἴσθι_38">, in the pregnant sense of ‘take care to know,’ ‘acquaint thyself with.’ Similarly in <ref n="Soph. OC 1149"> <title>O. C.</title> 1149</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἴσει</quote>=<quote xml:lang="grc">μαθήσει</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γήρᾳ_42"> refers to the change in his appearance; </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χρόνῳ_μακρῷ_42">, to the lapse of seven years (v. 14), which may have caused him to be forgotten.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐδ_ὑποπτεύσουσιν_42"> follows </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐ_γνῶσι_42">, as in <ref n="Soph. OC 450"> <title>O. C.</title> 450</ref> ff. <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδ’ ἥξει</quote> follows <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ μὴ λάχωσι</quote>. The latter example is so far less harsh that the verbs have different subjects; but it shows how these two forms of future statements could be strictly co-ordinated. No correction is probable. (<quote xml:lang="grc">ὑποπτεύσωσιν</quote> would require, of course, <quote xml:lang="grc">μηδ’</quote>.)</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὧδ_ἠνθισμένον_42">, with grey hair: <ref n="Soph. OT 742"> <title>O. T.</title> 742</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">λευκανθὲς κάρα</quote>: Erinna fr. 2 <quote xml:lang="grc">παυρολόγοι πολιαί, ταὶ γήραος ἄνθεα θνατοῖς</quote>. The schol., objecting that such a change would not suffice to disguise him, takes the word as=<quote xml:lang="grc">ἠσκημένον</quote> (an impossible sense for it). Another view strangely refers it to ‘a Phocian dress spangled with flowers’ (Paley). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Φωκεύς_45"> should not be changed to <quote xml:lang="grc">Φωκέως</quote>: it was desirable that the messenger himself should seem an alien. Similarly Orestes and Pylades come as <quote xml:lang="grc">Φωκῆς ἄνδρες</quote> (1107).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὁ_γὰρ_45">: other instances of the art., as demonstr. pron., immediately followed by <quote xml:lang="grc">γάρ</quote> (and not by <quote xml:lang="grc">μέν</quote> or <quote xml:lang="grc">δέ</quote>) are, <ref n="Soph. OT 1082"> <title>O. T.</title> 1082</ref>(<quote xml:lang="grc">τῆς</quote>), <ref n="Soph. OT 1102"> O. T. 1102</ref>(<quote xml:lang="grc">τῷ</quote>), <ref n="Soph. Phil. 154"> <title>Ph.</title> 154</ref>(<quote xml:lang="grc">τό</quote>). The art. <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ, ἡ</quote>, when it stands as demonstr. pron., is sometimes written <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅ, ἥ</quote>: a practice which is recognised by Eustathius (p. 23. 3, on <ref n="Hom. Il. 1.9"> <title>Il.</title> 1. 9</ref>), but which seems to rest on no good ground. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μέγιστος_46">, as in <quote xml:lang="grc">φίλος μέγιστος</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. Phil. 586"> <title>Ph.</title> 586</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1331"> <title>Ai.</title> 1331</ref>) simply, ‘greatest’ friend; not, ‘most powerful.’</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τυγχάνει_46">, without <quote xml:lang="grc">ὤν</quote>: <ref n="Soph. El. 313">cp. 313</ref>, <ref n="Soph. El. 1457">1457</ref>: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 9"> <title>Ai.</title> 9</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔνδον... τυγχάνει</quote> </cit>. Ast collects some ten examples from Plato; a few are subject to the doubt whether <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄν</quote> or <quote xml:lang="grc">ὤν</quote> has not dropped out after a like termination; but that does not apply to (<foreign xml:lang="lat">e.g.</foreign>) <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Hipp. Maj. 300a"> <title>Hipp. Ma.</title> 300 A</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡ δἰ ἀκοῆς ἡδονὴ...τυγχάνει καλή</quote> </cit>, or <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Tim. 61d"> <title>Tim.</title> 61 D</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τυγχάνει...δυνατὰ ἱκανῶς λεχθῆναι</quote> </cit>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δορυξένων_46">: a word applied by the tragedians to a prince or chief who is in armed alliance with the head of another state: see on <ref n="Soph. OC 632."> <title>O. C.</title> 632.</ref> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅρκον_47">, Reiske's correction of </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅρκῳ_47">, seems right. With <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅρκῳ</quote>, the choice is between two explanations. (1) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄγγελλε ὅρκῳ, προστιθεὶς</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">ὅρκον</quote>). This is exceedingly awkward, and is not really similar to <ref n="Aristoph. Birds 1004">Ar. <title>Av.</title> 1004</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀρθῷ μετρήσω κανόνι προστιθείς</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">κανόνα</quote>): for <quote xml:lang="grc">μετρήσω</quote> is most naturally followed by the dative of the instrument; while <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀγγέλλειν ὅρκῳ</quote> would be a strange phrase. (2) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄγγελλε ὅρκῳ προστιθεὶς</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ ἀγγελλόμενα</quote>): explained as an ‘inversion’ of <quote xml:lang="grc">προστιθεὶς ὅρκον τοῖς ἀγγελλομένοις</quote>. But it is hard to see how ‘adding the report to an oath’ could stand for ‘adding an oath to the report.’</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀναγκαίας_τύχης_48">, an accident ordained by <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀναγκη</quote>, fate ( <ref n="Soph. OC 605"> <title>O. C.</title> 605</ref>). The phrase recurs, with a somewhat different context, in <ref n="Soph. Aj. 485"> <title>Ai.</title> 485</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Aj. 803."> Ai. 803.</ref> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄθλοισι_48"> (from <quote xml:lang="grc">ἆθλος</quote>) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Πυθικοῖσιν_48">. Here and at v. 682 the schol. notes the anachronism,—to which Attic Tragedy was wholly indifferent. From very early times there was an <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀγών</quote> at Delphi, but for music and poetry only. Athletic contests were first added when, on the conquest of Crisa by the Delphic Amphictyony, the festival was revived with a new splendour. The year Ol. 48. 3, 586 B.C. was that from which the Pythiads were dated ( <ref n="Paus. 10. 7. 3">Paus. 10. 7. 3</ref>). Cp. Curtius, <title>Hist. Gr.</title> 1. p. 266.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τροχηλάτων_48"> implies ‘rapid,’ ‘whirling’: cp. <title>I. T.</title> 82 <quote xml:lang="grc">τροχηλάτου | μανίας.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἑστάτω_48">: be so ‘constituted’: <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, the whole story is to rest upon this basis. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Theaet. 171d">Plat. <title>Theaet.</title> 171 D</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ταύτῃ ἂν μάλιστα ἵστασθαι τὸν λόγον</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

data.quote:
ταύτῃ ἂν μάλιστα ἵστασθαι τὸν
                                        λόγον

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐφίετο_51"> (<foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ Φοῖβος</quote>, v. 35), like impf. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκέλευε.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λοιβαῖσι_51">, a general word, which could mean either the <quote xml:lang="grc">χοαί</quote> poured to the <quote xml:lang="grc">νέρτεροι</quote>, or the <quote xml:lang="grc">σπονδαί</quote> to the <quote xml:lang="grc">ὕπατοι</quote>. In this case, milk (perhaps mixed with honey) was poured on the mound (894).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καρατόμοις_χλιδαῖς_51">, ‘ornaments’ (luxuriant locks) ‘cut from the head.’ (This adj. elsewhere=‘beheaded.’) Cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 223">Soph. <title>Ph.</title> 223</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπιμένει με κόμας ἐμᾶς</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">δεῦσαι παρθένιον χλιδάν</quote>. The phrase is of the same type as <quote xml:lang="grc">κτύπος διόβολος</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. OC 1464">Soph. <title>O. C.</title> 1464</ref>) or <quote xml:lang="grc">αὔχημα εὔιππον</quote> (<foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 711). Cp. <ref n="Aesch. Lib. 7">Aesch. <title>Ch.</title> 7</ref>, where Orestes brings a <quote xml:lang="grc">πλόκαμος πενθητήριος</quote> to his father's grave.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="στέψαντες_51">: <ref n="Soph. El. 441">cp. 441</ref>: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 431"> <title>Ant.</title> 431</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">χοαῖσι τρισπόνδοισι τὸν νέκυν στέφει</quote>. Besides the offerings named here, flowers are mentioned below (896).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄψορρον_πάλιν_51">: <ref n="Soph. OT 430"> <title>O. T.</title> 430</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ πάλιν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄψορρος ὄκων τῶνδ’ ἀποστραφεὶς ἄπει</quote>; </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τύπωμα_54">, anything <emph>formed</emph> or <emph>moulded</emph>; <ref n="Soph. Phil. 162">Soph. <title>Ph.</title> 162</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μορφῆς τύπωμα</quote>, ‘the outline of his form.’ The vague word is here defined by </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χαλκόπλευρον_54">: which may be a reminiscence of the phrase used by Aesch. ( <ref n="Aesch. Lib. 686">Aesch. <title>Ch.</title> 686</ref>) in the same context, <quote xml:lang="grc">λέβητος χαλκέου πλευρώματα</quote>. The cinerary urn is described below as <quote xml:lang="grc">βραχὺς χαλκὸς</quote> (757), <quote xml:lang="grc">τεῦχος</quote> (1114), <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄγγος</quote> (1118), <quote xml:lang="grc">λέβης</quote> (1401). Cp. fr. 348 <quote xml:lang="grc">χαλκηλάτους λέβητας</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἠρμένοι_54">, pass.,=<quote xml:lang="grc">ἠρμένον ἔχοντες</quote> (schol.). Cp. Aeschin. or. 3 § 164 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπιστολὰς ἃς ἐξηρτημένος ἐκ τῶν δακτύλων περιῄεις</quote> (as <cit> <bibl n="Hor. S. 1.6.74">Hor. <title>Sat.</title> 1. 6. 74</bibl> <quote xml:lang="lat">suspensi loculos</quote> </cit>): <ref n="Xen. Anab. 7. 4. 16">Xen. <title>Anab.</title> 7. 4. 16</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐσπασμένοι τὰ ξίφη</quote>: <cit> <bibl n="Xen. Cyrop. 6.3.24">id. <title>Cyr.</title> 6. 3. 24</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">προβεβλημένοι...τοὺς θωρακοφόρους</quote> </cit>: <ref n="Plut. Phoc. 10">Plut. <title>Phoc.</title> 10</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πώγωνα...καθειμένος</quote>. See also <ref n="Soph. Trach. 157">Soph. <title>Tr.</title> 157</ref> n.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="που_55"> with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_σὺ_55"> (‘thou, too, doubtless knowest’): <ref n="Soph. El. 948">cp. 948</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">παρουσίαν μὲν οἶσθα καὶ σύ που φίλων, κ.τ.λ.</quote> Since he has a definite spot in mind, <quote xml:lang="grc">που</quote> would less fitly be taken with <quote xml:lang="grc">θάμνοις</quote>, as=‘somewhere.’</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μοι_55"> is inferior. As an enclitic, it could not go with the following partic. (‘hidden by me’). It could only be a kind of ethic dat. with <quote xml:lang="grc">οἶσθα</quote> (as nearly=<quote xml:lang="grc">σύνοισθά μοι</quote>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λόγῳ_κλέπτοντες_56">: cp. I. <ref n="Hom. Il. 1.131"> <title>Il.</title> 131</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μὴ</quote>... | <quote xml:lang="grc">κλέπτε νόῳ</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 992">Soph. <title>Ph.</title> 992</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κλέψας λόγοισιν ὥσθ’ ἃ βούλομαι τυχεῖν</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φέρωμεν_56"> is clearly right: it depends on <quote xml:lang="grc">ἥξομεν κ.τ.λ.</quote> (53), and continues the exposition of the plan which he is about to execute. <quote xml:lang="grc">φέροιμεν</quote> could depend only upon <quote xml:lang="grc">κεκρυμμένον</quote> (‘which <emph>was hidden</emph> in order that we <emph>might</emph> bring,’ etc.). Similarly in <ref n="Soph. OC 11"> <title>O. C.</title> 11</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πυθώμεθα</quote> has been corrupted in the MSS. to <quote xml:lang="grc">πυθοίμεθα</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔρρει_56">=<quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπόλωλε</quote>: <ref n="Soph. El. 925">cp. 925</ref>: <ref n="Soph. OT 560"> <title>O. T.</title> 560</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄφαντος ἔρρει</quote> (Laïus).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δέμας_56">, of a corpse, as 756, 1161: in Homer, always of the living ( <ref n="Soph. Ant. 205"> <title>Ant.</title> 205</ref> n.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τί_λυπεῖ_59"> <quote xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</quote>: <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, ‘It is true that it is ill-omened for the living to be described as dead; but why, in this case, need I care for the omen?’ Cp. <title>Helen.</title> 1050 (Helen to Menelaüs): <quote xml:lang="grc">βούλει λέγεσθαι μὴ θανὼν τεθνηκέναι</quote>; He replies:—<quote xml:lang="grc">κακὸς μὲν ὄρνις· εἰ δὲ κερδανῶ λέγων</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἕτοιμός εἰμι μὴ θανὼν λόγῳ θανεῖν. </quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔργοισι_59">: the plur., as in <ref n="Soph. OC 782"> <title>O. C.</title> 782</ref>(n.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κἀξενέγκωμαι_59">, ‘carry off’ from the enterprise: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 497"> <title>Tr.</title> 497</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μέγα τι σθένος ἁ Κύπρις ἐκφέρεται νίκας ἀεί</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δοκῶ_μέν_61">: <ref n="Soph. El. 547">cp. 547</ref>: <ref n="Soph. OC 995"> <title>O. C.</title> 995</ref> n.— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σὺν_κέρδει_61"> (<quote xml:lang="grc">ὄν</quote>), when fraught with gain: <ref n="Soph. El. 899">cp. 899</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς δ’ ἐν γαλήνῃ</quote> (<foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄντα</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">πάντ’ ἐδερκόμην τόπον</quote>. For <quote xml:lang="grc">σύν</quote>, <ref n="Soph. El. 430">cp. 430</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">σὺν κακῷ.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κακόν_61">, ‘ill-omened’; <ref n="Soph. OC 1433"> <title>O. C.</title> 1433</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1001."> <title>Ant.</title> 1001.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πολλάκις_62">. There were many such popular stories. Pythagoras was said to have hidden himself in a subterranean chamber, causing his death to be reported; and when he reappeared, he was supposed to have been born anew (schol.). Herodotus tells a similar story of the Thracian Salmoxis, a slave and disciple of Pythagoras, who thus converted the Thracians to a doctrine of immortality (4. 95). So, too, the poet Aristeas of Proconnesus disappeared for seven years,—as a sort of preliminary advertisement, it would seem, to his poem the <title>Arimaspeia</title> ( <ref n="Hdt. 4. 14">Her. 4. 14</ref>). It is vain to ask what particular story or stories Sophocles was thinking of; very possibly he knew those in Herodotus (cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 337">Soph. <title>O. C.</title> 337</ref> n.); but it was enough for him that his hearers would recognise the allusion to stories of that type. Hartung thinks that the reference is to Odysseus; but, as Odysseus did not contrive the rumour of his own death, the case is not in point.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λόγῳ_θνῄσκοντας_62">: for <quote xml:lang="grc">μάτην</quote> as =‘falsely,’ <ref n="Soph. El. 1298">cp. 1298</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 345."> <title>Ph.</title> 345.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκτετίμηνται_62">. The emphatic perf. might denote either (1) permanence,— ‘they are in greater honour <emph>thenceforth</emph>’; or (2) the instantaneous result,—‘<emph>forthwith.</emph>’ Perhaps the usage of the perf. pass. of <quote xml:lang="grc">τιμάω</quote> rather favours (1). Cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1304"> <title>O. C.</title> 1304</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τετίμηνται δορί</quote> (with <ref n="Thuc. 2. 45">Thuc. 2. 45</ref>, cited there): <ref n="Hom. Od. 7.69">Hom. <title>Od.</title> 7. 69</ref>(of Arêtè) <quote xml:lang="grc">τετίμηται</quote>.— The finite verb, instead of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκτετιμημένους</quote>, by a frequent idiom; <ref n="Soph. El. 192">cp. 192</ref> (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἀμφίσταμαι</quote>): <ref n="Soph. OC 351"> <title>O. C.</title> 351</ref> n.—<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκτιμάω</quote> is rare in classical Greek. <ref n="Aristot. Oec. 2.33">Arist. <title>Oec.</title> 2. 33</ref>(p. 1352 <emph>b</emph> 5) has <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκτετιμημένα</quote> as=‘things on which a high price is set,’ opp. to <quote xml:lang="grc">εὔωνα</quote>, ‘cheap.’ </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡς_65">, ‘as,’ seems better here than <quote xml:lang="grc">ὣς</quote>, ‘thus.’ It gives a smoother transition; and it is also more in accord with usage. Except in the phrases <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδ’ ὥς</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1042"> <title>Ant.</title> 1042</ref>), <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ ὥς</quote>, etc., Attic writers seldom use <quote xml:lang="grc">ὥς</quote>, ‘thus.’ Among the rare instances are <ref n="Aesch. Ag. 930">Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 930</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ πάντα δ’ ὣς πράσσοιμεν</quote>: <ref n="Plat. Prot. 326D">Plat. <title>Prot.</title> 326D</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὥσπερ οἱ γραμματισταὶ..., ὣς δὲ καὶ ἡ πόλις</quote>: <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> p. 338 A <quote xml:lang="grc">ὣς</quote> (<emph>v.l.</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">οὖν ποιήσετε</quote>: <ref n="Thuc. 3. 37">Thuc. 3. 37</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὣς οὖν χρὴ καὶ ἡμᾶς ποιοῦντας</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κἄμ_ἐπαυχῶ_65">: for the accus. (though the pron. refers to the subject of the verb), <ref n="Soph. El. 470">cp. 470</ref> f., <ref n="Soph. Trach. 706"> <title>Tr.</title> 706</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄπο_65">, not merely ‘after’ it, but as a result of it; cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 695"> <title>Ant.</title> 695</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 408."> <title>Ph.</title> 408.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δεδορκότ_65">,=<quote xml:lang="grc">βλέποντα</quote>, living: <ref n="Aesch. Eum. 322">Aesch. <title>Eum.</title> 322</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλαοῖσι καὶ δεδορκόσιν.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐχθροῖς_65"> is best taken with </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λάμψειν_65">: ‘alive, I shall shine as a star upon them,’ <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> to their terror.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄστρον_65">: not specifically ‘a <emph>baleful</emph> star,’—as when Achilles ( <ref n="Hom. Il. 22.26"> <title>Il.</title> 22. 26</ref> ff.) and Hector (<foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 11. 62) are likened to Seirius. It is simply an image of splendour; but to his foes, of course, he will prove an <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔλιος ἀστήρ</quote>.—Whitelaw: ‘So living, doubt not, from this falsehood's cloud | I on my dazzled foes, starlike, shall break.’</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔτι_65">, menacing: <ref n="Soph. El. 471">cp. 471</ref>: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 257."> <title>Tr.</title> 257.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πατρῴα_γῆ_67"> <quote xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</quote>: cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1040"> <title>Ph.</title> 1040</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’, ὦ πατρῴα γῆ θεοί τ’ ἐπόψιοι.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εὐτυχοῦντα_67">, proleptic: <ref n="Soph. El. 162">cp. 162</ref> f.: <ref n="Soph. OC 487"> <title>O. C.</title> 487</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">δέχεσθαι τὸν ἱκέτην σωτήριον.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὁδοῖς_67">, of a single journey: <title>O.C.</title> 553 (n.), <ref n="Soph. Ant. 226."> <title>Ant.</title> 226.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καθαρτής_70">: so in <ref n="Aesch. Lib. 968">Aesch. <title>Ch.</title> 968</ref> ff. the avenger is to drive the <quote xml:lang="grc">μύσος</quote> from the hearth, <quote xml:lang="grc">καθαρμοῖσιν ἀτᾶν ἐλατηρίοις.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρὸς_ὡρμημένος_70">: <ref n="Aesch. Lib. 940">Aesch. <title>Ch.</title> 940</ref>(of Orestes) <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ πυθόχρηστος φυγὰς</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">θεόθεν εὖ φραδαῖσιν ὡρμημένος</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλ_ἀρχέπλουτον_72">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">πέμψατε</quote> or the like, to be supplied from <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀποστείλητε</quote>, as <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐδῶ</quote> in <ref n="Soph. OT 241"> <title>O. T.</title> 241</ref> from <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπαυδῶ, δεῖ</quote> in <ref n="Soph. OC 1404"> <title>O. C.</title> 1404</ref> from <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ ἔξεστι, ἕκαστος</quote> in <ref n="Soph. Ant. 263"> <title>Ant.</title> 263</ref> from <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδείς</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀρχέπλουτον_72">, ‘<emph>master of</emph> my possessions’; cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀρχέλαος, ἀρχέπολις</quote> ( <ref n="Pind. P. 9">Pind. <title>P.</title> 9. 58</ref>). Others understand, ‘having <emph>ancient</emph> wealth,’=<quote xml:lang="grc">ἀρχαιόπλουτον</quote>. If, however, the verbal part of the compound denoted ‘beginning’ rather than ‘ruling,’ analogy would suggest that <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀρχέπλουτος</quote> should mean, ‘a <emph>founder</emph> of wealth’; cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀρχέκακος</quote> ( <ref n="Hom. Il. 5.63"> <title>Il.</title> 5. 63</ref>), <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀρχέχορος</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">ποῦς</quote>, <ref n="Eur. Tro. 151">Eur. <title>Tro.</title> 151</ref>), <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀρχέγονος</quote>, etc. In Pindar <title>P.</title> 4. 110, where Jason speaks of his <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀρχεδικᾶν τοκέων</quote>, the sense is strictly, ‘ancestors who <emph>founded</emph> the right’ to possession,—Cretheus, father of Aeson, having been the founder of Iolcus; not merely, ‘who held an ancient right.’—As to the form of the word, see Appendix.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καταστάτην_72">, as <emph>restoring</emph> its fortunes, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀποκαθιστάντα</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἴρηκα_ταῦτα_74">, one of those formulas which serve to close a speech, like <quote xml:lang="grc">πάντ’ ἐπίστασαι</quote>, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 402"> <title>Ant.</title> 402</ref> f. The old edd. write <quote xml:lang="grc">μὲν νῦν</quote>, which would mean that he reserved further details for another time.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μελέσθω_74"> is probably impers. (as <quote xml:lang="grc">μέλεται</quote> in Theocr. 1. 53); though <quote xml:lang="grc">μέλεταί τί μοι</quote> is a less rare constr. for this midd. form (<ref n="Soph. El. 1436">cp. 1436</ref>). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπιστάτης_76">, as controlling and regulating action; <ref n="Soph. El. 39">cp. 39</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἰώ_δύστηνος_77">. Dindorf deletes <quote xml:lang="grc">δύστηνος</quote>, because <quote xml:lang="grc">ἰώ μοι μοί</quote> is a frequent prelude to the entrance of an actor who laments (<emph>e.g. </emph> <ref n="Soph. Aj. 333"> Ai. 333</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Aj. 893"> Ai. 893</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Aj. 974"> Ai. 974</ref>). But why should we assume that this formula was invariable? An adj. is often added to such interjections; <ref n="Soph. Ant. 850"> <title>Ant.</title> 850</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἰὼ δύστανος</quote>: <ref n="Soph. OC 876"> <title>O. C.</title> 876</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἰὼ τάλας</quote>: <ref n="Soph. OT 1307"> <title>O. T.</title> 1307</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">αἰαῖ, φεῦ φεῦ, δύστανος</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_μὴν_78">, announcing a new comer: 1422: <ref n="Soph. OC 549"> <title>O. C.</title> 549</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θυρῶν_78"> is perhaps best taken as denoting <emph>the quarter whence</emph> the sound strikes the ear, so that it goes with <quote xml:lang="grc">αἰσθέσθαι</quote> (‘hear <emph>from</emph> the doors’). The order of the words, and the rhythm, favour this. It might, however, be a local gen., ‘<emph>at</emph> the doors,’ going with <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑποστενούσης</quote>: <ref n="Soph. El. 900">cp. 900</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔνδον_78">, did not the wide separation of the words make this so awkward. When Sophocles places a genitive long before the preposition which governs it, the genitive is usu. <emph>causal;</emph> and, as this sense is readily perceived, the delay of the prep. then matters less. See, <foreign xml:lang="lat">e.g.</foreign>, 578 f. <quote xml:lang="grc">τούτου...οὕνεκ’</quote>: <ref n="Soph. OT 857"> <title>O. T.</title> 857</ref> f. <quote xml:lang="grc">μαντείας...οὕνεκ’</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 598"> <title>Ph.</title> 598</ref> f. <quote xml:lang="grc">τίνος ...χάριν</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προσπόλων_τινὸς_78">: the old man conjectures that it is a slave, because a daughter of the house was not to be expected at the gates, especially at such an early hour: <ref n="Soph. El. 518">cp. 518</ref> n. But Orestes fancies that he recognises the voice. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θέλεις__80"> Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 651"> <title>O. T.</title> 651</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">θέλεις...εἰκάθω;</quote> </cit> <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Phil. 761"> <title>Ph.</title> 761</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">βούλει λάβωμαι;</quote> </cit> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κἀνακούσωμεν_80">, was taken by some from <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνακούω</quote>, and explained as ‘listen further’ (schol. in E). But no <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνακούω</quote> is extant; nor does it seem probable. If the traditional reading is sound, it must be referred to <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐνακούω</quote>. The only authority for that word, in Greek of the classical age, is Hippocrates, who uses it with two peculiar meanings:—(1) ‘to be sensitive,’ to sound, as <title>De Corde</title>, Kühn vol. I. p. 488 <quote xml:lang="grc">ταῦτα γὰρ οὐκ ἐνακούουσιν ἰαχῆς</quote>, or, generally, to any impression, as <title>De Humid.</title>, K. I. p. 157 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐνακούειν πολλῶν</quote>. (2) ‘To be obedient, amenable,’ to curative treatment; as <title>De artic.</title>, K. III. p. 229 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐνακούει τὰ τοιαῦτα τῆς ἰητρείης</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κἀπακούσωμεν_80">, is thus highly plausible. Among recent editors who receive it are Wecklein, Bellermann, and Michaelis A. (in his revision of Jahn's ed.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μηδὲν_πρόσθεν_82">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">ποιῶμεν</quote>: cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 497"> <title>Ant.</title> 497</ref> n.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὰ_Λοξίου_82">, his commands, v. 51.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀρχηγετεῖν_82"> (a verb which occurs only here), not merely=<quote xml:lang="grc">ἄρχεσθαι</quote>, but rather ‘to make an <emph>auspicious</emph> beginning’ (Lat. <emph>auspicari</emph>), as <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀρχηγέτης</quote> denoted the god or hero to whom a city or family traced its origin (<title>O.C.</title> 60 n.). This title was given especially to Apollo: <cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">Φοῖβος γὰρ ἀεὶ πολίεσσι φιληδεῖ | κτιζομένῃς</quote> <bibl n="Call. Ap. 54">(Callim. <title>Hym. Apoll.</title> 54)</bibl> </cit>. The Chalcidians of Euboea, when they founded Naxos in Sicily, placed an altar of Apollo <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἀρχηγέτης</quote> before the city ( <ref n="Thuc. 6. 3">Thuc. 6. 3</ref>: cp. Appian <title>Bell. Civ.</title> 5. 109). Pindar speaks of <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ...ἀρχαγέτας</quote>:..<quote xml:lang="grc">Ἀπόλλων</quote> in connection with the founding of Cyrene by Battus. </gloss>

data.quote:
Φοῖβος γὰρ ἀεὶ πολίεσσι φιληδεῖ |
                                        κτιζομένῃς

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πατρὸς_84">, possessive gen., as the offerings are due to him: cp. <ref n="Eur. Alc. 613">Eur. <title>Alc.</title> 613</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">νερτέρων ἀγάλματα.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λουτρά_84">, the <quote xml:lang="grc">λοιβαί</quote> of v. 52, the <quote xml:lang="grc">πηγαὶ γάλακτος</quote> of 895, regarded as offerings demanded by purity. So in v. 434 <quote xml:lang="grc">λουτρά</quote> are the <quote xml:lang="grc">χοαί</quote> of v. 406. Hesychius records the phrase <quote xml:lang="grc">χθόνια λουτρά</quote> in this sense. [But in <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1667">Soph. <title>Ph.</title> 1667</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">νεκρῷ λουτρὰ περιβαλεῖν</quote> refers to washing the corpse.]</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φέρει_ἡμῖν_84">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> brings (so as to place it) <emph>in our power;</emph> for this <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπί</quote>, cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 66"> <title>O. C.</title> 66</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1003."> <title>Ph.</title> 1003.</ref>Not, ‘brings <emph>in our case</emph>’ (like <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπ’ ἀνδρὶ τῷδ’</quote>, <ref n="Soph. OT 829"> <title>O. T.</title> 829</ref> n.); nor, ‘brings <emph>upon</emph> us’ ( <ref n="Soph. OC 1472"> <title>O. C.</title> 1472</ref>).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νίκην_84">, the ultimate victory: </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κράτος_δρωμένων_84">, the upper hand, the mastery, in our course of action. For the combination, cp. <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Laws 962a">Plat. <title>Legg.</title> 962 A</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">νίκην καὶ κράτος πολεμίων</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Dem. 19.130">Dem. or. 19 § 130</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">κράτος καὶ νίκην πολέμου</quote> </cit> (reversed by Plut. <title>Mor.</title>p. 412 C <quote xml:lang="grc">νίκην καὶ κράτος τοῦ πολέμου</quote>). For the pres. part., <ref n="Soph. El. 1333">cp. 1333</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ δρώμεν’</quote>, ‘your plans’; <ref n="Soph. Trach. 588."> <title>Tr.</title> 588.</ref>So <ref n="Soph. OC 116"> <title>O. C.</title> 116</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τῶν ποιουμένων</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὦ_ἁγνὸν_86">: the Sun-god abhors impurity ( <ref n="Soph. OT 1425"> <title>O. T.</title> 1425</ref> ff.). So too the <quote xml:lang="grc">αἰθήρ</quote> is <quote xml:lang="grc">ἁγνός</quote> ( <ref n="Aesch. PB 281">Aesch. <title>P. V.</title> 281</ref>). —These opening words beautifully express the sense of relief with which she passes from her sad vigil in the polluted house to the clear sunlight and free air of morning.—Similar utterances of sorrow to the elements are those in <ref n="Aesch. PB 88">Aesch. <title>P. V.</title> 88</ref> ff., <ref n="Eur. Andr. 91">Eur. <title>Andr.</title> 91</ref> ff.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γῆς_ἀήρ_86">, ‘air coextensive with earth,’—having a <quote xml:lang="grc">μοῖρα</quote>, a domain in space, equal to that of earth. Cp. Hamlet's phrase, ‘this goodly frame, the earth...this most excellent canopy, the air’ <ref>(2. 2. 311)</ref>. <cit> <bibl n="Hes. Th. 126">Hes. <title>Th.</title> 126</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">Γαῖα δέ τοι πρῶτον μὲν ἐγείνατο ἶσον ἑαυτῇ | Οὐρανὸν ἀστερόενθ’, ἵνα μιν περὶ πάντα καλύπτοι</quote> </cit>. A genitive after <quote xml:lang="grc">ἰσόμοιρος</quote> usu. denotes that <emph>in which</emph> persons share alike (as <ref n="Isaeus 6.25">Isae. or. 6 § 25</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἰσομοίρους...τῶν πατρῴων</quote>). Here it denotes the partner. Cp. <title>De Mund.</title> 6. 18 (p. 399 <emph>a</emph> 8) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἥλιος...καὶ οἱ τούτου</quote> [not <quote xml:lang="grc">τούτῳ</quote>] <quote xml:lang="grc">ἰσόδρομοι, ὅ τε Φωσφόρος καὶ ὁ Ἑρμῆς</quote>. So adjectives of similarity or equivalence can take a gen. (as <quote xml:lang="grc">προσφερής</quote> <ref n="Eur. Her. 130">Eur. <title>H. F.</title> 131</ref>, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀντίπαλος</quote> <ref n="Pind. O. 8">Pind. <title>O.</title> 8. 71</ref>). Such genitives are analogous to those which follow words expressing community or participation, as <quote xml:lang="grc">κοινός, συγγενής</quote>, etc.—Others, taking <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀήρ</quote> as that which is intermediate between <quote xml:lang="grc">γῆ</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">αἰθήρ</quote>, explain, ‘air, which has the same share in earth (as it has in <quote xml:lang="grc">αἰθήρ</quote>),’ —comparing <ref n="Aristoph. Birds 187">Ar. <title>Av.</title> 187</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν μέσῳ δήπουθεν ἀήρ ἐστι γῆς</quote> (where <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ οὐρανοῦ</quote> is to be supplied). This is very forced.</gloss>

data.quote:
Γαῖα δέ τοι πρῶτον μὲν ἐγείνατο ἶσον ἑαυτῇ
                                        | Οὐρανὸν ἀστερόενθ’, ἵνα μιν περὶ πάντα καλύπτοι

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἰσόμοιῤ_86"> is a necessary correction of </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἰσόμοιρος_86"> (cr. n.), which would require <quote xml:lang="grc">α^</quote> in <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀήρ</quote>,—a quantity found only in pseudoPhocylides 108, and an epigram quoted by Eustathius p. 17. 46. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πολλὰς_ᾔσθου_88">. Each of these two verses is an anapaestic dimeter lacking one long syllable (‘catalectic’), <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, a ‘paroemiac.’ Two successive paroemiacs were admissible only in anapaests of this ‘free’ or lyric character (n. on 86—120). These paroemiacs are purely spondaic; as ‘free’ anapaests also admitted the converse license, of resolving the long syllables, except the last, of the paroemiac ( <title>I. T.</title> 130 <quote xml:lang="grc">πόδα παρθένιον ὅσιον ὁσίας</quote>). Synesius, the bishop of Ptolemais, composed his fifth hymn wholly in these spondaic paroemiacs, a weighty and solemn measure; <quote xml:lang="grc">Ὑμνώμεν κοῦρον κούρας</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">νύμφας οὐ νυμφευθείσας, κ.τ.λ.</quote> See W. Christ, <title>Metrik</title> § 293 (2nd ed.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀντήρεις_88">, lit. ‘set opposite’ (hence of an ‘adversary,’ <ref n="Soph. Phil. 754">Soph. <title>Ph.</title> 754</ref>), here, ‘dealt from opposite,’ striking <emph>full</emph> on the breast, like <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνταία...πλαγά</quote> (195 f.). <quote xml:lang="grc">θρῆνος</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">κοπετός</quote> are similarly combined in <ref n="Soph. Aj. 631">Soph. <title>Ai.</title> 631</ref> ff. <quote xml:lang="grc">θρηνήσει, χερόπλακτοι δ’</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν στέρνοισι πεσοῦνται</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">δοῦποι</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πληγὰς_90"> must be preferred to </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πλαγὰς_90"> here, unless we are to write <quote xml:lang="grc">γᾶς</quote> (in 87), etc. As a rule, certainly, Doricism is a mark of lyric (as dist. from marching) anapaests; see <title>Ant.</title>, append. p. 248, and cp. W. Christ, <title>Metrik</title> § 288. But the fact that these anapaests, though lyric in general character, precede the first lyrics of the Chorus, may have led the poet to prefer Attic forms, as in the anapaestic Parodos of the <title>Ajax</title> (134 ff., where <quote xml:lang="grc">πληγὴ</quote> occurs in 137). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὑπολειφθῇ_91">, lit., ‘falls behind’; here =‘fails,’ like the intr. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκλέλοιπεν</quote> in 19. We need not conjecture <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑπολείπῃ</quote>, though the intrans. use is frequent with Aristotle. The subjunct. can follow </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ᾔσθου_91">, since the thought is, ‘hast heard’ (and still hearest). For the converse (an optat. after a primary tense which implies a secondary), cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 11"> <title>O. C.</title> 11</ref> n. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὰ_παννυχίδων_92"> is best taken as acc. governed by </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ξυνίσας_92">, rather than as a prefatory acc. of reference (‘as to...’): </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅσα_θρηνῶ_92"> (94) is epexegetic of it. <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ παννυχίδων</quote>, a periphrasis like <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ τῶν πολέμων</quote> ( <ref n="Thuc. 2. 11">Thuc. 2. 11</ref>), <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ τῆς τύχης</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1202">Soph. <title>Ph.</title> 1202</ref>), etc. The <quote xml:lang="grc">παννυχὶς</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἑορτή</quote>) was properly a joyous torch-light festival, as at the Lenaea ( <ref n="Aristoph. Frogs 371">Ar. <title>Ran.</title> 371</ref>), or the Bendideia ( <ref n="Plat. Rep. 328A">Plat. <title>Rep.</title> 328A</ref>). The irony is like that of <quote xml:lang="grc">κείνου χρηστήρια τἀνδρός</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. Aj. 220">Soph. <title>Ai.</title> 220</ref>), <quote xml:lang="grc">παιὰν Ἐρινύων</quote> ( <ref n="Aesch. Ag. 645">Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 645</ref>).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἤδη_92"> (which has been needlessly suspected) means merely, ‘ere now,’ implying the long duration of her grief.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἴκων_92"> is better than the proposed </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λέκτρων_92">, since (<emph>a</emph>) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μογερῶν_92"> suggests, not merely her own sorrow, but the troubles of the house; and (<emph>b</emph>) the antithesis is between her laments without and within the palace. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅσα_δύστηνον_94">: the only anapaestic dimeter in this <quote xml:lang="grc">θρῆνος</quote> which has not the caesura after the second foot. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κατὰ_αἶαν_95">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> at Troy. The whole form of this passage (95—99) seems clearly to show a reminiscence of <ref n="Hom. Od. 11.406"> <title>Od.</title> 11. 406</ref>—411, where the shade of Agamemnon says to Odysseus,— <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔτ’ ἐμέ γ’ ἐν νήεσσι Ποσειδάων ἐδάμασσεν</quote>,... | <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔτε μ’ ἀνάρσιοι ἄνδρες ἐδηλήσαντ’ ἐπὶ χέρσου</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλά μοι Αἴγισθος τεύξας θάνατόν τε μόρον τε</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔκτα σὺν οὐλομένῃ ἀλόχῳ, οἶκόνδε καλέσσας</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">δειπνίσσας, ὤς τίς τε κατέκτανε βοῦν ἐπὶ φάτνῃ</quote>. Sophocles follows the Homeric version in conceiving Agamemnon as slain at a banquet (194, <ref n="Hom. Od. 11.203"> Od. 203</ref>); and </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐξένισεν_95"> in v. 96 suggests a contrast with the entertainment which had been prepared for him at home.—Cp. also <ref n="Aesch. Eum. 625">Aesch. <title>Eum.</title> 625</ref> ff. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Ἄρης_96"> with <quote xml:lang="grc">α—</quote> (after Homeric precedent, <ref n="Hom. Il. 5.31"> <title>Il.</title> 5. 31</ref> etc.), as in <ref n="Soph. Ant. 139"> <title>Ant.</title> 139</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Aj. 254"> <title>Ai.</title> 254</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Aj. 614."> Ai. 614.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐξένισεν_96">. The <quote xml:lang="grc">ξένια</quote> with which Ares welcomes his guests are wounds and death. Archilochus fr. 7 <quote xml:lang="grc">ξείνια δυσμενέσιν λυγρὰ χαριζόμενοι</quote>. <title>Helen.</title> 480 <quote xml:lang="grc">θάνατος ξένιά σοι γενήσεται</quote>. <title>Anthol.</title> 6. 9 (arrows) <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀλοὰ ξείνια δυσμενέων</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κοινολεχὴς_97">, ‘paramour,’ as in <ref n="Aesch. Ag. 1441">Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 1441</ref>Cassandra is <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡ κοινόλεκτρος τοῦδε θεσφατηλόγος</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅπως_ὑλοτόμοι_98">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, with as little pity. But in <ref n="Hom. Il. 13.389"> <title>Il.</title> 13. 389</ref> ff., <quote xml:lang="grc">ἤριπε δ’, ὡς ὅτε τις δρῦς ἤριπεν</quote>, the point is the crash with which the stately tree falls.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σχίζουσι_98">, historic pres., following an aor. ( <ref n="Soph. Trach. 267"> <title>Tr.</title> 267</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Trach. 702"> Tr. 702</ref>); as it often also precedes it ( <ref n="Soph. Ant. 269"> <title>Ant.</title> 269</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 406"> Ant. 406</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 419"> Ant. 419</ref>). I. <ref n="Xen. Anab. 5. 12">Xen. <title>Anab.</title> 5. 12</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ξύλα σχίζων τις.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κάρα_98">, after <quote xml:lang="grc">ὃν</quote> (95), acc. defining the part: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1301"> <title>Ph.</title> 1301</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μέθες με...χεῖρα</quote> (n.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πέλεκει_98">: cp. <ref n="Hom. Il. 23.114"> <title>Il.</title> 23. 114</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑλοτόμους πελέκεας ἐν χερσὶν ἔχοντες</quote>. It was a two-edged axe (<cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀμφάκης,</quote> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 23.485">485</bibl> </cit>), a <quote xml:lang="grc">πέλεκυς ἀμφίστομος</quote> or <quote xml:lang="grc">δίστομος</quote>, <foreign xml:lang="lat">bipennis</foreign>, as dist. from the singleheaded axe, <quote xml:lang="grc">πέλεκυς ἑτερόστομος</quote> (Pollux 1. 137). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄλλης_100">, as in 885, instead of the more general <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄλλου.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φέρεται_100"> must be more than merely ‘<emph>proceeds</emph> from’: it implies a passionate utterance. Cp. <ref n="Pind. P. 1">Pind. <title>P.</title> 1. 87</ref>, <quote xml:lang="grc">εἴ τι καὶ φλαῦρον παραιθύσσει, μέγα τοι φέρεται</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">πὰρ σέθεν</quote>, where Gildersleeve well remarks that the image is that of sparks flying from an anvil (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἄκμονι</quote> v. 86), and renders <quote xml:lang="grc">φέρεται</quote> ‘rushes.’ Here the alternative version would be, ‘is brought as a tribute,’ but that is too weak.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀπ_ἄλλης_100"> | </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἢ_μοῦ_100"> (cr. n.) aims at making the anapaestic <quote xml:lang="grc">σύστημα</quote> (86—102) precisely equal in length to the <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀντισύστημα</quote> (103—120). But such precision cannot be demanded (see n. on 86—120). On the other hand, the proposed omission would rob these verses of their peculiar pathos, and would also leave <quote xml:lang="grc">φέρεται</quote> obscure. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλ_δὴ_103">: the combination <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλὰ...μὲν δὴ</quote> occurs also in <ref n="Soph. OT 523"> <title>O. T.</title> 523</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Trach. 627."> <title>Tr.</title> 627.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄστρων_105"> | <quote xml:lang="grc"> <emph>ῥιπάς</emph>. ῥιπή</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">ῥίπτω</quote>), ‘swing,’ ‘vibration,’ is here applied to the <emph>quivering</emph> rays of starlight; as in <ref n="Soph. Ant. 137"> <title>Ant.</title> 137</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 930"> Ant. 930</ref> to the <emph>gusts</emph> of fierce winds. (In <ref n="Soph. OC 1248"> <title>O. C.</title> 1248</ref>, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐννυχιᾶν ἀπὸ Ῥιπᾶν</quote>, the ref. is to the mountains called <quote xml:lang="grc">Ῥῖπαι</quote>: see n.) —Instead of <quote xml:lang="grc">λεύσσω μὲν...λεύσσω δὲ</quote>, we have the verb in the second clause only: cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1105"> <title>Ant.</title> 1105</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μόλις μέν, καρδίας δ’ ἐξίσταμαι</quote>. For the omission of <quote xml:lang="grc">μέν</quote> in the first of two such clauses, see on <ref n="Soph. Ant. 806"> <title>Ant.</title> 806</ref> f.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μὴ_οὐ_107">, after <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ λήξω</quote>: <ref n="Soph. OT 283"> <title>O. T.</title> 283</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τεκνολέτειῤ_107">, ‘<emph>slayer</emph> of her child’ (Itys, 148): as in <title>Suppl.</title> 60 ff. ‘the piteous bride of Tereus’ (Procne) sings of ‘her child's fate, and how he perished by her own hand.’ Cp. [Eur. ] <title>Rhes.</title> 550 <quote xml:lang="grc">παιδολέτωρ μελοποιὸς ἀηδονίς</quote>. Apollodorus follows this version, acc. to which Procne, the mother and slayer of Itys, becomes a nightingale, while her sister Philomela becomes a swallow (3. 14. 8). It may be doubted whether <quote xml:lang="grc">τεκνολέτειρα</quote> could mean merely, ‘having <emph>lost</emph> her child.’ </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπὶ_κωκυτῷ_108">: the prep. is not merely=‘with,’ but implies, ‘with <emph>continual</emph> wailing’: see n. on <ref n="Soph. Ant. 759"> <title>Ant.</title> 759</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπὶ ψόγοισι.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἠχὼ_108">, a resounding cry (of grief); cp. <ref n="Eur. Hipp. 790">Eur. <title>Hipp.</title> 790</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἴστε τίς ποτ’ ἐν δόμοις βοή</quote>; | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἠχὼ βαρεῖα προσπόλων μ’ ἀφίκετο</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Ἀΐδου_110"> gives a finer rhythm than <quote xml:lang="grc">Αιδου</quote> here. In 137 <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἀΐδα</quote> has a similar recommendation; and in 833 <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἀΐδαν</quote> is required by metre, as <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἀΐδᾳ</quote> is in <ref n="Soph. Phil. 861."> <title>Ph.</title> 861.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὦ_Ἑρμῆ_111">: <ref n="Soph. Aj. 832"> <title>Ai.</title> 832</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πομπαῖον Ἑρμῆν χθόνιον</quote>: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1548"> <title>O. C.</title> 1548</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἑ. ὁ πομπός</quote> (n.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Ἀρά_111">. So in <ref n="Soph. OC 1375"> <title>O. C.</title> 1375</ref> f. Oedipus invokes those <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀραί</quote> which he had uttered, calling upon them to be his allies (<quote xml:lang="grc">ξυμμάχους</quote>) against his sons; and afterwards separately invokes the Erinyes (1391). Sometimes, however, the Curse is itself called an Erinys ( <ref n="Aesch. Seven 70">Aesch. <title>Th.</title> 70</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἀρά τ’, Ἐρινὺς πατρὸς ἡ μεγασθενής</quote>): or <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἀραί</quote> is another title for the Erinyes collectively ( <ref n="Aesch. Eum. 417">Aesch. <title>Eum.</title> 417</ref>).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πότνια_111">, as the Erinyes are <quote xml:lang="grc">πότνιαι δεινῶπες</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. OC 84"> <title>O. C.</title> 84</ref>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σεμναὶ_112">: <ref n="Soph. OC 89"> <title>O. C.</title> 89</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">θεῶν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">σεμνῶν ἕδραν</quote> (at Colonus: cp. <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 42 n.): <ref n="Aesch. Eum. 1041">Aesch. <title>Eum.</title> 1041</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">δεῦρ’ ἴτε, σεμναί.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θεῶν_παῖδες_112">, in the general sense that they are called into existence and activity by the resolve of the gods to punish guilt: cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1075"> <title>Ant.</title> 1075</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἅιδου καὶ θεῶν Ἐρινύες</quote>. Mythologically, the Erinyes are <quote xml:lang="grc">Γῆς τε καὶ Σκότου κόραι</quote>, <ref n="Soph. OC 40"> <title>O. C.</title> 40</ref>(n.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὁρᾶθ_113">: cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 835"> <title>Ai.</title> 835</ref> f. <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰς ἀεί τε παρθένους</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀεί θ’ ὁρώσας πάντα τἀν βροτοῖς πάθη</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">σεμνὰς Ἐρινῦς τανύποδας</quote>: <ref n="Soph. OC 42"> <title>O. C.</title> 42</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰς πάνθ’ ὁρώσας</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αἳ_ὑποκλεπτομένους_114">: for the acc. with the pass. verb, cp. <ref n="Aesch. PB 171">Aesch. <title>P. V.</title> 171</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">σκῆπτρον τιμάς τ’ ἀποσυλᾶται</quote>: so <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀφαιροῦμαί τι, ἀποστεροῦμαί τι</quote>. Libanius has a reminiscence of this verse in the phrase <quote xml:lang="grc">εὐνὴν κακῶς ὑποκλέπτειν</quote> (4. p. 598. 24).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τείσασθε_115">: for the spelling, cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 810"> <title>O. T.</title> 810</ref> n. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἡμετέρου_116"> (=‘my’)...</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μοι_116">: cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 734"> <title>Ant.</title> 734</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πόλις γὰρ ἡμῖν</quote> (=<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμοὶ</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἁμὲ χρὴ τάσσειν ἐρεῖ; </quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καί_μοι_116"> should not be changed to <quote xml:lang="grc">κἀμοὶ</quote>: cp. <ref n="Soph. Trach. 684"> <title>Tr.</title> 684</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">καί μοι τάδ’ ἦν πρόρρητα</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄγειν_119"> is said of a weight, in one scale of a balance, which ‘draws up’ the weight in the opposite scale: <ref n="Dem. 22.76">Dem. or. 22 § 76</ref> (<quote xml:lang="grc">χρυσίδες</quote>, gold vessels) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄγουσα ἑκάστη μνᾶν</quote>, ‘weighing.’ So <quote xml:lang="grc">ἕλκειν</quote>, <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Minos 316a">Plat. <title>Minos</title> p. 316 A</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ πλεῖον ἕλκοντα βαρύτερα, τὰ δὲ ἔλαττον κουφότερα</quote> </cit>. Here, Electra herself—<foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, the power of endurance which she represents—is the weight in one scale, and the load of grief is the weight in the other (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἀντίρροπον</quote>). She can no longer ‘outweigh’ it,—<foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, bear up against it. The image is more forcible than the ordinary one of a burden, since it expresses the strain of the effort to maintain an equipoise between patience and suffering.</gloss>

data.quote:
τὰ πλεῖον ἕλκοντα βαρύτερα, τὰ δὲ ἔλαττον
                                        κουφότερα

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σωκῶ_119"> occurs only here and in <ref n="Aesch. Eum. 36">Aesch. <title>Eum.</title> 36</ref>: <quote xml:lang="grc">σῶκος</quote>, ‘strong,’ only in <ref n="Hom. Il. 20.72"> <title>Il.</title> 20. 72</ref>(as epithet of Hermes). The rt., acc. to Curtius (<title>Etym.</title> § 570, 5th ed.), is <emph>sa</emph>, whence <quote xml:lang="grc">σάο‐ς</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">σῶς</quote>), <quote xml:lang="grc">σῴζω</quote>, <emph>sa-nu-s.</emph> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δυστανοτάτας_121">, of guilt, as in 806: so 273 <quote xml:lang="grc">ταλαίνῃ</quote>, 275 <quote xml:lang="grc">τλήμων</quote> (and 439): <ref n="Soph. OT 888"> <title>O. T.</title> 888</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">δύσποτμος</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1026"> <title>Ant.</title> 1026</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄνολβος</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τάκεις_οἰμωγὰν_123">=<quote xml:lang="grc">ποιεῖ τακερὰν οἰμωγάν</quote>, <emph>makest a languishing</emph> lament. Cp. <ref n="Soph.
