Trach. 848"> <title>Tr.</title> 848</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τέγγει δακρύων ἄχναν</quote> (n.): <ref n="Soph. Aj. 55"> <title>Ai.</title> 55</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔκειρε...φόνον</quote>. For <quote xml:lang="grc">τήκομαι</quote>, said of pining in grief, <ref n="Soph. El. 283">cp. 283</ref>, <ref n="Eur. Med. 158">Eur. <title>Med.</title> 158</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μὴ λίαν τάκου δυρομένα σὸν εὐνάταν.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀκόρεστον_123">: cp. <ref n="Aesch. Ag. 1143">Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 1143</ref>(of the nightingale) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀκόρετος βοᾶς.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸν_πάλαι_123"> <foreign xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</foreign>, acc. depending on <quote xml:lang="grc">τάκεις οἰμωγάν</quote> as=<quote xml:lang="grc">οἰμώζεις</quote>: <ref n="Soph. El. 556">cp. 556</ref>, <ref n="Soph. El. 710">710</ref>: <ref n="Soph. OC 223"> <title>O. C.</title> 223 </ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">δέος ἴσχετε μηδὲν ὅσ’ αὐδῶ</quote> (n.).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πάλαι_123">, some seven or eight years ago (13 f., n.).— </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκ_123">, here no more than <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑπό</quote> (<title>Ph.</title> 335 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκ Φοίβου δαμείς</quote>, n.).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀθεώτατα_123">: <ref n="Soph. El. 1181">cp. 1181</ref> (n.).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κακᾷ_πρόδοτον_123">, betrayed (to death) by a dastardly hand. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χειρὶ_123">, following <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπάταις</quote>, denotes the violent deed. Sophocles thinks of both Clytaemnestra and Aegisthus as active agents in the murder (99 <quote xml:lang="grc">σχίζουσι</quote>). This is against understanding, ‘betrayed (by her) to (his) hand.’</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡς_123"> is properly an exclamation (‘how!’), as in <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς ὤφελον</quote>: here it stands, like <emph>utinam</emph>, with the optat. There are Homeric examples, as <ref n="Hom. Il. 18.107"> <title>Il.</title> 18. 107</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς ἔρις... ἀπόλοιτο</quote>: <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 22. 286 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς δή μιν σῷ ἐνὶ χροῒ πᾶν κομίσαιο</quote>: <ref n="Hom. Od. 17.243"> <title>Od.</title> 17. 243</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς ἔλθοι</quote>: which must be distinguished from instances of <quote xml:lang="grc">ὥς</quote> (‘thus’) with optat., as <ref n="Hom. Il. 14.142"> <title>Il.</title> 14. 142</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ ὁ μὲν ὣς ἀπόλοιτο</quote>: <ref n="Hom. Od. 1.47"> <title>Od.</title> 1. 47</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὣς ἀπόλοιτο καὶ ἄλλος</quote>. In Attic this <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς</quote> with optat. is rare: <ref n="Eur. Hipp. 407">Eur. <title>Hipp.</title> 407</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς ὄλοιτο παγκάκως.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὁ_πορὼν_123"> might refer to Clytaemnestra (for the masc., cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 464"> <title>Ant.</title> 464</ref> n.), but is rather general, including both the authors of the crime.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἴ_θέμις_123">, like <ref n="Soph. Trach. 809"> <title>Tr.</title> 809</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ θέμις δ’, ἐπεύχομαι</quote>: Aegisthus and Clytaemnestra are the rulers of Mycenae. Cp. <ref n="Eur. Med. 83">Eur. <title>Med.</title> 83</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄλοιτο μὲν μή· δεσπότης γάρ ἐστ’ ἐμός</quote>. And the Chorus might shrink from imprecations on the mother in her daughter's presence. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γενέθλα_128"> (fem. sing.), as 226: but <quote xml:lang="grc">γένεθλα</quote> (neut. pl.) <ref n="Soph. OT 180."> <title>O. T.</title> 180.</ref> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γενναίων_128">, in disposition (cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1636"> <title>O. C.</title> 1636</ref>).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παραμύθ_128"> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ιον_128"> might be nomin. in appos. with the subject of the verb, but is better taken as acc. in appos. with the sentence: <ref n="Soph. El. 564">cp. 564</ref> (<quote xml:lang="grc">ποινὰς</quote>), 966 (<quote xml:lang="grc">πημονὴν</quote>): <ref n="Soph. OT 603"> <title>O. T.</title> 603</ref>(n.): <ref n="Eur. Orest. 1105">Eur. <title>Or.</title> 1105</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἑλένην κτάνωμεν, Μενελέῳ λύπην πικράν</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ξυνίημι_131"> (<quote xml:lang="grc">ι^</quote>): as <ref n="Aristoph. Birds 946">Ar. <title>Av.</title> 946</ref> begins a trimeter with <quote xml:lang="grc">ξυνίημ’ ὅτι βούλει</quote>. The initial <quote xml:lang="grc">ι</quote> of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἵημι</quote> is properly long in pres. (and impf.) indic., imper., infin., and partic. This is the regular quantity in Attic: <ref n="Soph. El. 596">cp. 596</ref>. In Homeric verse the pres. indic. and imper., and the pres. part., (act. and midd.,) have <quote xml:lang="grc">ι—</quote> in arsis (and once, even in thesis, <ref n="Hom. Il. 12.274"> <title>Il.</title> 12. 274</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλὰ πρόσωἵεσθε</quote>): but <quote xml:lang="grc">ι^</quote> in thesis, as when <quote xml:lang="grc">ἵενται</quote> closes a verse. The impf., too, has <quote xml:lang="grc">ι^</quote> in thesis, as <ref n="Hom. Il. 1.479"> <title>Il.</title> 1. 479</ref>(<quote xml:lang="grc">ἵει</quote>). The Tragoedians, following epic precedent, sometimes shorten <quote xml:lang="grc">ι</quote> in these parts of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἵημι</quote>: thus <quote xml:lang="grc">ἵησιν</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">ι^</quote>) in lyrics, <ref n="Aesch. Seven 310.">Aesch. <title>Th.</title> 310.</ref>But it is noteworthy that in tragic <emph>dialogue</emph> the examples of <quote xml:lang="grc">ι^</quote> seem to be confined to the pres. part.; thus <ref n="Aesch. Seven 493">Aesch. <title>Th.</title> 493</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἱέντα</quote>: <ref n="Eur. Hec. 338">Eur. <title>Hec.</title> 338</ref> and <title>I. A.</title> 1101 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἱεῖσα</quote>: <title>I. T.</title> 298 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἱείς</quote>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τάδ_131">: your kindly purpose. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φυγγάνει_132">. This by-form of <quote xml:lang="grc">φεύγω</quote> occurs first in <ref n="Aesch. PB 513.">Aesch. <title>P. V.</title> 513.</ref>Greek presents in <quote xml:lang="grc">‐ανω</quote> are of two classes,—those in which the stem remains unchanged, as <quote xml:lang="grc">ἁμαρτ‐άνω</quote>; and those in which a nasal is added to it, as <quote xml:lang="grc">φυγγάνω, θιγγάνω, λαμβάνω</quote>, etc. (Curtius, <title>Greek Verb</title>, ch. IX, p. 174, Eng. tr.). In its compound forms, <quote xml:lang="grc">φυγγάνω</quote> was familiar to Attic prose: thus <ref n="Dem. 23.74">Dem. or. 23</ref> has § 74 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀποφυγγάνει</quote>: Aeschin. or. 3 § 208 <quote xml:lang="grc">καταφυγγάνῃ</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐδ_ἐθέλω_132">=<quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ οὐκ ἐθέλω</quote>: cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 222"> <title>Ai.</title> 222</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀγγελίαν ἄτλατον οὐδὲ φευκτάν</quote>: <ref n="Hom. Il. 24.25"> <title>Il.</title> 24. 25</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔνθ’ ἄλλοις μὲν πᾶσιν ἑήνδανεν, οὐδέ ποθ’ Ἥρῃ</quote>. So <quote xml:lang="grc">δέ</quote>=<quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλά</quote>, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 85"> <title>Ant.</title> 85</ref> n. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μὴ_οὐ_133">: 107 n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸν_ἄθλιον_133">: an adj., though not a predicate, is sometimes thus placed; <ref n="Soph. El. 1144">cp. 1144</ref>: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 393"> <title>Ph.</title> 393</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν μέγαν Πακτωλὸν εὔχρυσον</quote>: <ref n="Soph. OT 1199"> <title>O. T.</title> 1199</ref> f. <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰν γαμψώνυχα παρθένον</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">χρησμῳδόν</quote> (n.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παντοίας_134">, perh. a reminiscence of <ref n="Hom. Od. 15.245"> <title>Od.</title> 15. 245</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὃν περὶ κῆρι φίλει Ζεύς τ’ αἰγίοχος καὶ Ἀπόλλων</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">παντοίην φιλότητα</quote>. Literally: ‘reciprocating the graciousness (kindliness) of friendship in every form,’ —bound to me by a mutual friendship, which is sympathetic with every mood. (Not: ‘replacing’ kindliness in every relation,—as though these friends compensated her for the absence of paternal or fraternal sympathy.)</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλύειν_135">, to ‘wander’ in mind; to be wild with grief: cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1194"> <title>Ph.</title> 1194</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλύοντα χειμερίῳ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">λύπᾳ</quote>: and n. <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 174. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αἰαῖ_ἱκνοῦμαι_136">=152 <quote xml:lang="grc">αἰεὶ δακρύεις</quote>. The pause after <quote xml:lang="grc">αἰαῖ</quote> excuses the hiatus: cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1276"> <title>Ant.</title> 1276</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">φεῦ φεῦ, ὦ πόνοι</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τόν_λίμνας_137">: cp. <ref n="Dem. 9.42">Dem. or. 9 § 42</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν χρυσὸν τὸν ἐκ Μήδων εἰς Πελοπόννησον ἤγαγεν</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1076">Soph. <title>Ph.</title> 1076</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τά τ’ ἐκ νεὼς</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">στείλωσι</quote> (n.). As to the <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἀχερουσία λίμνη</quote>, cp. fr. 480 (where the <quote xml:lang="grc">ψυχή</quote> of Achilles speaks), <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀκτὰς ἀπαίωνάς τε καὶ μελαμβαθεῖς</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">λιποῦσαλίμνης ἦλθον.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παγκοίνου_137">: so <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1193"> <title>Ai.</title> 1193</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν πολύκοινον Ἅιδαν</quote>: <ref n="Aesch. Seven 860">Aesch. <title>Th.</title> 860</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πάνδοκον εἰς ἀφανῆ τε χέρσον.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀνστάσεις_137">: for the apocopè of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνά</quote>, cp. <ref n="Soph. Trach. 335"> <title>Tr.</title> 335</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὔτε_λιταῖσιν_139">. The strophic verse (123) probably represents the true metre: <quote xml:lang="grc">τάκεις ὧδ’ ἀκόρεστον οἰμωγάν</quote>. On this point most modern critics are agreed, though they differ as to whether the verse should be considered glyconic or dactylic. But the correction of v. 139 remains quite uncertain. Many edd. have received Hermann's ingenious emendation, <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔτε <emph>γόοισιν οὔτ’ ἄνταις</emph> </quote>. Doubtless <quote xml:lang="grc">ΑΝΤΑΙΣ</quote> could easily have generated <quote xml:lang="grc">ΛΙΤΑΙΣ</quote>. But the form <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄντη</quote> is most dubious. Hermann relies solely on Hesychius, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀντήσει, λιτανείαις, ἀντήσεσιν</quote>. He supposes that <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀντήσει</quote> was corrupted from <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄντῃσι</quote>: though it might also have come from <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀντήσε</quote>(<quote xml:lang="grc">ς</quote>)<quote xml:lang="grc">ι</quote>,—the second <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀντήσεσιν</quote> being a later addition. There is no other vestige of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄντη</quote>. Erfurdt's </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὔτε_εὐχαῖς_139"> would be satisfactory; but then we should have to assume that <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔτε λιταῖσιν</quote> arose from a perverse view of the metre. See Appendix. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλά_140">, so soon after the <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλά</quote> in 137, see 879—882: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 524"> <title>Ph.</title> 524</ref> n.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀπὸ_μετρίων_140">: deserting moderation (<quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ μέτρια</quote>).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀμήχανον_140">, admitting of no alleviation, like <quote xml:lang="grc">νόσων ἀμηχάνων</quote> <ref n="Soph. Ant. 363"> <title>Ant.</title> 363</ref>(n.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οιόλλυσαι_140"> here =<quote xml:lang="grc">προβαίνεις διολλυμένη</quote>: cp. <ref n="Dem. 21.139">Dem. or. 21 § 139</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">φθείρεσθαι πρὸς τοὺς πλουσίους</quote>. With the help given by <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπό</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰς</quote>, such a sense for the verb is not forced. It would be harsher to evolve the idea of motion from <quote xml:lang="grc">στενάχουσα</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐν_οἷς_142">, referring to the general sense of what precedes, ‘in which course.’</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀνάλυσις_κακῶν_142">, properly, a ‘dissolution,’ a ‘cancelling,’ of troubles. They are not dissipated by grieving. The parallel sense of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀναλύειν</quote> is common. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τί_μοι_142"> <foreign xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</foreign>: the ethic dat. nearly=‘I pray thee’ (887: <ref n="Soph. OC 1475"> <title>O. C.</title> 1475</ref> n.). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νήπιος_145">: for the general masc., <ref n="Soph. El. 771">cp. 771</ref>: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 151"> <title>Tr.</title> 151</ref>(n.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γονέων_145">, meaning <quote xml:lang="grc">πατρός</quote>: for the plur., <ref n="Soph. El. 346">cp. 346</ref>, <ref n="Soph. El. 498">498</ref>, <ref n="Soph. El. 594">594</ref>: <ref n="Soph. OT 366"> <title>O. T.</title> 366 n.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐμέ_ἄραρεν_147">, suits, is congenial to, me. In this intrans. sense <quote xml:lang="grc">ἤραρον</quote> would naturally take a dat.; as in <ref n="Hom. Od. 4.777"> <title>Od.</title> 4. 777</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μῦθον, ὃ δὴ καὶ πᾶσιν ἐνὶ φρεσὶν ἤραρεν ἡμῖν</quote>. For the acc. here, cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 584"> <title>Ai.</title> 584</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ γάρ μ’ ἀρέσκει</quote>. It may have been suggested by the acc. which follows this aor. when transitive; <quote xml:lang="grc">ἤραρε θυμὸν ἐδωδῇ</quote> ( <ref n="Hom. Od. 5.95"> <title>Od.</title> 5. 95</ref>), ‘satisfied.’ Simonides has <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀραρεῖν</quote> (fr. 41), but the form is otherwise epic only.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φρένας_147">, defining <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμέ</quote>: <ref n="Soph. El. 99">cp. 99</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κάρα</quote>, n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αἰὲν_Ἴτυν_148">. The <quote xml:lang="grc">υ—</quote> need not be explained by ictus, for it was originally long in these words, though in ordinary Attic usage it had become shortened. In <ref n="Hom. Od. 5.470"> <title>Od.</title> 5. 470</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐς κλιτὺν ἀναβάς</quote>, and a few passages of Tragedy (as <ref n="Eur. Her. 5">Eur. <title>H. F.</title> 5</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">στάχυς</quote>, <ref n="Eur. El. 1214"> <title>El.</title> 1214</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">γένυν</quote>), the <quote xml:lang="grc">υ—</quote> remains. Aristophanes, too, has <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἴτυ—ν ἐλελιζομένη</quote> ( <ref n="Aristoph. Birds 212"> <title>Av.</title> 212</ref>). On the other hand <quote xml:lang="grc">κλιτὺν</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">υ^</quote>) in <ref n="Soph. Trach. 271"> <title>Tr.</title> 271</ref> represents the later practice. (For the varied quantity here, cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 296"> <title>Ph.</title> 296</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πέτροισι πέτρον</quote>, n.) The reiterated <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἴτυν</quote> was heard in the nightingale's note; cp. Eur. fr. 773. 25 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀρθρευομένα γόοις</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἴτυν Ἴτυν πολύθρηνον</quote>, and <ref n="Aesch. Ag. 1144.">Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 1144.</ref> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὄρνις_149"> with <quote xml:lang="grc">ι^</quote>, as in <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1021"> <title>Ant.</title> 1021</ref>(n.).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀτυζομένα_149">, bewildered, distraught with grief: <ref n="Soph. El. 135">cp. 135</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλύειν.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Διὸς_ἄγγελος_149">, as the harbinger of spring. The nightingale appears in Attica about the end of March, or early in April ( <title>O. C.</title>, Introd. p. xii, n. 2). Cp. <ref n="Hom. Od. 24.344"> <title>Od.</title> 24. 344</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">Διὸς ὧραι</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Νιόβα_δ_150">, <quote xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</quote>: for this <quote xml:lang="grc">δέ</quote>, cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 1097"> <title>O. T.</title> 1097</ref>(<quote xml:lang="grc">Φοῖβε, σοὶ δὲ</quote>). By </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θεόν_150"> Electra means <quote xml:lang="grc">μακαρτάτην</quote>: cp. Sappho's <quote xml:lang="grc">φαίνεταί μοι κῆνος ἴσος θεοῖσιν</quote>. Niobe is happy in the highest, the divine, sense, because, by her perennial grief, she is true to the memory of those whom she has lost.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἅτ_150">, fem. of the epic relat. <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅς τε</quote>: so the neut. <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅ τε</quote> in <ref n="Soph. Trach. 824."> <title>Tr.</title> 824.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐν_πετραίῳ_150">: the stone into which Niobe was turned on Mount Sipylus is her ‘rocky tomb’: see nn. on <ref n="Soph. Ant. 825"> <title>Ant.</title> 825</ref> f. <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰν κισσὸς ὡς ἀτενὴς</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">πετραία βλάστα δάμασεν</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αἰεὶ_152"> is the best reading, since the point is that Niobe's grief is perpetual. <quote xml:lang="grc">αἰαῖ</quote> would be an interjection by Electra, an echo of the <quote xml:lang="grc">αἰαῖ</quote> in 136. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὔτοι_μούνᾳ_154">: <ref n="Soph. El. 289">cp. 289</ref>. <ref n="Cic. Tusc. 3.33.79">Cic. <title>Tusc.</title> 3. 33 § 79</ref> <quote xml:lang="lat">Ne illa quidem consolatio firmissima est, quanquam et usitata est et saepe prodest: Non tibi hoc soli.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρὸς_τι_155">, ‘in respect to whatever (grief).’</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῶν_περισσά_155">, ‘you are more excessive than those in the house,’—<foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, less moderate in showing sorrow: the gen., as after <quote xml:lang="grc">περιγίγνομαι, περιεῖναι, περισσεύω</quote> ( <ref n="Xen. Anab. 4. 8. 11">Xen. <title>An.</title> 4. 8. 11</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">περιττεύσουσιν ἡμῶν οἱ πολέμιοι</quote>). ‘They are equally affected by every one of those troubles which you lament so much more vehemently than they do.’ </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἷς_156">, the masc. of general reference (145 n.), should be retained, though </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῶν_ἔνδον_156"> (also masc.) alludes to the two sisters only.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὁμόθεν_156">, of the same stock, is more closely defined by </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γονᾷ_ξύναιμος_156">, which denotes the fraternal tie. <ref target="cl12">Cp. 12 n.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἵα_ζώει_158">. These words, explanatory of <quote xml:lang="grc">τῶν ἔνδον</quote>, seem to mean simply, ‘such as Chrysothemis, who is living,’ etc. (For <quote xml:lang="grc">οἵα Χρ. ζώει</quote>,=<quote xml:lang="grc">οἵα Χρ. ἐστίν, ἣ ζώει</quote>, cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 1451"> <title>O. T.</title> 1451</ref> n.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ζώει_158"> has more point when it is remembered that <emph>one</emph> sister had perished. The Ionic form occurs also in <ref n="Soph. OC 1213."> <title>O. C.</title> 1213.</ref>—Others understand: ‘considering <emph>the manner in which</emph> Chr.lives’ etc.;—<foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, Electra is found to be <quote xml:lang="grc">περισσά</quote> relatively to the standard of moderation which her sisters set. This seems to strain <quote xml:lang="grc">οἵα...ζώει</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_Ἰφιάνασσα_158">: so in <ref n="Hom. Il. 9.145"> <title>Il.</title> 9. 145</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">Χρυσόθεμις καὶ Λαοδίκη καὶ Ἰφιάνασσα</quote>, the name having the digamma. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀχέων_159"> is unquestionably the participle, familiar from the Homeric poems ( <ref n="Hom. Il. 2.724"> <title>Il.</title> 2. 724</ref>, <ref n="Hom. Il. 5.399"> Il. 5. 399</ref>, <ref n="Hom. Il. 18.446"> Il. 18. 446</ref>: <ref n="Hom. Od. 11.195"> <title>Od.</title> 11. 195</ref>). Orestes is conceived as pining in exile for the moment when he shall return as an avenger. <ref n="Soph. El. 171">Cp. 171</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀεὶ μὲν γὰρ ποθεῖ</quote>: <ref n="Soph. El. 602">602</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">δυστυχῆ τρίβει βίον</quote>. The mention of his present sorrow prompts the augury of his future triumph (160).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀχέων_159_2"> as gen. pl. of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄχος</quote>, joined it with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κρυπτᾷ_159">: <emph>semota a doloribus in iuventa felix.</emph> But (<emph>a</emph>) it seems impossible that <quote xml:lang="grc">κρυπτᾷ ἀχέων</quote> should mean, ‘secluded <emph>from</emph> sorrows,’ as if the idea of separation (or exemption) were sufficiently expressed by ‘hidden.’ And (<emph>b</emph>) in this context, where Electra is reminded that others suffer with her, a reference to the <emph>sorrow</emph> of the exile is evidently more fitting than an allusion to his immunity from her particular troubles. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὄλβιος_ὃν_160"> <quote xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</quote> The respect in which he is ‘happy’ is defined by the following clause, according to a frequent poetical idiom. <ref n="Hes. Th. 954">Hes. <title>Th.</title> 954</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄλβιος, ὃς μέγα ἔργον ἐν ἀθανάτοισιν ἀνύσσας</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ναίει ἀπήμαντος</quote>. <ref n="Hom. Od. 11.450">Hom. <title>Od.</title> 11. 450</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄλβιος· ἦ γὰρ τόν γε πατὴρ φίλος ὄψεται ἐλθών</quote>. And so with other words: <ref n="Hom. Od. 1.8">Hom. <title>Od.</title> 1. 8</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">νήπιοι, οἳ κατὰ βοῦς Ὑπερίονος ἠελίοιο</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ησθιον</quote>: <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 3. 161 <quote xml:lang="grc">σχέτλιος, ὅς ῥ) ἔριν ὦρσε κ.τ.λ.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δέξεται_εὐπατρίδαν_162">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, will receive him, so that he shall be once more a noble of the land, instead of an exile; for the proleptic force, <ref n="Soph. El. 68">cp. 68</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">δέξασθέ μ’ εὐτυχοῦντα</quote> (n.). So Orestes exultingly imagines how men will say of him ( <ref n="Aesch. Eum. 757">Aesch. <title>Eum.</title> 757</ref>), <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἀργεῖος ἁνὴρ αὖθις, ἔν τε χρήμασιν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">οἰκεῖ πατρῴοις</quote>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Διὸς_εὔφρονι_162"> | </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βήματι_162">, by the kindly guidance of Zeus; <quote xml:lang="grc">βῆμα</quote> here having a sense parallel with that of the causal tenses, <quote xml:lang="grc">βήσω, ἔβησα</quote>. Schol. <quote xml:lang="grc">βήματι· ἀντὶ ὁδῷ, πομπῇ</quote>. This is certainly bold, though not too much so (I think) for Sophocles. No correction seems probable (see cr. n.). The most ingenious, perhaps, is Mekler's <quote xml:lang="grc">εὐπατριδᾶν ἕδος</quote> (in appos. with <quote xml:lang="grc">γᾶ...Μυκηναίων</quote>). It is, however, somewhat weak; and the rhythm seems to favour the slight pause after <quote xml:lang="grc">εὐπατρίδαν.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γᾶν_162">, notwithstanding </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γᾶ_162"> in 161: <ref n="Soph. El. 375">cp. 375</ref>, 379 (<quote xml:lang="grc">γόων</quote>): 511, 515 (<quote xml:lang="grc">αἰκίαις, αἰκία</quote>): 871, 873 (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἡδονῆς, ἡδονὰς</quote>): <ref n="Soph. OC 554"> <title>O. C.</title> 554</ref> n.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Ὀρέσταν_162">, emphatically placed at the end, is drawn into the case of the relative </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὃν_162">: cp. <ref n="Hom. Od. 1.69"> <title>Od.</title> 1. 69</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">Κύκλωπος κεχόλωται, ὃν ὀφθαλμοῦ ἀλάωσεν</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀντίθεον Πολύφημον</quote>. <ref n="Aesch. Seven 553">Aesch. <title>Th.</title> 553</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τῷδ’, ὃν λέγεις τὸν Ἀρκάδα</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀκάματα_164"> with initial <quote xml:lang="grc">α^</quote>: but in <ref n="Soph. Ant. 339"> <title>Ant.</title> 339</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀκαμάταν</quote> with <quote xml:lang="grc">α—</quote> (n.). For the neut. plur. as adv., <ref n="Soph. El. 786">cp. 786</ref>: <ref n="Soph. OT 883"> <title>O. T.</title> 883</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑπέροπτα</quote> (n.).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀνύμφευτος_164"> is merely a rhetorical amplification of the thought expressed by </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄτεκνος_164">, and hence the poet is indifferent to the order of the words; just as in 962 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄλεκτρα</quote> <emph>precedes</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνυμέναια</quote>, and as Oedipus forebodes the fate of his daughters, <quote xml:lang="grc">χέρσους φθαρῆναι κἀγάμους</quote> (<title>O.T.</title> 1502). Antigone makes a similar lament ( <ref n="Soph. Ant. 813"> <title>Ant.</title> 813</ref> ff., <ref n="Soph. Ant. 917"> Ant. 917</ref> f.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἰχνῶ_164">, simply ‘go about’ (<quote xml:lang="grc">περιέρχομαι</quote>, schol.), here implying her loneliness. Not =<quote xml:lang="grc">οἴχομαι</quote> (‘I am lost’), as some take it. <quote xml:lang="grc">οἰχνέω</quote> is, indeed, a poetical by-form of <quote xml:lang="grc">οἴχομαι</quote>, but does not share this sense. <ref n="Soph. El. 313">Cp. 313</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Aj. 564."> <title>Ai.</title> 564.</ref>This verb is not extant in Eur. ; Aesch. uses compounds of it, but only in lyrics (<quote xml:lang="grc">εἰς‐</quote>, <ref n="Aesch. PB 122">Aesch. <title>P. V.</title> 122</ref>; <quote xml:lang="grc">δι‐</quote>, <ref n="Aesch. Eum. 315">Aesch. <title>Eum.</title> 315</ref>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δάκρυσι_μυδαλέα_166">: as <title>Scut.</title> 270 (<quote xml:lang="grc">κόνις</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">δάκρυσι μυδαλέη</quote>. The <quote xml:lang="grc">υ</quote> is properly short, as in <quote xml:lang="grc">μυδάω</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. OT 1278"> <title>O. T.</title> 1278</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 410"> <title>Ant.</title> 410</ref>).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸν_ἀνήνυτον_166">: the art. means, ‘<emph>that</emph> endless doom of mine’; <ref n="Soph. El. 176">cp. 176</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν ὑπεραλγῆ</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1187"> <title>Ai.</title> 1187</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰν ἄπαυστον</quote>... | ...<quote xml:lang="grc">μόχθων ἄταν</quote>. (Cp. <ref n="Soph. Trach. 476"> <title>Tr.</title> 476</ref>, <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ δεινὸς ἵμερος</quote>, with n. in appendix there.)</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἶτον_ἔχουσα_166">: <ref n="Hom. Il. 9.559"> <title>Il.</title> 9. 559</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλκυόνος πολυπενθέος οἶτον ἔχουσα</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὧν_ἔπαθ_169">. The schol. understands, ‘the benefits which he has received’ at Electra's hands, who saved him from perishing with his father (12, 1128, 1350). And it might be said that she is here thinking of his ingratitude. But it seems worthier of her heroic nature that she should mean,—‘he forgets <emph>his wrongs</emph>—those great wrongs which he is bound to avenge.’ This agrees, too, with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὧν_ἐδάη_169">, ‘what he has learned’ by the messages which she has sent from Mycenae to Phocis, as to the subsequent conduct of the partners in crime. ‘He forgets his father's murder, and his sister's misery.’</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τί_ἀγγελίας_169">: cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1229"> <title>Ant.</title> 1229</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν τῷ συμφορᾶς</quote> (n.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀπατώμενον_169">, ‘disappointed’ by the result. The message is poetically identified with the hope which it inspires. Cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 630"> <title>Ant.</title> 630</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπάτας λεχέων</quote>, a cheating (of his hope), a disappointment, concerning marriage. The partic. here expresses the leading idea of the sentence: ‘what comforting message comes to me that is not belied?’ (The <emph>pres.</emph> part., because the thought is, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀεὶ ἀπατᾶται</quote>.) Cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1038"> <title>O. C.</title> 1038</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">χωρῶν ἀπείλει νῦν</quote> (n.): <ref n="Soph. Trach. 592"> <title>Tr.</title> 592</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ εἰδέναι χρὴ δρῶσαν</quote>. For </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τί_οὐκ_169"> as=<quote xml:lang="grc">πᾶν τι</quote>, cp. <title>O.T.</title> 1526 (n.).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐχί_μοι_169">, for </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐκ_ἐμοί_169">, is specious; but <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμοί</quote> may be defended by the antithesis with </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὁ_δέ_169">. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ποθεῖ_δ_171">: <ref n="Soph. El. 319">cp. 319</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">φησίν γε· φάσκων δ’ οὐδὲν ὧν λέγει ποεῖ</quote>. There, as here, there is a touch of mournful bitterness, which <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ ἀξιοῖ</quote> brings out. As to the frequent messages sent to Electra by Orestes, <ref n="Soph. El. 1154">cp. 1154</ref>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔτι_οὐρανῷ_174">=154 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄχος ἐφάνη βροτῶν</quote>. Hermann saves the </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐν_174"> of the MSS. by reading <quote xml:lang="grc">μέγας ἔτ’ ἐν</quote> etc.; but the simple dat. of place seems warranted by such instances as 313 (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἀγροῖσι</quote>), <ref n="Soph. OT 1451"> <title>O. T.</title> 1451</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ναίειν ὄρεσιν</quote>, <ref n="Hes. WD 8">Hes. <title>Op.</title> 8</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">αἰθέρι ναίειν</quote>, <ref n="Pind. N. 10">Pind. <title>N.</title> 10. 58</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">θεὸς ἔμμεναι οἰκεῖν τ’ οὐρανῷ</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸν_ὑπεραλγῆ_176">: for the art., <ref target="cl166">cp. 166 n.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νέμουσα_176">, ‘assigning,’ or ‘committing,’ it to Zeus. The verb is used as in <quote xml:lang="grc">νέμειν μοῖραν</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. Trach. 1238"> <title>Tr.</title> 1238</ref>) or <quote xml:lang="grc">νέμειν γέρα</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. OC 1396"> <title>O. C.</title> 1396</ref>) <quote xml:lang="grc">τινί</quote>. Wrath against evildoers is an attribute and a prerogative of Zeus, to whom the injured should leave the task of inflicting retribution. As the avenger of blood, Zeus was styled <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλάστωρ, ἀλιτήριος, παλαμναῖος, τιμωρός</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μήτε_ὑπεράχθεο_177"> (<quote xml:lang="grc">τούτοις</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">οὓς <emph>ἐχθαίρεις μήτε ἐπιλάθου</emph> </quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτῶν</quote>). For </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἷς_177"> (by attraction, for <quote xml:lang="grc">οὕς</quote>), cp. <title>H.</title> 3. 5. 18 <quote xml:lang="grc">σὺν οἷς εἶχεν ᾔει</quote>. The clauses are co-ordinate, but the emphasis is upon <quote xml:lang="grc">μὴ ὑπεράχθεο</quote>. ‘Without forgetting thy foes, refrain from excess of wrath against them.’ The Chorus allow that, as Electra has said (145), she cannot forget the murder of her father. They only counsel moderation of behaviour. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εὐμαρὴς_θεός_179">, a god who brings ease,—soothing difficulties, and making burdens tolerable (cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 7"> <title>O. C.</title> 7</ref>, <ref n="Soph. OC 437"> O. C. 437</ref>). <quote xml:lang="grc">εὐμαρής</quote> has here an active sense: cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 44"> <title>Ph.</title> 44</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">φύλλον...νώδυνον</quote>, a herb that soothes pain. <ref n="Eur. Her. 17">Eur. <title>H. F.</title> 17</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">συμφορὰς δὲ τὰς ἐμὰς</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξευμαρίζων</quote>. Philon vol. 2. p. 43 (<foreign xml:lang="lat">ap.</foreign> Nauck) describes <quote xml:lang="grc">χρόνος</quote> as <quote xml:lang="grc">ἱκανὸς καὶ πένθος ἀνελεῖν καὶ θυμὸν σβέσαι καὶ φόβον θεραπεῦσαι· πάντα γὰρ ἐξευμαρίζει</quote>. For this use of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θεός_179">, cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 27">Soph. <title>O. T.</title> 27</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὔτε_γὰρ_180">, after <quote xml:lang="grc">χρόνος <emph>γὰρ</emph> </quote> in 179: Sophocles often thus uses <quote xml:lang="grc">γάρ</quote> in two successive clauses ( <ref n="Soph. Aj. 20"> <title>Ai.</title> 20</ref> f., <ref n="Soph. Aj. 215"> Ai. 215</ref> f., <ref n="Soph. Aj. 514"> Ai. 514</ref> f., <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1262"> Ai. 1262</ref> f.; <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1158"> <title>Ph.</title> 1158</ref> f.; <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1255"> <title>Ant.</title> 1255</ref> f.). Here the second <quote xml:lang="grc">γάρ</quote> introduces a reason for thinking that, in this instance, Time will bring a remedy.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Κρῖσαν_180">. Most MSS. have <quote xml:lang="grc">Κρίσαν</quote>: Triclinius wrote <quote xml:lang="grc">Κρίσσαν</quote>. But in favour of <quote xml:lang="grc">Κρῖσαν</quote> Hermann appeals to a grammarian of Hadrian's age, Dracon of Stratonicea, in his treatise <quote xml:lang="grc">περὶ μέτρων</quote> (p. 21, 4).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βούνομον_181">, ‘grazed over by oxen,’ seems fitter here than </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βουνόμον_181">, ‘giving pasture to oxen’: but there is little to choose. Cp. Aesch. fr. 249 <quote xml:lang="grc">βούνομοί τ’ ἐπιστροφαί</quote>: and <ref n="Soph. OT 26">Soph. <title>O. T.</title> 26</ref>(n.).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βούνομον_ἀκτὰν_181"> is in appos. with </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Κρῖσαν_181">: see last n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀπερίτροπος_182">, ‘regardless.’ The word occurs only here; and <quote xml:lang="grc">περιτρέπομαι</quote> does not occur in a corresponding sense. But the poet has followed the analogy of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐντρέπομαι</quote> and the epic <quote xml:lang="grc">μετατρέπομαί τινος</quote> as=‘to regard.’ With a somewhat similar boldness, he has elsewhere used <quote xml:lang="grc">στραφείην</quote> with a gen. as=<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπιστραφείην</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1117"> <title>Ai.</title> 1117</ref>). The meaning is rightly given by Suidas <emph>s. v.</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπερίτροπος,—ἔχει ἐπιστροφὴν καὶ ἐπιμέλειαν ποιεῖται</quote>. In the quotation of the verse by Suidas there, the word <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνεπίτροπος</quote> (which would mean ‘without a guardian’) has accidentally been substituted for <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπερίτροπος</quote>. Suidas, like the schol., explains <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπερίτροπος</quote> by <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνεπίστροφος</quote>. They both notice a second, but clearly erroneous, view, acc. to which <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπερίτροπος</quote> has a twofold sense here:— (1) in relation to Orestes, ‘<emph>not returning</emph>,’ <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνεπέλευστος</quote>: (2) in relation to Hades, ‘regardless.’ </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παρὰ_Ἀχέροντα_183">, because his realm extends along its banks: see n. on <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1123"> <title>Ant.</title> 1123</ref> f. For the place of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀνάσσων_183"> after </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θεὸς_183">, <ref n="Soph. El. 695">cp. 695</ref>: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1316"> <title>Ph.</title> 1316</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰς...ἐκ θεῶν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">τύχας δοθείσας</quote>: <ref n="Soph. OT 1245"> <title>O. T.</title> 1245</ref>(n.). The ‘god’ is, of course, Hades; the King of the Dead is their avenger: cp110: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1075"> <title>Ant.</title> 1075</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἅιδου...Ἐρινύες</quote>. Some suppose, however, that the <quote xml:lang="grc">θεός</quote> meant is the spirit of Agamemnon. Prof. Campbell, who inclines to that view, thinks that the next best course is to refer the words to Hermes <quote xml:lang="grc">χθόνιος</quote> (111). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μὲν_185"> merely emphasises <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμέ</quote>: there is no corresponding clause with <quote xml:lang="grc">δέ</quote>: cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 11"> <title>Ant.</title> 11</ref>(<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμοὶ μὲν</quote>) n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὁ_βίοτος_185">, the best part of it: <ref target="cl962">see on 962</ref>, where she speaks of her sister as <quote xml:lang="grc">γηράσκουσαν</quote>. When <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ πολύς</quote> thus means <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ πλείων</quote>, the noun (with art.) usu. precedes it; as <ref n="Hdt. 1. 102">Her. 1. 102</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ στρατὸς...ὁ πολλός</quote>, <ref n="Thuc. 1. 24">Thuc. 1. 24</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τῆς δυνάμεως τῆς πολλῆς. </quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀνέλπιστος_185">, predic., has passed away from me without leaving me any hopes.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοκέων_185">, for these reasons. (1) She is saying that the best days of her life have gone by without giving her anything to hope for. It would be inappropriate to justify this (as the causal </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἅτις_185"> does) by saying that she is pining away ‘without <emph>parents</emph>,’ or a husband's care, while the mention of <emph>children</emph> is perfectly in place. (2) The very order of the words, <quote xml:lang="grc">τεκέων...ἀνήρ</quote>, is confirmed by vv. 164 f., <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄτεκνος...ἀνύμ‐</quote> <quote xml:lang="grc">φευτος</quote>. (3) If </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοκέων_185_2"> be right, it means that, while Agamemnon is dead, the living Clytaemnestra is a <quote xml:lang="grc">μήτηρ ἀμήτωρ</quote> (1154): but this is forced. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἁπερεί_189">, like the common <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡσπερεί</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. OT 264"> <title>O. T.</title> 264</ref>).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔποικος_189">..., an immigrant, an alien: cp. <title>Legg.</title> 742 A <quote xml:lang="grc">μισθωτοῖς, δούλοις καὶ ἐποίκοις</quote>, ‘hirelings, slaves or immigrants.’ <ref n="Hom. Il. 9.648"> <title>Il.</title> 9. 648</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡσεί τιν’ ἀτίμητον μετανάστην</quote>, ‘like some worthless sojourner’ (or ‘alien’). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἰκονομῶ_θαλάμους_190">: for the verb compounded with a noun similar in sense to <quote xml:lang="grc">θαλάμους</quote>, cp. <ref n="Soph. Trach. 760"> <title>Tr.</title> 760</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ταυροκτονεῖ...βοῦς</quote>: <ref n="Hom. Il. 4.3"> <title>Il.</title> 4. 3</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">νέκταρ ἐῳνοχόει</quote>. By <quote xml:lang="grc">οἰκονομῶ</quote> was meant properly the ‘management’ of a household, either by the master, or by a domestic of the higher grade, a <quote xml:lang="grc">ταμίας</quote> or <quote xml:lang="grc">οἰκονόμος</quote>, ‘house-steward.’ But here <quote xml:lang="grc">οἰκονομῶ θαλάμους</quote> denotes the discharge of humbler duties, in attending to the daily service of the house: as in <ref n="Aesch. Lib. 84">Aesch. <title>Ch.</title> 84</ref> the <quote xml:lang="grc">δμωαὶ</quote> are <quote xml:lang="grc">δωμάτων εὐθήμονες</quote>. Electra describes her own condition by the word <quote xml:lang="grc">δουλεύω</quote> in 814, <ref n="Aesch. Lib. 1192">Aesch. Ch. 1192</ref>: as in <ref n="Aesch. Lib. 135">Aesch. <title>Ch.</title> 135</ref> she says <quote xml:lang="grc">κἀγὼ μὲν ἀντίδουλος</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀεικεῖ_στολᾷ_191">: cp. the reference to her <quote xml:lang="grc">ζῶμα</quote> in 452; and the comments of Orestes on her whole appearance (1177, 1181). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κεναῖς_τραπέζαις_192">. <quote xml:lang="grc">κενὴ τράπεζα</quote>, a ‘bare,’ or scantily furnished, table, is opposed to <quote xml:lang="grc">τράπεζα πλήρης</quote> ( <ref n="Eur. Hipp. 110">Eur. <title>Hipp.</title> 110</ref>): it would be prosaic to insist that it must mean a table with nothing on it. While the docile Chrysothemis fares sumptuously (361), the rebel Electra is treated like a halfstarved slave. In v. 265 there is another hint of her privations (<quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ τητᾶσθαι</quote>).— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀμφίσταμαι_192">, because to lie at meals on a <quote xml:lang="grc">κλίνη</quote> was a luxury refused to the <quote xml:lang="grc">δούλη</quote>: such food as she receives must be taken standing. This touch seems quite in keeping with what she says below as to the treatment inflicted upon her (1196 <quote xml:lang="grc">λύμαισι</quote>). The plur. <quote xml:lang="grc">τραπέζαις</quote> refers to her experience from day to day.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἰκτρὰ_πλαγά_193">. At v. 95 it was noticed that verses 95—99 clearly show a reminiscence of <ref n="Hom. Od. 11.406"> <title>Od.</title> 11. 406</ref>—411,— the earlier part of the passage in which the departed Agamemnon relates his death to Odysseus. I believe that an instructive light on these verses is gained by observing that a later portion of the same passage was here present to the poet's mind,—viz., <ref n="Hom. Od. 24.418"> Od. vv. 418</ref>—424, where Agamemnon goes on to describe the scene at the murderous banquet:—<quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλά κε κεῖνα μάλιστα ἰδὼν ὀλοφύραο θυμῷ</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς ἀμφὶ κρητῆρα τραπέζας τε πληθούσας</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">κείμεθ’ ἐνὶ μεγάρῳ</quote> [cp. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κοίταις_193"> here], <quote xml:lang="grc">δάπεδον δ’ ἅπαν αἵματι θῦεν</quote>. | <quote xml:lang="grc">οἰκτροτάτην δ’ ἤκουσα ὄπα</quote> [cp. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἰκτρὰ_αὐδὰ_193">] <quote xml:lang="grc">Πριάμοιο θυγατρός</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">Κασσάνδρης, τὴν κτεῖνε Κλυταιμνήστρη δολόμητις</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀμφ’ ἐμοί· αὐτὰρ ἐγὼ ποτὶ γαίῃ χεῖρας ἀείρων</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">βάλλον, ἀποθνῄσκων περὶ φασγάνῳ</quote>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νόστοις_193"> might be governed by </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐν_193"> (cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 734"> <title>O. T.</title> 734</ref> n.), but is more simply taken as a temporal dat., denoting the occasion, like <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῖς ἐπινικίοις</quote> (<ref n="Plat. Sym. 174a">Plat. <title>Symp.</title> 174 A</ref>), etc.: cp. n. on <ref n="Soph. Ant. 691."> <title>Ant.</title> 691.</ref>For the poet. plur., cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 900"> <title>Ai.</title> 900</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὤμοι ἐμῶν νόστων</quote>. The plural was familiar in relation to the return from Troy; thus the poem ascribed to Agias (<emph>c.</emph> 750 B.C.) was entitled <quote xml:lang="grc">Νόστοι.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κοίταις_193_2">, ‘couch,’ here of feasting, as <quote xml:lang="grc">δείπνων</quote> (203) shows. This may be the sense, as Neue suggests, in <ref n="Eur. Hipp. 748">Eur. <title>Hipp.</title> 748</ref> f. <quote xml:lang="grc">κρῆναί τ’ ἀμβρόσιαι χέονται</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">Ζηνὸς μελάθρων παρὰ κοίταις</quote>. The word <quote xml:lang="grc">κοίτη</quote> (from stem <quote xml:lang="grc">κει</quote>) implies merely reclining, and does not necessarily involve the notion of sleeping.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅτε_οἱ_193">: for the hiatus cp. <ref n="Soph. Trach. 650"> <title>Tr.</title> 650</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἁ δέ οἱ</quote> (n.). The MS. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σοι_193"> is certainly wrong (see Appendix).— </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀνταία_193">, striking full: <ref n="Soph. El. 89">cp. 89</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀντήρεις</quote> (n.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γενύων_193">, the blades of the two-edged <quote xml:lang="grc">πέλεκυς</quote> (99 n.): <ref n="Soph. El. 485">cp. 485</ref>. Hence a pickaxe is <quote xml:lang="grc">γενῄς</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. Ant. 249"> <title>Ant.</title> 249</ref> n.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δόλος_ἔρος_197">: guile planned the deed,—<foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, devised the means of doing it: lust was ‘the slayer,’ as having supplied the motive. Some would transpose, reading <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔρος ἦν ὁ φράσας, δόλος ὁ κτείνας</quote>: <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, lust prompted the deed, and guile executed it. But this is tamer and more prosaic. There is a higher tragic force in the old reading.—The epic form <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔρος</quote> is not used by Aesch. , and by Soph. only here; by Eur. , in dialogue also, as <ref n="Eur. Hipp. 337">Eur. <title>Hipp.</title> 337</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ο<*>ον, μῆτερ, ἠράσθης ἔρον</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δεινὰν_δεινῶς_198">: <ref n="Soph. El. 989">cp. 989</ref>: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 166"> <title>Ph.</title> 166</ref> n.—The phrase </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δεινὰν_μορφάν_198"> must be viewed in the light of the following words, <quote xml:lang="grc">εἴτ’ οὖν θεὸς εἴτε βροτῶν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἦν ὁ ταῦτα πράσσων</quote>. The Chorus doubt whether the agency in the terrible crime was merely human. Perchance an evil <quote xml:lang="grc">δαίμων</quote> was there, wor<*>ng out the curse upon the line of Pelops (504—515). The <quote xml:lang="grc">δεινὴ μορφή</quote>, offspring of <quote xml:lang="grc">δόλος</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔρος</quote>, is the act of murder, embodied in the image of a supernatural <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλάστωρ</quote>. Sophocles may have had in mind the words of the Aeschylean Clytaemnestra ( <ref n="Aesch. Ag. 1500"> <title>Ag.</title> 1500</ref> f.); the slayer of Agamemnon, she says, was in truth the Avenger of the house, who took her form:—<quote xml:lang="grc">φανταζόμενος δὲ γυναικὶ νεκροῦ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦδ’ ὁ παλαιὸς δριμὺς ἀλάστωρ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἀτρέως, χαλεποῦ θοινατῆρος</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">τόνδ’ ἀπέτεισεν</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">τέλεον νεαροῖς ἐπιθύσας</quote>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἴτ_εἴτε_198">: <ref n="Soph. El. 560">cp. 560</ref>: <title>O.T.</title> 1049 n.— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βροτῶν_198">, partitive gen.: <title>M.</title> 1. 3. 9 <quote xml:lang="grc">ε<*>ναι τῶν σωφρονικῶν ἀνθρώπων</quote>.—Cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 1258"> <title>O. T.</title> 1258</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">λυσσῶντι δ’ αὐτῷ δαιμόνων δείκνυσί τις·</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδεὶς γὰρ ἀνδρῶν</quote>. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 243"> <title>Ai.</title> 243</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κακὰ δεννάζων ῥήμαθ’, ἃ δαίμων</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">κοὐδεὶς ἀνδρῶν ἐδίδαξεν</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πλέον_ἐχθίστα_201"> (instead of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐχθρά</quote>), by redundant emphasis: cp. <title>O.C.</title> 743 <quote xml:lang="grc">πλεῖστον ἀνθρώπων</quote>.. | <quote xml:lang="grc">κάκιστος.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δὴ_201"> with the superl., as <ref n="Soph. Aj. 858"> <title>Ai.</title> 858</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πανύστατον δή</quote>: <ref n="Thuc. 1. 50">Thuc. 1. 50</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μεγίστη δή</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νὺξ_203">, because the banquet was prolonged into the night; cp. <ref n="Hom. Od. 7.102"> <title>Od.</title> 7. 102</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">φαίνοντες νύκτας κατὰ δώματα δαιτυμόνεσσι.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δείπνων_203">, a poet. plur. ( <ref n="Soph. Trach. 268"> <title>Tr.</title> 268</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">δείπνοις</quote>), like <quote xml:lang="grc">γάμοι</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. Ant. 575"> <title>Ant.</title> 575</ref>), <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐλαί</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. Trach. 901"> <title>Tr.</title> 901</ref>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄχθη_204"> (a nomin., like <quote xml:lang="grc">νύξ</quote>) is defined by the following words. But instead of saying <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄχθη, θάνατοι αἰκεῖς, τοὺς ἴδε κ.τ.λ.</quote>, the poet has drawn <quote xml:lang="grc">θάνατοι αἰκεῖς</quote> into the relative clause. He thus gains more prominence for <quote xml:lang="grc">τοὺς <emph>ἐμὸς ἴδε πατήρ</emph> </quote>. The conjecture </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῖς_204"> (neut., ‘by which’) would enfeeble the passage. For the tragic plur. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θανάτους_204">, cp. <ref n="Soph. Trach. 1276"> <title>Tr.</title> 1276</ref>(Deianeira's death), <title>O.T.</title> 497 (that of Laïus): so below, 779 <quote xml:lang="grc">φόνους</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1313"> <title>Ant.</title> 1313</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μόρων</quote>: <cit> <bibl n="Eur. El. 137">Eur. <title>El.</title> 137</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">αἱμάτων</quote> </cit>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="διδύμαιν_χειροῖν_204">, the hand of Clytaemnestra and the hand of Aegisthus. <ref n="Soph. El. 1080">Cp. 1080</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">διδύμαν..Ἐρινύν</quote>. Nauck wishes to write </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="διδύμοιν_204">. But Meisterhans (<title>Gramm. d. Att. Inschr.</title> p. 49) quotes <quote xml:lang="grc">λιθίναιν</quote> from an Attic inscr. of 439 B.C. <ref n="Soph. El. 1133">Cp. 1133</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἷλον_πρόδοτον_207">. The murderous hands ‘took her life captive,’ since the crime placed her wholly in their power (264); and this was done by treachery. <quote xml:lang="grc">πρόδοτον</quote> is predicative, expressing the mode of capture; cp. <ref n="Thuc. 6. 102">Thuc. 6. 102</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡγούμενοι</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν κύκλον</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐρῆμον αἱρήσειν</quote>. But in <ref n="Soph. Trach. 240">Soph. <title>Tr.</title> 240</ref>, <quote xml:lang="grc">ᾕρει...ἀνάστατον</quote>.. | <quote xml:lang="grc">χώραν</quote>, the adj. is proleptic. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἷς_209">, not <quote xml:lang="grc">αἷς</quote>, since <quote xml:lang="grc">χειροῖν</quote> (206) implies the persons; cp. <title>O.C.</title> 730 <quote xml:lang="grc">τῆς ἐμῆς ἐπεισόδου·</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ὃν μήτ’ ὀκνεῖτε.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Ὀλύμπιος_209">: <ref n="Soph. El. 176">cp. 176</ref> n.: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 315"> <title>Ph.</title> 315</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἷ’ Ὀλύμπιοι θεοὶ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">δοῖέν ποτ’ αὐτοῖς ἀντίποιν’ ἐμοῦ παθεῖν</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ποίνιμα_210"> <quote xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</quote>: the alliteration (<quote xml:lang="grc">παρήχησις</quote>) adds bitter emphasis: for other examples, see <title>O.T.</title> 370 n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀγλαΐας_211">, the external splendour of their life (<ref n="Soph. El. 268">cp. 268</ref> f., 280); as in <ref n="Hom. Od. 17.310"> <title>Od.</title> 17. 310</ref> it is said of dogs whose value consists only in their beauty, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀγλαΐης δ’ ἕνεκεν κομέουσιν ἄνακτες</quote> (‘for ornament’). The word is especially fitting here, as suggesting triumph, for <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἀγλαΐα</quote> was especially the Grace of victory; cp. <ref n="Pind. O. 14">Pind. <title>O.</title> 14. 13</ref>(with Gildersleeve's note): and <ref n="Pind. O. 13">Pind. <title>O.</title> 13. 14</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">νικαφόρον ἀγλαΐαν.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀποναίατο_211">: for the Ionic form, cp. <title>O.T.</title> 1274 n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πόρσω_213">, restored by conjecture, in place of <quote xml:lang="grc">πρόσω</quote>, to <title>O.C.</title> 178, 181, 226, is here supported by L and other MSS. It is used by Pindar and by Eur. ( <ref n="Eur. Tro. 189">Eur. <title>Tro.</title> 189</ref>). The Homeric form, where a spondee is required, is <quote xml:lang="grc">πρόσσω</quote>: the Attic prose form, <quote xml:lang="grc">πόρρω</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γνώμαν_ἴσχεις_214">=<quote xml:lang="grc">γιγνώσκεις</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. Phil. 837"> <title>Ph.</title> 837</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 853"> Ph. 853</ref>).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐξ_οἵων_214">, ‘by what kind of conduct.’ (Not, ‘from what a position’ of possible comfort,—like that of her more prudent sisters.)</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὰ_παρόντ_214">, ‘in respect to present circumstances,’—‘as matters stand’; <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, already they are bad enough (217, 235). Cp. <ref n="Thuc. 4. 17">Thuc. 4. 17</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀεὶ γὰρ τοῦ πλέονος ἐλπίδι ὀρέγονται διὰ τὸ καὶ τὰ παρόντα ἀδοκήτως εὐτυχῆσαι</quote>: where, as here, <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ παρόντα</quote> is acc. of respect, not subj. of the inf. Groundless objections to <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ παρόν<*>)</quote> have prompted conjectures (cr. n.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἰκείας_214">, ‘caused by thyself’: <ref n="Soph. Aj. 260"> <title>Ai.</title> 260</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἰκεῖα πάθη</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">μηδενὸς ἄλλου παραπράξαντος</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πολύ_κακῶν_217">, a large measure of trouble; cp. <ref n="Soph. Trach. 497"> <title>Tr.</title> 497</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μέγα τι σθένος</quote>. After <quote xml:lang="grc">πολύς, τις</quote> has usually a limiting force, and occurs chiefly in negative phrases, such as <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ ἐν πολλῷ τινι ἔλασσον</quote> ( <ref n="Thuc. 6. 1">Thuc. 6. 1</ref>), <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ πολύ τι διαφέρει</quote> (<ref n="Plat. Rep. 484d">Plat. <title>Rep.</title> 484 D</ref>), <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ πολλοί τινες</quote>, etc.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὑπερεκτήσω_217"> (a compound found only here), above what was necessary. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὰ_δὲ_219">, ‘but those things’ (referring to <quote xml:lang="grc">πολέμους</quote>), <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> ‘such contests,’ </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐκ_δυνατοῖς_219">, ‘cannot be waged with the powerful,’ (<quote xml:lang="grc">ὥστε</quote>) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πλάθειν_219"> (<quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτοῖς</quote>), ‘so that one should come into conflict with them.’ The epexegetic inf. further explains the meaning of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐριστά</quote>. Such contentions must not be pushed to an actual trial of force with those who are stronger than ourselves. For the inf. thus defining an adj., cp. <ref n="Hom. Il. 21.482"> <title>Il.</title> 21. 482</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">χαλεπή τοι ἐγὼ μένος ἀντιφέρεσθαι</quote> (for thee to encounter): <ref n="Pind. N. 10">Pind. <title>N.</title> 10. 72</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">χαλεπὰ δ’ ἔρις ἀνθρώποις ὁμιλεῖν κρεσσόνων</quote> (<emph>how</emph> hard, they know only when they are fairly engaged in it).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐριστά_219"> (only here)=‘contested,’ then, ‘what can be contested’ (cp. <title>O.T.</title>, append. p. 225).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πλάθειν_219_2"> ( <ref n="Soph. Phil. 728"> <title>Ph.</title> 728</ref>), in a hostile sense; cp. <ref n="Hdt. 9. 74">Her. 9. 74</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅκως πελάσειε..τοῖσι πολεμίοισι</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 1093">Soph. <title>Tr.</title> 1093</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">λέοντ’, ἄπλατον θρέμμα</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δεινοῖς_ἠναγκάσθην_221">: dread causes forced her (at the first) to adopt this course; and they are still valid. For the reiteration, cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1101"> <title>Ph.</title> 1101</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ τλάμων τλάμων ἄρ’ ἐγώ</quote>: <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 1462 <quote xml:lang="grc">λείπομεν ὑμᾶς, λείπομεν ἤδη</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὀργά_222"> alludes to <quote xml:lang="grc">δυσθύμῳ..ψυχᾷ</quote> (218 f.). She knows that her resentment is shown with passion. The schol.'s paraphrase, <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦτο τὸ ἔπος, φησίν, οὐ λανθάνε<*> με ἀλλ’ οἶδα τὸ ἀσφαλὲς καὶ συμφέρον</quote>, might seem to suggest a different reading, such as <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ λάθει μ’ <*>ν δεῖ</quote>. But probably it was meant to explain </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔξοιδ_222"> only. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλὰ_γὰρ_223"> is here elliptical; ‘but (ye speak in vain), for,’ etc. Cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 167."> <title>Ai.</title> 167.</ref>In 256 there is no ellipse. Cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 148"> <title>Ant.</title> 148</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐν_δεινοῖς_223">: cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 185"> <title>Ph.</title> 185</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔν τ’ ὀδύναις</quote>.. | <quote xml:lang="grc">λιμῷ τ’ οἰκτρός.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σχήσω_223">: this form occurs also in 375 and <ref n="Soph. Aj. 684."> <title>Ai.</title> 684.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ταύτας_ἄτας_223">, these infatuated laments. (Others understand, ‘I will not <emph>put an end to</emph> these miseries which I suffer’: but <quote xml:lang="grc">σχήσω</quote> is then less fitting.) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὄφρα_225"> is not elsewhere used by Sophocles: Aesch. has it twice in lyrics ( <ref n="Aesch. Lib. 360">Aesch. <title>Ch.</title> 360</ref>, <ref n="Aesch. Eum. 340">Aesch. <title>Eum.</title> 340</ref>): Eur. never. For the omission of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄν</quote>, cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 764">Soph. <title>Ph.</title> 764</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἕως ἀνῇ. </quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βίος_με_225"> is simply, ‘while life is in me’: there is no reference to </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σχήσω_225">, such as Nauck assumes (‘I will not check these laments while life detains me’). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τίνι_καίρια_226">, ‘for <emph>in the judgment of</emph> what person who thinks aright,’ </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀκούσαιμ_ἔπος_226">, ‘could I possibly hear a word of comfort suited to my case?’ That is: What reason for desisting from these lamentations could possibly be suggested to me, which a right-minded person would think satisfactory? For the ethic dat. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τίνι_226"> <foreign xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</foreign>, cp. <title>O.C.</title> 1446 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνάξιαι γὰρ πᾶσίν ἐστε δυστυχεῖν</quote>, and <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 810 (n.). For </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρόσφορον_226">, ‘suitable,’ and hence ‘suited to one's needs,’ ‘profitable,’ cp. <title>O.C.</title> 1774 <quote xml:lang="grc">πρόσφορά θ’ ὑμῖν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ τῷ κατὰ γῆς</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄνετε_229"> is more than <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐᾶτε</quote>, since it implies relaxing a strain; <ref n="Soph. El. 721">cp. 721</ref>: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1101"> <title>Ant.</title> 1101</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄνες</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">κόρην</quote>), release her.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄλυτα_229">, irremediable; <ref n="Soph. El. 939">cp. 939</ref>: so <quote xml:lang="grc">λύσις</quote>, of a remedy ( <ref n="Soph. Ant. 598"> <title>Ant.</title> 598</ref> n.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κεκλήσεται_229">: they must be permanently accounted such: cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1368"> <title>Ai.</title> 1368</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">σὸν ἆρα τοὔργον, οὐκ ἐμόν, κεκλήσεται</quote>. So <quote xml:lang="grc">κεκλήσομαι</quote>, <title>O.T.</title> 522.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκ_229">, where the simple gen. would suffice, <ref n="Soph. El. 291">cp. 291</ref>, 987.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀνάριθμος_229"> has the second <quote xml:lang="grc">α</quote> short (as in <ref n="Aesch. Pers. 40">Aesch. <title>Pers.</title> 40</ref>, etc.). For the form of the word, and also for the gen. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θρήνων_229">, cp. <ref n="Soph. Trach. 247">Soph. <title>Tr.</title> 247</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡμερῶν ἀνήριθμον</quote> (n.). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλ_οὖν_233">=‘well, at any rate (though I speak in vain)’: </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γε_233"> emphasises </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εὐνοίᾳ_233">: ‘it is with <emph>good-will</emph> that I speak’: <ref n="Soph. El. 1035">cp. 1035</ref>: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 84"> <title>Ant.</title> 84</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μάτηρ_τις_234">: these words have been taken as indicating that the Chorus was composed (in part, at least) of women older than Electra. Her own tone to them rather suggests <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁμήλικες</quote> (134). (In <ref n="Soph. Trach. 526"> <title>Tr.</title> 526</ref> the words <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐγὼ δὲ μάτηρ μὲν οἷα φράζω</quote> are corrupt.) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μὴ_τίκτειν_235">, since a command is implied by <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐδῶ</quote> here, as elsewhere by <quote xml:lang="grc">λέγω, φωνῶ, ἐννέπω</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. Phil. 101"> <title>Ph.</title> 101</ref> n.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄταν_ἄταις_235">: the mere iteration suggests the notion of adding; but this is developed by the sense of the verb: cp. <title>Helen.</title> 195 <quote xml:lang="grc">δάκρυα δάκρυσί μοι φέρων</quote>: and n. on <title>O.T.</title> 175 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄλλον δ’ ἂν ἄλλῳ προσίδοις</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_μέτρον_236">...; for this <quote xml:lang="grc">καί</quote> prefixed to an indignant question, cp. <title>O.C.</title> 263 (n.): <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1247"> <title>Ph.</title> 1247</ref>: <ref n="Soph. Aj. 462."> <title>Ai.</title> 462.</ref>— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κακότατος_236"> is better attested than <quote xml:lang="grc">κακότητος</quote> here. In <title>O.C.</title> 521, too, the MSS. give <quote xml:lang="grc">κακότατ’</quote>. <ref n="Soph. El. 134">Cp. 134</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φέρε_236"> in the sense of <quote xml:lang="grc">φέρ’ εἰπέ</quote>: so <ref n="Aristoph. Ach. 541">Ar. <title>Ach.</title> 541</ref> ff. <quote xml:lang="grc">φέῤ, εἰ Λακεδαιμονίων τις κ.τ.λ.</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">καθῆσθ’ ἂν ἐν δόμοισιν; </quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπὶ_φθ_236">, in their case: for the prep., cp. <title>O.T.</title> 829 n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔβλαστ_238">: in what human being has such impiety ever been inborn? <ref n="Soph. El. 440">Cp. 440</ref>: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 563"> <title>Ant.</title> 563</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδ’ ὃς ἂν βλάστῃ μένει</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">νοῦς τοῖς κακῶς πράσσουσιν</quote>. The gloss <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐνομοθετήθη</quote> points to another interpretation of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔβλαστ’</quote>, viz., ‘has become usual’ (cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 296"> <title>Ant.</title> 296</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κακὸν νόμισμ’ ἔβλαστε</quote>): and to this the conjecture <quote xml:lang="grc">τίσι</quote> for <quote xml:lang="grc">τίνι</quote> (cr. n.) was adapted. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τούτοις_239">, the persons who approve such forgetfulness of the dead: cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 456"> <title>Ph.</title> 456</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅπου θ’ ὁ χείρων τἀγαθοῦ μεῖζον σθένει</quote>,... | <quote xml:lang="grc">τούτους ἐγὼ τοὺς ἄνδρας οὐ στέρξω ποτέ</quote> (n.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἴ_χρηστῷ_240">: prosperity is here conceived as a <emph>region</emph> of comfort, close to which the person is securely established. ‘When my lot is cast in pleasant places.’ <ref n="Soph. El. 1040">Cp. 1040</ref>: Eur. fr. 418 <quote xml:lang="grc">κακοῖς γὰρ οὐ σὺ πρόσκεισαι μόνη</quote>. Conversely, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1243">Soph. <title>Ant.</title> 1243</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνδρὶ πρόσκειται κακόν</quote>. (See n. on <ref n="Soph. Ant. 94.">Soph. <title>Ant.</title> 94.</ref>) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ξυνναίοιμ_241">: cp. <title>O.T.</title> 1205 <quote xml:lang="grc">τίς ἄταις..τίς ἐν πόνοις</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ξύνοικος</quote>: <ref n="Plat. Rep. 587C">Plat. <title>Rep.</title> 587C</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡδοναῖς ξυνοικεῖ</quote>: conversely, <title>O.C.</title> 1134 <quote xml:lang="grc">κηλὶς κακῶν ξύνοικος</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1168"> <title>Ph.</title> 1168</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄχθος ὃ ξυνοικει—.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εὔκηλος_241">: the only instance of this Homeric form in Tragedy (for in <ref n="Eur. IA 644">Eur. <title>I.A.</title> 644</ref>Blomfield restored <quote xml:lang="grc">ἕκηλον</quote>). The stem of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἕκηλος</quote> being <quote xml:lang="grc">vεκ, εὔκηλος</quote> is perh. for <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐvέκηλος, ἔvκηλος</quote> (Curt. <title>Etym.</title> § 19). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γονέων_241"> | </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκτίμους_241">, not paying honour to parents: the gen. as with adjectives compounded with <quote xml:lang="grc">α</quote> privative (36). For this negative sense of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκ</quote> in composition, cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔκδικος, ἐξαίσιος, ἔξωρος.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκτίμους_ἵσχουσα_241">, restraining so that they shall not honour: for the proleptic adj., cp. <ref n="Aesch. Pers. 298">Aesch. <title>Pers.</title> 298</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄνανδρον τάξιν ἠρήμου θανών.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πτέρυγας_γόων_241">, as <ref n="Pind. I. 1">Pind. <title>I.</title> 1. 64</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πτερύγεσσιν ἀερθέντ’ ἀγλααῖς</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">Πιερίδων</quote>: cp. <ref n="Eur. Andr. 93">Eur. <title>Andr.</title> 93</ref>(<quote xml:lang="grc">γόους</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">πρὸς αἰθέρ’ ἐκτενοῦμεν.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὀξυτόνων_241">, as <ref n="Soph. Aj. 630"> <title>Ai.</title> 630</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀξυτόνους...ᾠδὰς</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">θρηνήσει</quote>: <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 321 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀξέων κωκυμάτων</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰ_γὰρ_244"> <quote xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</quote> She says:— ‘I will not cease to lament, and to invoke retribution on the murderers (209 f.). <title>For</title>, if they are <emph>not</emph> to pay with their blood for the blood which they have shed, there will be an end to regard for man and to fear of heaven.’</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γᾶ_244"> here=<quote xml:lang="grc">σποδός</quote>, of the dead: Eur. fr. 522 <quote xml:lang="grc">κατθανὼν δὲ πᾶς ἀνὴρ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">γῆ καὶ σκιά· τὸ μηδὲν εἰς οὐδὲν ῥέπει.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐδὲν_244"> (not <quote xml:lang="grc">μηδὲν</quote>) </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὢν_244">, though </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰ_244"> precedes: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 935"> <title>O. C.</title> 935</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">βίᾳ τε κοὐχ ἑκών</quote> (after <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ μή</quote>): <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1131"> <title>Ai.</title> 1131</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ...οὐκ ἐᾷς</quote>. Here the parataxis affords a special excuse for <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδέν</quote>,—viz., that this first clause, though formally dependent on <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ</quote>, is not really hypothetical: he <emph>is</emph> dead. In the second clause (<quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ..μὴ.. δώσουσ’</quote>), a real hypothesis, the negative is <quote xml:lang="grc">μή</quote>. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Lys. 10.13">Lys. or. 10 § 13</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ οὖν δεινόν, εἰ ὅταν μὲν δέῃ σε..τοὺς ἐχθροὺς τιμωρεῖσθαι, οὕτω τοὺς νόμους...λαμβάνεις, ὅταν δ’ ἕτερον παρὰ τοὺς νόμους εἴπῃς κακῶς, οὐκ ἀξιοῖς δοῦναι δίκην;</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
οὐκ οὖν δεινόν, εἰ ὅταν μὲν δέῃ σε..τοὺς
                                    ἐχθροὺς τιμωρεῖσθαι, οὕτω τοὺς νόμους...λαμβάνεις, ὅταν δ’
                                    ἕτερον παρὰ τοὺς νόμους εἴπῃς κακῶς, οὐκ ἀξιοῖς δοῦναι
                                    δίκην;

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πάλιν_244">, in recompense: <ref n="Soph. OT 100"> <title>O. T.</title> 100</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">φόνῳ φόνον πάλιν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">λύοντας.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀντιφόνους_δίκας_244">, a penalty which exacts blood for blood: cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1156"> <title>Ph.</title> 1156</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀντίφονον...στόμα</quote>: <ref n="Aesch. Eum. 464">Aesch. <title>Eum.</title> 464</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀντικτόνοις ποιναῖσι φιλτάτου πατρός</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔρροι_τ_249">: the <quote xml:lang="grc">τε</quote> should properly. follow <quote xml:lang="grc">αἰδώς</quote>: cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 258"> <title>O. T.</title> 258</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κυρῶ τ’ ἐγὼ</quote> instead of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐγώ τε κυρῶ.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αἰδὼς_249"> is respect for those opinions and feelings of mankind which condemn wrong-doing; as </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εὐσέβεια_249"> is reverence for the gods. Cp. <ref n="Hom. Od. 2.65"> <title>Od.</title> 2. 65</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄλλους τ’ αἰδέσθητε περικτίονας ἀνθρώπους</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">οἳ περιναιετάουσι· θεῶν δ’ ὑποδείσατε μῆνιν</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐγὼ_μὲν_251">: here <quote xml:lang="grc">μέν</quote> merely emphasises the pron.: cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 11"> <title>Ant.</title> 11</ref> n.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_251"> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸ_τοὐμὸν_251">: cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1313"> <title>Ai.</title> 1313</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅρα μὴ τοὐμὸν ἀλλὰ καὶ τὸ σόν</quote>. The chief stress is upon <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ σόν</quote>: but the meaning is not merely that her interests are to them as their own. These women, representing the people of Mycenae, desire the downfall of the usurper whose unpunished crime lays an <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄγος</quote> upon the land. Their attitude is, so far, like that of the Argive elders in the <title>Agamemnon.</title> They wish Electra, not to renounce her purpose, but quietly to await her opportunity (177 ff.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νίκα_253">: cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1353"> <title>Ai.</title> 1353</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">παῦσαι· κρα<*>εῖς τοι τῶν φίλων νικώμενος.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἅμα_253"> is best taken as an adv. (cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 814"> <title>Ai.</title> 814</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἅμ’ ἕψεται</quote>: <ref n="Soph. OT 471"> <title>O. T.</title> 471</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἅμ’ ἕπονται</quote>). It might, however, be a prep.; cp. <ref n="Soph. Trach. 563"> <title>Tr.</title> 563</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ξὺν Ἡρακλεῖ... εσπόμην</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πολλοῖσι_θρήνοις_255">, causal dat.; <ref n="Soph. El. 42">cp. 42</ref> n. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλὰ_γὰρ_256">: <ref n="Soph. El. 223">cp. 223</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἥτις_γυνή_257">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἴη</quote>: cp. <ref n="Hom. Il. 5.481"> <title>Il.</title> 5. 481</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τά τ’ ἔλδεται, ὅς κ’ ἐπιδευής</quote> (<foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc"><*>ησι</quote>): and <emph>id.</emph> 14. 376. Here the adj. combines the ideas of birth and character: <ref n="Soph. El. 989">cp. 989</ref>: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 874"> <title>Ph.</title> 874</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ εὐγενὴς γὰρ ἡ φύσις κἀξ εὐγενῶν</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 38"> <title>Ant.</title> 38</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἴτ’ εὐγενὴς πέφυκας εἴτ’ ἐσθλῶν κακή</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πατρῷα_πήματα_258">, the woes arising from her father's murder; and so, here, the woes of her father's house. For this large sense of the adj., cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1196"> <title>O. C.</title> 1196</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πατρῷα καὶ μητρῷα πήμαθ’ ἅπαθες</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 856"> <title>Ant.</title> 856</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πατρῷον...ἆθλον</quote>, an ordeal bequeathed by him. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κατ_εὐφρόνην_259"> is illustrated by 271 f.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θάλλοντα_259">: cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 258"> <title>Ph.</title> 258</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡ δ’ ἐμὴ νόσος</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀεὶ τέθηλε</quote>. <cit> <bibl n="Shak. ADO 5.1.76">Shakesp. <title>Much Ado</title> 5. 1. 76,</bibl> <quote xml:lang="eng">‘His May of youth and <emph>bloom</emph> of lustihood.’</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
‘His May of youth and   of
                                    lustihood.’

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ᾗ_261">, causal: <ref n="Soph. El. 160">cp. 160</ref> n.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρῶτα_ἔπειτα_261"> (266). The influence of the relat. pronoun </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ᾗ_261_2"> is confined to the first clause, as in <ref n="Soph. OC 632"> <title>O. C.</title> 632</ref> ff.: <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅτῳ πρῶτον μὲν...ἔπειτα δ’ ἱκέτης κ.τ.λ.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὰ_μητρὸς_261"> is not a mere synonym for <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡ μήτηρ</quote>, but rather denotes her mother's relations with her: <ref n="Soph. OC 268"> <title>O. C.</title> 268</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ μητρὸς καὶ πατρός. </quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔχθιστα_συμβέβηκεν_261">, have <emph>come to be</emph> such. This verb, though often joined with the participles of <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰμί</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">γίγνομαι</quote>, is rarely construed with a simple adj.: cp. however <ref n="Plat. Rep. 329D">Plat. <title>Rep.</title> 329D</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">νεότης χαλεπὴ τῷ τοιούτῳ ξυμβαίνει</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κἀκ_ἄρχομαι_264">. Cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 63"> <title>Ant.</title> 63</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀρχόμεσθ’ ἐκ κρεισσόνων</quote>: <ref n="Soph. OC 67"> <title>O. C.</title> 67</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκ τοῦ κατ’ ἄστυ βασιλέως τάδ’ ἄρχεται</quote>. <title>Hellen.</title> 3. 1. 6 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκείνῳ δ’ αὕτη ἡ χώρα δῶρον ἐκ βασιλέως ἐδόθη</quote>. In such phrases <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκ</quote> is somewhat more than a mere equivalent for <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑπό</quote> (124). It suggests the head and fount of authority; a sense fitting here. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λαβεῖν_τητᾶσθαι_265">. The aor. inf. expresses the act of receiving; the pres. inf., the state of privation. Cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 95"> <title>Ph.</title> 95</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξαμαρτεῖν...νικᾶν</quote>. For <quote xml:lang="grc">λαβεῖν</quote> (without art.) as subject of <quote xml:lang="grc">πέλει</quote>, cp. <ref n="Soph.
