Trach. 134"> <title>Tr.</title> 134</ref> f. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπέρχεται</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">χαίρειν τε καὶ στέρεσθαι</quote>: for the use of the art. with <quote xml:lang="grc">τητᾶσθαι</quote> only, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 495">Soph. <title>Ph.</title> 495</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ σοφοῖς</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ τοῖσι φαύλοις</quote>. Somewhat similar is <ref n="Soph. OC 808">Soph. <title>O. C.</title> 808</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">χωρὶς τό τ’ εἰπεῖν πολλὰ καὶ τὰ καίρια</quote>, unless <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ καίρια</quote> be read. For <quote xml:lang="grc">τητᾶσθαι</quote>, <ref n="Soph. El. 1326">cp. 1326</ref>: <ref n="Soph. OC 1200"> <title>O. C.</title> 1200</ref> n. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ποίας_266">...</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δοκεῖς_266"> <foreign xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</foreign>: cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 276"> <title>Ph.</title> 276</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">σὺ δή, τέκνον, ποίαν μ’ ἀναστασιν δοκεῖς</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτῶν βεβώτων ἐξ ὕπνου στῆναι τότε</quote>; </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἴδω_267"> is followed by </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰσίδω_δ_267"> and </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἴδω_δὲ_267"> (271). For such change of the word in ‘epanaphora,’ cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 898"> <title>Ant.</title> 898</ref> f. <quote xml:lang="grc">φίλη μὲν .. προσφιλὴς δὲ .. φίλη δὲ</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 530"> <title>Ph.</title> 530</ref> f. <quote xml:lang="grc">φίλτατον μὲν...ἥδιστος δ’...φίλοι δὲ.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μὲν_267"> is omitted in the first clause; <ref n="Soph. El. 105">cp. 105</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐσθήματα_268">, the royal robes. He carried Agamemnon's sceptre (420). Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Eur. El. 319">Eur. <title>El.</title> 319</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὃς δ’ ἐκεῖνον ἔκτανεν, | ἐς ταὐτὰ βαίνων ἅρματ’ ἐκφοιτᾷ πατρί, | καὶ σκῆπτρ’ ἐν οἷς Ἕλλησιν ἐστρατηλάτει, | μιαιφόνοισι χερσὶ γαυροῦται λαβών.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
ὃς δ’ ἐκεῖνον ἔκτανεν, | ἐς ταὐτὰ βαίνων
                                    ἅρματ’ ἐκφοιτᾷ πατρί, | καὶ σκῆπτρ’ ἐν οἷς Ἕλλησιν ἐστρατηλάτει,
                                    | μιαιφόνοισι χερσὶ γαυροῦται λαβών.

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκείνῳ_268">=<quote xml:lang="grc">τῷ πατρί</quote>, implied in <quote xml:lang="grc">πατρῴοις</quote>: so <ref n="Soph. OC 942"> <title>O. C.</title> 942</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτοὺς</quote> refers to <quote xml:lang="grc">πόλιν</quote> (<foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 939), and <ref n="Soph. Trach. 260"> <title>Tr.</title> 260</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τόνδε</quote> to <quote xml:lang="grc">π<*>λιν Εὐρυτείαν</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παρεστίους_λοιβάς_268"> (52 n.). In v. 1495 the words <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔνθαπερ κατέκτανες</quote> denote the place <emph>within</emph> the palace where Agamemnon was slain at the banquet (203). The words </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔνθ_ὤλεσεν_268"> here similarly denote the banqueting-hall. At the daily meals in the <quote xml:lang="grc">μέγαρον</quote>, Aegisthus, as master of the house, pours the libations to Hestia and other deities. Eustathius (on <ref n="Hom. Od. 22.335"> <title>Od.</title> 22. 335</ref>) rightly observes that the Homeric <quote xml:lang="grc">βωμὸς Ἑρκείου Διὸς</quote> in the open <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐλὴ</quote> ( <ref n="Hom. Il. 11.774"> <title>Il.</title> 11. 774</ref>) is distinct from the <quote xml:lang="grc">ἑστία</quote> proper in the megaron. His remark is illustrated by the phrase <quote xml:lang="grc">ξενίη τε τράπεζα</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἱστίη τ’ Ὀδυσῆος</quote> ( <ref n="Hom. Od. 14.158"> <title>Od.</title> 14. 158</ref>, etc.). See below, <ref n="Hom. Od. 14.419"> Od. 419</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τούτων_271"> (neut.), partitive gen.: ‘the crowning outrage in all this.’ Others make it fem., <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">τῶν ὕβρεων</quote>: or masc. (Clytaemnestra and Aegisthus). The last seems weaker. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὐτοέντην_272">, the form in <ref n="Soph. OT 107."> <title>O. T.</title> 107.</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐθέντης</quote>, u<*>ed by Aesch. and Eur. , does not occur in Sophocles. The second part of the compound is akin to <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔντεα, ἐντύνω</quote>: cp. Hesych. <quote xml:lang="grc">συνέντης· συνεργός</quote>. Phrynichus explains it by <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτόχειρ φονεύς</quote> (Lobeck, p. 120). Wilamowitz on <ref n="Eur. Her. 839">Eur. <title>H. F.</title> 839</ref> strangely holds that the post-classical sense of <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐθέντης</quote>, ‘master’ (whence <emph>effendi</emph>), was the primary one, quoting <title>Suppl.</title> 442 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅπου γε δῆμος αὐθέντης χθονός</quote>: but there Markland's correction, <quote xml:lang="grc">εὐθυντής</quote>, seems certain.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὐτοέντην_272_2"> from the schol., in place of </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὐτοφόντην_272">, the reading of all the MSS. The latter word occurs only in <ref n="Eur. Med. 1269.">Eur. <title>Med.</title> 1269.</ref>Most editors have followed Brunck. Bergk and Campbell retain <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτοφόντην</quote>, as Monk wished to do (<title>Mus. Crit.</title> 1. 73). They may be right. But <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτοέντην</quote> is <emph>exquisitius dictum</emph>, and also distinctively Sophoclean.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἡμὶν_272">, ethic dat., expressing indignant horror. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ταλαίνῃ_273">, like <quote xml:lang="grc">τλήμων</quote> (275), refers to infatuated guilt; <ref n="Soph. El. 121">cp. 121</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">δυστανοτάτας</quote> (n.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ταύτην_τῷδε_274">: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Phil. 1437"> <title>Ph.</title> 1437</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὗτος σὲ καὶ σὺ τόνδ’</quote> </cit>: and <ref n="Soph. Phil. 841">ib. 841</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1331">1331</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μιάστορι_275">, one who defiles by bloodshed, as <ref n="Soph. OT 353"> <title>O. T.</title> 353</ref>: but below, <ref n="Soph. El. 603">603</ref>, one who punishes the blood-guilty.— </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Ἐρινὺν_275">: <ref target="cl114">cp. 114 n.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐγγελῶσα_ποιουμένοις_277">, with mocking exultation in her course of conduct (<ref n="Soph. El. 85">cp. 85</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τῶν δρωμένων</quote>, n.). So <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐγγελῶσα</quote> in <ref n="Soph. El. 807">807</ref>. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐγγελᾶν τινι</quote> properly=to laugh <emph>at</emph> a person or thing; and that is strictly the sense here, since Clytaemnestra's deeds are Electra's misfortunes. Cp. <ref n="Aristoph. Ach. 1197">Ar. <title>Ach.</title> 1197</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κᾆτ’ ἐγχανεῖται ταῖς ἐμαῖς τύχαισιν</quote>. Sophocles uses also the simple <quote xml:lang="grc">γελᾶν</quote> in this sense, <ref n="Soph. Aj. 957">Soph. <title>Ai.</title> 957</ref>(<quote xml:lang="grc">ἄχεσιν</quote>), <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 1042 (<quote xml:lang="grc">κακοῖς</quote>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εὑροῦς_278">, if sound, means simply, ‘having ascertained.’ We may suppose that at least some interval had elapsed between the murder and the institution of these rites. The usurper could not at first feel secure. Having resolved to institute such a festival, Clytaemnestra was careful to see that the day of the month chosen should be precisely that on which the crime was committed. The word </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τότε_278">, implying some lapse of time, confirms this view: cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 650."> <title>Ai.</title> 650.</ref>For <quote xml:lang="grc">εὑρίσκειν</quote> as=‘to discover by reflection’ cp. <ref n="Soph. Trach. 1178"> <title>Tr.</title> 1178</ref> n.—Others take <quote xml:lang="grc">εὑροῦσα</quote> as=<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπειδὰν εὕρῃ</quote>, <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, ‘when she has found that day’ by its coming to her in the course of each successive month (Bellermann). So, too, Wecklein, who suggests that <quote xml:lang="grc">εὑροῦσα</quote> expresses joy at the happy <quote xml:lang="grc">εὕρημα</quote>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φρουροῦς_278"> (cr. n.) is, from a palaeographical point of view, slightly preferable to the rival conjecture </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τηροῦς_278">, and is supported by <ref n="Eur. Alc. 27">Eur. <title>Alc.</title> 27</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">φρουρῶν τόδ’ ἦμαρ</quote>. But no change seems necessary. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χοροὺς_ἵστησι_280">, the regular phrase ( <ref n="Hdt. 3. 48">Her. 3. 48</ref>, <ref n="Dem. 21.51">Dem. or. 21 § 51</ref>, etc.): whence <quote xml:lang="grc">Στησίχορος.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μηλοσφαγεῖ_ἱερὰ_280">: <ref n="Soph. El. 190">cp. 190</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἰκονομῶ θαλάμους</quote> (n.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔμμην_ἱερά_280">. Every month, on the date of Agamemnon's death, choruses sang paeans, victims were sacrificed to the saving gods, and a banquet (284) followed. Monthly celebrations were frequent in Greece. Thus offerings were made on the Athenian acropolis at each <quote xml:lang="grc">νουμηνία</quote> ( <ref n="Hdt. 8. 41">Her. 8. 41</ref>: [Dem. ] or. 25 § 99). There were also <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπιμήνια</quote> to Heracles at the Cynosarges (Athen. p. 234 E). At Sparta Apollo was propitiated on the first and the seventh of each month ( <ref n="Hdt. 6. 57">Her. 6. 57</ref>). Cp. <title>Legg.</title> 828 C <quote xml:lang="grc">θύοντας τούτων</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">τῶν θεῶν</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἑκάστοις ἔμμηνα ἱερὰ χορούς τε καὶ ἀγῶνας μουσικούς</quote>. Lucian <title>Parasit.</title> 15 <quote xml:lang="grc">αἱ πόλεις...τὰς μὲν δἰ ἔτους τὰς δὲ ἐμμήνους ἑορτὰς ἐπιτελοῦσι</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῖς_σωτηρίοις_280">: especially to Zeus <quote xml:lang="grc">Σωτήρ</quote>, and to Apollo <quote xml:lang="grc">προστατήριος</quote> (637). Artemis, too, was often worshipped as <quote xml:lang="grc">σώτειρα</quote> or <quote xml:lang="grc">σωσίπολις</quote>: but indeed all the greater Olympian deities shared this attribute. Cp. <ref n="Xen. Anab. 3. 2. 9">Xen. <title>An.</title> 3. 2. 9</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τῷ θεῷ τούτῳ</quote> (Zeus Sôter) <quote xml:lang="grc">θύσειν σωτήρια..συνεπεύξασθαι δὲ καὶ τοῖς ἄλλοις θεοῖς θύσειν κατὰ δύναμιν</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἡ_δύσμορος_282">. The article is omitted by Nauck (cr. n.). It is not necessary, but it adds emphasis. <quote xml:lang="grc">δύσμορος</quote>, referring to the speaker, takes the article in <ref n="Soph. Phil. 951"> <title>Ph.</title> 951</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1352"> Ph. 1352</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 919."> <title>Ant.</title> 919.</ref>Cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡ τάλαιν’</quote> below, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 304"> Ant. 304</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1138."> Ant. 1138.</ref>For the place of the art., as 1st syll. of 3rd foot, cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 257"> <title>O. C.</title> 257</ref>, <ref n="Soph. OC 280"> O. C. 280</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 95."> <title>Ant.</title> 95.</ref>— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κατὰ_στέγας_282"> goes with <quote xml:lang="grc">κλαίω</quote> rather than with <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁρῶσα</quote>. Seeing the festivities in front of the palace, she retires to weep in secret (285). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τέτηκα_283"> has the force of an intensive present, like <quote xml:lang="grc">γέγηθα, δέδοικα, κέκηδα</quote> (Tyrtaeus fr. 12. 38), <quote xml:lang="grc">μέμηνα</quote>, etc. (Cp. Curtius, <title>Gk. Verb</title>, ch. XVI., p. 378 Eng. transl.) So <ref n="Hom. Il. 3.176"> <title>Il.</title> 3. 176</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ καὶ κλαίουσα τέτηκα</quote>.—Cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 977"> <title>Ant.</title> 977</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κατὰ δὲ τακόμενοι</quote>... | <quote xml:lang="grc">κλαῖον</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πατρὸς_283"> <foreign xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</foreign> The normal order would be, <quote xml:lang="grc">τὴν πατρὸς ἐπωνομασμένην δυστάλαιναν δαῖτα</quote>. (As the words stand, the partic. would properly be predicative; ‘I lament that the feast has been called after him.’) Cp. <ref n="Thuc. 7. 23">Thuc. 7. 23</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">αἱ πρὸ τοῦ στόματος νῆες ναυμαχοῦσαι</quote>, and n. on <ref n="Soph. OT 1245.">Soph. <title>O. T.</title> 1245.</ref>For the gen., <ref n="Eur. Her. 1329">Eur. <title>H. F.</title> 1329</ref>(<quote xml:lang="grc">τεμένη</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπωνομασμένα σέθεν</quote>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὐτὴ_αὑτήν_285">. For </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὑτήν_285"> (=<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμαυτήν</quote>) cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1132"> <title>Ai.</title> 1132</ref>, <ref n="Soph. OT 138"> <title>O. T.</title> 138</ref>(n.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κλαῦσαι_285">, rather than <quote xml:lang="grc">κλαίειν</quote>, since the thought is that she is not allowed to complete the indulgence of her grief. <ref n="Soph. El. 788">Cp. 788</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἴμοι τάλαινα· νῦν γὰρ οἰμῶξαι πάρα</quote>. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 360"> <title>Ph.</title> 360</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπεὶ )δάκρυσα κεῖνον.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἡδονὴν_φέρει_285">: lit., ‘as much as my inclination makes it pleasant (to weep).’ <quote xml:lang="grc">φέρειν ἡδ</quote>.=‘to afford pleasure’ (but <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔχειν</quote>, ‘to feel’ it): <ref n="Soph. OC 779"> <title>O. C.</title> 779</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅτ’ οὐδὲν ἡ χάρις χάριν φέροι</quote>: <ref n="Soph. OT 1094"> <title>O. T.</title> 1094</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπὶ ἦρα φέροντα</quote>. For <quote xml:lang="grc">θυμός</quote>, cp. <ref n="Hdt. 8. 116">Her. 8. 116</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἢ ἄλλως σφι θυμὸς ἐγένετο θεήσασθαι τὸν πόλεμον</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἡ_γενναία_287">, noble in her professions (though not in her deeds), inasmuch as she claimed to be the instrument of heaven in avenging her daughter: <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡ γὰρ Δίκη νιν εἷλεν, οὐκ ἐγὼ μόνη</quote> (528). The Aeschylean Clytaemnestra speaks in the same lofty strain ( <ref n="Aesch. Ag. 1431"> <title>Ag.</title> 1431</ref> ff.).— Others understand, ‘this <emph>so-called</emph> noble woman,’ as if <quote xml:lang="grc">λόγοισι</quote> denoted words said <emph>of</emph> (not <emph>by</emph>) her. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φωνοῦσα_288"> expresses the loud tone in which the taunts are uttered: cp. <ref n="Soph. Trach. 267"> <title>Tr.</title> 267</ref>(Eurytus taunting Heracles), <quote xml:lang="grc">φωνεῖ δέ, δοῦλος κ.τ.λ.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δύσθεον_289">=<quote xml:lang="grc">ἄθεον, ἀσεβές</quote>: properly, ‘having untoward gods,’ as <quote xml:lang="grc">δυσδαίμων</quote>= ‘having evil fortune.’ The only other examples of <quote xml:lang="grc">δύσθεος</quote> are <title>Suppl.</title> 421, <ref n="Aesch. Ag. 1590"> <title>Ag.</title> 1590</ref>, <ref n="Aesch. Lib. 46"> <title>Ch.</title> 46</ref>, <ref n="Aesch. Lib. 191"> Ch. 191</ref>, <ref n="Aesch. Lib. 525."> Ch. 525.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μίσημα_289">, like <quote xml:lang="grc">στύγημα, δούλευμα</quote>, etc. ( <ref n="Soph. Ant. 320"> <title>Ant.</title> 320</ref> n.). Cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 991"> <title>Ph.</title> 991</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ μῖσος.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σοὶ_μόνῃ_289">: <ref n="Soph. El. 153">cp. 153</ref> n.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τέθνηκεν_289">, euphemistic for <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνῃρέθη</quote> (schol.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐν_πένθει_289">, as 847 <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν ἐν πένθει</quote>: <ref n="Plat. Rep. 605D">Plat. <title>Rep.</title> 605D</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν πένθει ὄντα</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκ_γόων_291">, instead of the simple gen.; <ref n="Soph. El. 231">cp. 231</ref>, 987.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἱ_θεοί_291">. Electra has invoked these deities as avengers (110). The prayer is that they may leave her in her present wretchedness. (Not, that, after death, she may lament for ever in the shades.) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τάδ_293">, instead of <quote xml:lang="grc">ταῦτ’</quote>, referring to the words just quoted: cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1045"> <title>Ph.</title> 1045</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">βαρύς τε καὶ βαρεῖαν ὁ ξένος φάτιν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">τήνδ’ εἶπ’</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἥξοντ_Ὀρέστην_293">. In vv. 778 ff. Clytaemnestra speaks as if this threat, which kept her in constant alarm, was made by Orestes himself. But his messages to Electra were secret (1155). The meaning is that rumours of his purpose reached her from friends in Phocis, such as Phanoteus (45). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παραστᾶς_295">, coming up to her in a threatening manner: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 992"> <title>O. C.</title> 992</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἴ τίς σε</quote>... | <quote xml:lang="grc">κτείνοι παραστάς</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἥτις_296"> after <quote xml:lang="grc">σόν</quote>, as in <ref n="Soph. OC 731"> <title>O. C.</title> 731</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὃν</quote> after <quote xml:lang="grc">τῆς ἐμῆς.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὑπεξέθου_296">, to Strophius at Crisa (180). <ref n="Soph. El. 1350">Cp. 1350</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑπεξεπέμφθην</quote>. <ref n="Thuc. 1. 89">Thuc. 1. 89</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">διεκουίζοντο εὐθὺς ὅθεν ὑπεξέθεντο</quote> (from Aegina) <quote xml:lang="grc">παῖδας καὶ γυναῖκας</quote>: <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 137 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἦλθε...καὶ ἐξ Ἄργους ἃ ὑπεξέκειτο</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλ_γ_298">. Though <quote xml:lang="grc">τείσουσ’ <emph>ἔτ’</emph> </quote> (cr. n.) is a specious conjecture, the text is confirmed by other examples of <quote xml:lang="grc">γε</quote> combined with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλά τοι</quote> in threats: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1064"> <title>Ant.</title> 1064</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ εὖ γέ τοι κάτισθι</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 1107"> <title>Tr.</title> 1107</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ εὖ γέ τοι τόδ’ ἴστε</quote>. For the spelling of <quote xml:lang="grc">τείσουσα</quote>, cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 810"> <title>O. T.</title> 810</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὑλακτεῖ_299">, the word used of a dog's bark, here describes a yell of rage, as in <ref n="Eur. Alc. 760">Eur. <title>Alc.</title> 760</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄμουσ’ ὑλακτῶν</quote> is said of the drunken Heracles.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σὺν_299"> is here an adv. (and not, as in 746, a case of tmesis): cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1288"> <title>Ai.</title> 1288</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅδ’ ἦν ὁ πράσσων ταῦτα, σὺν δ’ ἐγὼ παρών.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πέλας_299"> is combined with <quote xml:lang="grc">παρών</quote> as in <ref n="Soph. Aj. 83"> <title>Ai.</title> 83</ref>: so with <quote xml:lang="grc">παρέστατε</quote>, <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 1183: and <quote xml:lang="grc">παραστατήσειν</quote>, <ref n="Soph. OT 400."> <title>O. T.</title> 400.</ref>— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παρών_299"> implies support and aid; cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 373"> <title>Ph.</title> 373</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1405."> Ph. 1405.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πάντα_299"> often thus strengthens an adj., as <ref n="Soph. Aj. 911"> <title>Ai.</title> 911</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ πάντα κωφός, ὁ πάντ’ ἄϊδρις.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄναλκις_299">: <ref n="Hom. Od. 3.310"> <title>Od.</title> 3. 310</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνάλκιδος Αἰγίσθοιο</quote>: <ref n="Aesch. Ag. 1224">Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 1224</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">λέοντ’ ἄναλκιν.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἡ_βλάβη_299">, equiv. in sense to <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ πᾶς βλάβη ὤν</quote>: see on <ref n="Soph. Phil. 622"> <title>Ph.</title> 622</ref>, where this phrase is applied to Odysseus: and cp. <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 927 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ πῦρ σὺ καὶ πᾶν δεῖμα</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σὺν_γυναιξὶ_302">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> with Clytaemnestra for his ally.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὰς_μάχας_302">, such fights as he wages: for the art., cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 190"> <title>Ant.</title> 190</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοὺς φίλους ποιούμεθα</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐφήξειν_304">: cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 34"> <title>Ai.</title> 34</ref>(Ajax to Athena) <quote xml:lang="grc">καιρὸν δ’ ἐφήκεις</quote> (come to my aid). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μέλλων_τι_305">, intending to do something great,—as his frequent messages promised (1155). For this sense of <quote xml:lang="grc">δρᾶν τι</quote>, cp. <ref n="Soph. Trach. 160"> <title>Tr.</title> 160</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ ὥς τι δράσων εἷρπε</quote>, and <ref n="Soph. OC 732"> <title>O. C.</title> 732</ref>(n.): for <quote xml:lang="grc">μέλλων</quote> with pres. inf., <ref n="Soph. OT 967"> <title>O. T.</title> 967</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὰς_ἀπούσας_305">, simply, ‘all possible’ hopes; (not, ‘present, or more distant’:) cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1108"> <title>Ant.</title> 1108</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἴτ’ ἴτ’ ὀπάονες</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">οἵ τ’ ὄντες οἵ τ’ ἀπόντες</quote> (n.).</gloss>
lemma: τὰς οὔσας τε … καὶ τὰς ἀπούσας

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μου_305"> is unobjectionable, though I should prefer </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μοι_305"> (cr. n.). In <ref n="Soph. OC 355"> <title>O. C.</title> 355</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μοι</quote> is a probable correction of <quote xml:lang="grc">μου.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="διέφθορεν_305">. The perf. <quote xml:lang="grc">διέφθορα</quote> has always this act. sense where it occurs in Attic writers (Eur. , and the Comic poets). In <ref n="Hom. Il. 15.128"> <title>Il.</title> 15. 128</ref>(<quote xml:lang="grc">φρένας ἠλέ, διέφθορας</quote>) it has a pass. sense; so, too, with Hippocrates (8. 246), and with later writers, as Plutarch and Lucian. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σωφρονεῖν_307">, to observe such moderation as the Chorus recommend; </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εὐσεβεῖν_307">, to abstain from the unfilial behaviour which Clytaemnestra resents (596), and which Electra herself deplores as a cruel necessity (616—621). The passage is grievously marred by changing <quote xml:lang="grc">εὐσεβεῖν</quote>, as some do, to <quote xml:lang="grc">εὐστομεῖν</quote> (cr. n.). In <ref n="Aesch. Lib. 140">Aesch. <title>Ch.</title> 140</ref>Electra says, <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτῇ τέ μοι δὸς σωφρονεστέραν πολὺ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">μητρὸς γενέσθαι χεῖρά τ’ εὐσεβεστέραν</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔν_κακοῖς_307">: similarly <quote xml:lang="grc">τοι</quote> can separate the art. from its noun ( <ref n="Soph. OC 880"> <title>O. C.</title> 880</ref>, etc.). The vulg. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν τοῖς κακοῖς</quote> is not unmetrical (since not only <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῖς</quote>, but also <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν</quote>, coheres with <quote xml:lang="grc">κακοῖς</quote>), but is weak. In <ref n="Soph. Aj. 776"> <title>Ai.</title> 776</ref>, too, <quote xml:lang="grc">τοι</quote> was corrupted to <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῖς.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πολλή_ἀνάγκη_307">, as <ref n="Soph. OC 293"> <title>O. C.</title> 293</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Trach. 295."> <title>Tr.</title> 295.</ref>— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κἀπιτηδεύειν_κακά_307">: <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, as her circumstances are evil, so, on her own part (<quote xml:lang="grc">καί</quote>, <ref n="Soph. El. 1026">1026</ref>), she is driven to an evil behaviour, —<foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, to defying her mother: as she says in <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 621">621</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">αἰσχροῖς γὰρ αἰσχρὰ πράγματ’ ἐκδιδάσκεται</quote> </cit>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πάρεστιν_307">, as the context requires; for the opposition expressed by <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλά</quote> is merely to the negative form of the preceding clause, not to its sense. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὄντος_βεβῶτος_310">. The leading idea of the sentence is here expressed by the gen. absol., as so often by the participle in other cases (<title>O.C.</title> 1038 n.).—It was necessary for the plot that the absence of Aegisthus should be notified to the spectators at some early moment; Clytaemnestra reminds them of it at v. 517. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἦ_κάρτα_312"> recurs below, 1278; <ref n="Soph. Trach. 379"> <title>Tr.</title> 379</ref>; <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1359"> <title>Ai.</title> 1359</ref>: but only here as an independent affirmative, which is elsewhere <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ κάρτα</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. OC 65"> <title>O. C.</title> 65</ref>, <ref n="Soph. OC 301"> O. C. 301</ref>).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θυραῖον_312">, fem., as is <quote xml:lang="grc">θυραῖος</quote> in <ref n="Soph. Trach. 533"> <title>Tr.</title> 533</ref>(n.).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἰχνεῖν_312">: 165 n.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀγροῖσι_312">, like 174 <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐρανῷ</quote> n.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τυγχάνει_312">, without <quote xml:lang="grc">ὤν</quote>, 46 n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἦ_ἱκοίμην_314">..; The force of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἦ καί</quote>, which inquires with a certain eagerness (663, <title>O.T.</title> 368, 757), seems exactly in place here. The leader of the Chorus, not without trepidation, approaches the subject which is uppermost in their thoughts. With </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἦ_τν_314">, or </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἦ_δἂν_314"> (a crasis for which <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπειδὰν</quote> is quoted), there would be no interrogation. But neither seems nearly so fitting as <quote xml:lang="grc">ἦ κᾄν.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐς_ἱκοίμην_314"> instead of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐς λόγους ἱκοίμην σοι</quote>: cp. <ref n="Hdt. 2. 28">Her. 2. 28</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τῶν ἐμοὶ ἀπικομένων ἐς λόγους</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡς_νῦν_316"> is better here than </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὥς_νυν_316">.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἱστόρει_φίλον_316">; This punctuation is necessary unless the text is to be altered. It has been called ‘harsh.’ But it is not more abrupt than <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰδέναι θέλω</quote> in 318, and it suits the slight surprise with which Electra hears the question. The conjecture </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τό_316"> (or </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τά_316">) <quote xml:lang="grc">σοι φίλον</quote> throws an awkward stress on the enclitic <quote xml:lang="grc">σοι</quote>: and <quote xml:lang="grc">σοὶ</quote> would be inappropriate.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἱστόρει_φίλον_316_2">, the words could mean only, ‘ask <emph>what it is that</emph> you wish’; not, ‘ask <emph>whatever</emph> you wish.’ Classical Greek writers use <quote xml:lang="grc">τίς</quote> instead of <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅστις</quote> only where there is an indirect question (as <quote xml:lang="grc">λέγε τί χρῄζεις</quote>). In the Appendix I have examined the alleged exceptions to this rule. It will be found that, when they are real, they are post-classical. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_δὴ_317">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, without further preface: <ref n="Soph. El. 892">cp. 892</ref>: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 245"> <title>Ant.</title> 245</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ δὴ λέγω σοι</quote>: <ref n="Soph. OC 31"> <title>O. C.</title> 31</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῦ_φής_317">...; Cp. <ref n="Hom. Od. 11.174"> <title>Od.</title> 11. 174</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰπὲ δέ μοι πατρός τε καὶ υἱέος</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 439"> <title>Ph.</title> 439</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀναξίου μὲν φωτὸς ἐξερήσομαι</quote> (n.).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἥξοντος_317">, about to come (soon), </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἢ_μέλλοντος_317">, or delaying? Do his messages indicate zeal, or do they not? Cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1627"> <title>O. C.</title> 1627</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τί μέλλομεν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">χωρεῖν</quote>; The words could also mean, ‘about to come soon, or (at least) <emph>purposing</emph> to do so’ (cp. <ref n="Soph. Trach. 75"> <title>Tr.</title> 75</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπιστρατεύειν..ἢ μέλλειν ἔτι</quote>): but the antithesis recommends the former view.— The participles explain <quote xml:lang="grc">τί</quote>, being equiv. to <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅτι ἥξει, ἢ μέλλει</quote>; cp. <ref n="Aesch. Ag. 271">Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 271</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">εὖ γὰρ φρονοῦντος ὄμμα σοῦ κατηγορεῖ</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φησίν_γε_319">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἥξειν</quote>: <ref n="Soph. El. 171">cp. 171</ref> f.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ποεῖ_319">: for the spelling, cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 120"> <title>Ph.</title> 120</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_ἔγωγ_321">. It depends on the context in each case whether <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ μήν</quote> signifies (1) ‘and <emph>indeed</emph>,’ as in 556; or (2) ‘and <emph>yet</emph>,’ as here, and' in 1045, 1188. For the addition of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γε_321">, <ref n="Soph. El. 1045">cp. 1045</ref> n.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐκ_ὄκνῳ_321">, a dat. of manner (=<quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ ὀκνοῦσα</quote>). The emphasis is upon this phrase. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀρκεῖν_322">=<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπαρκεῖν</quote>, as in <ref n="Soph. OC 262."> <title>O. C.</title> 262.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπεί_323">, ‘for <emph>else</emph>’; <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ μὴ ἐπεποίθη</quote>. So in <title>O.T.</title> 433 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπεὶ</quote> implies <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ ᾔδη.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μακρὰν_323"> means, ‘so long as I actually have lived.’ ‘If I had not (hitherto) been confident, I should not have continued to live so long.’</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νῦν_323"> is better than </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νυν_323">. The Chorus wish Electra to cease speaking of Orestes, because they regard Chrysothemis as a partisan of Clytaemnestra and Aegisthus.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δόμων_323">, gen. of the place whence, with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φέρουσαν_323">: cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 613"> <title>Ph.</title> 613</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄγοιντο νήσου</quote>: <ref n="Soph. OT 152"> <title>O. T.</title> 152</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">Πυθῶνος..ἔβας</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅμαιμον_325"> is further defined, as in 12: <ref n="Soph. El. 156">cp. 156</ref>. <cit> <bibl n="Lys. 32.4">Lys. or. 32 § 4</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀδελφοὶ...ὁμοπάτριοι καὶ ὁμομήτριοι.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φύσιν_325">, adverbial acc., ‘by birth’: <ref n="Soph. El. 1125">cp. 1125</ref>:— <ref n="Soph. Trach. 380"> <title>Tr.</title> 380</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πατρὸς μὲν οὖσα γένεσιν Εὐρύτου</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Χρυσόθεμιν_326">: the name occurs nowhere else in the play, except in v. 158. Eur. uses it once, <ref n="Eur. Orest. 23">Eur. <title>Or.</title> 23</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">Χρυσόθεμις Ἰφιγένειά τ’ Ἠλέκτρα τ’ ἐγώ</quote>. For the place of the name in the sentence, <ref n="Soph. El. 695">cp. 695</ref>.—<quote xml:lang="grc"> <emph>ἐντάφια</emph>, ἐναγίσματα</quote>, offerings for the grave of Agamemnon, viz., (1) libations, <quote xml:lang="grc">χοαί</quote>, and (2) some other articles, such as flowers, and perhaps cakes, described at v. 434 by the word <quote xml:lang="grc">κτερίσματα</quote>, as distinct from <quote xml:lang="grc">λουτρά</quote>. Cp. v. 405 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔμπυρα</quote> (n.). It is evident that, even if she is attended by a <quote xml:lang="grc">πρόσπολος</quote>, Chrysothemis carries some, at least, of the gifts in her own hands: <ref n="Soph. El. 431">cp. 431</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὧν ἔχεις χεροῖν</quote>.—For the tribrach in the 5th foot, cp. <title>O.T.</title> 719 n. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἷα_327"> is nom. to </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νομίζεται_327">, not acc., as though <quote xml:lang="grc">φέρειν</quote> were understood: <ref n="Soph. El. 691">cp. 691</ref>. To pay funeral rites was <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ νομιζόμενα ποιεῖν</quote> ( Aeschin. or. 1 § 14) or <quote xml:lang="grc">φέρειν</quote> ( <ref n="Dem. 18.243">Dem. or. 18 § 243</ref>). Isae. or. 2 § 46 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐναγίζῃ αὐτῷ καθ’ ἕκαστον ἐνιαυτόν</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρὸς_ἐξόδοις_328">, close to the thoroughfare of the <quote xml:lang="grc">θυρών</quote> or vestibule ( <ref n="Soph. OT 1242"> <title>O. T.</title> 1242</ref>): cp. <ref n="Aesch. Seven 33">Aesch. <title>Th.</title> 33</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πυλῶν ἐπ’ ἐξόδοις</quote>. These words go with </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φωνεῖς_328">, while </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐλθοῦσα_328"> emphasises her boldness in seeking such publicity. As </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὖ_328"> implies, she has often done so before; <ref n="Soph. El. 517">cp. 517</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅς σ’ ἐπεῖχ’ ἀεὶ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">μή τοι θυραίαν γ’ οὖσαν αἰσχύνειν φίλους</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θυμῷ_χαρίζεσθαι_331">: cp. Antiphon <quote xml:lang="grc"> <title>τετρ.</title>Γ. γ.</quote> § 2 (<quote xml:lang="grc">μέθη</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπαίρει τῷ θυμῷ χαρίζεσθαι</quote>: Eur. fr. 31 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀργῇ γὰρ ὅστις εὐθέως χαρίζεται</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">κακῶς τελευτᾷ</quote>. So <ref n="Soph. OC 855">Soph. <title>O. C.</title> 855</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀργῇ χάριν δούς</quote>. The verb <quote xml:lang="grc">χαρίζομαι</quote> is nowhere else used by Sophocles. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καίτοι_οἶδα_332">, as in <ref n="Soph. OT 1455"> <title>O. T.</title> 1455</ref>: cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 441"> <title>Ai.</title> 441</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">καίτοι τοσοῦτόν γ’ ἐξεπίστασθαι δοκῶ.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κἀμαυτήν_ὅτι_332">, instead of <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅτι κἀγὼ</quote>: <ref n="Soph. El. 520">cp. 520</ref> f.: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 444"> <title>Ph.</title> 444</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦτον οἶσθ’ εἰ ζῶν κυρεῖ</quote>; (n.).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄν_332">, cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 339"> <title>O. T.</title> 339</ref> n.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἶ_φρονῶ_332">: cp. <ref n="Aristoph. Ach. 446">Ar. <title>Ach.</title> 446</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">Τηλέφῳ δ’ ἁγὼ φρονῶ</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὑφειμένῃ_335">, <emph>submisse:</emph> cp. <ref n="Eur. Alc. 524">Eur. <title>Alc.</title> 524</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κατθανεῖν ὑφειμένην</quote>, ‘resigned to die.’ Here the figurative </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πλεῖν_335"> gives a special sense to the partic., viz., ‘with lowered sail.’ So <ref n="Aristoph. Frogs 1220">Ar. <title>Ran.</title> 1220</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑφέσθαι μοι δοκεῖ·</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ ληκύθιον γὰρ τοῦτο πνευσεῖται πολύ</quote>. <ref n="Plut. Luc. 3">Plut. <title>Luc.</title> 3</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μεθ’ ἡμέραν μὲν ὑφειμένοις πλέων τοῖς ἱστίοις καὶ ταπεινοῖς, νύκτωρ δὲ ἐπαιρομένοις</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_δοκεῖν_336"> <quote xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</quote> Her thought is, <quote xml:lang="grc">δοκεῖς μὲν δρᾶν τι, πημαίνεις δὲ οὔ</quote>: ‘you have merely the semblance of being active against our foes, without really harming them. I will not imitate you.’ The first <quote xml:lang="grc">μὴ</quote> affects all that follows it. Such a combination of independent negatives is especially frequent in denials of illogical conduct; since Greek idiom loved to bring out a want of consistency by a parataxis with <quote xml:lang="grc">μέν</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">δέ</quote>. Thus <title>Alcib.</title> I. p. 124 C <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐγὼ γάρ τοι οὐ περὶ μὲν σοῦ λέγω ὡς χρὴ παιδευθῆναι, περὶ δ’ ἐμοῦ οὔ</quote>. <ref n="Dem. 18.179">Dem. or. 18 § 179</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ εἶπον μὲν ταῦτα, οὐκ ἔγραψα δέ</quote> (‘I did not say these things and then fail to propose them’). Similar is <ref n="Soph. OC 277">Soph. <title>O. C.</title> 277</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ μὴ θεοὺς τιμῶντες εἶτα τοὺς θεοὺς</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">†μοίραις†ποεῖσθε μηδαμῶς</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοιαῦτα_ἄλλα_337">. She wishes Electra's behaviour to be a faithful copy of her own. Cp. <ref n="Hdt. 1. 191">Her. 1. 191</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τά περ ἡ τῶν Βαβυλωνίων βασίλεια ἐποίησε..., ἐποίεε καὶ ὁ Κῦρος ἕτερα τοιαῦτα</quote>. <ref n="Plat. Rep. 372D">Plat. <title>Rep.</title> 372D</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">γηραιοὶ τελευτῶντες ἄλλον τοιοῦτον βίον τοῖς ἐκγόνοις παραδώσουσιν</quote>.—The MS. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλὰ_337"> is impossible. Thus placed, it could not have the ‘appealing’ force claimed for it by Schneidewin (‘<title>Come</title>, thus do thou also’): <ref n="Soph. El. 411">cp. 411</ref> n. It is strange that any doubts should be felt as to </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄλλα_337"> (cr. n.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καίτοι_δίκαιον_338"> <quote xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</quote> Chrysothemis, like Ismene ( <ref n="Soph. Ant. 65"> <title>Ant.</title> 65</ref>), recognises the duty from which she shrinks. The poet's object is not to contrast a good with a base nature, but the heroic with the commonplace.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κρίνεις_338">, decide, choose. The contrast is between the pronouns rather than between the verbs: thus the sense would be the same, if we had, <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐχ ᾗ ἐγὼ κρίνω, ἀλλ’ ᾗ σύ</quote>. (Cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 54"> <title>O. T.</title> 54</ref> n.)</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰ_δεῖ_338">=<quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ μέλλω</quote>: cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 1110"> <title>O. T.</title> 1110</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ χρή τι κἀμέ κ.τ.λ.</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 749"> <title>Tr.</title> 749</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ χρὴ μαθεῖν σε.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐλευθέραν_338">, whereas Electra is a slave (1192).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ζῆν_338">. A monosyllable, followed by a pause, can begin the verse even when, as here, it is non-emphatic: so <ref n="Soph. OT 1448"> <title>O. T.</title> 1448</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">θοῦ</quote>. But more often it has emphasis, as <ref n="Soph. OT 546"> <title>O. T.</title> 546</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">σοῦ</quote>, <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 986 <quote xml:lang="grc">ζῇ.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πάντ_338">, adv. (301).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀκουστέα_338">: for the plur., cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 677"> <title>Ant.</title> 677</ref> f. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀμυντέα...ἡσσητέα</quote> (n.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δεινόν_γε_341">. For this <quote xml:lang="grc">γε</quote> in comment, cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1225"> <title>Ph.</title> 1225</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">δεινόν γε φωνεῖς</quote> (n.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῆς_τικτούσης_342">, as <ref n="Soph. OT 1247"> <title>O. T.</title> 1247</ref>, the pres. part. expressing the permanent relationship; cp. <ref n="Eur. Ion 1560">Eur. <title>Ion</title> 1560</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἥδε τίκτει σ’</quote> (‘is thy mother’), and <ref n="Soph. OT 437">Soph. <title>O. T.</title> 437</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μέλειν_342">: schol. <quote xml:lang="grc">φροντίζειν</quote>. The personal use of <quote xml:lang="grc">μέλειν</quote> was admitted by Aesch. ( <ref n="Aesch. Ag. 370">Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 370</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">θεοὺς βροτῶν ἀξιοῦσθαι μέλειν</quote>), and Eur. ( <ref n="Eur. Her. 772">Eur. <title>H. F.</title> 772</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">θεοὶ τῶν ἀδίκων</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">μέλουσι</quote>). We are not obliged to assume it here, nor in <ref n="Soph. Aj. 688">Soph. <title>Ai.</title> 688</ref>, <quote xml:lang="grc">Τεύκρῳ τ’, ἢν μόλῃ, σημήνατε</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">μέλειν μὲν ἡμῶν, εὐνοεῖν δ’ ὑμῖν ἅμα</quote>: but in both places it gives the simplest construction. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τἀμὰ_νουθετήματα_343">: the possessive pron.=an objective gen., <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμοῦ</quote>: cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 969"> <title>O. T.</title> 969</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὠμῷ πόθῳ</quote>: <ref n="Soph. OC 332"> <title>O. C.</title> 332</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">σῇ... προμηθίᾳ.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κείνης_διδακτά_343">: cp. <ref n="Soph. Trach. 934"> <title>Tr.</title> 934</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκδιδαχθεὶς τῶν κατ’ οἶκον</quote>: <ref n="Soph. OT 1437"> <title>O. T.</title> 1437</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μηδενὸς προσήγορος.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκ_σαυτῆς_343">: <ref n="Soph. El. 885">cp. 885</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔπειθ_345">, ‘then,’ ‘after that’; <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, ‘such being the case,’—that you side with Clytaemnestra. This use of the word in logical inference is not rare (cp. <ref n="Hom. Il. 5.812"> <title>Il.</title> 5. 812</ref>, <ref n="Hom. Il. 10.243"> Il. 10. 243</ref>). Others render it: (1) ‘Further’—which does not fit the context: or (2) ‘And <emph>yet</emph>,’ ‘nevertheless,’—a sense which <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔπειτα</quote> seldom bears except in a question; <foreign xml:lang="lat">e.g.</foreign>, <ref n="Eur. Alc. 821">Eur. <title>Alc.</title> 821</ref> f. <quote xml:lang="grc">ΘΕ. γυνὴ μὲν οὖν ὄλωλεν Ἀδμήτου, ξένε. ΗΡ. τί φής; ἔπειτα δῆτά μ’ ἐξενίζετε</quote>; Nor is that sense so suitable here.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἑλοῦ_γε_345">. The effect of <quote xml:lang="grc">γε</quote> is merely to emphasise the verb,—opposing a definite <emph>choice</emph> to a compromise. <ref n="Soph. El. 411">Cp. 411</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">συγγένεσθέ γ’</quote>: 1035 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπίστω γ’</quote>. When <quote xml:lang="grc">γε</quote> is thus added to the imperative, it is more often in such combinations as <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅρα γε μήν</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. OC 587"> <title>O. C.</title> 587</ref>), or <quote xml:lang="grc">παῦσαί γε μέντοι</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. Aj. 483"> <title>Ai.</title> 483</ref>).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φρονεῖν_κακῶς_345">, to be imprudent. The chief theme of the timid sister's speech (328—340) has been prudence; as in 994 she insists on <quote xml:lang="grc">τὴν εὐλάβειαν</quote>, and Electra says (1027) <quote xml:lang="grc">ζηλῶ σε τοῦ νοῦ, τῆς δὲ δειλίας στυγῶ</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῶν_φίλων_346">, meaning esp. her father: <ref n="Soph. El. 241">cp. 241</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">γονέων</quote>: 652 <quote xml:lang="grc">φίλοισι</quote>. (In 368, however, <quote xml:lang="grc">φίλους</quote>=Electra and Orestes.) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἥτις_347">, causal, because v. 346 describes the course which she is actually taking. The words <quote xml:lang="grc">λέγεις μὲν..ἐκδείξειας ἄν</quote> correspond with </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φρονοῦσα_347">, as illustrating her prudence; while 349 f. explain the sense in which she forgets her father.— </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μῖσος_347">: Electra puts bluntly what Chrysothemis veiled by the euphemism <quote xml:lang="grc">οἷ’ αὐτοῖς φρονῶ</quote> (334). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πάντα_349">, adv.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τιμωρουμένης_349">: the midd., as in 399, where the active would be normal. ‘To avenge one’ is usu. <quote xml:lang="grc">τιμωρεῖν τινι</quote> (the accus., denoting the person chastised, being often omitted), as <ref n="Soph. OT 136"> <title>O. T.</title> 136</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">γῇ τῇδε τιμωροῦντα</quote>. ‘To <emph>punish</emph> a person’ is usu. <quote xml:lang="grc">τιμωρεῖσθαί τινα</quote> (to which a dat. of the person avenged can be added): <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1258"> <title>Ph.</title> 1258</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅς σε τιμωρήσεται</quote>. Conversely in <ref n="Soph. OT 107"> <title>O. T.</title> 107</ref>, <ref n="Soph. OT 140"> O. T. 140</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τιμωρεῖν</quote>= <quote xml:lang="grc">τιμωρεῖσθαι</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὔτε_τε_350">: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1397"> <title>O. C.</title> 1397</ref> n.: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1321"> <title>Ph.</title> 1321</ref> f., <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1363."> Ph. 1363.</ref> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τήν_δρῶσαν_350">: more pointed here than <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμέ τε δρῶσαν</quote>: ‘<emph>her</emph> who <emph>does</emph> act.’ </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρὸς_κακοῖσι_351">, ‘in addition to the miseries’ of the family.—Not:—‘Do not these things involve cowardice in addition to being base?’ That would require <quote xml:lang="grc">πρὸς τῷ κακῷ</quote>, as Plat. <title>Crito</title>p. 46 A <quote xml:lang="grc">μὴ ἅμα τῷ κακῷ καὶ αἰσχρὰ ᾖ</quote> (=<quote xml:lang="grc">ἅμα τῷ κακὰ εἶναι</quote>).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δειλίαν_ἔχει_351">, cp. <ref n="Dem. 18.279">Dem. or. 18 § 279</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ δὲ δὴ καὶ τοὺς πρὸς ἐμὲ αὐτὸν ἀγῶνας ἐάσαντα νῦν ἐπὶ τόνδ’ ἥκειν καὶ πᾶσαν ἔχει κακίαν</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπεὶ_352">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, if there be indeed any good in such counsels. <ref n="Soph. El. 323">Cp. 323</ref>. For this controversial <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπεί</quote> with the imperat., see <ref n="Soph. OT 390"> <title>O. T.</title> 390</ref>, <ref n="Soph. OC 969."> <title>O. C.</title> 969.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἢ_ἐμοῦ_352">: <ref n="Soph. El. 565">cp. 565</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἢ )γὼ φράσω</quote>. For the parenthesis, Schneid. cp. <ref n="Eur. Cycl. 121">Eur. <title>Cycl.</title> 121</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">σπείρουσι δ’, ἢ τῷ ζῶσι, Δήμητρος στάχυν</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπαρκούντως_354"> occurs nowhere else in classical Greek: though, as Dindorf notes, it is cited by the grammarian Joannes Philoponus (7th cent. A.D. ) in his <quote xml:lang="grc">τονικὰ παραγγέλματα</quote>, <ref>p. 39. 17</ref>. But the corresponding sense of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπαρκεῖν</quote>, though rare, is well-attested: <cit> <bibl>Solon fr. 5, 1</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τόσον κράτος, ὅσσον ἐπαρκεῖ</quote> </cit>. It seems unnecessary, then, to read <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπαρκούντως</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προσάπτειν_356">, render as a tribute: cp. <ref n="Hom. Il. 24.110"> <title>Il.</title> 24. 110</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τόδε κῦδος Ἀχιλλῆι προτιάπτω</quote>. <ref n="Pind. N. 8">Pind. <title>N.</title> 8. 36</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς παισὶ κλέος</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">μὴ τὸ δύσφαμον προσάψω</quote>. <ref n="Plat. Soph. 231A">Plat. <title>Soph.</title> 231A</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μὴ μεῖζον αὐτοῖς προσάπτωμεν γέρας</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἴ_χάρις_356">, if any gratification can be felt in the nether world,— <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅπου τὸ χαίρειν μηδαμοῦ νομίζεται</quote> ( <ref n="Aesch. Eum. 423">Aesch. <title>Eum.</title> 423</ref>). For <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκεῖ</quote>=<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν Ἅιδου</quote>, cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 855">Soph. <title>Ai.</title> 855</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 76.">Soph. <title>Ant.</title> 76.</ref> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἡμὶν_357">, ethic dat., (thou, whom I am asked to regard as hating,) 272.— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ξύνει_357">, here=‘art their <emph>ally</emph>’: whereas in 263 the same phrase, applied to Electra, means merely that she dwells in the same house. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὰ_δῶῤ_359">, the privileges (in regard to soft living) which the rulers confer upon her.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐφ_οἶσι_359">, as in 333, instead of the simple dat.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χλιδᾷς_359">, <emph>superbis.</emph> Eur. fr. 986 <quote xml:lang="grc">πλούτῳ χλιδῶσα θνητὰ δή, γύναι, φρόνει</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὑπεικάθοιμι_361">: on these forms, see <ref n="Soph. OT 651"> <title>O. T.</title> 651</ref> n.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πλουσία_361">: in contrast with the <quote xml:lang="grc">κεναὶ τράπεζαι</quote> of 192.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="περιρρείτω_361">, be superabundant; a sense of <quote xml:lang="grc">περιρρεῖν</quote> not elsewhere found in Greek of this age; but cp. <ref n="Plut. Per. 16">Plut. <title>Per.</title> 16</ref>(referring to the domestic economy of Pericles), <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδενὸς οἷον</quote> (as is usual) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν οἰκίᾳ μεγάλῃ καὶ πράγμασιν ἀφθόνοις περιρρέοντος</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοὐμὲ_λυπεῖν_363">: ‘For me, let it be food enough that I do not pain myself (by a base compliance with the murderers)’: <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμὲ</quote> for <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμαυτήν</quote>, as <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμοί</quote> for <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμαυτῷ</quote> in <ref n="Soph. Ant. 736"> <title>Ant.</title> 736</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄλλῳ γὰρ ἢ )μοὶ χρή με τῆσδ’ ἄρχειν χθονός</quote>; For the figurative sense of <quote xml:lang="grc">βόσκημα</quote>, cp. <ref n="Aesch. Lib. 26">Aesch. <title>Ch.</title> 26</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">δἰ αἰῶνος δ’ ἰυγμοῖσι βόσκεται κέαρ</quote>. The phrase <quote xml:lang="grc">λυπεῖν ἑαυτόν</quote> seems to have been familiar: <ref n="Eur. Cycl. 336">Eur. <title>Cycl.</title> 336</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς τοὐμπιεῖν γε καὶ φαγεῖν τοὐφ’ ἡμέραν</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">Ζεὺς οὗτος ἀν‐</quote> <quote xml:lang="grc">θρώποισι τοῖσι σώφροσι</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">λυπεῖν δὲ μηδὲν α<*>τόν</quote>: Eur. (?) fr. 174 (Nauck) <quote xml:lang="grc">μὴ οὖν ἔθελε λυπεῖν σαυτόν</quote> (where Hermann conjectured <quote xml:lang="grc">μή νυν θέλε</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">λυπεῖν σεαυτόν</quote>). The tone of the phrase here is explained by the context. In the preceding verses Electra has fully set forth her view: now she is summing it up, in words suited to a hearer of whose sympathy she despairs. ‘Enough for me if I do not offend my own sense of right’; <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, ‘I must obey my own instincts,—as you follow yours.’</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐδ_σὺ_365"> <quote xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</quote>, <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐρῴης</quote> (or <quote xml:lang="grc">ἤρας</quote>), as <quote xml:lang="grc">οὖσα</quote>=<quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ εἴης</quote> (or <quote xml:lang="grc">ἦσθα</quote>): for the ellipse of a verb after <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄν</quote>, cp. <ref n="Soph. Trach. 462"> <title>Tr.</title> 462</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 115."> <title>Ph.</title> 115.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παῖδα_366">, where the dat. <quote xml:lang="grc">παιδὶ</quote> is also admissible: cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 838"> <title>Ant.</title> 838</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κεκλῆσθαι_366">: cp. fr. 83 <quote xml:lang="grc">καταρκεῖ τοῦδε κεκλῆσθαι πατρός</quote>. By forgetting her duty to her father, she as it were repudiates him, and will be known only as Clytaemnestra's daughter. Here (as in 341 f.) it is implied that the paternal claim on filial piety is naturally stronger than the maternal ( <ref n="Aesch. Eum. 658">Aesch. <title>Eum.</title> 658</ref> ff.: <ref n="Eur. Orest. 552">Eur. <title>Or.</title> 552</ref> f.). In the case of a son, <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ μητρὸς καλεῖσθαι</quote> conveyed a reproach of effeminacy: thus in Soph. fr. 139 (from a satyrplay) a boy is described as now too old <quote xml:lang="grc">μητρὸς καλεῖσθαι παῖσα, τοῦ πατρὸς παρόν</quote>. (See, too, <ref n="Eur. El. 933">Eur. <title>El.</title> 933 ff.</ref>) </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πλείστοις_367">: as below, in vv. 975— 985, she imagines how she and her sister, if they act nobly, will be praised by all. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μηδὲν_369">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἴπῃς</quote>: </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρὸς_ὀργὴν_369">, ‘angrily’; like <quote xml:lang="grc">πρὸς βίαν, πρὸς ἡδονήν</quote>, etc. ( <ref n="Soph. Phil. 90"> <title>Ph.</title> 90</ref> n.): <ref n="Aristoph. Frogs 856">Ar. <title>Ran.</title> 856</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">σὺ δὲ μὴ πρὸς ὀργήν, Αἰσχύλ’, ἀλλὰ πραόνως</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔλεγχ’</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀμφοῖν_370"> is best taken as dat. fem., ‘for both of you,’ ‘on both your parts’: though (notwithstanding its position) it could be also gen. fem. The objection to taking it as dat. masc. with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῖς_λόγοις_370"> is that the noun or pron. joined to <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄμφω</quote> or <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀμφοτέρω</quote> is usu. dual, as <ref n="Soph. OC 483"> <title>O. C.</title> 483</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξ ἀμφοῖν χεροῖν</quote>, <ref n="Soph. OT 683"> <title>O. T.</title> 683</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀμφοῖν ἀπ’ αὐτοῖν</quote>. Similarly <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀμφότεροι</quote> usu. takes a plur.; for <ref n="Hom. Il. 21.115"> <title>Il.</title> 21. 115</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">χεῖρε πετάσσας</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀμφοτέρας</quote> is exceptional. A discrepancy in the number of the verb is more frequent, as <ref n="Plat. Rep. 478A">Plat. <title>Rep.</title> 478A</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">δυνάμεις δὲ ἀμφότεραί ἐστον</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῆσδε_αὕτη_371">, referring to the same person, as <ref n="Soph. Phil. 841"> <title>Ph.</title> 841</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦδε...τοῦτον</quote> (n.). Electra is in need of caution, and Chrysothemis of loyalty. For </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πάλιν_371">, <ref n="Soph. El. 1434">cp. 1434</ref>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐγὼ_μὲν_372">: 251 n.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πως_372">, <emph>fere: </emph> <ref n="Soph. Aj. 327"> Ai. 327</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοιαῦτα γάρ πως καὶ λέγει κὠδύρεται. </quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μύθων_372">, in a disparaging sense, as <ref n="Eur. Andr. 744">Eur. <title>Andr.</title> 744</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοὺς σοὺς δὲ μύθους ῥᾳδίως ἐγὼ φέρω</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐδ_373">, ‘nor.’ Brunck wrote </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κοὐδ_373">, wishing <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδέ</quote> to mean ‘not even’ (cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1429"> <title>O. C.</title> 1429</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδ’ ἀγγελοῦμεν φλαῦρα</quote>). But this is unnecessary, esp. as </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ποτέ_373"> follows. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἰὸν_374">: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1771"> <title>O. C.</title> 1771</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἰόντα φόνον</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῖσιν ὁμαίμοις.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σχήσει_374">: 223. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸ_δεινὸν_376">: so Antigone to Ismene, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 95"> <title>Ant.</title> 95</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔα με</quote>.. | <quote xml:lang="grc">παθεῖν τὸ δεινὸν τοῦτο.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰ_μοι_376">. Elmsley proposed <quote xml:lang="grc">δὲ</quote> instead of <quote xml:lang="grc">γάρ</quote>. But the spondee can stand in the 5th foot, since </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰ_376"> coheres with the following words (the metrical effect being as that of one word, <foreign xml:lang="lat">e.g.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξαιρούμενον</quote>): so <ref n="Soph. OC 115"> <title>O. C.</title> 115</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν γὰρ τῷ μαθεῖν</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γὰρ_379">, prefatory: 32.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γόων_379">, though v. 375 ends with the same word: <ref n="Soph. El. 161">cp. 161</ref>, 163 (<quote xml:lang="grc">γᾶ...γᾶν</quote>). This may have prompted the weak <emph>v.l.</emph> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λόγων_379">. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐνταῦθα_380">=<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐνταυθοῖ</quote>, as in <ref n="Soph. Trach. 1193."> <title>Tr.</title> 1193.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔνθα_προσόψει_380">: <ref n="Soph. El. 436">cp. 436</ref>: <title>O.T.</title> 1412 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκρίψατ’, ἔνθα μήποτ’ εἰσόψεσθ’ ἔτι</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 800"> <title>Tr.</title> 800</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Aj. 659."> <title>Ai.</title> 659.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ζῶσα_380">, implying that it will be a living death; cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 888"> <title>Ant.</title> 888</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ζῶσα τυμβεύειν</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κατηρεφεῖ_380">, lit. ‘roofed over’; the <quote xml:lang="grc">στέγη</quote> meant is a vault or dungeon, not a natural cavern (though the adj. would suit that also). So the sepulchral chamber of Antigone is a <quote xml:lang="grc">κατηρεφὴς τύμβος</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. Ant. 885"> <title>Ant.</title> 885</ref>, n.: cp. <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 774, and 891).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χθονὸς_ἐκτὸς_380">, because the usurpers might well fear the sympathy which disaffected Mycenaeans (like the women of the Chorus) would feel with Electra. Some critics seek to alter the text (cr. n.), thinking that the prison ought to be either in or near the house, or at least in Argolis. But they have overlooked Electra's own words in 391, referring to this threat—<quote xml:lang="grc">ὅπως ἀφ’ ὑμῶν ὡς προσώτατ’ ἐκφύγω</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὑμνήσεις_380">, <emph>decantabis:</emph> cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 658"> <title>Ant.</title> 658</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πρὸς ταῦτ’ ἐφυμνείτω Δία</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ξύναιμον</quote>. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 292"> <title>Ai.</title> 292</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">βαἴ, ἀεὶ δ’ ὑμνούμενα</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρὸς_ταῦτα_383"> is often joined to the imperat. in warning or menace; <ref n="Soph. El. 820">cp. 820</ref>; <ref n="Soph. OT 426"> <title>O. T.</title> 426</ref>; <ref n="Soph. OC 455"> <title>O. C.</title> 455</ref>; <ref n="Soph. Ant. 658"> <title>Ant.</title> 658</ref>; <ref n="Soph. Aj. 971"> <title>Ai.</title> 971</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1066"> Ai. 1066</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1115."> Ai. 1115.</ref> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κοὔ_383">, the conjecture of Blaydes. But </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καί_383"> is right. She says, ‘reflect (now), and <emph>do not</emph> blame me after the event.’ This is manifestly fitter than, ‘reflect, and you <emph>will not</emph> blame me,’ etc.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἦ_δὴ_385"> <quote xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</quote> The formula </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἦ_δὴ_385_2"> expresses lively surprise, just as in <ref n="Soph. Phil. 565"> <title>Ph.</title> 565</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">η<*> ταῦτα δὴ Φοῖνίξ τε χοἱ ξυνναυβάται</quote> | ..<quote xml:lang="grc">δρῶσιν</quote>..; There is no reason for preferring </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἦ_γὰρ_385"> (cr. n.), which is slightly less animated. It may be noticed that <quote xml:lang="grc">ἦ γάρ</quote>, as used by Sophocles, usually asks whether an inference from the previous speaker's words is correct (‘am I to understand that...’): see <emph>e.g. </emph> <ref n="Soph. OT 1000"> O. T. 1000</ref>, <ref n="Soph. OT 1039"> O. T. 1039</ref>, <ref n="Soph. OT 1173"> O. T. 1173</ref>; <ref n="Soph. Phil. 248"> <title>Ph.</title> 248</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 322"> Ph. 322</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 654."> Ph. 654.</ref>But here the question is virtually no more than an astonished comment.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_βεβούλευνται_385">: <quote xml:lang="grc">καί</quote> emphasises the verb: cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 726"> <title>Ant.</title> 726</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἱ τηλικοίδε καὶ διδαξόμεσθα δή</quote>..; <ref n="Soph. OT 772"> <title>O. T.</title> 772</ref> n. For the perf. pass. with middle force, <ref n="Soph. El. 947">cp. 947</ref>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλ_ἐξίκοιτο_387">: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 44"> <title>O. C.</title> 44</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ ἵλεῳ...δεξαίατο.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῦδέ_οὕνεκ_387">, ‘for that matter,’ ‘if that is all’; <ref n="Soph. El. 605">cp. 605</ref>, 787: <ref n="Soph. OC 22"> <title>O. C.</title> 22</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τίνα_τόνδ_388"><*> cp. <ref n="Soph. Trach. 184"> <title>Tr.</title> 184</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τίν’</quote> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἶπας, <*> γεραιέ, τόνδε μοι λόγον</quote> <emph>; </emph> <ref n="Soph. OC 68"> O. C. 68</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τάλαινα_388">, ‘misguided,’ as Ismene says to Antigone, <quote xml:lang="grc">οἴμοι ταλαίνης</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. Ant. 82"> <title>Ant.</title> 82</ref>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἴ_τῶνδε_389">, with a shade of irony, ‘anything of this kind’: <ref n="Soph. OC 1034"> <title>O. C.</title> 1034</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">νοεῖς τι τούτων</quote> <emph>; </emph> <ref n="Soph. OT 1140"> O. T. 1140</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">λέγω τι τούτων κ.τ.λ.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ποῦ_φρενῶν_390">; Cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 42"> <title>Ant.</title> 42</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ποῦ γνώμης ποτ’ εἶ</quote>; (n.). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅπως_391"> without <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄν</quote>, as in 688.— </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὑμῶν_391">, bitterly identifying her sister with her foes, whom she has hitherto called ‘<emph>them</emph>’ (348, 355, 361).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προσώτατ_391">. Several recent editions read </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προσωτάτω_φύγω_391"> with L. Dindorf, on the ground that this was the correct form of the superl. adv. It is certainly the only form which elsewhere occurs in classical But HerodotusAttic. , at least, could say <quote xml:lang="grc">προσώτατα ἀπικέσθαι</quote> (2. 103); as also <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ ἀνώτατα</quote> (2. 125), <quote xml:lang="grc">οἱ...κατώτατα ἑστεῶτες</quote> (7. 23), where an Attic writer would have used <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνωτάτω, κατωτάτω</quote>. Pindar has <quote xml:lang="grc">περαίτερον ἄλλων</quote> (<title>O.</title> 8. 63), instead of <quote xml:lang="grc">περαιτέρω</quote>. Thucydides uses <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐγγύτατα</quote> (1. 13 etc.) even more frequently than <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐγγυτάτω</quote> (3. 38 etc.). It seems rash, then, to asser<*> that Sophocles could not possibly have used <quote xml:lang="grc">προσώτατα</quote>, especially when it is so decidedly commended by euphony. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βίου_παρόντος_392">, as compared with the life in the vault (3<*>1). Electra herself had said that it sufficed for her (354). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καλὸς_γὰρ_393"> <quote xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</quote> For this use of <quote xml:lang="grc">γὰρ</quote> in a sarcastic retort, cp. <ref n="Aristoph. Ach. 71">Ar. <title>Ach.</title> 71</ref>, where the <quote xml:lang="grc">πρέσβυς</quote> has described himself and his colleagues as <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπολλύμενοι</quote>, and Dicaeopolis rejoins, <quote xml:lang="grc">σφόδρα γὰρ ἐσῳζόμην ἐγώ κ.τ.λ.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θαυμάσαι_393">: cp. <ref n="Thuc. 1. 138">Thuc. 1. 138</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄξιος θαυμάσαι</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῖς_φίλοις_395">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">τῷ πατρί</quote>, as in 346. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰκαθεῖν_396">: <ref n="Soph. El. 361">cp. 361</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ταῦτα_397">, cogn. acc., <quote xml:lang="grc">ταύτην τὴν θωπείαν</quote>. For the verb, cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1336."> <title>O. C.</title> 1336.</ref>— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐκ_ἐμοὺς_397"> <foreign xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</foreign>: <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμοὺς</quote> is predicative, like <quote xml:lang="grc">τίν’</quote> in 388 (n.). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γε_398"> emphasises <quote xml:lang="grc">καλόν</quote>: </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μέντοι_398">= ‘however’: <ref n="Soph. OT 442"> <title>O. T.</title> 442</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μὴ_ξ_398">. The crasis of <quote xml:lang="grc">μή</quote> with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκ</quote> occurs also in <ref n="Soph. Aj. 278"> <title>Ai.</title> 278</ref>, <ref n="Soph. OT 1075"> <title>O. T.</title> 1075</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 467"> <title>Ph.</title> 467</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Trach. 727"> <title>Tr.</title> 727</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Trach. 1235."> Tr. 1235.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πεσεῖν_398">, to be ruined; as <ref n="Soph. OT 50"> <title>O. T.</title> 50</ref>, <ref n="Soph. OT 146"> O. T. 146</ref>, <ref n="Soph. OT 376"> O. T. 376</ref>: <ref n="Soph. OC 395."> <title>O. C.</title> 395.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="συγγνώμην_400">. Ismene defends herself by a like excuse,—<quote xml:lang="grc">αἰτοῦσα τοὺς ὑπὸ χθονὸς</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ξύγγνοιαν ἴσχειν</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. Ant. 65"> <title>Ant.</title> 65</ref>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τἄπη_401">, maxims, sentiments: cp. <ref n="Aesch. Seven 717">Aesch. <title>Th.</title> 717</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ ἄνδρ’ ὁπλίτην τοῦτο χρὴ στέργειν ἔπος.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρὸς_κακῶν_401">, it befits them: for this use of <quote xml:lang="grc">πρός</quote>, cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 319"> <title>Ai.</title> 319</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Aj. 581"> Ai. 581</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1071."> Ai. 1071.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μή_πω_403">, ironical; cp. <ref n="Eur. Hec. 1278">Eur. <title>Hec.</title> 1278</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μή πω μανείη Τυνδαρὶς τοσόνδε παῖς</quote>: id. <ref n="Eur. Med. 365">Eur. <title>Med.</title> 365</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ οὔ τι ταύτῃ ταῦτα· μὴ δοκεῖτέ πω</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἷπερ_ὁδοῦ_404">. The gen. is partitive, just as in 1035 <quote xml:lang="grc">οἷ..ἀτιμίας</quote>. The only peculiarity is that <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁδοῦ</quote> is used in an abstract sense,—‘to that point of <emph>journeying</emph>’;—as we might have <quote xml:lang="grc">οἷ πορείας</quote>, or <quote xml:lang="grc">οἷ πλανημάτων</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῷ_φέρεις_405">. <quote xml:lang="grc">ποῖ</quote> has better authority than <quote xml:lang="grc">τῷ</quote> (cr. n.), but the latter is certainly to be preferred here.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔμπυρα_405"> probably refers to some articles of food, perhaps cakes, which she (or a handmaid) was carrying to be burned at the grave (<ref n="Soph. El. 326">cp. 326</ref> n.). Thus Lucian, speaking of offerings to the dead, says (<title>Charon</title> 22), <quote xml:lang="grc">καίουσί τε τὰ πολυτελῆ δεῖπνα, καὶ ἐς τὰ ὀρύγματα ο<*>=νον καὶ μελίκρατον, ὡς γοῦν εἰκάσαι, ἐκχέουσιν</quote>. Chrysothemis, in her reply, naturally speaks of the <quote xml:lang="grc">χοαί</quote>, since they formed the most characteristic part of the rite. But it seems impossible that the word <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔμπυρα</quote> should directly denote the libations, as was supposed by the schol. (<quote xml:lang="grc">τάδ’ ἔμπυρα· ταύτας τὰς σπονδάς</quote>), and by Triclinius. There is nothing to show that the term <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔμπυρα</quote>, ‘burnt offerings,’ was ever extended to offerings generally; or that <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔμπυρα</quote> could mean, ‘offerings at a <quote xml:lang="grc">πυρά</quote>’ (as the grave is called in 901). In the only other place where Sophocles uses the word <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔμπυρα</quote>, it has its ordinary sense ( <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1005"> <title>Ant.</title> 1005</ref>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τυμβεῦσαι_χοάς_406">, to <emph>offer</emph> them <emph>at</emph> the tomb; cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1063"> <title>Ai.</title> 1063</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">σῶμα τυμβεῦσαι τάφῳ</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βροτῶν_407">, though referring to the dead; <ref n="Soph. El. 462">cp. 462</ref>: <ref n="Aesch. Lib. 129">Aesch. <title>Ch.</title> 129</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">χέουσα τάσδε χέρνιβας βροτοῖς</quote> (to Agamemnon). But in <ref n="Soph. Ant. 851">Soph. <title>Ant.</title> 851</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">βροτοί</quote> are opposed to <quote xml:lang="grc">νεκροί</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὃν_αὐτή_408">. Not </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅν_γ_408">: she is finishing Electra's sentence for her. Cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 105"> <title>Ph.</title> 105</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 985."> Ph. 985.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῷ_ἤρεσεν_409">; The spondee in the 5th foot is correct, since <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦτ’</quote> coheres with its verb, giving the effect of a single word. (<ref n="Soph. El. 376">Cp. 376</ref>.) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δείματος_410">, a word often used of a terrifying dream: <ref n="Aesch. Lib. 523">Aesch. <title>Ch.</title> 523</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔκ τ ὀνειράτων</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ νυκτιπλάγκτων δειμάτων πεπαλμένη</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">χοὰς ἔπεμψε τάσδε δύσθεος γυνή</quote>. <ref n="Eur. Hec. 69">Eur. <title>Hec.</title> 69</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τί ποτ’ αἴρομαι ἔννυχος οὕτω</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">δείμασι, φάσμασιν</quote>; Lycophion 225 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπῶσαι νυκτίφοιτα δείματα</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δοκεῖν_ἐμοί_410">: cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 82"> <title>O. T.</title> 82</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ εἰκάσαι μέν, ἡδύς</quote> (n.). At v. 426 she speaks positively; as if the recital of the dream had raised her surmise into certainty. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θεοὶ_πατρῷοι_411">, the gods of the Pelopid house: see on <ref n="Soph. OC 756"> <title>O. C.</title> 756</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πρὸς θεῶν πατρῴων</quote>: and for the synizesis in <quote xml:lang="grc">θεοί</quote>, also <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 964 n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="συγγένεσθέ_γ_411">: cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 275"> <title>O. T.</title> 275</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">εὖ ξυνεῖεν εἰσαεὶ θεοί</quote>: <ref n="Aesch. Lib. 460">Aesch. <title>Ch.</title> 460</ref>(the Chorus invoking Agamemnon) <quote xml:lang="grc">ξὺν δὲ γενοῦ πρὸς ἐχθρούς</quote>. For <quote xml:lang="grc">γε</quote>, <ref n="Soph. El. 345">cp. 345</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλὰ_νῦν_411">, ‘now at least,’—though not sooner. Cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1276"> <title>O. C.</title> 1276</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πειράσατ’ ἀλλ’ ὑμεῖς γε</quote> (n.). She hails the dream as a sign from the nether world that vengeance is imminent, and invokes the gods of her house to co-operate with the <quote xml:lang="grc">χθόνιοι</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἴποιμ_τότε_413">. When <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄν</quote> is the second syllable of the 5th foot, it is usually preceded by an elision; as in <ref n="Eur. Andr. 935">Eur. <title>Andr.</title> 935</ref>, <ref n="Eur. Andr. 1184">Eur. Andr. 1184</ref>; <ref n="Eur. Phoen. 1619">Eur. <title>Phoen.</title> 1619</ref>, <ref n="Eur. Phoen. 1626">Eur. Phoen. 1626</ref>; <ref n="Eur. Heraclid. 456">Eur. <title>Heracl.</title> 456</ref>; Eur. fr. 362 <quote xml:lang="grc">κἀποσώσαἰ ἂν πατρός</quote> (Pors. <title>Suppl. Praefat.</title> p. xxxiv). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπὶ_σμικρὸν_414">, lit. ‘to a small extent,’ like <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπὶ πολύ, ἐπὶ μέγα, ἐπὶ μακρόν</quote>, etc. Cp. <ref n="Plat. Soph. 254B">Plat. <title>Soph.</title> 254B</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κοινωνεῖν..τὰ μὲν ἐπ’ ὀλίγον, τὰ δ’ ἐπὶ πολλά</quote>. G. Wolff's reading, </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπὶ_σμικρῶν_414">, could mean, ‘in a few words,’ but is less natural here.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φράσαι_414"> is almost redundant, as in <title>O.C.</title> 35, 50, 1582. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σμικροὶ_λόγοι_415">, here in the sense of ‘few,’ ‘brief,’ rather than trivial. Cp. <title>O.T.</title> 120 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἓν γὰρ πόλλ’ ἃν ἐξεύροι μαθεῖν</quote>. <title>O.C.</title> 443 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔπους σμικροῦ χάριν</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πατρὸς_ὁμιλίαν_417">= <quote xml:lang="grc">πατέρα αὖθις ὁμιλοῦντα</quote>: cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 872"> <title>Ai.</title> 872</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ναὸς κοινόπλουν ὁμιλίαν</quote>: <ref n="Eur. Heraclid. 581">Eur. <title>Heracl.</title> 581</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑμεῖς δ’, ἀδελφῶν ἡ παροῦσ’ ὁμιλία</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">εὐδαιμονοῖτε</quote>. So <ref n="Soph. Phil. 868">Soph. <title>Ph.</title> 868</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἰκούρημα...ξένων</quote> (n.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐφέστιον_πῆξαι_417">: cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 1411"> <title>O. T.</title> 1411</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">θαλάσσιον ἐκρίψατ’</quote> (n.). The floor of the Homeric megaron was not of wood or stone, but merely of earth trodden hard; Odysseus, when he sets up the axes, digs a trench in it ( <ref n="Hom. Od. 21.120"> <title>Od.</title> 21. 120</ref>). <quote xml:lang="grc">πῆξαι</quote>, then, affords no reason against referring <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐφέστιον</quote> to the hearth in the megaron (270 n.).
