But the vision of the tree becomes more intelligible if we imagine the sceptre planted at the altar of Zeus Herkeios in the open <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐλή</quote> of the house ( <ref n="Soph. Ant. 487"> <title>Ant.</title> 487</ref>). The suppliants at the household altars of Oedipus can say of themselves, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἑζόμεσθ’ ἐφέστιοι</quote> (<title>O.T.</title> 32).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὑφόρει_ποτέ_417"> <foreign xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</foreign>: <ref n="Soph. El. 268">cp. 268</ref> n. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔκ_τε_421"> may have been preferred by the poet to </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκ_δὲ_421"> on account of <quote xml:lang="grc">τανῦν δ’.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βρύοντα_421">, luxuriant (with foliage): cp. <ref n="Hom. Il. 17.56"> <title>Il.</title> 17. 56</ref>(<quote xml:lang="grc">ἔρνος</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">βρύει ἄνθεϊ</quote>: <ref n="Soph. OC 16"> <title>O. C.</title> 16</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ᾧ_γενέσθαι_421">: for the inf. in a relative clause of oratio obliqua, cp. <ref n="Hdt. 6. 117">Her. 6. 117</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄνδρα οἱ δοκέειν ὁπλίτην ἀντιστῆναι μέγαν, τοῦ τὸ γένειον τὴν ἀσπίδα πᾶσαν σκιάζειν</quote>. <ref n="Thuc. 2. 102">Thuc. 2. 102</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">λέγεται δὲ καὶ Ἀλκμαίωνι..., ὅτε δὴ ἀλᾶσθαι αὐτὸν..., τὸν Ἀπόλλω...χρῆσαι κ.τ.λ.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="του_παρόντος_424"> has better MS. authority than <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦ παρόντος</quote>, and is preferred by most editors; but the choice is nicely balanced. (1) </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῦ_424"> gives the simplest construction for </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παρόντος_424">, and the better rhythm. But it is also somewhat strange, as implying that the presence of one person, and no more, was to be expected on such an occasion. In 927, <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦ πλησίον παρόντος ἡνίκ’ ὤλλυτο</quote>, the reference is definitely to the paedagogus. (2) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="του_424"> is strongly recommended by <ref n="Eur. Med. 67">Eur. <title>Med.</title> 67</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἤκουσά του λέγοντος, οὐ δοκῶν κλύειν</quote>. The constr. then is <quote xml:lang="grc">τοιαῦτα ἐξηγουμένου ἔκλυόν του, παρόντος ἡνίκα ἡλίῳ δείκνυσι τὸ ὄναρ</quote>: and <quote xml:lang="grc">παρόντος</quote> is properly predicative,=‘inasmuch as he was present’ (and therefore able to tell). On the whole, I now prefer <quote xml:lang="grc">του</quote>. Hartung, to show the constr., points thus, <quote xml:lang="grc">τοιαῦτά του, παρόντος ἡνίχ’ κ.τ.λ.</quote>: needlessly, I think.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Ἡλίῳ_τοὖναρ_424">. Cp. <title>I. T.</title> 42 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἃ καινὰ δ’ ἥκει νὺξ φέρουσα φάσματα</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">λέξω πρὸς αἰθέῤ, εἴ τι δὴ τόδ’ ἔστ’ ἄκος</quote>. Schol.: <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῖς γὰρ παλαιοῖς ἔθος ἦν ἀποτροπιαζομένους</quote> (by way of expiation) <quote xml:lang="grc">τῷ ἡλίῳ διηγεῖσθαι τὰ ὀνείρατα</quote>. The popular attributes of<quote xml:lang="grc">Ἥλιος</quote> suggest more than one reason for such a custom. 1. He is the god of light and purity, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἁγνὸς θεός</quote> ( <ref n="Pind. O. 7">Pind. <title>O.</title> 7. 60</ref>), who dispels the terrors of darkness. 2. As the all-seeing god, <quote xml:lang="grc">πανόπτης</quote>, he is especially the detector of guilt ( <ref n="Hom. Od. 8.270">Hom. <title>Od.</title> 8. 270</ref>), able to reveal the lurking danger which an evil dream might foreshadow. 3. And, generally, he is a saving power (<quote xml:lang="grc">σωτήρ</quote>, <ref n="Paus. 8. 31. 7">Paus. 8. 31. 7</ref>): cp. <title>Suppl.</title> 213 <quote xml:lang="grc">καλοῦμεν αὐγὰς Ἡλίου σωτηρίους</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐ_πλὴν_426"> <quote xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</quote>: <ref n="Soph. El. 410">cp. 410</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πέμπει_κείνη_426">. Ellendt agrees with Brunck in writing <quote xml:lang="grc">μ’ ἐκείνη</quote>, on the ground that the demonstrative pron. gains emphasis by the elision. But there is really nothing to choose. The fact that the best MS. (L) has the rarer form may be allowed to turn the scale. (Cp. <ref n="Soph. Trach. 1091."> <title>Tr.</title> 1091.</ref>) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῶν_ἐγγενῶν_428">, ‘the gods of our race’: cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 199"> <title>Ant.</title> 199</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">γῆν πατρῴαν καὶ θεοὺς τοὺς ἐγγενεῖς</quote> (n.). The phrase of Electra, <quote xml:lang="grc">θεοὶ πατρῷοι</quote> (411),—recalling the memory of her father,—would be less fitting for Chrysothemis. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀβουλίᾳ_429">, causal dat.; <ref n="Soph. El. 398">cp. 398</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξ ἀβουλίας</quote>. (In <ref n="Soph. Trach. 597"> <title>Tr.</title> 597</ref>, <quote xml:lang="grc">αἰσχύνῃ πεσεῖ</quote>, the dat. is one of manner.)</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σὺν_κακῷ_429">,= <quote xml:lang="grc">κακὸν παθοῦσα</quote> (383 f.). <ref n="Soph. El. 61">Cp. 61</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">σὺν κέρδει.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μέτει_429">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">με</quote>, ‘thou wilt come in search of me,’ to help thee: schol. <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτὴ μετελεύσῃ με καὶ ἀξιώσεις μετὰ σοῦ γενέσθαι</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλ_431">, beginning the appeal: <title>O.T.</title> 14, <ref n="Soph. OC 238"> <title>O. C.</title> 238</ref> f.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὦ_φίλη_431">. The bitter feeling seen in vv. 391 and 403 has passed away before the new hope, and she speaks with affectionate earnestness. — </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τούτων_μὲν_431">, as opposed to the offerings recommended in 449. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐ_θέμις_432">, it is not sanctioned by usage, </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐδ_ὅσιον_432">, nor is it pious towards the gods: <emph>ius fasque vetant.</emph> Cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 74"> <title>Ant.</title> 74</ref> n.: Plat. <title>Phaedo</title>p. 108 A <quote xml:lang="grc">τῶν ὁσίων τε καὶ νομίμων</quote> (meaning rites in honour of the dead): <ref n="Aristoph. Thes. 676">Ar. <title>Th.</title> 676</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅσια καὶ νόμιμα</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐχθρᾶς_γυναικὸς_432">: for <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπό</quote>, ‘on the part of,’ <ref n="Soph. El. 1469">cp. 1469</ref>: <title>O.C.</title> 1289 <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ ταῦτ’ ἀφ’ ὑμῶν ... βουλήσομαι</quote> | ..<quote xml:lang="grc">κυρεῖν ἐμοί</quote>. The prep., emphasising the quarter from which the offerings come, is suitable here.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κτερίσματ_432">, used at 931 as including libations (894), but here distinguished from them. See on <ref n="Soph. OC 1410"> <title>O. C.</title> 1410</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ ἐν τάφοισι θέσθε κἀν κτερίσμασιν</quote>. The verb <quote xml:lang="grc">κτερίζειν</quote> occurs in <ref n="Soph. Ant. 204"> <title>Ant.</title> 204</ref>(n.).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λουτρὰ_432"> =<quote xml:lang="grc">χοάς</quote>: 84 n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλ_πνοαῖσιν_435"> <quote xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</quote>, ‘cast them to the winds—or bury them deep in the earth.’ The first thought is a passionate utterance of scorn and loathing. Cp. <ref n="Eur. Ba. 350">Eur. <title>Bacch.</title> 350</ref>, where Pentheus, in his rage against Teiresias, cries, <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ στέμματ’ ἀνέμοις καὶ θυέλλαισιν μέθες</quote>. <ref n="Eur. Tro. 418">Eur. <title>Tro.</title> 418</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἀργεἶ ὀνείδη καὶ Φρυγῶν ἐπαινέσεις</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνέμοις φέρεσθαι</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">παραδίδωμ’</quote>. <cit> <bibl n="Apollon. 1.1334">Ap. Rhod. 1. 1334</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ ἀνέμοισι | δώομεν ἀμπλακίην</quote> </cit>. Theocr. 22. 167 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἴσκον</quote> (<emph>dicebam</emph>) <quote xml:lang="grc">τοιάδε πολλά· τὰ δ’ εἰς ὑγρὸν ᾤχετο κῦμα</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">πνοιὴ ἔχοισ’ ἀνέμοιο</quote>. (So, too, the Latin poets: <ref n="Verg. A. 11.795">Verg. <title>Aen.</title> 11. 795</ref>: <ref n="Hor. Carm. 1.26.1">Hor. <title>C.</title> 1. 26. 1</ref>: <ref n="Ov. Tr. 1.2.15">Ov. <title>Trist.</title> 1. 2. 15</ref>: <ref n="Tib. 1.5.35">Tibull. 1. 5. 35</ref>, etc.) The passage is wretchedly enfeebled by the conjecture </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ῥοαῖσιν_435"> (as if she were to <emph>sink</emph> them in a river).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κρύψον_435"> adapts the general notion (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἀφάνισον</quote>) to <quote xml:lang="grc">κόνει</quote>: with <quote xml:lang="grc">πνοαῖσιν</quote> we supply <quote xml:lang="grc">παράδος</quote>, or the like. The zeugma is of a common type: <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Od. 9.166"> <title>Od.</title> 9. 166</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="hexameter"> <l>ἐς γαῖαν ἐλεύσσομεν …</l> <l>καπνόν τ’ αὐτῶν τε φθογγὴν ὀΐων τε καὶ αἰγῶν</l> </lg> </quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Od. 15.374">15. 374</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ μείλιχον ἔστιν ἀκοῦσαι | οὔτ’ ἔπος οὔτε τι ἔργον</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Od. 20.312">20. 312</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἴνοιό τε πινομένοιο | καὶ σίτου</quote> </cit>: <ref n="Pind. P. 4">Pind. <title>P.</title> 4. 104</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔτε ἔργον</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔτ’ ἔπος εὐτράπελον κείνοισιν εἰπών</quote>: <ref n="Aesch. PB 21">Aesch. <title>P. V.</title> 21</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἵν’ οὔτε φωνὴν οὔτε του μορφὴν βροτῶν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄψει</quote>.</gloss>

data.quote:
οὐ μείλιχον ἔστιν ἀκοῦσαι | οὔτ’ ἔπος οὔτε τι
                                    ἔργον

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νιν_435"> here and in 624=<quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτά</quote>: it stands for the masc. pl. in <ref n="Soph. OT 868"> <title>O. T.</title> 868</ref>, and for the fem. in <ref n="Soph. OC 43."> <title>O. C.</title> 43.</ref> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔνθα_ποτ_435">, with fut.: <ref n="Soph. El. 380">cp. 380</ref> n. The conjecture </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔνθεν_435"> is specious, but needless; the meaning is, ‘in a place <emph>where</emph> they will have no access’ to his tomb, <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, where they will be <emph>remote</emph> from it.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εὐνὴν_435"> is peculiarly fitting here, since the offerings are those of a false wife. Cp. <ref n="Aesch. Lib. 318">Aesch. <title>Ch.</title> 318</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔνθα σ’ ἔχουσιν εὐναί</quote>. <title>Anthol. Pal.</title> append. 260 <quote xml:lang="grc">κεῖμαι ἐς αὐχμηροὺς καὶ ἀλαμπέας Ἄϊδος εὐνάς</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλ_θάῃν_437">. If the offerings are buried deep in the ground, they will thereby be committed to the care of the <quote xml:lang="grc">νέρτεροι</quote>: cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 658"> <title>Ai.</title> 658</ref> ff. <quote xml:lang="grc">κρύψω τόδ’ ἔγχος τοὐμόν, ἔχθιστον βελῶν</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">γαίας ὀρύξας ἔνθα μή τις ὄψεται</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ αὐτὸ νὺξ Ἅιδης τε σῳζόντων κάτω</quote>. When Clytaemnestra passes to the nether world, let her find these treasures laid up for her there. They will be witnesses to her conscious guilt.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔστ_ἂν_437"> for </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅταν_437"> is unnecessary, since </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σῳζέσθω_437">=<quote xml:lang="grc">σῳζόμενα <*>.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κειμήλἰ_437">: cp. <ref n="Hom. Il. 23.618"> <title>Il.</title> 23. 618</ref>(of a cup), <quote xml:lang="grc">τῆ νῦν, καὶ σοὶ τοῦτο, γέρον, κειμήλιον ἔστω</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀρχὴν_439">, <emph>omnino</emph>, preceding the negative, as in <ref n="Soph. Ant. 92"> <title>Ant.</title> 92</ref>(n.), <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1239."> <title>Ph.</title> 1239.</ref>— </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τλημονεστάτη_439">: <ref n="Soph. El. 275">cp. 275</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔβλαστε_440">, with initial <quote xml:lang="grc">ε^</quote>, as in 1095, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1311"> <title>Ph.</title> 1311</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξ ἧς ἔβλαστες</quote> (n.): but above, in 238, this <quote xml:lang="grc">ε</quote> is long. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅν_τῷδ_441">: for <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅδε</quote> after a relat., cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1332"> <title>O. C.</title> 1332</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἷς ἂν σὺ προσθῇ, τοῖσδ’ ἔφασκ’ εἶναι κράτος</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 86"> <title>Ph.</title> 86</ref> f.: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 23"> <title>Tr.</title> 23</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Trach. 820."> Tr. 820.</ref> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπέστεφε_441">: 53 n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὐτῇ_441"> shows that the dat. must be influenced by <quote xml:lang="grc">προς‐</quote> <quote xml:lang="grc">φιλῶς</quote>, though it would be sufficiently explained by <quote xml:lang="grc">δέξασθαι</quote>: cp. <ref n="Eur. Hec. 535">Eur. <title>Hec.</title> 535</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">δέξαι χοάς μοι τάσδε</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δέξεσθαι_441">. Sophocles has joined <quote xml:lang="grc">δοκῶ</quote> (1) with the <emph>future</emph> infinitive in at least nine places:— <ref n="Soph. OT 355"> <title>O. T.</title> 355</ref>, <ref n="Soph. OT 368"> O. T. 368</ref>, <ref n="Soph. OT 399"> O. T. 399</ref>, <ref n="Soph. OT 401"> O. T. 401</ref>: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 14"> <title>Ph.</title> 14</ref>: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 1138"> <title>Tr.</title> 1138</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Trach. 1171"> Tr. 1171</ref>: <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1086"> <title>Ai.</title> 1086</ref>: <ref n="Soph. El. 471."> <title>El.</title> 471.</ref>(2) With the infin. (<emph>pres.</emph> or <emph>aor.</emph>) and <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄν</quote> in <ref n="Soph. OT 584"> <title>O. T.</title> 584</ref>: <ref n="Soph. OC 748"> <title>O. C.</title> 748</ref>: <ref n="Soph. Aj. 263"> <title>Ai.</title> 263</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1078"> Ai. 1078</ref>: <ref n="Soph. El. 312"> <title>El.</title> 312</ref>, <ref n="Soph. El. 614."> El. 614.</ref>(3) With the simple aor. inf. in <ref n="Soph. El. 805"> <title>El.</title> 805</ref>, and <ref n="Soph. Phil. 276"> <title>Ph.</title> 276</ref>: in both of which places the reference is to past time.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δέξεσθαι_441_2"> rather than </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δέξασθαι_441">, or else would have added <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄν</quote> to the aor. inf. In three of those passages which have the <emph>fut.</emph> inf., the <emph>aor.</emph> inf. would have suited the metre equally well ( <ref n="Soph. OT 368"> <title>O. T.</title> 368</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">λέξειν</quote>: <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 399 <quote xml:lang="grc">παραστατήσειν</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 1171"> <title>Tr.</title> 1171</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πράξειν</quote>). It is a different question whether <quote xml:lang="grc">δοκεῖ δέξασθαι</quote> could, or could not, refer to the future: see Appendix.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θανὼν_ἄτιμος_441">, ruthlessly slain: <ref n="Soph. El. 98">cp. 98</ref>: 1181: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1069"> <title>Ant.</title> 1069</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ψυχήν τ’ ἀτίμως ἐν τάφῳ κατῴκισας</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐμασχαλίσθη_441">. The verb occurs only here and in <ref n="Aesch. Lib. 439">Aesch. <title>Ch.</title> 439</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμασχαλίσθη δέ γ’, ὡς τόδ’ εἰδῇς</quote>. In his <title>Troilus</title> Sophocles used the phrase <quote xml:lang="grc">πλήρη μασχαλισμάτων</quote>, and probably also <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν μασχαλισμόν</quote> (fr. 566). The explanation given by the scholiasts and the lexicographers dates at least from Aristophanes of Byzantium (see Appendix). Murderers used to cut off the extremities of their victim, and suspend these at his arm-pits (<quote xml:lang="grc">μασχάλαι</quote>) and from his neck. Hence <quote xml:lang="grc">μασχαλίζω</quote> is paraphrased by <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀκρωτηριάζω</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κἀπὶ_λουτροῖσιν_441"> <foreign xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</foreign>: ‘and, for ablution, she wiped off the blood-stains (from her sword) on his head.’ <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπὶ</quote> here= ‘with a view to’ (cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 792"> <title>Ant.</title> 792</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπὶ λώβᾳ</quote>, <ref n="Soph. OT 1457"> <title>O. T.</title> 1457</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπὶ...κακῷ</quote>), and <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπὶ λουτροῖς</quote>= <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπὶ καθάρσει</quote>. The action was a symbolical way of saying, ‘on thy head, not mine, be the guilt,’—as though the victim had provoked his own fate (thus Clytaemnestra claimed to be the avenger of Iphigeneia). So the Greek commentators explain; see schol. on 446, <quote xml:lang="grc">ὥσπερ τὰς ἐπὶ τῷ μύσει κηλῖδας ἀποτρεπόμενοι</quote>: and Eustathius p. 1857. 7 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς εἰς κεφαλὴν δῆθεν ἐκείνοις</quote> (the victims) <quote xml:lang="grc">τρεπομένου τοῦ</quote> <quote xml:lang="grc">κακοῦ</quote>. Cp. <ref n="Hom. Od. 19.92"> <title>Od.</title> 19. 92</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔρδουσα μέγα ἔργον, ὃ σῇ κεφαλῇ <*>ναμάξεις</quote>, ‘of which thou shalt take the stain on thine own head,’ <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> ‘of which the guilt shall rest upon thy head’: imitated by <ref n="Hdt. 1. 155">Her. 1. 155</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ μὲν γὰρ πρότερον ἐγώ τε ἔπρηξα καὶ ἐγὼ ἐμῇ κεφαλῇ ἀναμάξας φέρω</quote>. Besides the proverbial <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰς κεφαλὴν σοί</quote> ( <ref n="Aristoph. Peace 1063">Ar. <title>Pax</title> 1063</ref> etc.), cp. <ref n="Dem. 18.24">Dem. or. 18 § 294</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἷς ἂν εἰκότως..τὴν τῶν γεγενημένων αἰτίαν ἐπὶ τὴν κεφαλὴν ἀναθεῖεν ἅπαντες</quote>.—This is better than to take <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπὶ λουτροῖς</quote> as=‘for washing (of the corpse),’—<foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, in lieu of the <quote xml:lang="grc">λουτρά</quote> which it was the duty of relatives to give the dead ( <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1405"> <title>Ai.</title> 1405</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">λουτρῶν ὁσίων</quote>: <ref n="Soph. OC 1602"> <title>O. C.</title> 1602</ref> n.: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 901"> <title>Ant.</title> 901</ref>).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐξέμαξ_ν_441">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡ Κλυταιμνήστρα</quote>: not <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ νέκυς</quote>, which would require <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξεμάξατ’</quote>. The change of subject is softened by the transition from a relative clause (<quote xml:lang="grc">ὑφ’ ἧς κ.τ.λ.</quote>) to an independent sentence (<ref n="Soph. El. 188">cp. 188</ref> ff., <ref n="Soph. OC 424"> <title>O. C.</title> 424</ref>, note in Appendix, p. 278); and Greek idiom was tolerant in this matter: see on <ref n="Soph. Trach. 362"> <title>Tr.</title> 362</ref> ff.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἆρα_μὴ_441"> ( <ref n="Soph. Ant. 632"> <title>Ant.</title> 632</ref>), like <quote xml:lang="grc">μῶν</quote> (‘can it be that..?’). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐκ_ἔστιν_448">, finally rejecting the supposition, like <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ ἔστι ταῦτα</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. Trach. 449"> <title>Tr.</title> 449</ref>: <ref n="Soph. Aj. 470"> <title>Ai.</title> 470</ref>).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ταῦτα_δὲ_448"> <foreign xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</foreign> Here <quote xml:lang="grc">σὺ δὲ</quote> marks an antithesis, not of persons, but of clauses, and serves merely to emphasise the second clause. This is a peculiarly Ionic usage. <ref n="Hom. Il. 9.300"> <title>Il.</title> 9. 300</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ δέ τοι</quote> (=<quote xml:lang="grc">σοι</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἀτρείδης μὲν ἀπήχθετο</quote>.. | .. <quote xml:lang="grc">σὺ δ’ ἄλλους περ Παναχαιοὺς</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">τειρομένους ἐλέαιρε</quote>: 6. 46 <quote xml:lang="grc">ζώγρει, Ἀτρέος υἱέ, σὺ δ’ ἄξια δέξαι ἄποινα</quote> [<quote xml:lang="grc">ζώγρει</quote>=<quote xml:lang="grc">μὴ κτεῖνε</quote>]: 4. 491 <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦ μὲν ἅμαρθ)· ὁ δὲ Λεῦκον, Ὀδυσσέος ἐσθλὸν ἑταῖρον</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">βεβλήκει</quote>. <ref n="Hdt. 3. 68">Her. 3. 68</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ μὴ αὐτὴ Σμέρδιν..γινώσκεις, σὺ δὲ παρὰ Ἀτόσσης πύυευ</quote>: 7. 159 <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ δ’ ἄρα μὴ δικαιοῖς ἄρχεσθαι, σὺ δὲ μηδὲ βοήθεε</quote>. The Attic poets took the idiom from the Ionians: <ref n="Aesch. Ag. 1060">Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 1060</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ δ’ ἀξυνήμων οὖσα μὴ δέχει λόγον</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">σὺ δ’ ἀντὶ φωνῆς φράζε καρβάνῳ χερί</quote>. It is rarer in Attic prose: <ref n="Xen. Anab. 4. 2.">Xen. <title>An.</title> 4. 2.</ref>§§ 5, <ref n="Xen. Anab. 6">Xen. An. 6</ref>: <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτοὶ ἐνταῦθ’ ἔμενον, ὡς τὸ ἄκρον κατέχοντες· οἱ δ’ οὐ κατεῖχον</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βοστρύχων_φόβας_449">, since the offering at the grave was to be merely a lock of hair (<quote xml:lang="grc">πλόκαμος πενθητήριος</quote>, <ref n="Aesch. Lib. 8">Aesch. <title>Ch.</title> 8</ref>), in token of grief. The hair is not now to be cut short, as for a recent death ( <title>Helen.</title> 1053 <quote xml:lang="grc">γυναικείοις ἂν οἰκτισαίμεθα</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">κουραῖσι καὶ θρήνοισι</quote>). The censure of Helen in <ref n="Eur. Orest. 128">Eur. <title>Or.</title> 128</ref>, <quote xml:lang="grc">εἴδετε παρ’ ἄκρας ὡς ἀπέθρισεν τρίχας</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">σῴζουσα κάλλος</quote>, refers to the time of her mother's death.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κἀμοῦ_ταλαίνης_449"> is a possessive genitive, going with <quote xml:lang="grc">τήνδ’ ἀλιπ. τρίχα</quote>. It is so placed in the sentence as to heighten the pathos:—‘give him a lock cut from (thine own head),—and (give) on <emph>my</emph> part..this hair,’ etc. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλιπαρῆ_451">. The schol.'s paraphrase <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐχμηράν</quote>, gives the sense which we require. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Eur. El. 183">Eur. <title>El.</title> 183</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">σκέψαι μου πιναρὰν κόμαν | καὶ πέπλων τρύχη τάδ’ ἐμῶν</quote> </cit>. But <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλιπαρής</quote>, the negative of <quote xml:lang="grc">λιπαρής</quote>, could mean only, ‘not earnest,’ ‘not persevering.’ In v. 1378 Electra says (to Apollo), <quote xml:lang="grc">ἥ σε πολλὰ δὴ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀφ’ ὧν ἔχοιμι λιπαρεῖ προὔστην χερί</quote>, i.e., ‘with a zealous, a devout, hand.’ And we learn from the scholiast here that a variant, probably as old as Didymus (<foreign xml:lang="lat">circ.</foreign> 30 C.B. ), for <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλιπαρῆ</quote> was </gloss>

data.quote:
σκέψαι μου πιναρὰν κόμαν | καὶ πέπλων τρύχη
                                    τάδ’ ἐμῶν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λιπαρῆ_451">: <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅ ἐστιν, ἐξ ἧς αὐτὸν λιπαρήσομεν, ὡς εἰ ἔλεγεν ἱκέτιν τρίχα</quote>, ‘a <emph>suppliant</emph> lock.’ It is surely manifest, however, that <quote xml:lang="grc">λιπαρὴς θρίξ</quote> could not mean, ‘a lock of hair <emph>offered by a suppliant.</emph>’ We may safely, then, reject Hermann's corresponding interpretation of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλιπαρῆ</quote>, <emph>comam non accommodatam supplicationi.</emph> Nor do I now think that <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλιπαρὴς θρίξ</quote> could mean ‘not <emph>cared for</emph>,’ ‘neglected.’</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τήνδε_τ_451"> (which is preferable to <quote xml:lang="grc">τήνδε γ’</quote> here), followed by </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλίπαρον_451"> (cr. n.), ‘not sleek or glossy,’ as with unguents. I incline to this view. The genuine word was in any case probably <emph>negative</emph>, parallel with <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ χλιδαῖς ἠσκημένον</quote> in 452.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ζῶμα_452"> here = <quote xml:lang="grc">ζώνην</quote>, a sense which recurs only in later Greek; as in <title>Anth. Pal.</title> 6. 272 a woman dedicates her <quote xml:lang="grc">ζῶμα</quote> to Artemis. (As to the <quote xml:lang="grc">ζῶμα</quote> of the Homeric warrior, see <title>Introd. to Homer</title>, p. 65, n. 3.)</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χλιδαῖς_452"> (<ref n="Soph. El. 52">cp. 52</ref>, 360), such as embroidery, or metal work. <quote xml:lang="grc">ζῶναι</quote> were often elaborate and costly. A golden girdle, found in Ithaca, has as clasp a knot of metal ornamented with garnets, etc. (<title>Dict. of Ant.</title>, 2nd ed., vol. 1. p. 427).—The <quote xml:lang="grc">ζῶμα</quote> is to be laid on the grave, as ribands etc. sometimes were: cp. schol. <ref n="Aristoph. Lys. 603">Ar. <title>Lys.</title> 603</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰς ταινίας ἃς τοῖς νεκροῖς ἔπεμπον οἱ φίλοι</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προσπίτνουσα_453">, at the tomb.— </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὐτὸν_453">, ‘himself’ (rather than merely ‘him’), as the next verse shows. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐξ_χερός_455">: <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκ</quote> ex presses the condition: cp. <ref n="Soph. Trach. 875"> <title>Tr.</title> 875</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξ ἀκινήτου ποδός</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 91"> <title>Ph.</title> 91</ref> n.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ζῶντ_455">, instead of dying first, as his foes hope. The thought is, ‘may he live to do it.’ </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀφνεωτέραις_457">. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀφνεός</quote> is used by Pindar, Theognis, and Aesch. (though only in lyrics, <ref n="Aesch. Pers. 3">Aesch. <title>Pers.</title> 3</ref> and fr. 96): <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀφνειός</quote> is the only Homeric form.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="στέφωμεν_457">: <ref n="Soph. El. 53">cp. 53</ref>, 441.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δωρούμεθα_457">: cp. <ref n="Pind. O. 6">Pind. <title>O.</title> 6. 78</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐδώρησαν θεῶν κάρυκα λιταῖς θυσίαις</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἶμαι_οὖν_459">: ‘Now (<quote xml:lang="grc">οὖν</quote>) I <emph>think</emph> (<quote xml:lang="grc">οἶμαι μὲν</quote>) that our father is already aiding us of his own accord; but still (<quote xml:lang="grc">ὅμως δέ</quote>, 461) pray for his help.’ Here the particles <quote xml:lang="grc">μὲν οὖν</quote> have each their separate force, as in <ref n="Soph. OT 483"> <title>O. T.</title> 483</ref>, <ref n="Soph. OC 664"> <title>O. C.</title> 664</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 65"> <title>Ant.</title> 65</ref>: not their compound force, ‘nay rather’ (1503).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μέλον_459"> be kept, we must supply <quote xml:lang="grc">ἦν</quote>, and take <quote xml:lang="grc">οἶμαι</quote> as parenthetic. This is possible, but harsh. <quote xml:lang="grc">οἶμαι</quote> cannot govern <quote xml:lang="grc">μέλον</quote>, as though it were <quote xml:lang="grc">οἶδα</quote>. (Nor can <quote xml:lang="grc">μέλον τι</quote> be the subject to <quote xml:lang="grc">πέμψαι</quote>, as Paley takes it: ‘I think that some concern affecting him also sent these dreams.’) Probably </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μέλειν_459"> was corrupted to </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μέλον_459_2"> through a misunderstanding of the construction. The sense is, <quote xml:lang="grc">νομίζω ὅτι καὶ ἐκείνῳ ἔμελέ τι</quote> (adv., ‘in some degree’) <quote xml:lang="grc">πέμψαι κ.τ.λ.</quote> She means that, though the <emph>gods</emph> below are the primary authors of the vision, the spirit of the dead was also in some measure active. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σοί_461">=<quote xml:lang="grc">σεαυτῇ</quote>: <ref n="Soph. El. 363">cp. 363</ref> (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμέ</quote>= <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμαυτήν</quote>), n. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βροτῶν_462">: <ref n="Soph. El. 407">cp. 407</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρὸς_εὐσέβειαν_464">=<quote xml:lang="grc">εὐσεβῶς</quote>: <ref n="Soph. El. 369">cp. 369</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πρὸς ὀργὴν</quote> (n.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δράσω_466">. Chrysothemis here accepts the lock of hair and the girdle which Electra offers to her. We must suppose that, after leaving the scene, she puts Clytaemnestra's gifts somewhere out of sight.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸ_δίκαιον_466"> <foreign xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</foreign> The sense is, ‘When a duty has once become clear, there is no more room for disputing,— one should act forthwith.’ Two constructions are possible; I prefer the first.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δυοῖν_466"> is taken by Hermann and others to mean Electra and the Chorus. ‘The right view (of the matter) affords no ground for me to contend against you both.’ But the controversy of Chrysothemis has been with Electra only. She is yielding to a new perception of her duty, not to a majority of voices. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παρ_ὑμῶν_469">, on your part: cp. <ref n="Soph. Trach. 596"> <title>Tr.</title> 596</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μόνον παρ’ ὑμῶν εὖ στεγοίμεθ’</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πικρὰν_470">, to my cost: <ref n="Eur. Ba. 356">Eur. <title>Bacch.</title> 356</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς ἂν λευσίμου δίκης τυχὼν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">θάνῃ, πικρὰν βάκχευσιν ἐν Θήβαις ἰδών</quote>. <title>I. A.</title> 1315 <quote xml:lang="grc">πικράν</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">πικρὰν ἰδοῦσα δυσελέναν</quote>.— For </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δοκῶ_τολμήσειν_470">, cp. <ref n="Soph. Trach. 706"> <title>Tr.</title> 706</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁρῶ δέ μ’ ἔργον δεινὸν ἐξειργασμένην</quote> (n.).—For </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔτι_470">, <ref n="Soph. El. 66">cp. 66</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰ_γὼ_472"> <quote xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</quote> The sanguine prediction of the Chorus in <ref n="Soph. OT 1086"> <title>O. T.</title> 1086</ref> ff. has a similar preface,—<quote xml:lang="grc">εἴπερ ἐγὼ μάντις εἰμὶ καὶ κατὰ γνώμαν ἴδρις.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γνώμας_λειπομένα_472">, <emph>failing in</emph> it: cp. <ref n="Soph. Trach. 936"> <title>Tr.</title> 936</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔτ’ ὀδυρμάτων</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐλείπετ’ οὐδέν</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἁ_Δίκα_475">, because she has sent the dream, a presage of her own advent. In <ref n="Aesch. Lib. 33">Aesch. <title>Cho.</title> 33</ref>Clytaemnestra's vision is itself called <quote xml:lang="grc">δόμων ὀνειρόμαντις</quote>. The fact that <quote xml:lang="grc">μάντις</quote> has just preceded gives no ground for suspecting <quote xml:lang="grc">πρόμαντις</quote>: <ref n="Soph. El. 163">cp. 163</ref> (<quote xml:lang="grc">γᾶν</quote> after <quote xml:lang="grc">γᾶ</quote>) n. For <quote xml:lang="grc">Δίκη</quote> as an avenging power, <ref n="Soph. El. 528">cp. 528</ref>: <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1390"> <title>Ai.</title> 1390</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μνήμων τ’ Ἐρινὺς καὶ τελεσφόρος Δίκη</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δίκαια_κράτη_475">. The goddess Justice is here conceived as being in her person the victorious avenger. The words mean literally, ‘bearing away just triumphs of prowess.’ Cp. <ref n="Hom. Il. 13.486"> <title>Il.</title> 13. 486</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">αἶψά κεν ἠὲ φέροιτο μέγα κράτος ἠὲ φεροίμην</quote>: for the plur. <quote xml:lang="grc">κράτη</quote>, <title>Suppl.</title> 951 <quote xml:lang="grc">εἴη δὲ νίκη καὶ κράτη τοῖς ἄρσεσι</quote>: and for <quote xml:lang="grc">χεροῖν κράτη</quote>, above, V. 37 <quote xml:lang="grc">χειρὸς ἐνδίκους σφαγάς</quote>, 206 <quote xml:lang="grc">θανάτους αἰκεῖς διδύμαιν χειροῖν</quote>.—Others render: ‘carrying just victory <emph>in her hands</emph>,’ like Eris in <ref n="Hom. Il. 11.4"> <title>Il.</title> 11. 4</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πολέμοιο τέρας μετὰ χερσὶν ἔχουσαν</quote>. The use of <quote xml:lang="grc">φέρεσθαι</quote> as=<quote xml:lang="grc">φέρειν</quote> is rare, but not unexampled: Campbell quotes <ref n="Hdt. 7. 50">Her. 7. 50</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πολλὴν φορβὴν φερόμενοι πορευόμεθα</quote>: and we might add <ref n="Hom. Il. 6.177."> <title>Il.</title> 6. 177.</ref>But the picture thus given seems less clear and strong. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μέτεισιν_477">, will come in pursuit (of the guilty): <ref n="Aesch. Lib. 273">Aesch. <title>Cho.</title> 273</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ μὴ μέτειμι τοῦ πατρὸς τοὺς αἰτίους.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐ_χρόνου_477">: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 397"> <title>O. C.</title> 397</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἥξοντα βαιοῦ κοὐχὶ μυρίου χρόνου</quote> (n.). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὕπεστί_θράσος_479"> is followed by </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κλύουσαν_479"> as if <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑφέρπει</quote> or <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑπῆλθέ με</quote> had preceded. Cp. <ref n="Aesch. Pers. 913">Aesch. <title>Pers.</title> 913</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">λέλυται γὰρ ἐμοὶ γυίων ῥώμη</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">τήνδ’ ἡλικίαν ἐσιδόντ’ ἀστῶν</quote>: <ref n="Aesch. Lib. 410">Aesch. <title>Cho.</title> 410</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πέπαλται δ’ αῦτέ μοι φίλον κέαρ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">τόνδε κλύουσαν ο<*>κτον</quote>. In other examples the presence of an infin. modifies the boldness: <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1006">Soph. <title>Ai.</title> 1006</ref> f. <quote xml:lang="grc">ποῖ γὰρ μολεῖν μοι δυνατόν, εἰς ποίους βροτούς</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῖς σοῖς ἀρήξαντ’ ἐν πόνοισι μηδαμοῦ</quote>; <ref n="Eur. Med. 814">Eur. <title>Med.</title> 814</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">σοὶ δὲ συγγνώμη λέγειν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">τάδ’ ἐστί, μὴ πάσχουσαν, ὡς ἐγώ, κακῶς</quote>. (Cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 838">Soph. <title>Ant.</title> 838</ref> n.)</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἁδυπνόων_ὀνειράτων_479">: cp. the invocation of Sleep, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 828"> <title>Ph.</title> 828</ref>, <quote xml:lang="grc">εὐαὲς ἡμῖν ἔλθοις</quote>. In <ref n="Aesch. Lib. 32">Aesch. <title>Cho.</title> 32</ref> ff. the dream is described as ‘breathing resentment’ against the murderers: <quote xml:lang="grc">τορὸς γὰρ ὀρθόθριξ φόβος</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">δόμων ὀνειρόμαντις, ἐξ ὕπνου κότον</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">πνέων</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀμναστεῖ_ς_482">. The pronoun <quote xml:lang="grc">σε</quote> is indispensable here, and could have easily fallen out after <quote xml:lang="grc">φύσας</quote>. To add it there is better than to read <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀμναστεῖ σ’ ὁ φύσας</quote>: for the <quote xml:lang="grc">γε</quote> after the verb, marking assurance, is expressive. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χαλκόπλακτος_484">, act., ‘striking with bronze’; cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 688"> <title>Ph.</title> 688</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀμφιπλάκτων ῥοθίων</quote>, ‘billows that beat around him’ (n.).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γένυς_484">: <ref n="Soph. El. 159">cp. 159</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">γενύων</quote> (n.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐν_αἰκίαις_487">: <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν</quote> denotes the manner; cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1682"> <title>O. C.</title> 1682</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν ἀφανεῖ τινι μόρῳ φερόμενον</quote>. The penultimate of <quote xml:lang="grc">αἰκία</quote> is always long (511, <ref n="Soph. OC 515"> O. C. 515</ref>: <title>P.V.</title> 93, 178, 600: <ref n="Eur. Ba. 1374">Eur. <title>Bacch.</title> 1374</ref>). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_πολύχειρ_488">: as with the might of a resistless host. <ref n="Soph. El. 36">Cp. 36</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λόχοις_490">: cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1074"> <title>Ant.</title> 1074</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τούτων σε λωβητῆρες ὑστεροφθόροι</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">λοχῶσιν Ἅιδου καὶ θεῶν Ἐρινύες.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χαλκόπους_490">: so <title>O.T.</title> 418 <quote xml:lang="grc">δεινόπους ἀρά</quote> (n.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄλεκτρ_ἄνυμφα_492">: <ref n="Soph. El. 1154">cp. 1154</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀμήτωρ</quote>: <title>O.T.</title> 1214 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄγαμον γάμον</quote>. The epithets, which properly describe the <quote xml:lang="grc">γάμοι</quote>, are given to the whole phrase: <ref n="Soph. El. 1290">cp. 1290</ref>: 1390: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 794"> <title>Ant.</title> 794</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">νεῖκος ἀνδρῶν ξύναιμον</quote> (n.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γάμων_ἁμιλλήμαθ_492">, eager haste for marriage; the plur. is fitting, since both the partners in guilt were striving for that goal. (In <ref n="Eur. Hipp. 1140">Eur. <title>Hipp.</title> 1140</ref> f. <quote xml:lang="grc">νυμφιδίων δ’ ἀπόλωλε φυγᾷ σᾷ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">λέκτρων ἅμιλλα κούραις</quote>, the sense is, ‘competition’ for marriage.)</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπέβα_492">, like <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπέπεσε</quote>, suggesting the violence of the passion which seized them: so <ref n="Soph. Phil. 194"> <title>Ph.</title> 194</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπέβη</quote> is said of the sufferings sent upon the hero by Chrysè. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρὸ_συνδρῶσιν_495">: ‘therefore I am confident that we shall never see (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἡμῖν</quote>, ethic dat.) the portent draw near to the murderess and her accomplice without giving them cause to complain of it’;—<foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, ‘we shall assuredly find that the dream has been an omen of their ruin.’</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρὸ_τῶνδε_495"> sound? I think so. The sense is, ‘<emph>for</emph> (=on account of) these things,’ <quote xml:lang="grc">πρό</quote> being used as <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑπέρ</quote> is in <ref n="Soph. OT 165"> <title>O. T.</title> 165</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄτας ὕπερ</quote>, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 932"> <title>Ant.</title> 932</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">βραδυτῆτος ὕπερ</quote>. This is a rare, but not unexampled, sense of <quote xml:lang="grc">πρό</quote>, in which the notions ‘before’ and ‘by reason of it’ were associated, just as in Lat. <emph>prae</emph> and our own ‘for.’ See <ref n="Hom. Il. 17.666"> <title>Il.</title> 17. 666</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μή μιν Ἀχαιοὶ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀργαλέου πρὸ φόβοιο ἕλωρ δηίοισι λίποιεν</quote>,=<emph>prae timore</emph>, ‘<emph>for</emph> fear’ (not, as Düntzer explains, ‘forward on the path of flight’: cp. Leaf <emph>ad loc.</emph>). <ref n="Soph. Trach. 505"> <title>Tr.</title> 505</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κατέβαν πρὸ γάμων</quote>, ‘entered the contest <emph>for</emph> the marriage’ (not ‘before’ it, which would there be pointless).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μή_ἡμῖν_495">, the first <quote xml:lang="grc">μήποτε</quote> (absent from L) was probably an attempt to fill the gap: unless, indeed, <quote xml:lang="grc">μ’ ἔχει</quote> should be <quote xml:lang="grc">θράσος</quote> (as Wunder thought).—For other conjectures, see Appendix. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀψεγές_497">: schol. <quote xml:lang="grc">θαρρῶ ὅτι τοῖς δρῶσι ταῦτα τὰ ἄδικα καὶ συνδρῶσιν αὐτοῖς οὐκ ἔσται ἄψεκτος ὁ ὄνειρος· παθόντες γὰρ ψέξουσι τὸ ὀφθέν</quote>.—Bergk, whom Hartung follows, reads </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀψεφές_497">, ‘unheeded,’ a word known only from Hesychius: <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀψεφές, ἀφρόντιστον· Σοφοκλῆς Φαίδρᾳ</quote>. (Hesych. has also <quote xml:lang="grc">ψέφει· δέδοικεν...φροντίζει</quote>.)</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πελᾶν_497">, ‘Attic’ fut. inf. of <quote xml:lang="grc">πελάζω</quote>: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1060"> <title>O. C.</title> 1060</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πελῶσ’</quote>: <ref n="Aesch. PB 282">Aesch. <title>P. V.</title> 282</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πελῶ</quote>. (In <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1150">Soph. <title>Ph.</title> 1150</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πελᾶτ’</quote> should be <quote xml:lang="grc">πηδᾶτ’</quote>.)—Others take <quote xml:lang="grc">πελᾶν</quote> as pres. inf. of <quote xml:lang="grc">πελάω</quote> (as in <title>Hom. hymn.</title> 7. 44 <quote xml:lang="grc">πελάαν</quote>: cp. n. on <title>O.C.</title>, <emph>l.c.</emph>). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῖς_δρῶσι_498">, Clytaemnestra, to whom the dream came: </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῖς_συνδρῶσιν_498">, Aegisthus (<ref n="Soph. El. 97">cp. 97</ref> ff.): for the plur. <ref n="Soph. El. 146">cp. 146</ref> (<quote xml:lang="grc">γονέων</quote>), n. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μαντεῖαι_βροτῶν_499">, means of divination for men. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εὖ_κατασχήσει_503">, come safely into harbour,—as a seafarer was said <quote xml:lang="grc">κατέχειν εἰς γῆν</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. Phil. 221"> <title>Ph.</title> 221</ref> n.). Cp. <ref n="Soph. Trach. 826"> <title>Tr.</title> 826</ref>(of oracles) <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ τάδ’ ὀρθῶς</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔμπεδα κατουρίζει</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἱππεία_505"> here=driving of horses, like <quote xml:lang="grc">ἱπποσύνη</quote> ( <ref n="Hom. Il. 4.303"> <title>Il.</title> 4. 303</ref> etc.); a word used by <ref n="Eur. Her. 374">Eur. <title>H. F.</title> 374</ref> with ref. to the raids of Centaurs (<quote xml:lang="grc">χθόνα Θεσσαλῶν ἱππείαις ἐδάμαζον</quote>).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αἰανὴς_506"> suits the idea of <emph>persistent</emph> calamity. Whatever its real etymology, it was associated with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀεί</quote> ( <ref n="Aesch. Eum. 572">Aesch. <title>Eum.</title> 572</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐς τὸν αἰανῆ χρόνον</quote>, <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 672 <quote xml:lang="grc">αἰανῶς μένοι</quote>), and was used to denote that which pains by wearying, or wearing (as <ref n="Pind. P. 1">Pind. <title>P.</title> 1. 82</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κόρος...αἰανής</quote>: <ref n="Pind. I. 1">Pind. <title>I.</title> 1. 49</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">λιμὸν αἰανῆ</quote>).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εὖτε_508">, ‘since,’=<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξ οὖ</quote>, like <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅτε</quote> ( <ref n="Thuc. 1. 13">Thuc. 1. 13</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔτη δ’ ἐστὶ μάλιστα τριακόσια...ὅτε Ἀμεινοκλῆς Σαμίοις ἦλθε</quote>).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὁ_Μυρτίλος_508">. The legendary scene was at Geraestus (now Cape Mandelo), the s. promontory of Euboea: <ref n="Eur. Orest. 990">Eur. <title>Or.</title> 990</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">Πέλοψ ὁπότε πελάγεσι διεδίφρευδε, Μυρτίλου φόνον</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">δικὼν ἐς οἶδμα πόντου</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">λευκοκύμοσιν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">πρὸς Γεραιστίαις</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ποντίων σάλων</quote>|<quote xml:lang="grc">ᾐόσιν ἁρματεύσας</quote>. Tzetzes on Lycophron 156 <quote xml:lang="grc">ῥίπτεται παρὰ Πέλοπος περὶ Γεραιστὸν ἀκρωτήριον. ὁ δὲ τελευτῶν ἀρὰς ἀρᾶται τοῖς Πελοπίδαις δεινάς κ.τ.λ.</quote> To Myrtilus was sometimes traced the name of the <quote xml:lang="grc">Μυρτῷον πέλαγος</quote> which lay S. of Euboea, E. of the Peoponnesus, and W. of the Cyclades. Pausanias (8. 14. 12) rejects this etymology, supposing Myrtilus to have perished on the coast of Elis. The Myrtoan sea was probably so called from the islet Myrto near Geraestus.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκοιμάθη_508">: cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 831"> <title>Ai.</title> 831</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">καλῶ θ’ ἅμα</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">πομπαῖον Ἑρμῆν χθόνιον εὖ με κοιμίσαι</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παγχρυσέων_δίφρων_510">, the golden chariot given to Pelops by Poseidon: see <ref n="Pind. O. 1">Pind. <title>O.</title> 1. 87</ref>, in n. on 505. This plur. (750), as denoting <emph>one</emph> chariot (like <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄχεα, ἅρματα</quote>), is not Homeric. <quote xml:lang="grc">παγχρυσέων</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">¯¯˘¯</quote>), the epic form, suits the metre here. Cp. <ref n="Soph. Trach. 1099">Soph. <title>Tr.</title> 1099</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">χρυσέων</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">¯˘¯</quote>). Dindorf, keeping the MS. <quote xml:lang="grc">παγχρύσων</quote>, inserts <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκ</quote> after it. The strophic test is absent in an epode. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρόρριζος_512"> here= <quote xml:lang="grc">πανώλεθρος</quote>, as in <ref n="Aristoph. Frogs 587">Ar. <title>Ran.</title> 587</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πρόρριζος αὐτός, ἡ γυνή, τὰ παιδία</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">κάκιστ’ ἀπολοίμην</quote>. It would strain the word to understand, ‘torn from his place’ in the chariot.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκριφθείς_512">. In <ref n="Hdt. 6. 86">Her. 6. 86</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκτέτριπται...πρόρριζος</quote> is said of a man whose family is exterminated (cp. <ref n="Eur. Hipp. 684">Eur. <title>Hipp.</title> 684</ref>); but <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκτριφθείς</quote> is manifestly unsuitable here. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔλειπεν_514"> (cr. n.) is confirmed by metre, since every other v. of this epode has anacrusis, which </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔλιπεν_514"> would exclude. For the intrans. <quote xml:lang="grc">λείπω</quote>, cp. <ref n="Eur. Her. 133">Eur. <title>H. F.</title> 133</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ δὲ κακοτυχὲς οὐ λέλοιπεν ἐκ τέκνων</quote>: <title>Helen.</title> 1156 <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔποτ’ ἔρις</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">λείψει κατ’ ἀνθρώπων πόλεις</quote>. And <quote xml:lang="grc">ἕλειπεν</quote> in turn confirms </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἴκου_514"> as against </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἴκους_514">. For </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πω_514"> suits the imperf. in this sense (‘was never yet absent from the house’), but would require the aor. if the sense were, ‘has never yet left the house.’ For the sing. <quote xml:lang="grc">οἴκου</quote> (=family), <ref n="Soph. El. 978">cp. 978</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πολύπονος_515">. <quote xml:lang="grc">πολυπάμονας</quote> is very possibly the word which was read by the schol. on 508 (see cr. n.). But <quote xml:lang="grc">πολύπονος</quote>, the reading of our MSS., suits the context far better. Even if the <emph>v. l.</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἴκους</quote> were right, such an epithet as ‘wealthy’ is jarring here. In an epode, conjecture had freer scope than in a strophe; and <quote xml:lang="grc">πολυπάμονας</quote> may have been prompted by the desire to define <quote xml:lang="grc">οἴκους</quote>.— Remark </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αἰκία_515"> so soon after <quote xml:lang="grc">αἰκίαις</quote> (511): <ref n="Soph. El. 163">cp. 163</ref> (<quote xml:lang="grc">γᾶν</quote>), n. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀνειμένη_516"> is emphasised by </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μέν_516">, which has no corresponding <quote xml:lang="grc">δέ</quote>: the implied antithesis is, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπέχειν δέ σε ἐγὼ μόνη οὐ δύναμαι</quote> (519 f.). Cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 578"> <title>Ant.</title> 578</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκ δὲ τοῦδε χρὴ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">γυναῖκας εἶναι τάσδε μηδ’ ἀνειμένας</quote>, where see n.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡς_ἔοικας_516">: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 1241"> <title>Tr.</title> 1241</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="στρέφει_516">, <emph>versaris</emph>, go about (instead of remaining in the seclusion of the <quote xml:lang="grc">γυναικωνῖτις</quote>): cp. <title>H.</title> 6. 4. 16 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν τῷ φανερῷ ἀναστρεφομένους</quote>. <ref n="Soph. Trach. 907"> <title>Tr.</title> 907</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄλλῃ δὲ κἄλλῃ δωμάτων στρωφωμένη</quote>. The simple <quote xml:lang="grc">στρέφομαι</quote> is very rarely so used; but cp. Solon fr. 4. 23 <quote xml:lang="grc">ταῦτα μὲν ἐν δήμῳ στρέφεται κακά</quote>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὖ_516"> (328). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μή_γ_518">: <quote xml:lang="grc">τοι</quote> (Lat. <emph>sane</emph>) here limits with an ironical force, while <quote xml:lang="grc">γε</quote> lays stress on the adj. The combination <quote xml:lang="grc">μή τοί...γε</quote> occurs also in <ref n="Soph. OC 1407"> <title>O. C.</title> 1407</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Aj. 472"> <title>Ai.</title> 472</ref>: <quote xml:lang="grc">μή τοι</quote>, without <quote xml:lang="grc">γε</quote>, in <ref n="Soph. OC 1439"> <title>O. C.</title> 1439</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 544."> <title>Ant.</title> 544.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αἰσχύνειν_φίλους_518">: said from an Athenian point of view (on <ref n="Soph. Ant. 578"> <title>Ant.</title> 578</ref>). The Homeric maiden of noble birth, such as Nausicaa, has more freedom. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καίτοι_520">: you disregard my authority, <emph>and yet</emph> complain of it as oppressive.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δὴ_520"> goes with </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πολλούς_520">: <ref n="Soph. El. 202">cp. 202</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐχθίστα δή</quote> (n.).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐξεῖπάς_ὡς_520">: <ref n="Soph. El. 332">cp. 332</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θρασεῖα_520">, ‘insolent’: cp. <title>Legg.</title> 630 B <quote xml:lang="grc">θρασεῖς καὶ ἄδικοι καὶ ὑβρισταί</quote>. <title>I. T.</title> 275 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνομίᾳ θρασύς</quote>. <ref n="Aesch. PB 42">Aesch. <title>P. V.</title> 42</ref>(of <quote xml:lang="grc">Κράτος</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀεί γε δὴ νηλὴς σὺ καὶ θράσους πλέως</quote>. The conj. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τραχεῖα_520"> is plausible, but needless.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄρχω_520"> ‘rule’: it should not be taken with the partic., as= ‘provoke with insult’ (552). <ref n="Soph. El. 264">Cp. 264</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κἀκ τῶνδ’ ἄρχομαι</quote>: 597 <quote xml:lang="grc">δεσπότιν</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὕβριν_ἔχω_523">: cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 300"> <title>Ant.</title> 300</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πανουργίας...ἔχειν</quote> (n.). In the next clause, </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κακῶς_κλύουσα_523">, etc., are the emphatic words: ‘Insolent I am not; my words to you are only such as you address to me.’ </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πατὴρ_γάρ_525"> <quote xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</quote> A comma should follow <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄλλο</quote>, since <quote xml:lang="grc">τέθνηκεν</quote> depends on <quote xml:lang="grc">πρόσχημά σοί</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐστιν</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς</quote>: cp. <ref n="Hdt. 7. 157">Her. 7. 157</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πρόσχημα μὲν ποιεύμενος ὡς ἐπ’ Ἀθήνας ἐλαύνει</quote>.—If there is no comma after <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄλλο</quote>, the constr. becomes most awkward. Thus Paley makes <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς</quote> depend on <quote xml:lang="grc">κλύω</quote> (‘I am told’) supplied from v. 524. Campbell renders: ‘your father, that is what you are always holding forth, (how that he) died by my act’; adding: ‘By a kind of attraction, the main sentence, as it proceeds, becomes subordinated to the parenthesis.’</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρόσχημα_525"> here=<quote xml:lang="grc">σκῆψις, πρόφασις</quote>. <ref n="Soph. El. 682">Cp. 682</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἡ_Δίκη_528">. The <quote xml:lang="grc">γάρ</quote>, which has been impugned (cr. n.), is right. ‘He certainly died, and by my hand; <emph>for</emph> Justice, whose instrument I was, required his death.’ As to <quote xml:lang="grc">Δίκη</quote> the avenger, <ref n="Soph. El. 175">cp. 175</ref> f. (n.). <ref n="Aesch. Ag. 1432">Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 1432</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μὰ τὴν τέλειον τῆς ἐμῆς παιδὸς Δίκην</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἄτην τ’, <*>ρινύν θ’, αἷσι τόνδ’ ἔσφαξ’ ἐγώ</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ᾗ_ἀρήγειν_529"> <quote xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</quote> The inf. represents the apodosis; <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, ‘It was your duty <emph>to help</emph>,’ implies, ‘you would have helped,’ <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀρωγὸς ἂν ἦσθα</quote>. Cp. <ref n="Eur. Med. 586">Eur. <title>Med.</title> 586</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">χρῆν ς’, εἴπερ ἦσθα μὴ κακός, πείσαντ’ ἐμὲ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">γαμεῖν γάμον τόνδ’</quote> (<foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, <quote xml:lang="grc">πείσας ἂν ἐγάμεις</quote>). <cit> <bibl n="Lys. 32.23">Lys. or. 32 § 23</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἱ ἐβούλετο δίκαιος εἶναι,... ἐξῆν αὐτῷ... μισθῶσαι τὸν ο<*>κον</quote> </cit> (<foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμίσθωσεν ἄν</quote>). </gloss>

data.quote:
εἱ ἐβούλετο δίκαιος εἶναι,... ἐξῆν αὐτῷ...
                                    μισθῶσαι τὸν ο<*>κον

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μοῦνος_531"> (<title>O.T.</title> 304 n.) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Ἑλλήνων_531">. He of all men—the father of the maiden —was the one who resolved on sacrificing her. The sacrifice was, indeed, approved by the other chiefs ( <ref n="Aesch. Ag. 230">Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 230</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">φιλόμαχοι βραβῆς</quote>); and several persons took part in the deed itself (<foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 240 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἕκαστον θυτήρων</quote>). But, in the first instance, when Calchas spoke, the decision rested with Agamemnon. Cp. <ref n="Aesch. Ag. 205">Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 205</ref>—225, ending with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔτλα δ’ οὖν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">θυτὴρ γενέσθαι θυγατρός</quote>. Sophocles, like Aeschylus, ignores the legend used by Euripides, that Iphigeneia was not really slain at Aulis, but wafted by Artemis to the Tauric Chersonese.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῦ_τίνων_534">: <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦ</quote> (neut.) <quote xml:lang="grc">χάριν</quote>, ‘wherefore?’ (as in <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1029"> <title>Ph.</title> 1029</ref>, <quote xml:lang="grc">τί μ’ ἀπάγεσθε; τοῦ χάριν</quote>; ): <quote xml:lang="grc">τίνων</quote> (masc.) <quote xml:lang="grc">χάριν</quote>; ‘for the sake of what men?’ ‘Wherefore—to please <emph>whom</emph>—did he immolate her?’ This is the only construction of the words which fits <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἀργείων</quote> in 535. The twofold question also suits the vehemence of the speaker, who is seeking to drive the point home.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλ_μετῆν_536"> <quote xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</quote>: cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 48"> <title>Ant.</title> 48</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ οὐδὲν αὐτῷ τῶν ἐμῶν μ’ εἴργειν μέτα</quote> (=<quote xml:lang="grc">μέτεστι</quote>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλ_δῆτα_537">. Here the first supposition is introduced by <quote xml:lang="grc">πότερον</quote>, and the second by <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλὰ δῆτα</quote>, as in <ref n="Soph. Aj. 460"> <title>Ai.</title> 460</ref>—466. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλά</quote> was regularly used in thus putting the imagined arguments of an adversary,—the figure called <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑποφορά</quote> in Greek rhetoric, and in Latin <foreign xml:lang="lat">subiectio</foreign>, because <quote xml:lang="lat">subicimus id quod oportet dici</quote> (<ref>Cornificius, 4. 23. 33</ref>). <ref n="Xen. Anab. 5. 8. 4">Xen. <title>An.</title> 5. 8. 4</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πότερον ᾔτουν τί σε καί, ἐπεί μοι οὐκ ἐδίδους, ἔπαιον; ἀλλ’ ἀπῄτουν; ἀλλὰ περὶ παιδικῶν μαχόμενος; ἀλλὰ</quote> <quote xml:lang="grc">μεθύων ἐπαρῴνησα</quote>; In this verse, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλὰ</quote> introduces both the supposed argument and the reply, since </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κτανὼν_537">=<quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ ἔκτανεν</quote> (as=‘granting that he slew’). Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Andoc. 1.148">Andoc. or, 1. § 148</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τίνα γὰρ καὶ ἀναβιβάσομαι δεήσομενον ὑπὲρ ἐμαυτοῦ; τὸν πατέρα; ἀλλὰ τέθνηκεν. ἀλλὰ τοὺς ἀδελφούς; ἀλλ’ οὐκ εἰσίν. ἀλλὰ τοὺς παῖδας; ἀλλ’ οὔπω γεγένηνται</quote> </cit>.</gloss>

data.quote:
τίνα γὰρ καὶ ἀναβιβάσομαι δεήσομενον ὑπὲρ
                                    ἐμαυτοῦ; τὸν πατέρα; ἀλλὰ τέθνηκεν. ἀλλὰ τοὺς ἀδελφούς; ἀλλ’ οὐκ
                                    εἰσίν. ἀλλὰ τοὺς παῖδας; ἀλλ’ οὔπω γεγένηνται

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀντὶ_ἀδελφοῦ_537"> here=‘in his stead,’ <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> ‘to save him from slaying <emph>his</emph> child’: not, ‘for his sake.’ Nor is it short for <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀντὶ</quote> [<quote xml:lang="grc">τῶν τοῦ</quote>] <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀδελφοῦ.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τἄμ_537">: for the neut. referring to persons, <ref n="Soph. El. 972">cp. 972</ref>: <ref n="Soph. OT 1195"> <title>O. T.</title> 1195</ref> n. In <ref n="Soph. Ant. 48"> <title>Ant.</title> 48</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τῶν ἐμ<*>ν</quote>, and in <ref n="Soph. OT 1448"> <title>O. T.</title> 1448</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τῶν γε σῶν</quote>, may be masc., like <ref n="Soph. OC 832"> <title>O. C.</title> 832</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοὺς ἐμοὺς ἄγω</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παῖδες_διπλοῖ_539">. The schol. observes that, according to Homer, Helen bore to Menelaüs only a daughter Hermionè ( <ref n="Hom. Od. 4.14"> <title>Od.</title> 4. 14</ref>,—indicated, though not named, in <ref n="Hom. Il. 3.175"> <title>Il.</title> 3. 175</ref>); but that Hesiod mentioned also a son (fr. 131): <quote xml:lang="grc">ἣ τέκεθ’ Ἑρμιόνην δουρικλειτῷ Μενελάῳ</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁπλότατον δ’ ἔτεκεν Νικόστρατον, ὄζον Ἄρηος</quote>. Sophocles follows Hesiod, since Menelaüs could not have been expected to sacrifice an only child.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἢ_542"> here introduces a third supposition (<ref n="Soph. El. 530">cp. 530</ref>), and in 544 a fourth.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἢ_Ἅιδης_542">: the absence of caesura gives a harsh emphasis to the words; <ref n="Soph. El. 530">cp. 530</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δαίσασθαι_543">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτὰ</quote> (fr. 731 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὠμόβρως ἐδαίσατο</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν Ἀστάκειον παῖδα</quote>): epexegetic inf.: <ref n="Soph. El. 1277">cp. 1277</ref>: <ref n="Eur. Med. 1399">Eur. <title>Med.</title> 1399</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">φιλίου χρῄζω στόματος</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">παίδων ὁ τάλας προσπτύξασθαι</quote> (<foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτό</quote>, cp. <ref n="Eur. Phoen. 1671">Eur. <title>Phoen.</title> 1671</ref>): <ref n="Plat. Crito 52">Plat. <title>Crito</title> 52</ref>B <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδ’ ἐπιθυμία σε ἄλλης πόλεως οὐδ’ ἄλλων νόμων ἔλαβεν εἰδέναι</quote>.—The destroying gods, such as Ares ( <ref n="Hom. Il. 5.289"> <title>Il.</title> 5. 289</ref>) and Thanatos ( <ref n="Eur. Alc. 844">Eur. <title>Alc.</title> 844</ref>), were supposed to rejoice, like the dead ( <ref n="Hom. Od. 11.96">Hom. <title>Od.</title> 11. 96</ref>, <ref n="Eur. Hec. 536">Eur. <title>Hec.</title> 536</ref>), in draughts of blood.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λῄσασθαι_543"> (‘to make his prey’) is correct in form ( <ref n="Eur. Tro. 866">Eur. <title>Tro.</title> 866</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐλῄσατο</quote>), but weaker than the vulgate. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πανώλει_544"> expresses intense hatred, as in <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1357"> <title>Ph.</title> 1357</ref>Odysseus is <quote xml:lang="grc">τῷ πανώλει παιδὶ τῷ Λαερτίου.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παρεῖτο_544">, lit., ‘had been neglected,’—allowed to pass out of his thoughts. This plpf. (identical in form with the 2nd aor.) is somewhat rare: cp. however <ref n="Thuc. 4. 103">Thuc. 4. 103</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">καθεῖτο</quote> (followed by <quote xml:lang="grc">καθειστήκει</quote>): <title>Critias</title> 117 C <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀφεῖτο</quote> (preceded by <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκεχειρούργητο</quote>).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Μενέλεω_δ_544"> as=<quote xml:lang="grc">τῶν δὲ Μενέλεω</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">παίδων</quote>); but that is clearly what is meant. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀβούλου_546">, inconsiderate, — not taking proper thought for his children; cp. <ref n="Soph. Trach. 139"> <title>Tr.</title> 139</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τίς ὧδε</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">τέκνοισι Ζῆν’ ἄβουλον εἶδεν; </quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κακοῦ_γνώμην_546">, perverse,— in choosing to sacrifice his own offspring, rather than another's. There is a bitter irony in this mode of characterising what she regards as unnatural cruelty. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δοκῶ_μέν_547">: 61 n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰ_καὶ_547">, though (as is the case): cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 408"> <title>O. T.</title> 408</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ καὶ τυραννεῖς</quote>, and appendix on <ref n="Soph. OT 305."> <title>O. T.</title> 305.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰ_λάβοι_548">: cp. <ref n="Aesch. Ag. 37">Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 37</ref>(<quote xml:lang="grc">οἶκος</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ φθογγὴν λάβοι</quote>. In the <title>Odyssey</title> the departed spirits are conceived unable to recognise or to accost the living, until they have tasted the offerings of blood (11. 153).—Similarly Oedipus imagines how Laïus would testify, if he could return to life ( <ref n="Soph. OC 998"> <title>O. C.</title> 998</ref>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῖς_πεπραγμένοις_549">, causal dat.; cp. <ref n="Thuc. 3. 98">Thuc. 3. 98</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῖς πεπραγμένοις φοβούμενος τοὺς Ἀθηναίους</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 1127">Soph. <title>Tr.</title> 1127</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γνώμην_σχοῦσα_551"> <quote xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</quote>: first attain to a just view of the matter, and then, but not sooner, blame thy neighbours. Schol.: <quote xml:lang="grc">οἷον λογισμῷ χρησαμένη δικαίῳ τότε</quote> [=<quote xml:lang="grc">εἶτα</quote>] <quote xml:lang="grc">τοὺς πέλας ψ<*>γε ἡμᾶς</quote>. The usual sense of the aor. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔσχον</quote> (‘came to have,’ ‘acquired’) is a reason for taking the words thus, rather than as referring to the subject of <quote xml:lang="grc">δοκῶ</quote>, ‘though I have held a just view’; in the latter case, we should have expected <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔχουσα</quote>. The aor. part. of the simple <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔχω</quote> is not frequent ( <ref n="Hom. Od. 4.70"> <title>Od.</title> 4. 70</ref> and <ref n="Thuc. 5. 2">Thuc. 5. 2</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">σχών</quote>: <ref n="Antiph. 5.67">Antiphon or. 5 § 67</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">σχόντες</quote>: <ref n="Hdt. 4. 203">Her. 4. 203</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">σχοῦσι</quote>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐρεῖς_οὐχὶ_552"> <quote xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</quote> The antithesis implied by <quote xml:lang="grc">μὲν</quote> is given by vv. 554 f.: ‘I have not, indeed, provoked this discussion; still, I should like to speak.’ For </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐρεῖς_552"> preceding the negative, cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 223"> <title>Ant.</title> 223</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄναξ, ἐρῶ μὲν οὐχ κ.τ.λ.</quote> For the constr., </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐρεῖς_ὡς_552">, <ref n="Soph. El. 520">cp. 520</ref>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄρξασά_λυπηρὸν_552">: cp. fr. 339 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄρξασι Φρυξὶ τὴν κατ’ Ἀργείων ὕβριν</quote>. This constr., rare for <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄρχω</quote>, is regular for <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑπάρχω</quote>, as <ref n="Dem. 19.280">Dem. or. 19 § 280</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">εὐεργεσίας ἃς ὑπῆρξαν εἰς ἡμᾶς.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τάδ_ἐξήκους_552">, alluding to the reproaches in vv. 518, 529, etc. Cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 378"> <title>Ph.</title> 378</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">δηχθεὶς πρὸς ἁξήκουσεν</quote>, ‘stung by the abuse.’ </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῦ_ὕπερ_554">. She will speak justly in the cause of both. The pathos of Iphigeneia's death is not diminished, but enhanced, if Agamemnon acted against his own will.—Hermann, reading <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦ τεθνηκότος <emph>γ’</emph> </quote>, took <quote xml:lang="grc">γε</quote> to imply that she would not defend <emph>herself.</emph> But the double <quote xml:lang="grc">τε</quote> suits the promise of impartiality. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_μὴν_556">, lit., ‘and verily,’— expressing assent to the request: cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 221"> <title>Ant.</title> 221</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λόγους_με_556">=<quote xml:lang="grc">προσεφώνεις με</quote>: <ref n="Soph. El. 123">cp. 123</ref> ff. <quote xml:lang="grc">τάκεις...οἰμωγὰν...Ἀγαμέμνονα</quote>: <title>O.C.</title> 1120 <quote xml:lang="grc">τέκνα...μηκύνω λόγον</quote> (speak at length to my children). <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξάρχω</quote> was said esp. of beginning a musical strain ( <ref n="Eur. Tro. 148">Eur. <title>Tro.</title> 148</ref> ff. <quote xml:lang="grc">μολπὰν...οἵαν... ἐξῆρχον θεούς</quote>). Here it perhaps suggests the same idea, with a tinge of irony: ‘if you always began in such a key,’—viz., of filial deference. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_σοι_558">: the same formula as in 892 and <ref n="Soph. Ant. 245"> <title>Ant.</title> 245</ref>,—<quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ δὴ</quote> expressing prompt compliance. Cp. also 1436, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1464."> Ant. 1464.</ref>The sense of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πατέρα_558"> is relative to the speaker, and not (as would be more natural) to the subject of </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φὴς_558">: see on <ref n="Soph. Trach. 1125"> <title>Tr.</title> 1125</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τῆς πατροφόντου μητρός</quote>. —For the doubled <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄν</quote>, <ref n="Soph. El. 333">cp. 333</ref> f. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἴτ_εἴτε_560">: <ref n="Soph. El. 199">cp. 199</ref> n. We supply <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔκτεινας</quote>: cp. <ref n="Aesch. Eum. 468">Aesch. <title>Eum.</title> 468</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">σὺ δ’, εἰ δικαίως εἴτε μή, κρῖνον δίκην</quote>, where <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔκτεινα</quote> is understood from v. 463.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λέξω_560">: the fut. here implies the sense of a task from which the speaker will not shrink; ‘I have to tell thee’;—as elsewhere the fut. bespeaks indulgence for a request: <title>O.T.</title> 1446 <quote xml:lang="grc">προστρέψομαι</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Aj. 825"> <title>Ai.</title> 825</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">αἰτήσομαι</quote>. The conjecture </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δείξω_560"> seems unnecessary. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡς_γ_561">: <quote xml:lang="grc">γε</quote>, emphasising <quote xml:lang="grc">δίκῃ</quote>, is suitable here, since <quote xml:lang="grc">δικαίως</quote> (560) expressed the first of two alternatives. For the omission of the object to <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔκτεινας</quote>, cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 801"> <title>Ph.</title> 801</ref>(<quote xml:lang="grc">ἔμπρησον</quote>), n.— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔσπασεν_561">, a stronger word than <quote xml:lang="grc">εἵλκυσε</quote>: cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 791"> <title>Ant.</title> 791</ref>(of Erôs) <quote xml:lang="grc">σὺ καὶ δικαίων ἀδίκους φρένας παρασπᾷς ἐπὶ λώβᾳ</quote>.— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πειθὼ_561">: cp. <ref n="Hom. Od. 3.264"> <title>Od.</title> 3. 264</ref>(Aegisthus) <quote xml:lang="grc">πόλλ’ Ἀγαμεμνονέην ἄλοχον θέλγεσκ’ ἐπέεσσιν</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κυναγὸν_563">: the Doric form, as in <quote xml:lang="grc">λοχαγός, ὁδαγός</quote>, etc. ( <ref n="Soph. Ant. 715"> <title>Ant.</title> 715</ref> n.): but <quote xml:lang="grc">κυνηγετεῖν</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. Aj. 5"> <title>Ai.</title> 5</ref>), <quote xml:lang="grc">κυνηγία, κυνηγέτης.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ποινὰς_563">, acc. in appos. with the sentence; <ref n="Soph. El. 129">cp. 129</ref> n., <title>P.V.</title> 563 <quote xml:lang="grc">τίνος ἀμπλακίας ποινὰς ὀλέκει</quote>; <ref n="Eur. Alc. 6">Eur. <title>Alc.</title> 6</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">καί με θητεύειν πατὴρ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">θνητῷ παρ’ ἀνδρὶ τῶνδ’ ἄποιν’ ἠνάγκασεν</quote>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὰ_ἔσχ_563">, ‘she stayed those frequent winds,’ which ordinarily blow from the coast of Greece,—causing a calm of unusual length. For </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὰ_πολλὰ_563">, <ref n="Soph. El. 931">cp. 931</ref>: <title>O.C.</title> 87 <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ πόλλ’ ἐκεῖν’ ὅτ’ ἐξέχρη κακά</quote>: <ref n="Aesch. Ag. 1456">Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 1456</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μία τὰς πολλάς, τὰς πάνυ πολλὰς</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ψυχὰς ὀλέσασ’</quote>. For </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔσχε_563"> as=‘stopped,’ cp. <title>O.C.</title> 888 <quote xml:lang="grc">βουθυτοῦντά μ’...ἔσχετ’</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πλοῖα_563"> for <quote xml:lang="grc">πολλὰ</quote> (cr. n.) would be an attractive remedy, if the idea of a <emph>calm</emph> were not admissible. It may be noticed, however, that the word <quote xml:lang="grc">πλοῖον</quote>, admitted by Aesch. as a synonym for <quote xml:lang="grc">ναῦς</quote> ( <ref n="Aesch. Ag. 625">Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 625</ref>), occurs in Soph. only once (fr. 127), and is not extant in </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Αὐλίδι_563">. Aulis was so named from the channel (<quote xml:lang="grc">αὐλός</quote>), as other towns were named from <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐλών</quote> ‘a valley.’ It stood on the Boeotian coast, in the territory of Tanagra, about three miles s. of the point where, at Chalcis in Euboea, the Euripus is narrowest. The site was a rocky peninsula (hence <quote xml:lang="grc">Αὐλίδα πετρήεσσαν</quote>, <ref n="Hom. Il. 2.496"> <title>Il.</title> 2. 496</ref>), having on the south of it a large basin, known as the <quote xml:lang="grc">βαθὺς λιμήν</quote> (Strabo 3. p. 403). Pausanias saw at Aulis an ancient temple of Artemis, with two statues of the goddess (9. 19. 6). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἢ_φράσω_565">, a self-correction, as in 352 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἢ μάθ’ ἐξ ἐμοῦ</quote>. It is not <quote xml:lang="grc">θέμις</quote> for a mortal to cross-question a goddess face to face.—For the simple gen. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κείνης_565">, <ref n="Soph. El. 668">cp. 668</ref>: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 370"> <title>Ph.</title> 370</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μαθεῖν ἐμοῦ</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡς_κλύω_566">: implying the possibility of other accounts. Aeschylus does not define the offence which had angered Artemis ( <ref n="Aesch. Ag. 132"> <title>Ag.</title> 132</ref>). According to Euripides, Agamemnon had rashly vowed that he would sacrifice to her the fairest creature that the year should bring forth (<title>I.T.</title> 20).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θεᾶς_ἄλσος_566">, a sacred precinct near the temple of Artemis at Aulis, mentioned in <ref n="Eur. IA 1544">Eur. <title>I.A.</title> 1544</ref>, <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἀρτέμιδος ἄλσος λείμακάς τ’ ἀνθεσφόρους</quote>, and called <quote xml:lang="grc">πολύθυτον</quote>, <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 185. At many places on the eastern coasts of Greece Artemis was worshipped, esp. as <quote xml:lang="grc">λιμενοσκόπος</quote> (see on <ref n="Soph. Trach. 637">Soph. <title>Tr.</title> 637</ref>).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παίζων_566">, ‘amusing himself,’ ‘taking his pleasure.’ The allusion is to the pursuit of game, though <quote xml:lang="grc">παίζω</quote> had no definitely technical sense like that of our word ‘sport.’ We find the verb associated with hunting in <ref n="Hom. Od. 6.104"> <title>Od.</title> 6. 104</ref> ff. (Artemis) <quote xml:lang="grc">τερπομένη κάπροισι καὶ ὠκείῃς ἐλάφοισι·</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">τῇ δέ θ’ ἅμα νύμφαι, κοῦραι Διὸς αἰγιόχοιο</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀγρονόμοι παίζουσι</quote>. Dindorf quotes <ref n="Plut. Alex. 23">Plut. <title>Alex.</title> 23</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πολλάκις δὲ παίζων καὶ ἀλώπεκας ἐθήρευε καὶ ὄρνιθας</quote>. Tzetzes, in telling the story, says, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξελθὼν ἐπὶ κυνηγέσιον</quote> (on Lycophr. 183).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ποδοῖν_566">: <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, by the sound of his feet; not by their touch. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔλαφον_568">, ‘a stag.’ The word can also mean ‘a hind,’ as in fr. 86 <quote xml:lang="grc">κεροῦσσα...ἔλαφος.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="στικτὸν_568"> ( <ref n="Soph. Phil. 184"> <title>Ph.</title> 184</ref>)= <quote xml:lang="grc">βαλιόν</quote>: <ref n="Eur. Ba. 111">Eur. <title>Bacch.</title> 111</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">στικτῶν...νεβρίδων</quote>: <cit> <bibl>Kaibel <title>Epigr.</title> 1082. 4</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">στικτὴν ἔλαφον</quote> </cit>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κεράστην_568">: cp. the Homeric <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔλαφον κεραόν</quote> ( <ref n="Hom. Il. 3.24"> <title>Il.</title> 3. 24</ref>, etc.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὗ_σφαγὰς_568">, <emph>concerning</emph> the slaughter of it: cp. <ref n="Hdt. 1. 31">Her. 1. 31</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ κατὰ τὸν Τέλλον...εἴπας</quote>: <ref n="Hdt. 2.3">id. 2. 3</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κατὰ μὲν δὴ τὴν τροφὴν τῶν παιδίων τοσαῦτα ἔλεγον</quote>. Others understand (less well, I think), ‘<emph>on the occasion of</emph> the slaughter’ (‘<emph>beim</emph> Erlegen desselben,’ G. Wolff; and so Nauck). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκκομπάσας_τυγχάνει_569">, ‘he chanced to utter a certain (irreverent) boast,’ </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βαλών_569">, ‘after hitting’ (with an arrow, or perh. with a javelin). <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔπος τι</quote> is euphemistic: cp. Hyginus <ref n="Hyg. Fab. 98"> <title>Fab.</title> 98</ref> <emph>superbiusque in Dianam locutus est.</emph> The vaunt would naturally follow, not precede, the hit. And so we read elsewhere. Schol. <ref n="Eur. Orest. 647">Eur. <title>Or.</title> 647</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">καυχησάμενος δὲ ἐπὶ τῇ ἐπιτυχίᾳ, καὶ εἰπὼν ὡς οὐδ’ ἂν αὐτὴ ἡ Ἄρτεμις οὕτως ἔβαλεν, ἐπειράθη τῆς θεοῦ ὀργιζομένης κ.τ.λ.</quote> Proclus <title>Chrest.</title> p. 455 <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἀγαμέμνων ἐπὶ θήρᾳ βαλὼν ἔλαφον ὑπερβάλλειν ἔφησε καὶ τὴν Ἄρτεμιν</quote>. Tzetzes on Lycophr. 183 <quote xml:lang="grc">τρώσας ἔλαφον μέγα καυχήσας ἐφθέγξατο οὕτως· Οὐδὲ ἡ Ἄρτξμις</quote> (<foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὕτως ἂν ἔβαλεν</quote>).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βαλὼν_569"> with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔπος_569">: ‘he chanced to <emph>let fall</emph> some boastful word.’ But this is objectionable on two grounds. (1) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκβάλλειν ἔπος</quote> is a very frequent phrase; <ref n="Hom. Il. 18.324"> <title>Il.</title> 18. 324</ref>: <ref n="Hom. Od. 4.503"> <title>Od.</title> 4. 503</ref>: <ref n="Pind. P. 2">Pind. <title>P.</title> 2. 81</ref>: <ref n="Aesch. Ag. 1663">Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 1663</ref>, <ref n="Aesch.
