Lib. 47">Aesch. <title>Ch.</title> 47</ref>: <ref n="Hdt. 6. 69">Her. 6. 69</ref>, etc. But <quote xml:lang="grc">βάλλειν ἔπος</quote> does not seem to occur: even in <ref n="Aristoph. Frogs 595">Ar. <title>Ran.</title> 595</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ βαλεῖς τι μαλθακόν, κἀκβαλεῖς</quote> is a <emph>v.l.</emph>, and probably right; cp. <title>Vesp.</title> 1289 <quote xml:lang="grc">σκωμμάτιον...ἐκβαλῶ</quote>. (2) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκκομπάσας</quote>, combined with <quote xml:lang="grc">βαλών</quote> in this sense, would be awkwardly redundant.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σφαγὰς_569"> as=<emph>iugulum</emph>, understanding, ‘he uttered a vaunt, and hit the stag with his missile in the throat.’ (<title>Mus. Crit.</title> 1. 77.) </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκ_τοῦδε_570">, <emph>idcirco.</emph> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μηνίσασα_570">, of divine wrath, as in <title>O.C.</title> 965, <ref n="Soph. Trach. 274"> <title>Tr.</title> 274</ref>: and so <quote xml:lang="grc">μῆνις</quote>, <ref n="Soph. Aj. 656"> <title>Ai.</title> 656</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Aj. 757."> Ai. 757.</ref>But human resentment is denoted by the verb in <title>O.C.</title> 1274, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1177"> <title>Ant.</title> 1177</ref>; and by the noun in <title>O.T.</title> 699, <title>O.C.</title> 1328.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Λητῴα_κόρη_570">: cp. <title>O.T.</title> 267 <quote xml:lang="grc">τῷ Λαβδακείῳ παιδί</quote> (n.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡς_571">: the will of the goddess was interpreted by Calchas. Nothing would be gained by reading <quote xml:lang="grc">ἕως</quote> (monosyll.).— </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀντίσταθμον_571">,=<quote xml:lang="grc">ἀντίρροπον</quote>, ‘in compensation for.’</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκθύσειε_571">. The midd. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκθύεσθαι</quote> is to <emph>expiate</emph> (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἄγος</quote>), or to <emph>propitiate</emph> (<quote xml:lang="grc">θεόν</quote>). But the act. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκθύειν</quote> is merely a strengthened <quote xml:lang="grc">θύειν</quote>, ‘to make a sacrifice of,’ implying some cruelty or violence: <ref n="Eur. Cycl. 371">Eur. <title>Cyc.</title> 371</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐφεστίους ἱκτῆρας ἐκθύεις δόμων</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὧδ_ἦν_573">, instead of <quote xml:lang="grc"><*>δ’ εἶχε</quote> or <quote xml:lang="grc">τοιαῦτα ἦν</quote>. When an adv. is joined with <quote xml:lang="grc">εἶναι</quote>, the verb is more often impers., as in <quote xml:lang="grc">εὖ γὰρ ἔσται</quote> ( <ref n="Eur. Med. 89">Eur. <title>Med.</title> 89</ref>).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὰ_θύματ_573">: for the poet. pl., cp. <ref n="Soph. Trach. 627"> <title>Tr.</title> 627</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ τῆς ξένης</quote>... | <quote xml:lang="grc">προσδέγματ’.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λύσις_573">, ‘release’ from the detention at Aulis.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρὸς_οἶκον_573">. The weather, which stopped the voyage to Troy, would also hinder some of the islanders from going home; but most of the allies from the mainland could have reached their respective ports with less difficulty; and we can only suppose that Calchas threatened them with the wrath of Artemis. In <ref n="Aesch. Ag. 212">Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 212</ref>Agamemnon speaks as if a return home were possible, though disgraceful. In <ref n="Eur. IA 94">Eur. <title>I.A.</title> 94</ref> ff. he is on the point of dismissing the army, when Menelaüs interferes. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πολλὰ_575"> goes with both participles. Nauck objects that it must mean ‘<emph>often</emph>,’ and conjectures <quote xml:lang="grc">λιασθείς</quote>. But see <ref n="Hom. Il. 11.556"> <title>Il.</title> 11. 556</ref> f., <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς Αἴας τότ’ ἀπὸ Τρώων τετιημένος ἦτορ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἤϊε, πόλλ’ ἀέκων</quote>. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 254"> <title>Ph.</title> 254</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ πόλλ’ ἐγὼ μοχθηρός.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀντιβὰς_575">: cp. <ref n="Hdt. 6. 73">Her. 6. 73</ref>(<quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐδικαίευν ἔτι ἀντιβαίνειν</quote>. <title>Geo.</title> 4. 301 <title>Multa reluctanti.</title> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μόλις_575">, <emph>aegre: </emph> <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1105">Ant. 1105</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μόλις μέν, καρδίας δ’ ἐξίσταμαι</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 329.">Soph. <title>Ph.</title> 329.</ref>Aeschylus describes the struggle in the father's soul, until <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνάγκας ἔδυ λέπαδνον</quote> ( <ref n="Aesch. Ag. 206"> <title>Ag.</title> 206</ref>—221). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰ_οὖν_577">, ‘but if it <emph>was</emph> with the wish,’ etc.,—referring to Clytaemnestra's words in 537 ff. Cp. <ref n="Aesch. Ag. 1042">Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 1042</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ δ’ οὖν ἀνάγκη τῆσδ’ ἐπιρρέποι τύχης</quote> (‘but if the doom of slavery <emph>should</emph> befall’).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐρῶ_σόν_577">, ‘for I will state thy plea (537 ff.) also’: cp. <ref n="Soph. Trach. 479"> <title>Tr.</title> 479</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">δεῖ γὰρ καὶ τὸ πρὸς κείνου λέγειν</quote>. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1313"> <title>Ai.</title> 1313</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅρα μὴ τοὐμὸν ἀλλὰ καὶ τὸ σόν</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τούτου_οὕνεκ_578">: for the large interval between the case and this prep., cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 857"> <title>O. T.</title> 857</ref> f.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νόμῳ_578">, ‘rule,’ ‘principle’; <ref n="Soph. Ant. 908"> <title>Ant.</title> 908</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Trach. 616."> <title>Tr.</title> 616.</ref> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅρα_580">...</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μὴ_τιθῇς_580">. The subjunctive here is supported by our best MS., L, which has <quote xml:lang="grc">τίθηις</quote>, while in 584 it has <quote xml:lang="grc">τίθης</quote>. In 584 </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τίθης_580"> is clearly right: ‘see that you <emph>are not</emph> making a false excuse’ (she is actually making it). Cp. <ref n="Plat. Theaet. 145C">Plat. <title>Theaet.</title> 145C</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅρα μὴ παίζων ἔλεγεν</quote>: and other examples in n. on <ref n="Soph. Phil. 30.">Soph. <title>Ph.</title> 30.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τιθεῖσα_νόμον_580">: cp. n. on <ref n="Soph. Ant. 8"> <title>Ant.</title> 8</ref>: <ref n="Eur. Alc. 57">Eur. <title>Alc.</title> 57</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πρὸς τῶν ἐχόντων, Φοῖβε, τὸν νόμον τίθης</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰ_κτενοῦμεν_582">. Note two points of syntax here. (1) We have two protases, not co-ordinate: the first of them, <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ κτενοῦμεν</quote>, states the primary condition, on which everything else depends. ‘<emph>If</emph> that is to be the rule, <emph>then</emph> (<emph>if</emph> you got your due) you would die.’ Cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 782"> <title>Ai.</title> 782</ref> f.: <title>Suppl.</title> 1084 <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ δ’ ἦμεν νέοι | δὶς καὶ γέροντες, εἴ τις ἐξημάρτανε</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">διπλοῦ βίου λαχόντες ἐξωρθούμεθ’ ἄν</quote>. (2) The first protasis has the fut. indic.: the second protasis and the apodosis have the optat. with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄν</quote>, not as a softened future, but in a potential sense. Cp. <ref n="Thuc. 1. 142">Thuc. 1. 142</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">φρούριον δ’ εἰ ποιήσονται, τῆς μὲν γῆς βλάπτοιεν ἄν τι μέρος</quote>. <ref n="Eur. Tro. 730">Eur. <title>Tro.</title> 730</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ γάρ τι λέξεις ᾦ χολώσεται στρατός</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔτ’ ἂν ταφείη παῖς ὅδ’, οὔτ’ οἴκτου τύχοι</quote>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρώτη_582">: <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, no one has more fully deserved the penalty. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰσόρα_584">, instead of <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅρα</quote>: <ref n="Soph. El. 611">cp. 611</ref>, 997.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐκ_οὖσαν_584">,=<quote xml:lang="grc">ψευδῆ</quote>, unreal, false: cp. <ref n="Aristoph. Frogs 1052">Ar. <title>Ran.</title> 1052</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ΕΥ. πότερον δ’ οὐκ ὄντα λόγον τοῦτον περὶ τῆς Φαίδρας ξυνέθηκα; ΑΙΣ. μὰ Δἴ, ἀλλ’ ὄντ’.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τίθης_584">: <ref target="cl580">see on 580</ref> f.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰ_θέλεις_584">, here merely a form of ironical courtesy: but in <title>O.T.</title> 343=‘if you choose.’ </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῷ_παλαμναίῳ_587">, more forcible than <quote xml:lang="grc">φονεῖ</quote>: cp. <ref n="Soph. Trach. 1207"> <title>Tr.</title> 1207</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">φονέα γενέσθαι καὶ παλαμναῖον σέθεν</quote> (n.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρόσθεν_ἐξαπώλεσας_587">. The point of <quote xml:lang="grc">πρόσθεν</quote> is that, having <emph>first</emph> used his aid to slay her husband, she has put him in that husband's place. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παιδοποιεῖς_589">. The active voice of this verb is rarer than the middle, but is proved by metre in <ref n="Eur. Heraclid. 524">Eur. <title>Heracl.</title> 524</ref>, <ref n="Aristoph. Eccl. 615.">Ar. <title>Eccl.</title> 615.</ref>In both forms it is usually said of the man.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εὐσεβεῖς_589">: schol. rec.: <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐννόμους, ἤγουν ἐξ ἐννόμου γάμου</quote>. ‘Stainless,’ as being the legitimate offspring of a lawful marriage.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κἀξ_εὐσεβῶν_589">: <quote xml:lang="grc">τουτέστιν ἐννόμῳ γάμῳ ἡρμοσμένων</quote> (schol.). The reference is to both parents, not to Agam. only.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βλαστόντας_589">. Some MSS., including L, have <quote xml:lang="grc">βλαστῶντας</quote>: but, though <quote xml:lang="grc">βλαστέω</quote> may be classical, <quote xml:lang="grc">βλαστάω</quote> occurs only in late Greek, as schol. <ref>Pind. 4. 65</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">βλαστᾷ</quote>: Hermas <title>Past.</title> p. 57 (<foreign xml:lang="lat">ap.</foreign> Veitch) <quote xml:lang="grc">βλαστῶντα.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκβαλοῦσ_ἔχεις_589">, an emphatic perfect, like <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀτιμάσας ἔχει</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. Ant. 22"> <title>Ant.</title> 22</ref>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πῶς_ἄν_591">; Cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 451"> <title>Ph.</title> 451</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ποῦ χρὴ τίθεσθαι ταῦτα, ποῦ δ’ αἰνεῖν</quote>...;</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἢ_591"> is better here than </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἦ_591">. ‘How can such an act be approved? <title>Or</title> will you plead that, though bad in itself, it is ex cused by the provocation?’—The constr. is:—<quote xml:lang="grc">ἢ ἐρεῖς ὡς <emph>καὶ ταῦτα</emph> λαμβάνεις ἀντίποινα τῆς θυγατρός</quote>; lit., ‘that thou takest this also as compensation,’ etc. (This seems truer than to make <quote xml:lang="grc">ταῦτα</quote> an acc. of respect, ‘in regard to this also.’) The traditional reading, </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_τοῦτ_591">, admits of the same rendering. But Greek idiom favours the assimilation of the pronoun to </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄποινα_591"> (cp. <title>O.C.</title> 88 n.); and <quote xml:lang="grc">ταῦτ’</quote> would easily have become <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦτ’</quote>, if it had been supposed that the sense was, ‘Wilt thou <emph>say this</emph> too?’ </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αἰσχρῶς_δ_593">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐρεῖς</quote>. I leave the traditional </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δ_593">: for, though it might well be absent, it adds a certain vivacity to the retort. ‘Will that be your plea? Nay, it will be a shameful one.’</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐάν_λέγῃς_593">, ‘if you <emph>do</emph> allege it’ (as it is scarcely conceivable that you should). Here <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ</quote> goes closely with the verb: cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 90"> <title>Ant.</title> 90</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ καὶ δυνήσει γ’</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐχθροῖς_γαμεῖσθαι_593">. Cp. <emph><*>r.</emph> 1236 f., where Hyllus demurs to marrying lolè (whom he regards as indirectly the cause of his father's death): <quote xml:lang="grc">κρεῖσσον κἀμέ γ’, ὦ πάτερ, θανεῖν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἢ τοῖσιν ἐχθίστοισι συνναίειν ὁμοῦ</quote>. Aegisthus is, in Electra's view, an <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐχθρός</quote>, primarily as having helped to murder Agamemnon. As the son of Thyestes, and the murderer of Atreus, he is in a further sense the <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐχθρός</quote> of all Atreidae.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλ_γὰρ_595">, elliptical: ‘But (I will argue no more), for,’ etc.: <ref n="Soph. El. 223">cp. 223</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πᾶσαν_γλῶσσαν_595">, poet. for <quote xml:lang="grc">πᾶσαν φωνὴν ἵης</quote>: cp. fr. 843. 3 <quote xml:lang="grc">πολλὴν γλῶσσαν ἐκχέας μάτην</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 323"> <title>Tr.</title> 323</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">διήσει γλῶσσαν</quote> (n.). <title>Legg.</title> 890 D <quote xml:lang="grc">πᾶσαν, τὸ λεγόμενον, φωνὴν ἱέντα</quote>, <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, ‘with all possible earnestness.’ <title>Euthyd.</title>p. 293 A <quote xml:lang="grc">πᾶσαν ἤδη φωνὴν ἠφίειν, δεόμενος τοῖν ξένοιν...σῶσαι ἡμᾶς</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κακοστομοῦμεν_597">: the verb occurs only here: cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">εὐστομεῖν</quote> (<title>O.C.</title> 18). The conj. <quote xml:lang="grc">κακορροθοῦμεν</quote> is illustrated by <ref n="Eur. Hipp. 340">Eur. <title>Hipp.</title> 340</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ξυγγόνους κακορροθεῖς</quote>, but is needless.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καί_δεσπότιν_597">. It is not easy to decide whether <quote xml:lang="grc">καί</quote> here means (1) ‘and <emph>indeed</emph>,’—or (2) like <quote xml:lang="grc">καίτοι</quote>, ‘and <emph>yet.</emph>’ The latter sense may be illustrated by <ref n="Soph. Trach. 1048"> <title>Tr.</title> 1048</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 332"> <title>Ant.</title> 332</ref>, and <ref n="Aristoph. Kn. 1250">Ar. <title>Eq.</title> 1250</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">χαίρων ἄπιθι· καί σ’ ἄκων ἐγὼ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">λείπω</quote>. It would refer to <quote xml:lang="grc">τὴν μητέρα</quote>. ‘You complain that I revile my mother. <title>And yet</title> you are no mother in my view, but a mistress.’ But the other interpretation is simpler. <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ</quote> will then refer to the instance of harshness already given (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἣ πᾶσαν ἵης γλῶσσαν</quote>). ‘<title>And indeed</title> I regard you as being (in all things) as much a mistress as a mother.’ [I formerly understood:— ‘And indeed it is even worse,—it is a case of a slave taunting her mistress’: but this is perhaps too artificial.] For <quote xml:lang="grc">δεσπότιν</quote>, <ref n="Soph. El. 264">cp. 264</ref>, and 1192 <quote xml:lang="grc">δουλεύω</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰς_ἡμᾶς_597"> is best taken as=<quote xml:lang="grc">εἰς ἐμέ</quote>, since Orestes (601) is beyond the reach of the domestic tyranny denoted by <quote xml:lang="grc">δεσπότιν</quote>. <ref n="Soph. El. 116">Cp. 116</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡμετέρου</quote>. There is no awkwardness in </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἣ_597"> (599), since it follows <quote xml:lang="grc">νέμω</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βίον_μοχθηρόν_599">: see vv. 189 ff.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔκ_σοῦ_599">=<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκ σοῦ τε</quote>: <ref n="Soph. El. 249">cp. 249</ref> f. (n.).— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κακοῖς_ξυνοῦσα_599">: <ref n="Soph. El. 241">cp. 241</ref> (<quote xml:lang="grc">ξυνναίοιμ’</quote>): <title>O.T.</title> 303 <quote xml:lang="grc">νόσῳ σύνεστιν.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="συννόμου_599">: so <title>O.C.</title> 340 <quote xml:lang="grc">αἱ...σύννομοι</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὁ_ἄλλος_601">: not, ‘the other’ (of us two), which would be <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ ἕτερος</quote> (though Theocr. 6. 45 has <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔδαλλος</quote> for <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδέτερος</quote>): but, ‘that other one,’—that other member of our family,—of whom she was thinking when she spoke of <quote xml:lang="grc">τοὺς πρόσθεν εὐσεβεῖς</quote> (589). Some critics understand, ‘and he, <emph>besides.</emph>’ For this use of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄλλος</quote>, see <title>O.T.</title> 290 n.: but it does not seem in place here. —No emendation (cr. n.) is probable, or needful.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χεῖρα_σὴν_601">: <ref target="cl11">see on 11</ref>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τρίβει_βίον_601">, in weary exile: <ref n="Soph. El. 159">cp. 159</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀχέων</quote>: <ref n="Aristoph. Pl. 526">Ar. <title>Pl.</title> 526</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀδυνηρότερον τρίψεις βίοτον</quote>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δή_σοὶ_601"> rather than </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δή_σοι_601"> is that <quote xml:lang="grc">σοὶ</quote>, placed thus, would almost necessarily receive a slight emphasis when the verse was spoken. But the chief emphasis is on <quote xml:lang="grc">μιάστορα</quote>, and, so far as the sense is concerned, an enclitic <quote xml:lang="grc">σοι</quote> would suffice. Whitelaw's version brings this out:—‘O<*> whom I hear thee rate me that he lives | Reared up by me, for vengeance.’</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μιάστορα_601">, here, the avenger of a crime, like <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλάστωρ</quote>: so <ref n="Aesch. Eum. 176">Aesch. <title>Eum.</title> 176</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἕτερον ἐν κάρᾳ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">μιάστορ’ ἐκ κείνου πάσεται</quote>. <ref n="Eur. Med. 1371">Eur. <title>Med.</title> 1371</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἵδ’ εἰσίν, οἴμοι, σῷ κάρᾳ μιάστορες</quote>.—<ref n="Soph. El. 275">Cp. 275</ref>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῦδέ_οὕνεκα_605">: 387 n. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰς_ἅπαντας_606">: cp. <title>O.T.</title> 93 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐς πάντας αὔδα</quote> (n.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χρῇς_606">. As to the forms <quote xml:lang="grc">χρῇς, χρῇ</quote>, see on <ref n="Soph. Ant. 887"> <title>Ant.</title> 887</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἴτε χρῇ θανεῖν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">εἴτ’ ἐν τοιαύτῃ ζῶσα τυμβεύειν στέγῃ</quote>,—where <quote xml:lang="grc">ζῶσα</quote> excludes <quote xml:lang="grc">χρή</quote>. Here, as in <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1373"> <title>Ai.</title> 1373</ref>(<quote xml:lang="grc">σοὶ δὲ δρᾶν ἔξεσθ’ ἃ χρῇς</quote>), <quote xml:lang="grc">χρὴ</quote> is possible, but certainly less natural than a verb in the 2nd person.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="στόμαργον_606">. It is probable that <quote xml:lang="grc">στόμαργος</quote> was only another form of <quote xml:lang="grc">στόμαλγος</quote>, which is not found; though <quote xml:lang="grc">στομαλγία</quote> was preferred to <quote xml:lang="grc">στομαργία</quote>, as <quote xml:lang="grc">γλωσσαλγία</quote> to <quote xml:lang="grc">γλωσσαργία</quote>. Both <quote xml:lang="grc">γλώσσαλγος</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">γλώσσαργος</quote> occur in later Greek. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῶνδε_ἴδρις_608">, ironi cal, as though these <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔργα</quote> were praiseworthy accomplishments. The <emph>v.l.</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">κακῶν</quote>, for <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔργων</quote>, is very inferior.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σχεδόν_τι_608">, ‘almost,’ <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, ‘it may perhaps be said that...’; cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 470"> <title>Ant.</title> 470</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">σχεδόν τι μώρῳ μωρίαν ὀφλισκάνω</quote> (n.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐ_καταισχύνω_608">: commonly in a good sense: cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1304"> <title>Ai.</title> 1304</ref> f.: <ref n="Hom. Il. 6.209"> <title>Il.</title> 6. 209</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μηδὲ γένος πατέρων αἰσχυνέμεν</quote>. <ref n="Eur. Orest. 1169">Eur. <title>Or.</title> 1169</ref>(Orestes speaking of his father), <quote xml:lang="grc">ὃν οὐ καταισχυνῶ</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὁρῶ_πνέουσαν_610"> <quote xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</quote>: ‘I see that she (Electra) is breathing anger; but whether she has justice on her side, of that I <emph>no longer</emph> see any regard (on her part).’ Electra's speech, which began with temperate argument, has passed (at v. 595) into a strain of angry reproach— closing with the avowal that she would have wished to see Orestes take blood for blood (604 f.). The leader of the Chorus has once before reproved Electra's vehemence (213—220). Here the utterance is exactly parallel with that of the Chorus in <ref n="Soph. Ant. 471"> <title>Ant.</title> 471</ref> f. (after Antigone's defiant speech to Creon),—<quote xml:lang="grc">δηλοῖ τὸ γέννημ’ ὠμὸν ἐξ ὠμοῦ πατρὸς</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">τῆς παιδός· εἴκειν δ’ οὐκ ἐπίσταται κακοῖς</quote>.—For </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μένος_πνέουσαν_610"> cp. <ref n="Aesch. Lib. 33">Aesch. <title>Ch.</title> 33</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κότον</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">πνέων</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σὺν_ξύνεστι_610"> is an unusual pleonasm, but analogous to <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐνεῖναι ἔν τινι</quote> (<title>O.C.</title> 116), <quote xml:lang="grc">παρεῖναι παρά τινι</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1056"> <title>Ph.</title> 1056</ref>), <quote xml:lang="grc">προσθέσθαι πρός τινι</quote> ( <ref n="Aesch. Pers. 531">Aesch. <title>Pers.</title> 531</ref>), etc. Cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1251">Soph. <title>Ph.</title> 1251</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ξὺν τῷ δικαίῳ</quote>. It would be awkward (1) to understand <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ μένος ξύνεστιν αὐτῇ</quote> (schol.); or (2) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκείνη ξύνεστι τῷ μένει</quote> (Herm. ). The conj. of Blaydes, <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ δὲ σοὶ δίκη</quote> etc., yields a clear phrase (cp. <title>O.T.</title> 274 f.): but <quote xml:lang="grc">σοὶ</quote> is objectionable. He understands, ‘I see that Electra is angry; but you (Clytaemnestra) do not consider whether she is in the right.’ Paley, who adopts this conjecture, takes <quote xml:lang="grc">πνέουσαν</quote> to mean Clyt., and <quote xml:lang="grc">σοὶ</quote>, Electra.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ποίας_δεῖ_612">. The Chorus having said that Electra takes no <quote xml:lang="grc">φροντὶς</quote> as to whether she is in the right, Clytaemnestra rejoins, ‘And what manner of <quote xml:lang="grc">φροντὶς</quote> do <title>I</title> need in regard to <emph>her</emph>?’— <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, ‘Must I not indeed be on my guard against her, seeing that she is capable of anything?’ (<ref n="Soph. El. 614">614 f.</ref>). Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Aristoph. Cl. 1032">Ar. <title>Nub.</title> 1032</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">δεινῶν δέ σοι βουλευμάτων ἔοικε δεῖν πρὸς αὐτόν</quote> </cit>. The sense recommends </gloss>

data.quote:
δεινῶν δέ σοι βουλευμάτων ἔοικε δεῖν πρὸς
                                    αὐτόν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐμοὶ_612"> in preference to </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μοι_612">. The order of words also favours it, since <quote xml:lang="grc">μοι</quote> would have been better placed after <quote xml:lang="grc">δεῖ</quote>.—As to </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δεῖ_612">, remark that <quote xml:lang="grc">δεῖ <emph>μοί</emph> τινος</quote> is normal: see, <foreign xml:lang="lat">e.g.</foreign>, fr. 852: <ref n="Aesch. Ag. 848">Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 848</ref>: <ref n="Eur. Med. 565">Eur. <title>Med.</title> 565</ref>: <ref n="Thuc. 1. 71">Thuc. 1. 71</ref>§ 3: <ref n="Plat. Rep. 370D">Plat. <title>Rep.</title> 370D</ref>, <ref n="Plat. Rep. 389D">Plat. Rep. 389D</ref>, <ref n="Plat. Soph. 253A">Plat. <title>Soph.</title> 253A</ref>, <ref n="Plat. Meno 79">Plat. <title>Men.</title> 79</ref>C, <ref n="Plat. Criti. 108b"> <title>Critias</title> 108 B</ref>, <ref n="Plat. Laws 674c"> <title>Legg.</title> 674 C</ref>, <ref n="Plat. Laws 881a">881 A</ref>: while <quote xml:lang="grc">δεῖ <emph>μέ</emph> τινος</quote> is much rarer (<ref n="Aesch. PB 86">Aesch. <title>P.V.</title> 86</ref>, <ref n="Eur. Hipp. 23">Eur. <title>Hipp.</title> 23</ref>, etc.). On the other hand, with the inf., <quote xml:lang="grc">δεῖ <emph>με</emph> λέγειν</quote> is normal, and <quote xml:lang="grc">δεῖ <emph>μοι</emph> λέγειν</quote> extremely rare ( <ref n="Xen. Anab. 3. 4. 35">Xen. <title>An.</title> 3. 4. 35</ref>).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_ταῦτα_614">, ‘and that too’: <title>O.T.</title> 37, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 322."> <title>Ant.</title> 322.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τηλικοῦτος_614">, fem. only here and in <title>O.C.</title> 751. The sense is, ‘so mature,’—old enough to know better. <ref n="Soph. El. 185">Cp. 185</ref> f., and 962 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄλεκτρα γηράσκουσαν</quote>. Not, ‘so young.’</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἆρα_614">, when the expected answer is ‘yes,’ has an ironical tone; ‘pray, are you satisfied that she would...?’: cp. <title>O.T.</title> 822 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἆρ’ ἔφυν κακός</quote>; —followed by <quote xml:lang="grc">ἆρ’ οὐχὶ πᾶς ἄναγνος; </quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χωρεῖν_ἂν_614"> (=<quote xml:lang="grc">ὅτι χωροίη ἂν</quote>) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰς_ἔργον_614">: cp. <ref n="Aristoph. Lys. 543">Ar. <title>Lys.</title> 543</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐθέλω δ’ ἐπὶ πᾶν ἰέναι</quote>. So <ref n="Soph. Ant. 301">Soph. <title>Ant.</title> 301</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">παντὸς ἔργου</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εὖ_ἐπίστω_616">, as in <title>O.T.</title> 658. (In <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1240"> <title>Ph.</title> 1240</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">εὖ νῦν ἐπίστω</quote> is fitter.) <quote xml:lang="grc">νυν</quote> precedes a vowel also in <title>O.T.</title> 644, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 705"> <title>Ant.</title> 705</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1129."> <title>Ai.</title> 1129.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔξωρα_616">, ‘unseasonable,’ from <quote xml:lang="grc">ὥρα</quote> in the sense of <quote xml:lang="grc">καιρός</quote>: not, ‘unsuited to my age.’ In Aeschin. or. 1. § 95 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔξωρος</quote>= ‘past one's prime.’</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προσεικότα_616">: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 902"> <title>Ph.</title> 902</ref> f. <quote xml:lang="grc">τὴν αὑτοῦ φύσιν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅταν λιπών τις δρᾷ τὰ μὴ προσεικότα</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλὰ_γὰρ_619">, elliptical: ‘but (I have excuse), for,’ etc.: <ref n="Soph. El. 223">cp. 223</ref>, 595.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἡ_δυσμένεια_619">: cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 95"> <title>Ant.</title> 95</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὴν ἐξ ἐμοῦ δυσβουλίαν</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 631"> <title>Tr.</title> 631</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν πόθον τὸν ἐξ ἐμοῦ.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐξαναγκάζει_619">: <ref n="Soph. El. 256">cp. 256</ref>.— </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αἰσχροῖς_619">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">πράγμασι</quote>: <ref n="Soph. El. 308">cp. 308</ref> f.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θρέμμα_619"> is not necessarily scornful (see <ref n="Soph. Phil. 243"> <title>Ph.</title> 243</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc"><*> τοῦ γέροντος θρέμμα Λυκομήδους</quote>); but it is sometimes applied to monsters ( <ref n="Soph. Trach. 1093"> <title>Tr.</title> 1093</ref>, etc.), or used, as here, in a disparaging tone, like our word ‘creature’; cp. <ref n="Aesch. Seven 182">Aesch. <title>Th.</title> 182</ref>(said to the Chorus), <quote xml:lang="grc">θρέμματ’ οὐκ ἀνασχετά</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἦ_ἐγὼ_619"> <foreign xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</foreign>, a bitter echo of Electra's phrase (619 f.): ‘Yes, indeed,—they draw only too many words from thee.’ Others understand (less well, I think):—‘Indeed, my <emph>forbearance</emph> in word and deed encourages thee to say too much.’ </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νιν_624">=<quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτά</quote>, the <quote xml:lang="grc">πόλλ’ ἄγαν</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἔπη</quote>): <ref n="Soph. El. 436">cp. 436</ref>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εὑρίσκεται_624">, ‘find for themselves,’—hardly more than <quote xml:lang="grc">εὑρίσκει</quote> (but in <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1023"> <title>Ai.</title> 1023</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ηὑρόμην</quote>=‘gained’).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐ_μὰ_626"> is here followed by a second negative, as in 1239 f.: <ref n="Hom. Il. 1.86"> <title>Il.</title> 1. 86</ref>, <ref n="Hom. Il. 23.44"> Il. 23. 44</ref>: <ref n="Aristoph. Frogs 1043">Ar. <title>Ran.</title> 1043</ref>, etc.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δέσποιναν_626">. This title, given to Athena in <ref n="Soph. Aj. 38"> <title>Ai.</title> 38</ref>, is not Homeric in ref. to a goddess, but is so used by Pindar (fr. 122. 14 <quote xml:lang="grc">δέσποινα Κύπρου</quote>). At Athens it was more especially applied to Persephone ( <title>Legg.</title> 796 B <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡ...παρ’ ἡμῖν κόρη καὶ δέσποινα</quote>).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Ἄρτεμιν_626">. Clytaemnestra calls u<*>n the virgin-goddess to witness her threat, because she regards Electra as guilty <*> unmaidenly conduct (516 ff.). Electra has already appealed to Artemis (563), and again invokes her in 1238.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θράσους_ἀλύξεις_626">, ‘thou shalt not escape (punishment) <emph>for</emph> this boldness’; causal gen.: cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 931"> <title>Ant.</title> 931</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τούτων τοῖσιν ἄγουσιν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">κλαύμαθ’ ὑπάρξει</quote>. <title>Nub.</title> 1239 <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔτοι</quote>... | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμοῦ καταπροΐξει</quote> (on my account). See n. on <ref n="Soph. Ant. 488"> <title>Ant.</title> 488</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ<*> ἀλύξετον</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">μόρου κακίστου</quote>, where the gen. denotes the penalty. And so <quote xml:lang="grc">θράσους</quote> is sometimes taken here: but ‘escape from <emph>boldness</emph>’ surely could not mean, ‘escape from the <emph>penalty of</emph> boldness.’ It is different when (<foreign xml:lang="lat">e.g.</foreign>) <quote xml:lang="grc">φεύγων τόδ’ αἷμα κοινόν</quote> ( <ref n="Aesch. Lib. 1038">Aesch. <title>Ch.</title> 1038</ref>) means, ‘flying from (the stain of) a kinsman's murder.’— </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μόλῃ_626">: <ref n="Soph. El. 313">cp. 313</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὁρᾷς_628">, in reproach; cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 687"> <title>O. T.</title> 687</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 735"> <title>Ant.</title> 735</ref>: <ref n="Eur. Andr. 87">Eur. <title>Andr.</title> 87</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ορᾳς</quote>; <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπαυδᾷς ἐν κακοῖς φίλοισι σοῖς.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκφέρει_628">: <ref n="Thuc. 3. 84">Thuc. 3. 84</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπαιδευσίᾳ ὀργῆς πλεῖστον ἐκφερόμενοι</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παρεῖσα_628"> (cr. n.), though doubtless a mere conjecture, was a very natural one; for <quote xml:lang="grc">μεθιέναι</quote> (prop., ‘to let go’) seldom means, ‘to permit.’ </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐδὲ_630"> goes with <quote xml:lang="grc">θῦσαι</quote>. For the aor. inf., implying, ‘to complete the sacrifice,’ <ref n="Soph. El. 532">cp. 532</ref>, 285.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὑπ_βοῆς_630">, ‘with hushed clamour,’ <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, in silence. In <ref n="Aristoph. Birds 959">Ar. <title>Av.</title> 959</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">εὐφημἴ ἔστω</quote> is the notice that the sacrifice is going to begin: see on <ref n="Soph. Phil. 8">Soph. <title>Ph.</title> 8</ref> ff. For <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑπό</quote>, denoting the accompaniment, <ref n="Soph. El. 711">cp. 711</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Trach. 419"> <title>Tr.</title> 419</ref>(n.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπειδὴ_γ_630">: <quote xml:lang="grc">γε</quote> necessarily emphasises <quote xml:lang="grc">σοί</quote>: thus arranged, the words could not mean, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπειδή γέ σοι</quote>. She means, ‘after giving <emph>you</emph> leave to say what you would, am <title>I</title> to meet with no forbearance?’</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πᾶν_λέγειν_630">: <ref n="Plat. Apol. 39A">Plat. <title>Apol.</title> 39A</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐάν τις τολμᾷ πᾶν ποιεῖν καὶ λέγειν</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κελεύω_632">, not ‘command,’ but ‘exhort,’ ‘beg’; cp. <title>Athen. Resp.</title> 2. 18 <quote xml:lang="grc">κωμωδεῖν...τὸν μὲν δῆμον οὐκ ἐῶσιν, ἰδίᾳ δὲ κελεύουσιν</quote> (‘encourage’ it). For the asyndeton, cp. <ref n="Aesch. PB 937">Aesch. <title>P. V.</title> 937</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">σέβου, προσεύχου, θῶπτε τὸν κρατουντ’ ἀεί.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοὐμὸν_στόμ_632">: <title>O.T.</title> 426 <quote xml:lang="grc">Κρέοντα καὶ τοὐμὸν στόμα</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">προπηλάκιζε</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔπαιρε_632">), and to place upon the altar. There is a resemblance to the scene in the <title>Oedipus Tyrannus</title>, where Iocasta, gravely alarmed, brings a suppliant branch and gifts of frankincense to Apollo <quote xml:lang="grc">Λύκειος</quote>, before the house (<title>O.T.</title> 911—923).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἡ_μοι_632">, said to the <quote xml:lang="grc">πρόσπολος</quote>. For the nom. with art., instead of a voc., cp. Plat. <title>Symp.</title>p. 218 B <quote xml:lang="grc">οἱ δὲ οἰκέται... πύλας...τοῖς ὠσὶν ἐπίθεσθε</quote>. [In <ref n="Aristoph. Ach. 242">Ar. <title>Ach.</title> 242</ref>, <quote xml:lang="grc">πρόϊθ’ ἐς τὸ πρόσθεν ὀλίγον ἡ κανηφόρος</quote>, the correction <quote xml:lang="grc">προΐτω )ς</quote> may be right, though it is not necessary.]</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θύματα_πάγκαρπα_632">, commonly called (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἡ</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">παγκαρπία</quote>, or, for metre's sake, <quote xml:lang="grc">παγκάρπεια</quote>: fr. 366 (from the <quote xml:lang="grc">Μάντεις</quote>, in a list of objects used for sacred rites), <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐνῆν δὲ παγκάρπεια συμμιγὴς ὀλαῖς</quote> (=<quote xml:lang="grc">οὐλαῖς</quote>): Eur. fr. 912. 4 (spoken to Zeus) <quote xml:lang="grc">θυσίαν απορον παγκαρπείας</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">δέξαι πλήρη προχυθεισαν</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λυτηρίους_δειμάτων_635"> (410 n.): <ref n="Soph. El. 447">cp. 447</ref>, 1490. So Iocasta asks Apollo for a <quote xml:lang="grc">λύσιν εὐαγῆ</quote> (<title>O.T.</title> 921).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀνάσχω_635"> has been much suspected: Nauck even pronounces it ‘undoubtedly’ corrupt. Yet cp. <ref n="Eur. El. 592">Eur. <title>El.</title> 592</ref>, where the Chorus are exhorting Electra to pray for a happy result: <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔνεχε χέρας, ἄνεχε λόγον</quote> (‘uplift thy prayer’), | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἵει λιτὰς ἐς θεούς</quote>. There, the phrase is associated with the uplifting of the hands in supplication ( <ref n="Hom. Il. 3.318"> <title>Il.</title> 3. 318</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">θεοῖσι δὲ χεῖρας ἀνέσχον</quote>). The same thought may be present here, though we need not assume it.— Not, ‘that I may lift up (the <quote xml:lang="grc">θύματα</quote>) <emph>as</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">εὐχάς</quote>.’ </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κλύοις_ἂν_637">, a reverent petition; <ref n="Soph. El. 1491">cp. 1491</ref>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προστατήριε_637">: see on <ref n="Soph. Trach. 208"> <title>Tr.</title> 208</ref> f. <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν εὐφαρέτραν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἀπόλλω προστάταν</quote>. Like Artemis ( <ref n="Aesch. Seven 449">Aesch. <title>Th.</title> 449</ref>), Apollo was thus called as ‘defender’ of the house before which his image or altar stood. Here Clytaemnestra is invoking him more especially as <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλεξίκακος</quote>, averter of evil: <ref n="Aristoph. Birds 61">Ar. <title>Av.</title> 61</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἄπολλον ἀποτρόπαιε</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κεκρυμμένην_638">, here=<quote xml:lang="grc">αἰνιγματώδη</quote>, covert in meaning, though spoken aloud,—because she merely alludes to her vision, without describing it, and without naming the persons to whom she refers (644—654). So Theogn. 681 <quote xml:lang="grc">ταῦτά μοι ᾐνίχθω κεκρυμμένα τοῖς ἀγαθοῖσιν</quote>. The ancients associated evil with any prayer which could not be freely uttered. The maxim <quote xml:lang="grc">μετὰ φωνῆς εὔχεσθαι</quote> was ascribed to Pythagoras (Clemens <ref n="Clem. Al. Strom. 4. 26"> <title>Strom.</title> 4. 26</ref>§ 173). Cp. <title>O.C.</title> 131 n. ‘Not every one is ready,’ says <cit> <bibl n="Pers. 2.6">Persius (2. 6),</bibl> <quote xml:lang="lat">murmurque humilesque susurros | Tollere de templis et aperto vivere voto.</quote> </cit> Cp. <ref n="Hor. Ep. 1.6.59">Horace <title>Epp.</title> 1. 16. 59.</ref> </gloss>

data.quote:
murmurque humilesque susurros | Tollere de
                                    templis et aperto vivere voto.

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐ_φίλοις_638">, meaning Electra: for the plur., <ref n="Soph. El. 652">cp. 652</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρὸς_φῶς_640"> with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀναπτύξαι</quote>: cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 580"> <title>Ph.</title> 580</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">δεῖ δ’ αὐτὸν λέγειν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰς φῶς ὃ λέξει</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πολυγλώσσῳ_641">, ‘garrulous,’ agrees with Clytaemnestra's complaints of Electra in 520, 623. <quote xml:lang="grc">παλιγγλώσσῳ</quote>, in the sense of ‘ill-omened’ (<quote xml:lang="grc">βλασφήμῳ</quote> Hesych.), would be no gain.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σπείρῃ_641">: cp. fr. 592 <quote xml:lang="grc">μὴ σπεῖρε πολλοῖς τὸν παρόντα δαίμονα</quote> (‘spread it abroad’).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ματαίαν_641"> seems here to mean ‘rash,’ ‘reckless,’ rather than ‘false.’ If Clytaemnestra openly avowed her fears or hopes, Electra would merely wish to publish them. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὧδ_643"> refers to 638: ‘hear my prayer in this covert form, for I on my part (<quote xml:lang="grc">κἀγὼ</quote>) can use no other.’ Cp. <ref n="Soph. Trach. 554"> <title>Tr.</title> 554</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τῇδ’ ὑμῖν φράσω</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γὰρ_644">, prefatory (32).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δισσῶν_ὀνείρων_644"> (neut., cp. <ref n="Eur. Her. 518">Eur. <title>H. F.</title> 518</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄνειρα</quote>), ‘ambiguous’ dreams, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀμφιβόλων</quote>. The ordinary sense of <quote xml:lang="grc">δισσὰ ὄνειρα</quote> would be (1) ‘two dreams,’ or (2) ‘two sets of dreams.’ But it can mean also, (3) ‘dreams <emph>of two kinds</emph>’: cp. <ref n="Aristot. Rh. 1.15">Arist. <title>Rhet.</title> 1. 15</ref>§ 13 <quote xml:lang="grc">μάρτυρές εἰσι διττοί</quote> (are of two kinds), <quote xml:lang="grc">ο<*></quote> <quote xml:lang="grc">μὲν παλαιοὶ οἱ δὲ πρόσφατοι</quote>. And ‘dreams of two kinds’ are here, ‘dreams which admit of two interpretations,’—<foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, which may be either good or bad. Cp. <ref n="Luc. Alex. 10">Lucian <title>Alex.</title> 10</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">διττούς τινας καὶ ἀμφιβόλους καὶ λοξοὺς χρησμοὺς συγγράφων</quote>. [The <emph>sing.</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">διττόν</quote> often expresses ‘ambiguity,’ as in <ref n="Aristot. Pol. 2.1261b">Arist. <title>Pol.</title> 2. 3</ref>(p. 1261 <emph>b</emph> 20), <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ γὰρ ‘πάντεσ’ διττόν</quote>: but that is different.]</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Λύκεἰ_ἄναξ_644">: Iocasta, too, appeals to Apollo in this quality (<title>O.T.</title> 919). Both as a god of light, and as a destroyer of foes, the <quote xml:lang="grc">Λύκειος</quote> is fitly invoked here: see above on 6. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐσθλά_ἐχθρά_646">. Since the sceptre which put forth the luxuriant growth was that which <title>Aegisthus</title> now carries (420 f.), Clytaemnestra might well regard the dream as so far susceptible of a good meaning. On the other hand, the apparition of Agamemnon (<quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ πανώλης</quote>, 544) must needs disquiet her. And so the import of the vision as a whole seemed doubtful.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔμπαλιν_μέθες_646">, <emph>retro mitte</emph>, ‘allow to <emph>recoil</emph>’ upon them: so <quote xml:lang="grc">στρέφειν ἔμπαλιν</quote> ( <ref n="Eur. Med. 923">Eur. <title>Med.</title> 923</ref>, etc.). <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔμπαλιν</quote> would be weak here if it meant merely, ‘on the contrary.’ </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἴ_τινες_648">, meaning Electra and Orestes. Even before the dream, this thought had haunted her (293 ff.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλ_ὧδε_650"> <quote xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</quote>, <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">δός</quote>: <ref n="Soph. El. 72">cp. 72</ref> n. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀβλαβεῖ_βίῳ_650">: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 168"> <title>Tr.</title> 168</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ζῆν ἀλυπήτῳ βίῳ</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δόμους_651">: as she says to Aegisthus in <ref n="Aesch. Ag. 1672">Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 1672</ref>, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐγὼ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ σὺ θήσομεν κρατοῦντε τῶνδε δωμάτων καλῶς.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀμφέπειν_651">, attend to, here, ‘sway’: <ref n="Pind. O. 1">Pind. <title>O.</title> 1. 12</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">θεμιστεῖον ὃς ἀμφέπει σκᾶπτον</quote>. Cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1118">Soph. <title>Ant.</title> 1118</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀμφέπεις</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἰταλίαν</quote>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φίλοισι_651">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, Aegisthus: for the plur., <ref n="Soph. El. 346">cp. 346</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εὐημεροῦσαν_653"> goes adverbially with <quote xml:lang="grc">ξυνοῦσαν</quote>, ‘consorting in prosperity.’ The acc. is better than the nom., since it includes this condition in her prayer.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_τέκνων_653"> (<quote xml:lang="grc">τούτοις</quote>) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅσων_653"> <foreign xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</foreign>: ‘and with (those) of my children, from whom,’ etc.: <quote xml:lang="grc">τέκνων</quote> is partitive gen., the antecedent to <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅσων</quote> being understood. Cp. <ref n="Plat. Rep. 387E">Plat. <title>Rep.</title> 387E</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀρθῶς ἄρ’ ἂν...θρήνους... γυναιξὶ...ἀποδιδοῖμεν, καὶ...ὅσοι κακοὶ τῶν ἀνδρῶν</quote>, <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> (<quote xml:lang="grc">τούτοις</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅσοι</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐμοὶ_πρόσεστιν_653">, attends upon me; cp. <ref n="Soph. Trach. 453"> <title>Tr.</title> 453</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐλευθέρῳ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ψευδεῖ καλεῖσθαι κὴρ πρόσεστιν οὐ καλή</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πᾶσιν_ἡμῖν_656">: <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, to herself, Aegisthus, and the loyal children (652 ff.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὰ_πάντα_657">,—her wishes concerning her <emph>foes.</emph> When the news from Phocis comes a moment later, it is as if the god had answered the unspoken prayer.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πῶς_εἰδείην_657">, a courteous mode of inquiry; <ref n="Soph. El. 1103">cp. 1103</ref>: <title>O.T.</title> 924 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἆρ’ ἂν παρ’ ὑμῶν, ὦ ξένοι, μάθοιμ’ ὅπου</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ τοῦ τυράννου δώματ’ ἐστὶν Οἰδίπου</quote>; For <quote xml:lang="grc">πῶς ἄν</quote> in wishes, cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 531"> <title>Ph.</title> 531</ref> f. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἦ_καὶ_663">: <ref n="Soph. El. 314">cp. 314</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπεικάζων_κυρῶ_663">: cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 223"> <title>Ph.</title> 223</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τύχοιμ’ ἂν εἰπών</quote>: <title>Suppl.</title> 588 <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ τόδ’ ἂν γένος λέγων</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξ Ἐπάφου κυρήσαις</quote>. (But below, in 1176, <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰπὼν κυρεῖς</quote> has not this sense.)</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρέπ_γὰρ_663">: cp. <title>Suppl.</title> 1056 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς οὐκ ἐπ’ ἀνδρὶ πένθιμος πρέπεις ὁρᾶν</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μάλιστα_πάντων_665">, in reply, as <ref n="Aristoph. Birds 1531">Ar. <title>Av.</title> 1531</ref>, <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Phaedrus 262c"> <title>Phaedr.</title> 262 C</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πάντων γέ που μάλιστα</quote> </cit>. So <quote xml:lang="grc">μάλιστά γε</quote> (<title>O.T.</title> 994), and <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ μάλιστα</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἥδε_πάρα_665">, yonder (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἥδε</quote>) is she for whom you ask: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 384"> <title>Ant.</title> 384</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἥδ’ ἔστ’ ἐκείνη</quote>: <title>O.C.</title> 138 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅδ’ ἐκεῖνος ἐγώ</quote>. <ref n="Soph. El. 1115">Cp. 1115</ref>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὦ_665"> prefixed to </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χαῖρε_665"> marks joyous excitement, as in <ref n="Soph. Aj. 91"> <title>Ai.</title> 91</ref>, <ref n="Eur. El. 1334">Eur. <title>El.</title> 1334</ref>, etc. It occurs also in Comedy, <ref n="Aristoph. Ach. 872">Ar. <title>Ach.</title> 872</ref>, <ref n="Aristoph. Kn. 1254">Ar. <title>Eq.</title> 1254</ref>, <ref n="Aristoph. Lys. 853.">Ar. <title>Lys.</title> 853.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐδεξάμην_ῥηθέν_668">, ‘I welcome the omen’ (of <quote xml:lang="grc">λόγους ἡδεῖς</quote>). For the aor., <ref n="Soph. El. 1322">cp. 1322</ref>, 1479: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1307"> <title>Ant.</title> 1307</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνέπταν</quote>: <title>O.C.</title> 1466 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔπτηξα</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Aj. 693"> <title>Ai.</title> 693</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔφριξα</quote>: and n. on <title>O.T.</title> 337.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰδέναι_σου_668">: <ref n="Soph. El. 565">cp. 565</ref> n. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Φανοτεὺς_670">: 45 n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρᾶγμα_μέγα_670">, lit., ‘in furtherance of an important matter’; <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, for the purpose of acquainting you with important news. Cp. <ref n="Eur. Alc. 1149">Eur. <title>Alc.</title> 1149</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν προκείμενον πόνον</quote> | ...<quote xml:lang="grc">πορσυνῶ μολών</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸ_ποῖον_671">; cp. <title>O.T.</title> 120 n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐν_ξυνθεὶς_673">: cp. Eur. fr. 362. 5 <quote xml:lang="grc">βραχεῖ δὲ μύθῳ πολλὰ συλλαβὼν <*>ρῶ</quote>: <ref n="Thuc. 2. 41">Thuc. 2. 41</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ξυνελών τε λέγω</quote>. It has been supposed that the spectators were intended to perceive a second meaning in <quote xml:lang="grc">ξυνθείς</quote>,—that of ‘composing’ a false story (see n. on 584); but this would surely be unfitting here. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τί_φής_675">...; This lively phrase ( <title>Nub.</title> 1444, <ref n="Aristoph. Lys. 710"> <title>Lys.</title> 710</ref>) serves to show that her excitement is of a joyful kind. It is in a different tone from the <quote xml:lang="grc">πῶς ε<*>πας</quote> which greets similar announcements elsewhere (<title>O.T.</title> 943, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 414"> <title>Ph.</title> 414</ref>).— </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ξεῖνε_675">: cp. <title>O.C.</title> 33 n.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ταύτης_675">, scornful: cp. <title>O.T.</title> 429 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἦ ταῦτα δῆτ’ ἀνεκτὰ πρὸς τούτου κλύειν</quote>; </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νῦν_πάλαι_676">: cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 181"> <title>Ant.</title> 181</ref> n. The variants (cr. n.) seem to have been prompted by ignorance of the fact that <quote xml:lang="grc">πάλαι</quote> could refer to a recent moment.— Cp. <ref n="Soph. Trach. 877"> <title>Tr.</title> 877</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ΧΟ. τέθνηκεν ἡ τάλαινα; ΤΡ. δεύτερον κλύεις</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐδέν_ἔτι_677">: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1217"> <title>Ph.</title> 1217</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔτ’ οὐδέν εἰμι</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὰ_πρᾶσς_678">: as ‘to be over-busy’ is <quote xml:lang="grc">περισσὰ πράσσειν</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. Ant. 68"> <title>Ant.</title> 68</ref>) or <quote xml:lang="grc">δρᾶν</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. Trach. 617"> <title>Tr.</title> 617</ref>). <ref n="Plat. Rep. 433A">Plat. <title>Rep.</title> 433A</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ τὰ ἐαυτοῦ πράττειν καὶ μὴ πολυπραγμονεῖν.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="διόλλυται_678">, historic pres., as <title>O.T.</title> 560 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔρρει</quote>, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1175"> <title>Ant.</title> 1175</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">αἱμάσσεται</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κἀπεμπόμην_680">...</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_φράσω_680">: ‘<emph>as ...so</emph>’<emph>: </emph> <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1112">Ant. 1112</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτός τ’ ἔδησα καὶ παρὼν ἐκλύσομαι</quote> (n.). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γὰρ_681">: 32 n. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρόσχημ_ἀγῶνος_682"> (defining gen.)=<quote xml:lang="grc">πρόσχημ’ ἀγωνιστικόν</quote>, while </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Ἑλλάδος_682"> is possessive gen.: ‘the pride of Greece, (consisting in) a festival.’ Cp. <ref n="Aesch. Lib. 183">Aesch. <title>Ch.</title> 183</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">καρδίας κλυδώνιον</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">χολῆς</quote> (defining gen.): <ref n="Eur. Her. 449">Eur. <title>H. F.</title> 449</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">δακρύων</quote> (defining gen.).. | ..<quote xml:lang="grc">ὄσσων πηγάς</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρόσχημα_682">, anything ‘put forward’ (<ref n="Soph. El. 525">cp. 525</ref>), here means, that which is put forward as an ornament or glory: cp. <ref n="Hdt. 5. 28">Her. 5. 28</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡ Μίλητος..μάλιστα δὴ τότε ἀκμάσασα καὶ δὴ καὶ τῆς Ἰωνίης ἦν πρόσχημα</quote> (‘glory’): Polyb. 3. 15. 3 <quote xml:lang="grc">Καινὴν πόλιν, ἥτις ὡσανεὶ πρόσχημα καὶ βασίλειον</quote> (‘capital’) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἦν Καρχηδονίων ἐν τοῖς κατὰ τὴν Ἰβηρίαν τόποις</quote>: Strabo 10. 450 (of Calydon and Pleuron) <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ δὲ παλαιὸν πρόσχημα τῆς Ἑλλάδος ἦν ταῦτα τὰ κτίσματα</quote>: <ref n="Plut. Alex. 17">Plut. <title>Alex.</title> 17</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">Σάρδεις, τὸ πρό‐</quote> <quote xml:lang="grc">σχημα τῆς ἐπὶ θαλάττῃ τῶν βαρβάρων ἡγεμονίας</quote>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Δελφ_ἄθλων_682">: <ref n="Soph. El. 48">cp. 48</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὀρθίων_683">: cp. <title>I. A.</title> 94 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀρθίῳ κηρύγματι</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">Ταλθύβιον εἶπον πάντ’ ἀφιέναι στρατόν</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δρόμον_κρίσις_683">. There were three classes of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀγῶνες</quote> at the Pythian festival; (1) the <quote xml:lang="grc">μουσικοί</quote>, in music, poetry, etc., which, as Plut. (<title>Quaest. Conv.</title> 2. 4) shows, came first; (2) the <quote xml:lang="grc">γυμνικοί</quote>, footraces, and trials of skilled strength; and (3) the <quote xml:lang="grc">ἱππικοί</quote>, chariot-races and horse-races. Little is known as to the details. But this passage shows that at Delphi, as in the Greek games generally, (<emph>a</emph>) the <quote xml:lang="grc">γυμνικοὶ ἀγῶνες</quote>, or a large part of them, preceded the <quote xml:lang="grc">ἱππικοί</quote>: and (<emph>b</emph>) of the <quote xml:lang="grc">γυμνικοί</quote>, the foot-races came first. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰσῆλθε_685">, came into the lists (like <quote xml:lang="grc">κατέβη</quote>): <ref n="Soph. El. 700">cp. 700</ref>: <ref n="Dem. 18.319">Dem. or. 18 § 319</ref> (of an Olympian victor) <quote xml:lang="grc">τῶν εἰσελθόντων πρὸς αὐτὸν ἄριστα ἐμάχετο</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἰσώσας_τἀφέσει_686">: ‘having made the end of his course even with the starting-place,’—<foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, having run back again to that place in the stadion from which he started. Musgrave's conjecture, <quote xml:lang="grc">τἀφέσει</quote> for </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῇ_φύσει_686">, affords the best solution here. If it be right, then the foot-race meant is either (<emph>a</emph>) the <quote xml:lang="grc">δίαυλος</quote>, in which the competitor rounded the <quote xml:lang="grc">καμπτὴρ</quote> at the further end of the course, and returned to the starting-place ( <ref n="Aesch. Ag. 344">Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 344</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κάμψαι διαύλου θάτερον κῶλον πάλιν</quote>): or better, (<emph>b</emph>) the <quote xml:lang="grc">δόλιχος</quote>, in which he performed that double course several times. (The number of stadia in the <quote xml:lang="grc">δόλιχος</quote> was usually, if not always, an <emph>even</emph> number: cp. art. ‘Stadium’ in Smith's <title>Dict. Ant.</title> vol. II., 2nd ed. The schol. on <ref n="Aristoph. Birds 292">Ar. <title>Av.</title> 292</ref> asserts, indeed, <quote xml:lang="grc">δολιχοδρόμοι δὲ οἱ ἕπτα</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">δρόμους</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">τρέχοντες</quote>. Cp. IX.Bacch. ) The race in which the course was traversed only once (properly called <quote xml:lang="grc">στάδιον</quote>) cannot be intended, since the verse could not mean, ‘having finished the race <emph>as swiftly as</emph> he began it’: still less, ‘having made the end seem <emph>simultaneous with</emph> the start,’—by his marvellous speed. [This last was Musgrave's own view, which he sought to support from Antipater Sidonius, <ref> <title>Epigr.</title> 39</ref>: <quote xml:lang="grc">ἢ γὰρ ἐφ’ ὑσπλήγγων ἢ τέρματος εἶδέ τις ἄκρου</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἠΐθεον, μέσσῳ δ’ οὔποτ’ ἐνὶ σταδίῳ</quote>.]</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῇ_φύσει_686_2">, and other attempts to correct it, are discussed in the Appendix. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νίκης_γέρας_687">. In the Pythian games the prize was a wreath of laurel; hence Paus. says <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνείλετο τὴν δάφνην</quote> of a Pythian victor (10. 7. 7), as <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν κότινον ἀνείλετο</quote> of a victor at Olympia (6. 13. 3). The laurel for this purpose was brought from the vale of Tempè, the bearer being a <quote xml:lang="grc">παῖς ἀμφιθαλής</quote> (<foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, one whose parents were both living), schol. <title>Argum.</title> <title>Pyth.</title> He was attended by a flute-player, <title>De Music.</title> c. 14 <quote xml:lang="grc">τῷ κατακομίζοντι παιδὶ τὴν Τεμπικὴν δάφνην εἰς Λελφοὺς παρομαρτεῖ αὐλητής</quote>. A palm-branch was also given, the palm (<quote xml:lang="grc">φοῖνιξ</quote>) being sacred to Apollo: <title>Quaest. Conv.</title> 8. 4. 4 <quote xml:lang="grc">κἀκεῖ</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">Πυθοῖ</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">πρῶτον ἐπὶ τιμῇ τοῦ θεοῦ δάφνῃ καὶ φοίνικι τοὺς νικῶντας ἐκόσμησαν, ἅτε δὴ καὶ τῷ θεῷ...φοίνικας ἀνατιθέντες</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χὥπως_μὲν_688"> <quote xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</quote>: ‘and, to speak briefly, where there is much that might be told (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν πολλοῖσι</quote>), I do not know the deeds and triumphs of such a man,’—<foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, I have never seen, or heard of, his equal. Then, in 690, <quote xml:lang="grc"> <emph>ἓν δ’ ἴσθ’</emph> κ.τ.λ., δέ</quote> answers to the <quote xml:lang="grc">μέν</quote> in 688: <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>: ‘The <emph>briefest</emph> way, indeed (<quote xml:lang="grc">μέν</quote>), of describing his exploits, is simply to say that they were matchless. But (<quote xml:lang="grc">δέ</quote>) one particular you must be told,—viz., that he won in <emph>all</emph> the contests.’—For <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν πολλοῖσι</quote>, cp. <ref n="Pind. P. 9">Pind. <title>P.</title> 9. 83</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">βαιὰ δ’ ἐν μακροῖσι ποικίλλειν, ἀκοὰ σοφοις</quote>: ‘to adorn a few things, where the choice is large, best pleases a poet's ear’: <ref n="Thuc. 8. 53">Thuc. 8. 53</ref>§ 1 <quote xml:lang="grc">λόγους ἐποιοῦντο ἐν τῷ δήμῳ κεφαλαιοῦντες ἐκ πολλῶν</quote>.—For <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅπως</quote>, without <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄν</quote>, <ref n="Soph. El. 390">cp. 390</ref>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅσων_690">, by attraction for <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅσους.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰσεκήρυξαν_690">: the only other instance of this compound in a classical writer is <ref n="Aristoph. Ach. 135">Ar. <title>Ach.</title> 135</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἕτερος ἀλαζὼν οὗτος ἐσκηρύττεται</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βραβῆς_690">, the form of the nom. plur. for <quote xml:lang="grc">ευ</quote> stems which prevails in Attic inscrr. down to the middle of the 4th cent. B.C. (Meisterhans, <title>Gramm. Att. Inschr.</title> § 31.) —These are the judges who award the prizes and regulate the contests: <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰσεκήρυξαν</quote>, because they ordered the <quote xml:lang="grc">κῆρυξ</quote> to do so (cp. n. on 693 ff.). The <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀγωνοθέται</quote> of the Pythian games were the Amphictyons,—the games being held in April, in the Delphic month <quote xml:lang="grc">Βουκάτιος</quote> (=the Attic Munychion), when the <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐαρινὴ πυλαία</quote> took place at Delphi (cp. Boeckh <title>C. I. G.</title> 1688: Aeschin. or. 3 § 254). The Amphictyons appointed <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπιμεληταί</quote>, like the Olympic <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἑλλανοδίκαι</quote>, to superintend the festival ( <title>Quaest. Conv.</title> 7. 5. 1), and the <quote xml:lang="grc">βραβῆς</quote> here are these, or their subordinates. Cp. <title>Legg.</title> 949 A <quote xml:lang="grc">γυμνικῶν τε καὶ ἱππικῶν ἄθλων ἐπιστάτας τε καὶ βραβέας</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δρομων_διαύλων_691">. For the attempts to amend this v., see Appendix. The criticism of vv. 690—692 turns upon these points. (1) The word <quote xml:lang="grc">διαύλων</quote> cannot be right. At each festival there was only one <quote xml:lang="grc">δίαυλος</quote>. The plur. cannot be defended on the ground that a winner of the <quote xml:lang="grc">δίαυλος</quote> might have to run twice,— viz., first in one of several groups, and then in the final heat ( <ref n="Paus. 6. 13. 3">Paus. 6. 13. 3</ref>). (2) If the sing. <quote xml:lang="grc">διαύλου</quote> be read, it is still difficult to complete the verse in any probable manner. (3) Omitting <quote xml:lang="grc">διαύλων</quote>, we might expand <quote xml:lang="grc">πένταθλ’ ἃ νομίζεται</quote> on the hypothesis that the intrusion of <quote xml:lang="grc">διαύλων</quote> had caused the mischief: <foreign xml:lang="lat">e.g.</foreign>, <quote xml:lang="grc">δρόμων, πόνων τε πεμπὰς ἣ νομίζεται</quote>. Pindar often uses <quote xml:lang="grc">πόνος</quote> as=<quote xml:lang="grc">ἆθλος</quote>. (4) But it seems more probable that v. 691 is an interpolation, arising perhaps from a marginal gloss, and intended to explain a general phrase in the text. Thus, if the poet had written simply, <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅσων γὰρ εἰσεκήρυξαν βραβῆς</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄθλων ἐνεγκὼν</quote> etc., then the reference to the diaulos and the pentathlon in v. 691 might have been prompted by a wish to define <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄθλων</quote>. And the interpolation would itself account for the change of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄθλων</quote> in 692 to <quote xml:lang="grc">τούτων</quote>. (Some, indeed, think it enough to delete 691, leaving <quote xml:lang="grc">τούτων</quote>: but the neut. plur. of the pron. seems too vague here.) (5) Nauck's view, that the poet wrote, <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅσων γὰρ εἰσεκήρυξαν βραβῆς</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">δρόμων ἐνεγκὼν</quote> etc., leaves two difficulties. (<emph>a</emph>) There would then have been less excuse for interpolating an allusion to the pentathlon. (<emph>b</emph>) The tone of vv. 688 f. would lead us to suppose that the <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔργα καὶ κράτη</quote> of Orestes had not been confined to footraces, but had included some feats in the other branches of the <quote xml:lang="grc">γυμνικοὶ ἀγῶνες</quote>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐνεγκὼν_692">, in the sense of the midd.: cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 590"> <title>O. T.</title> 590</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">φέρω</quote>, n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τἀπινίκια_692"> here=<quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ νικητήρια</quote> ( <title>Legg.</title> 833 C). The word usu. means either (1) songs of victory, or (2) with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἱερά</quote> understood, a sacrifice in honour of it. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὠλβίζετ_693">: the impf. refers to the series of victories; </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀνακαλ_693">.=<quote xml:lang="grc">ὅτε ἀνεκαλεῖτο</quote>. The official proclamation would be merely, <quote xml:lang="grc">Ὀρέστης Ἀγαμέμνονος Ἀργεῖος</quote>. Cp. <title>Ol.</title> 5. 8 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὃν πατέρ’ Ἄκρων’ ἐκάρυξε</quote> (the victor <emph>caused</emph> to be proclaimed), <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ τὰν νέοικον ἕδραν</quote> (Camarina). <ref n="Dem. 18.319">Dem. or. 18 § 319</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">νικῶν ἀνηγορεύετο</quote>. <title>Plut.</title> 585 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνεκήρυττεν...τοὺς νικῶντας</quote>. <title>Pyth.</title> I. 32 <quote xml:lang="grc">κᾶρυξ ἀνέειπέ νιν</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Ἀγαμέμνονος_693"> is irregularly placed between <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦ</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀγείραντος</quote>: <ref n="Soph. El. 183">cp. 183</ref> (<quote xml:lang="grc">θεὸς</quote>): <title>O.T.</title> 1245 <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν ἤδη Λά<*>ον πάλαι νεκρόν.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀγείραντος_693">, as <ref n="Thuc. 1.9">Thuc. I. 9</ref> (of Agam.) <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν στόλον ἀγεῖραι</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_τοιαῦθ_696">, a form of summary used in transitions: <ref n="Plat. Theaet. 173B">Plat. <title>Theaet.</title> 173B</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ οὗτοι μὲν δὴ τοιοῦτοι.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βλάπτῃ_696">, disable, impede one in his career: <ref n="Soph. Aj. 456"> <title>Ai.</title> 456</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ δέ τις θεῶν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">βλάπτοι, φύγοι τἂν χὡ κακὸς τὸν κρείσσονα</quote>. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1103"> <title>Ant.</title> 1103</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">συντέμνουσι γὰρ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">θεῶν ποδώκεις τοὺς κακόφρονας βλάβαι</quote> (n.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἰσχύων_696">, without <quote xml:lang="grc">τις</quote>,—a rare usage, which has, however, epic precedent; <ref n="Hom. Il. 13.287"> <title>Il.</title> 13. 287</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδέ κεν ἔνθα τεόν κε μένος καὶ χεῖρας ὄνοιτο</quote>: 22. 199 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς δ’ ἐν ὀνείρῳ οὐ δύναται φεύγοντα διώκειν</quote>: <ref n="Hom. Od. 5.400"> <title>Od.</title> 5. 400</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ ὅτε τόσσον ἀπῆν ὅσσον τε γέγωνε βοήσας</quote>. So <title>O.T.</title> 517 <quote xml:lang="grc">φέρον</quote>, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 687"> <title>Ant.</title> 687</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">καλῶς ἔχον</quote>, without <quote xml:lang="grc">τι</quote>. <ref n="Soph. El. 1323">Cp. 1323</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄλλης_ἡμέρας_698">, ‘on another day’; not, ‘on the <emph>next</emph> day,’ which would require <quote xml:lang="grc">τῆς</quote>. Cp. <ref n="Xen. Anab. 3. 4. 1">Xen. <title>An.</title> 3. 4. 1</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μείναντες δὲ ταύτην τὴν ἡμέραν τῇ ἄλλῃ ἐπορεύοντο</quote>. (In <title>Criton</title> 44 A <quote xml:lang="grc">τῆς ἐπιούσης</quote>=‘tomorrow,’ and <quote xml:lang="grc">τῆς ἑτέρας</quote>, ‘the day after tomorrow.’)</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἱππικῶν_698">, prob. neut.: cp. <title>H.</title> 7. 4. 29 <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ δρομικὰ τοῦ πεντάθλου</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἡλίου_τέλλοντος_699">. The passage of Xen. just cited, referring to the Olympic festival of 364 B.C., shows that the chariot-races then preceded the pentathlon, on the same day,—and must therefore have begun in the early morning.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Ἀχαιός_701">. If Sophocles were more careful in regard to details of the heroic age, ‘Achaean,’ as designating a man from a particular district, would naturally point to Phthiotis in southern Thessaly, where the subjects of Achilles ‘were called Myrmidons, Hellenes, and <title>Achaeans</title>’<*> <ref n="Hom. Il. 2.684"> <title>Il.</title> 2. 684</ref>). Herodotus can still speal of <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἀχαιΐη</quote> (7. 173) and <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἀχαιοί</quote> (7. 197) in this sense,—meaning those whom in 7. 132 he calls <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἀχαιοὶ οἱ Φθιῶται</quote>. But a poet who makes Orestes compete at the Pythian games against a Barcaean (727) was just as likely to use <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἀχαιὸς</quote> in the local sense which it acquired after the Dorian conquest of Peloponnesus,—a man from ‘Achaia’ on the Corinthian Gulf. If this is (as I suppose) what he really meant, then we see how his mind worked in making this list; we have (1) two Peloponnesians; (2) then he goes south to Cyrenaica for two more competitors; and (3) the remaining six come from Northern Greece. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Λίβυες_702">, in a geographical sense only: none but Hellenes could compete. These men are from the Greek Libya,— <quote xml:lang="grc">Κυρηναία</quote> or <quote xml:lang="grc">Κυρηναϊκή</quote>,—that wide and high table land which projects into the Mediterranean, 200 miles south of Peloponnesus, between the Great Syrtis on the west and the steppes of Marmarica on the east. In the seventh century B.C. this country was colonised by Dorians from Peloponnesus and the islands. Cyrene (whose name survives in <title>Grennah</title>) was founded near the coast in 631 B.C. by Battus and his followers from Thera. Barca (cp. v. 727), about 52 miles S.W. of it, and more inland, was founded by Greek seceders from Cyrene, with a mixture of native Libyans, about 550 B.C. ( <ref n="Hdt. 4. 160">Her. 4. 160</ref>). It was taken by the Persians in 510 B.C. (<foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 201), and was thenceforth of small importance. Under the Ptolemies, its old sea-port, then named Ptolemais (and still, in its ruins, <title>Dolmêta</title>), became a member of the Pentapolis. All Cyrenaica has been known since the middle ages as <title>Barca</title>, now a province of Tripoli.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ζυγωτῶν_702"> is merely a general epithet. In a <quote xml:lang="grc">τέθριππον</quote>, only the two middle horses were under the yoke (721 f.).— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπιστάται_702">, ‘masters,’ ‘controllers,’ of chariots; as a warrior is <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅπλων ἐπιστάτης</quote> ( <ref n="Aesch. Pers. 379">Aesch. <title>Pers.</title> 379</ref>), and an oarsman <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐρετμῶν ἐπιστάτης</quote> ( <title>Helen.</title> 1267). The Cyrenaeans were famous both as horsebreeders and as charioteers: <quote xml:lang="grc">φασὶ δὲ αὐτοὺς καὶ πρώτους ἅρμα ζεῦξαι, διδαχθέντας ὑπὸ Ποσειδῶνος· τὸ δὲ ἡνιοχεῖν ὑπὸ Ἀθηνᾶς</quote> (Hesych. <emph>s.v.</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">Βαρκαίοις ὄχοις</quote>). <ref n="Hdt. 4. 189">Her. 4. 189</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τέσσερας ἵππους συζευγνύναι παρὰ Λιβύων οἱ Ἕλληνες μεμαθήκασι</quote>. Pindar's fourth and fifth Pythian odes celebrate a victory in the chariot-race (466 B.C.) won by Arcesilas IV., <quote xml:lang="grc">εὐίππου βασιλῆι Κυράνας</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κἀκεῖνος_703">, Orestes, who was living with Strophius, king of Crisa (180), close to the scene of the games.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐν_τούτοισι_703">. Nauck pronounces <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν</quote> ‘impossible,’ and writes </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπὶ_703"> (‘in addition to’). There would be force in this objection, if the poet were enumerating the competitors as <emph>drawn up in line.</emph> But we cannot assume that the order of mention here is identical with the order fixed by lot for the start (709 f.); indeed, the chances would have been against the two <quote xml:lang="grc">Λίβυες</quote> being next each other. The Homeric narrative of the chariot-race illustrates this; for the order in which the five competitors are first enumerated ( <ref n="Hom. Il. 23.288"> <title>Il.</title> 23. 288</ref>—350) differs from that in which they are afterwards placed by lot for the start (<foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 352—357). Therefore <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν τούτοισι</quote> may well mean, ‘<emph>among</emph> these,’— the competitors being here imagined as a group.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Θεσσαλὰς_ἵππους_703">. Thessaly owed its fame as a horse-breeding country to (1) its level plains, the best in Greece for that purpose; and (2) the reliance of the wealthy oligarchies upon cavalry (cp. <ref n="Aristot. Pol. 4.1289b">Arist. <title>Pol.</title> 4. 3. 3</ref>). An oracle <foreign xml:lang="lat">ap.</foreign> schol. <ref n="Hom. Il. 2.761"> <title>Il.</title> 2. 761</ref> recommends <quote xml:lang="grc">ἵππον Θεσσαλικὴν Λακεδαιμονίαν τε γυναῖκα</quote>. Helen, says Theocritus, is an ornament to her company, such as <quote xml:lang="grc">ἢ κάπῳ κυπάρισσος, ἢ ἅρματι Θεσσαλὸς ἵππος</quote> (<ref n="Theoc. 18"> <title>Idyll.</title> 18. 30</ref>). <cit> <bibl n="Luc. 6.396">Lucan 6. 396</bibl> <quote xml:lang="lat">Primus ab aequorea percussis cuspide saxis | Thessalicus sonipes, bellis feralibus omen, | Exsiluit.</quote> </cit> The Thessalian cavalry was reputed <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀρίστη τῶν ἐν Ἕλλησι</quote> ( <ref n="Hdt. 7. 196">Her. 7. 196</ref>). Thessalian skill in riding and driving was proverbial (<ref n="Plat. Hipp. Maj. 284a">Plat. <title>Hipp. ma.</title> 284 A</ref>: <ref n="Isoc. 15.298">Isocr. or. 15. § 298</ref>). </gloss>

data.quote:
Primus ab aequorea percussis cuspide saxis |
                                    Thessalicus sonipes, bellis feralibus omen, | Exsiluit.

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ξανθαῖσι_705">. This epithet for the <title>Aetolian</title> mares may have been suggested to the poet by a Homeric reminiscence. It is by a chestnut horse (<quote xml:lang="grc">φοῖνιξ</quote>, <ref n="Hom. Il. 23.454"> <title>Il.</title> 23. 454</ref>) that Idomeneus recognises from afar the team of Diomedes, <quote xml:lang="grc">Αἰτωλὸς γενεήν</quote> (<foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 471).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πώλοις_705">. Throughout this passage, <quote xml:lang="grc">πῶλος</quote> is a mere synonym for <quote xml:lang="grc">ἵππος</quote> (725, 735, 738, 748). Yet special races for <quote xml:lang="grc">πῶλοι</quote>, as distinct from <quote xml:lang="grc">ἵπποι τέλειοι</quote>, had been established in the Pythian games before 500 B.C. ( <ref n="Paus. 10. 7. 7">Paus. 10. 7. 7</ref>). At Olympia, however, no special race for <quote xml:lang="grc">πῶλοι</quote> existed before 384 B.C. (<emph>id.</emph> 5. 8. 10).—The Aetolian, like Orestes, drives <emph>mares</emph>, which were most generally used. In the Homeric chariot-race, however ( <ref n="Hom. Il. 23"> <title>Il.</title> 23</ref>), there are three teams of horses against two of mares; and the horses win the first and second places.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Μάγνης_705">: from Magnesia, that mountainous tract which stretches southward along the east coast of Thessaly from the mouth of the Peneius to the Gulf of Pagasae. Here the name serves to recall the legends of the knightly Minyae. In <ref n="Pind. P. 4">Pind. <title>P.</title> 4. 117</ref>, Jason at Iolcus says, <quote xml:lang="grc">λευκίππων δὲ δόμους πατέρων...φράσσατέ μοι</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λεύκιππος_706">, or <quote xml:lang="grc">λευκόπωλος</quote>, is often an epithet of deities or heroes, as in <ref n="Pind. O. 6">Pind. <title>O.</title> 6. 95</ref>(Persephonè); <ref n="Pind. P. 1">Pind. <title>P.</title> 1. 66</ref>(the Dioscuri); Ibycus fr. 16 (the sons of Poseidon, the <quote xml:lang="grc">Μολίονε</quote> of <ref n="Hom. Il. 11.709"> <title>Il.</title> 11. 709</ref>), etc. And white horses are especially praised for swiftness: <ref n="Hom. Il. 10.437"> <title>Il.</title> 10. 437</ref>(the Thracian horses of Rhesus) <quote xml:lang="grc">λευκότεροι χιόνος, θείειν δ’ ἀνέμοισιν ὁμοῖοι</quote>: like those of Turnus ( <ref n="Verg. A. 12. 84"> <title>Aen.</title> 12. 84</ref>), <emph>qui candore nives anteirent</emph>, <emph>cursibus auras.</emph> Hence the proverbs; <cit> <bibl n="Pl. As. 2.2">Plaut. <title>Asin.</title> 2. 2. 13</bibl> <quote xml:lang="lat">quadrigis albis</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Hor. S. 1.7">Hor. <title>Sat.</title> 1. 7. 8</bibl> <quote xml:lang="lat">equis praecurreret albis.</quote> </cit> Yet <ref n="Verg. G. 3.82">Verg. <title>G.</title> 3. 82</ref> says <quote xml:lang="lat">color deterrimus albo.</quote> It might be surmised that the reputation of white horses for <emph>speed</emph> rested less upon fact than upon their poetical association with divine or heroic persons.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Αἰνιὰν_706">. The <quote xml:lang="grc">Αἰνιᾶνες</quote> (Ion. <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἐνιῆνες</quote>, <ref n="Hom. Il. 2.749"> <title>Il.</title> 2. 749</ref>, where the <quote xml:lang="grc">ι</quote> is short, and <ref n="Hdt. 7. 132">Her. 7. 132</ref>) were a tribe in the south of Thessaly, dwelling in the upper valley of the Spercheius, among the highlands of Oeta. The Malians were their neighbours on the south-east, and the Dolopes on the north-west; on the north, they touched the ancient Phthiotis. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῶν_θεοδμήτων_707">, an epithet bestowed on Athens by Eur. also (<title>I. T.</title> 1449, <ref n="Eur. Hipp. 974"> <title>Hipp.</title> 974</ref>), recalls the legends of Cecrops, Athena, Poseidon; as in <ref n="Hom. Il. 8.519"> <title>Il.</title> 8. 519</ref> the same epithet is given to the Trojan <quote xml:lang="grc">πύργοι</quote> built by Apollo, and in <ref n="Pind. O. 6">Pind. <title>O.</title> 6. 59</ref> to Delos, the island which Zeus called forth from the deep. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄλλος_708">, ‘besides’: <ref n="Aesch. Seven 424">Aesch. <title>Th.</title> 424</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">γἱγας ὅδ’ ἄλλος</quote>: cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 38">Soph. <title>Ph.</title> 38</ref> n.— </gloss>
