<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκπληρῶν_708">, making up that number: <ref n="Hdt. 7. 186">Her. 7. 186</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὗτοι...ἐκπληροῦσι τὰς ἴσας μυριάδας ἐκείνοισι</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="στάντες_709">: so at the start of the Homeric chariot-race, <ref n="Hom. Il. 23.358"> <title>Il.</title> 23. 358</ref>, <quote xml:lang="grc">στὰν δὲ μεταστοιχί</quote> (‘in line’).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅθ_αὐτοὺς_709">. The traditional </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅθ_709"> can hardly be right: we should perh. read </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἵν_709">. The use of <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅθι</quote> in Tragedy is elsewhere confined to lyric passages, and even in these the <quote xml:lang="grc">ι</quote> is never elided; <title>O.C.</title> 1044; <ref n="Eur. Hipp. 125">Eur. <title>Hipp.</title> 125</ref>, <ref n="Eur. Hipp. 1127">Eur. Hipp. 1127</ref>; <ref n="Eur. IA 548">Eur. <title>I.A.</title> 548</ref>, <ref n="Eur. IA 1285">Eur. I.A. 1285</ref>, <ref n="Eur. IA 1294">Eur. I.A. 1294</ref>: in <title>Suppl.</title> 124 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁπόθι</quote>. The elision occurs, indeed, in <ref n="Hom. Il. 2.572"> <title>Il.</title> 2. 572</ref>, <ref n="Hom. Od. 4.426"> <title>Od.</title> 4. 426</ref>; but would be foreign to Attic practice. (Cp. <title>O. C.</title>, p. 289, append. on 1436.) The simplest remedy would be </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅτ_709">, which is not necessarily excluded by <quote xml:lang="grc">στάντες</quote>: the sense might be, ‘having taken their stations, <emph>when</emph> these had been assigned.’ But we should rather expect a word meaning ‘where’; and if the local sense of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἵν’</quote> had been explained by a marginal gloss <quote xml:lang="grc">οὗ</quote> or <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅθι</quote>, either of these might have given rise to <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅθ’</quote>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βραβῆς_709">: <ref n="Soph. El. 690">cp. 690</ref>. The phrase </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἱ_τεταγμένοι_709"> is illustrated by <ref n="Paus. 5. 9. 5.">Paus. 5. 9. 5.</ref>At Olympia three Hellanodicae had charge of the <quote xml:lang="grc">ἵππων δρόμος</quote>, and three of the pentathlon; while the rest supervised the other contests. The total number of these judges was ten, acc. to Pausanias, from Ol. 25 to Ol. 103 (680—368 B.C.), when it became twelve.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔπηλαν_709">. Each competitor casts his <quote xml:lang="grc">κλῆρος</quote>, or lot,—usu. a potsherd (<quote xml:lang="grc">ὄστρακον</quote>), or a small stone,—into a helmet, which one of the <quote xml:lang="grc">βραβῆς</quote> shakes, and the places are settled by the order in which the lots jump out. <ref n="Hom. Il. 23.352"> <title>Il.</title> 23. 352</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἂν δ’ ἔβαν ἐς δίφρους, ἐν δὲ κλήρους ἐβάλοντο·</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">πάλλ’ Ἀχιλεύς, ἐκ δὲ κλῆρος θόρε Νεστορίδαο</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χαλκῆς_711">, as <quote xml:lang="grc">χαλκοστόμου</quote> in <ref n="Soph. Aj. 17."> <title>Ai.</title> 17.</ref> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὑπαὶ_711"> (1419) is admitted in trimeters by Aesch. and Soph. , <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1035">Soph. <title>Ant.</title> 1035</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἱ_δ_711">, though referring to the subject of <quote xml:lang="grc">ᾖξαν</quote>: <ref n="Soph. El. 448">cp. 448</ref> (<quote xml:lang="grc">σὺ δὲ</quote>) n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὁμοκλήσαντες_712">. Sophocles has imitated the Homeric description of the chariots setting off, <ref n="Hom. Il. 23.362"> <title>Il.</title> 23. 362</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἱ δ’ ἅμα πάντες ἐφ’ ἵπποιιν μάστιγας ἄειραν</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">πέπληγόν θ’ ἱμᾶσιν ὁμόκλησάν τ’ ἐπέεσσιν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐσσυμένως·...ὑπὸ δὲ στέρνοισι κονίη</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἵστατ’ ἀειρομένη κ.τ.λ.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐν_δὲ_713">, tmesis, as in <ref n="Soph. Ant. 420"> <title>Ant.</title> 420</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν δ’ ἐμεστώθη.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κροτητῶν_713"> is usually explained, and perhaps rightly, as ‘rattling’ (or more strictly, ‘rattled’ along by the horses): cp. <ref n="Hom. Il. 15.453"> <title>Il.</title> 15. 453</ref>(<quote xml:lang="grc">ἵπποι</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">κείν’ ὄχεα κροτέοντες</quote>: 11. 160 <quote xml:lang="grc">κείν’ ὄχεα κροτάλιζον</quote>. Sophocles has used <quote xml:lang="grc">κροτητὸς</quote> with ref. to sound in fr. 220 <quote xml:lang="grc">κροτητὰ πηκτίδων μέλη</quote>, ‘songs resounding from the harp,’ under the touch of the <emph>plectrum.</emph> <ref n="Aristoph. Kn. 552">Ar. <title>Eq.</title> 552</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">χαλκοκρότων ἵππων κτύπος</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φορεῖθ_715">: as to the omission of the syllabic augment in <quote xml:lang="grc">ῥήσεις</quote>, see on <ref n="Soph. OT 1249."> <title>O. T.</title> 1249.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κέντρων_716">. There is no allusion to a <emph>whip</emph> in this narrative. Leaf on <ref n="Hom. Il. 23.387"> <title>Il.</title> 23. 387</ref>, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄνευ κέντροιο θέοντες</quote>, remarks that the <quote xml:lang="grc">κέντρον</quote> mentioned there is identical with the <quote xml:lang="grc">μάστιγα φαεινὴν</quote> v. 384, and refers to the Burgon amphora in the British Museum, where the chariot-driver wields a long pliant rod, with two points like arrow-heads at the end.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡς_ὑπερβάλοι_716">: ‘<emph>in order that</emph> each of them might pass the wheels...(of the others).’ For </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τις_716"> as=<quote xml:lang="grc">ἕκαστός τις</quote>, cp. <ref n="Hom. Il. 2.382"> <title>Il.</title> 2. 382</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">εὖ μέν τις δόρυ θηξάσθω</quote>: <ref n="Thuc. 1. 40">Thuc. 1. 40</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοὺς...ξυμμάχους αὐτόν τινα κολάζειν</quote>.— I formerly understood: ‘<emph>whenever</emph> anyone passed their wheels’ (<quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτῶν</quote> with <quote xml:lang="grc">χνόας</quote>). Cp. <ref n="Hdt. 7. 119">Her. 7. 119</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς δὲ δείπνου γίνοιτο ὥρη</quote>: and so <emph>id.</emph> 1. 17 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς...ἀπίκοιτο</quote>. But this would imply that no driver used his goad until a rival was about to pass him; whereas we require rather a picture of the eagerness which each man felt to outstrip the rest.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χνόας_716">, the box at the centre of a wheel, in which the axle turns; the ‘nave’ (akin to navel), or ‘hub’: cp. <ref n="Aesch. Seven 153">Aesch. <title>Th.</title> 153</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀξόνων...χνόαι</quote>. The Homeric word is <quote xml:lang="grc">πλήμνη</quote>: others are <quote xml:lang="grc">σῦριγξ</quote> (721 n.), and <quote xml:lang="grc">χοῖνιξ</quote> (n. on 505).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φρυάγμαθ_ἱππικά_716">=<quote xml:lang="grc">ἵππους φρυασσομένους</quote>. This is the moment after the start, and no one has yet a clear lead. Each driver seeks, first, to bring his own wheels in front of his rival's wheels; next, to bring them past the heads of his rival's horses. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὁμοῦ_νῶτα_718"> <quote xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</quote> The driver who has <emph>just passed</emph> his rival feels the breath of the pursuing horses on his back: cp. <ref n="Hom. Il. 23.380"> <title>Il.</title> 23. 380</ref>(Eumelus, in the chariot-race, closely pursued by Diomedes) <quote xml:lang="grc">πνοιῇ δ’ Εὐμήλοιο μετάφρενον εὐρέε τ’ ὤμω</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">θέρμετ’</quote>. But the driver who is being <emph>overtaken</emph> sees his wheels flecked with foam from the mouths of his rival's horses.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τροχῶν_βάσεις_718">=<quote xml:lang="grc">τροχοὺς βαίνοντας.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰσέβαλλον_718">, intrans. (as when it means ‘to invade’), ‘kept rushing in.’ We cannot supply <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀφρόν</quote>, as object, from <quote xml:lang="grc">ἤφριζον</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὑπ_αὐτὴν_720">, <emph>close</emph> under it: cp. <ref n="Hom. Il. 13.614"> <title>Il.</title> 13. 614</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἦ τοι ὁ μὲν κόρυθος φάλον ἤλασεν ἱπποδασείης</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄκρον ὑπὸ λόφον αὐτόν</quote>: <ref n="Xen. Anab. 4. 1. 2">Xen. <title>An.</title> 4. 1. 2</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ...ὄρη...ὑπὲρ αὐτοῦ τοῦ ποταμοῦ ἐκρέματο.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐσχάτην_στήλην_720">, the stone pillar (<quote xml:lang="grc">νύσσα, καμπτήρ</quote>) which marked the turning-point <emph>at each end</emph> of the course. A vase-painting in Panofka, <title>Bilder Antiken Lebens</title>, pl. iii, no. 10, shows a chariot-race in which the <quote xml:lang="grc">νύσσα</quote> is simply a pillar, with a fillet twined round it. <ref n="Plat. Sym. 4. 6">Plat. <title>Symp.</title> 4. 6</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἁρματηλατοῦντα δεῖ ἐγγὺς μὲν τῆς στήλης κάμψαι.— ἔχων</quote>, <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοὺς ἵππους</quote>, directing his course: cp. <ref n="Hom. Il. 23.325"> <title>Il.</title> 23. 325</ref>(the skilful driver does not force the pace at first), <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ ἔχει ἀσφαλέως καὶ τὸν προὔχοντα δοκεύει</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔχριμπτ_σύριγγα_721">, brought the nave of his (left) wheel close to the post at each successive turning. <quote xml:lang="grc">σῦριγξ</quote> (‘pipe’) is strictly the <emph>opening</emph> in the nave (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἡ ὀπὴ τοῦ τροχοῦ</quote>, schol. on 716) which forms the socket of the axle; <title>Suppl.</title> 181 <quote xml:lang="grc">σύριγγες οὐ σιγῶσιν ἀξονήλατοι</quote> (the sockets of the whirling axles). Here it is a synonym for the <quote xml:lang="grc">χνόη</quote> (717) or nave itself. That part of the wheel is rightly named here, because it projects slightly. Thus in v. 745 it is the <quote xml:lang="grc">χνόη</quote> that strikes the post. For </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔχριμπτ_721"> cp. <ref n="Hom. Il. 23.334"> <title>Il.</title> 23. 334</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τῷ σὺ μάλ’ ἐγχρίμψας ἐλάαν σχεδὸν ἅρμα καὶ ἵππους</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δεξιὸν_κτλ_721"> In a <quote xml:lang="grc">τέθριππον</quote> the four horses were harnessed abreast. The two in the middle were under the yoke (<quote xml:lang="grc">ζύγιοι</quote>), which was affixed to the pole (<quote xml:lang="grc">ῥυμός</quote>), and drew by the yoke-collars (<quote xml:lang="grc">λέπαδνα</quote>) only, not being in traces. They were called respectively <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ μέσος δεξιὸς</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ μέσος ἀριστερός</quote> (schol. <title>Nub.</title> 122). The two outside horses drew by traces (<quote xml:lang="grc">σειραί</quote>) only, attached to their collars, and fastened to the <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄντυξ</quote> of the car at its lower edge: hence <quote xml:lang="grc">σειραῖοι, σειραφόροι</quote>. In turning from right to left, the righthand trace-horse had most work to do; hence <quote xml:lang="grc">δεξιόσειρος</quote>, fig., a strong helper at need ( <ref n="Soph. Ant. 140"> <title>Ant.</title> 140</ref> n.). The Homeric poems speak of <emph>one</emph> trace-horse besides the two yoke-horses (<quote xml:lang="grc">παρήορος</quote>, the traces being <quote xml:lang="grc">παρηορίαι</quote>): <ref n="Hom. Il. 8.87"> <title>Il.</title> 8. 87</ref>; 16. 152, <ref n="Hom. Il. 16.471"> Il. 471</ref>: <ref n="Hom. Od. 4.590."> <title>Od.</title> 4. 590.</ref> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρὶν_μὲν_723"> is defined by </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔπειτα_δ_723">: up to the moment of the Aenian's disaster.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἕστασαν_723">, plpf., epic ( <ref n="Hom. Il. 2.777"> <title>Il.</title> 2. 777</ref> etc.), and also Attic ( <ref n="Thuc. 4. 56">Thuc. 4. 56</ref>, etc.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄστομοι_724">, schol. <quote xml:lang="grc">σκληρόστομοι</quote>. The only extant example of the word in this sense; for in Aesch. fr. 442 <quote xml:lang="grc">στόμις</quote> (‘hard-mouthed’) is clearly right: see Nauck there (2nd ed.). Xenophon uses <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄστομος</quote> of hounds which have ‘no mouths’ for holding their prey ( <ref n="Arr. Cyn. 3.3"> <title>Cyn.</title> 3. 3</ref>): he expresses ‘hard-mouthed’ by <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπειθής</quote> ( <ref n="Aristoph. Kn. 3."> <title>Eq.</title> 3.</ref>§ 6), and calls the ‘hard side’ of a horse's mouth <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡ ἄδικος γνάθος</quote> (<foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> § 5). Cp. <title>Anth. Plan.</title> 361 <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔτε χαλινοῖς</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">δύστομος ἱππείη σοῖς ἀπίθησε λόγοις.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βίᾳ_φέρουσιν_724">: <ref n="Eur. Hipp. 1223">Eur. <title>Hipp.</title> 1223</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">αἱ δ’ ἐνδακοῦσαι στόμια πυριγενῆ γνάθοις</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">βίᾳ φέρουσιν</quote>. So <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκφέρειν</quote>, <ref n="Xen. Horse. 3">Xen. <title>Eq.</title> 3</ref>§ 5.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκ_ὑποστροφῆς_724">=<quote xml:lang="grc">ὑποστρέψαντες</quote>, a phrase like <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξ ὑπερτέρας χερός</quote> (455). Cp. Polyb. 2. 25. 3 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξ ὑποστροφῆς ἀπήντων</quote>, ‘wheeling round, they went to meet the enemy.’ </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τελοῦντες_κτλ_726">, ‘finishing the sixth and now (entering upon) the seventh round.’ The more general sense of <quote xml:lang="grc">τελοῦντ<*>ες</quote> (‘doing’) is evolved from the special; cp. on <quote xml:lang="grc">κρύψον</quote> in 436.—Nauck finds the reading suspicious, ‘since <quote xml:lang="grc">πῶλος</quote> is almost always fem.’ It often is so (705 n.); but often, too, masc.: <foreign xml:lang="lat">e.g.</foreign> <ref n="Aesch. Ag. 1641">Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 1641</ref>(<quote xml:lang="grc">κριθῶντα πῶλον</quote>), <ref n="Aesch. Lib. 749">Aesch. <title>Ch.</title> 749</ref>, <ref n="Xen. Horse. 1. 1.">Xen. <title>Eq.</title> 1. 1.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Βαρκαίοις_ὄχοις_726">: <ref n="Soph. El. 702">cp. 702</ref> n.: for the pl. (like the Homeric <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄχεα</quote>), meaning one chariot, cp. fr. 611 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄχοις Ἀκεσταίοισιν ἐμβεβὼς πόδα</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐξ_κακοῦ_728">. Other chariots were close upon the Aenian and the Barcaean. The foremost of these collided with each other in the effort to avoid the wreck, and meanwhile those which were in the rear ran into them. Thus the words </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔθραυε_ἐνέπιπτε_728"> vividly describe the process.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ναυαγίων_728">: cp. [Dem. ] or. 61 § 29 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν τοῖς ἱππικοῖς ἀγῶσιν ἡδίστην θέαν παρέχεται τὰ ναυαγοῦντα.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Κρισαῖον_πέδον_728"> (not <quote xml:lang="grc">πεδίον</quote>), not the whole Crisaean plain, but the whole hippodrome: cp. n. on 699. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γνοὺς_δ_731"> <quote xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</quote> At the moment when the Aenian ran into the Barcaean, the Athenian was among the <emph>foremost</emph> drivers behind these two. On seeing the crash, he drew aside, reining in his horses, and allowed the chariots behind him to rush past in the middle of the course. They were all wrecked or disabled. He then resumed the race.—As Arist. say<*> ( <ref n="Aristot. Rh. 1.9">Arist. <title>Rhet.</title> 1. 9.</ref>§ 30, referring to Plat. <title>Menex.</title>p. 235 D), <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ χαλεπὸν Ἀθηναίους ἐν Ἀθηναίοις ἐπαινεῖν</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κἀνοκωχεύει_731">: cp. <ref n="Hdt. 9. 13">Her. 9. 13</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πρὶν μέν νυν ἢ πυθέσθαι ἀνεκώχευε</quote> (‘he held his hand,’ ‘kept quiet’). It may have been a nautical term: <emph>id.</emph> 6. 116 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνοκωχεύσαντες τὰς νέας</quote> (‘after <emph>lying to</emph>’). The verb is Ionic and poetical; but <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνοκωχή</quote>, from which it comes, is used by Thuc. (1. 40 etc.). In Soph. fr. 304 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀκωχεύουσιν</quote> should perh. replace <quote xml:lang="grc">κωχεύουσιν</quote>. The simple <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀκωχὴ</quote> occurs only in lexicons (<title>Etym. M.</title> 596. 51): <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄκωχα</quote>, an Ionic perf. of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔχω</quote> (Attic <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔσχηκα</quote>), only in <ref n="Hom. Il. 2.218"> <title>Il.</title> 2. 218</ref>, where the MSS. have <quote xml:lang="grc">συνοχωκότε</quote>, but Cobet (<title>Misc. Crit.</title> 304) would read <quote xml:lang="grc">συνοκωχότε</quote>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κλύδων_731">, a surging mass: cp. <title>Legg.</title> 758 A <quote xml:lang="grc">πόλις...ἐν κλύδωνι τῶν ἄλλων πόλεων διαγομένη</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔσχατος_μὲν_734"> corresponds with <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅπως δ’</quote> (736): he was then last, indeed; <emph>but</emph>, when he saw that only one competitor was left, he pressed to the front.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὑστέρας_ἔχων_734"> explains <emph>why</emph> he was <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔσχατος</quote>: he was purposely keeping his horses behind; and </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φέρων_734">, again, gives the motive of this; <emph>because</emph> he relied on the finish. For </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πίστιν_φέρων_734"> as=<quote xml:lang="grc">πιστεύων</quote>, see <ref n="Soph. OT 1445"> <title>O. T.</title> 1445</ref> n. [Cp. H. NewmanJ. , <title>Apologia</title>, p. 56: ‘with the racer in the Tragedy, look forward steadily and hopefully to the event, <quote xml:lang="grc">τῷ τέλει πίστιν φέρων</quote>.’]</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅπως_ὁρᾷ_736">: when Orestes sees the Athenian. This reading is confirmed by the first hand in L. It should be noted, however, that with the other possible reading, <quote xml:lang="grc"> <emph>ὁ δ’ ὡς</emph>, ὁ δ’</quote> could still refer to Orestes: <ref target="cl448">see on 448</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐνσείσας_737"> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐνσείειν</quote> is sometimes ‘to drive in with force’: cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1274"> <title>Ant.</title> 1274</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν δ’ ἔσεισεν ἀγρίαις ὁδοῖς</quote> (‘hurled me’ into them): here the notion is, ‘sent vibrating through their ears.’ </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κἀξισώσαντε_ζυγὰ_738">: Orestes, who had kept on the inside all through (720), would quickly gain when the Athenian paused (732).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τότ_ἄλλοθ_738">: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Phaedrus 237e"> <title>Phaedr.</title> 237 E</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοτὲ μὲν ἡ ἑτέρα, ἄλλοτε δὲ ἡ ἑτέρα κρατεῖ.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
τοτὲ μὲν ἡ ἑτέρα, ἄλλοτε δὲ ἡ ἑτέρα
                                    κρατεῖ.

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄλλος_ἅτερος_738">. Cp. <ref n="Hom. Il. 9.313"> <title>Il.</title> 9. 313</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅς χ’ ἕτερον μὲν κεύθῃ ἐνὶ φρεσίν, ἄλλο δὲ εἴπῃ</quote>: <ref n="Hdt. 1. 32">Her. 1. 32</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄλλο μὲν ἔχει, ἑτέρου δὲ ἐπιδέεται</quote>. So here <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄλλος</quote> is loosely substituted for <quote xml:lang="grc">ἕτερος</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κάρα_προβάλλων_738"> <foreign xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</foreign>: ‘showing his head in front of the (two) chariots.’ The neck-and-neck race is described as it would appear to a spectator at one side of the hippodrome, who saw the drivers in profile. Each charioteer is leaning forward in his car (as so often seen on vases). The head, now of one driver, now of the other, would be seen in front.—Not: ‘bringing the head of his equipage in front (of the other team).’</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προβάλλων_738"> in partitive appos. with the subject of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἠλαυνέτην</quote>, cp. <ref n="Plat. Phaedrus 248a"> <title>Phaedr.</title> 248 A</ref> (<quote xml:lang="grc">αἱ ψυχαὶ</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">ξυμπεριφέρονται,...ἑτέρα πρὸ τῆς ἑτέρας πειρωμένη γενέσθαι</quote>: and n. on <ref n="Soph. Ant. 259"> <title>Ant.</title> 259</ref> ff. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοὺς_πάντας_741">. Six rounds still remained to be run when Orestes and the Athenian were left alone (<ref target="cl726">see on 726</ref>). The word <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔπειτα</quote> is vague, but probably the disaster of Orestes is conceived as happening in the middle of the last (or twelfth) round.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀσφαλεῖς_741"> is proleptic, ‘in safety’: it is needless to write <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀσφαλής.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὠρθοῦθ_741">, ‘had a prosperous course’: cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 675"> <title>Ant.</title> 675</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τῶν δ’ ὀρθουμένων</quote>, ‘of those whose course is fair’ (n.). This is explained by </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὀρθὸς_δί_741"> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φρων_741">, where the sense of the adj., varying from that of the verb, saves the language from seeming too redundant. Cp. n. on <ref n="Soph. Trach. 613"> <title>Tr.</title> 613</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">καινῷ καινὸν ἐν πεπλώματι</quote>. The prep. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκ</quote> denotes the condition (‘with his chariot safe’): <ref n="Soph. El. 455">cp. 455</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λύων_ἀριστερὰν_743">. He was turning sharply round the goal from right to left, and was therefore pulling the rein of the left trace-horse. He slackened this rein a moment too soon, thus letting the horse draw with more force. The effect was to create an angular velocity, which brought the left wheel into collision with the goal. See note in Appendix. The notion that <quote xml:lang="grc">λύων</quote> has supplanted a word of the opposite sense (‘tightening’) appears mistaken. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χνόας_745">. The wooden nave (717), in which the axle turned, was broken across (</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μέσας_745">) by striking the <quote xml:lang="grc">στήλη</quote>, and the left wheel came off. <quote xml:lang="grc">χνόας</quote> might also denote the end of the axle itself (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἀκραξόνιον</quote>): but it seems needless to assume this sense, which is less suited to <quote xml:lang="grc">μέσας</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. Trach. 781"> <title>Tr.</title> 781</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μέσου</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">κρατὸς διασπαρέντος</quote>). So in <ref n="Eur. Hipp. 1234">Eur. <title>Hipp.</title> 1234</ref>, the wheels have struck a stone, <quote xml:lang="grc">σύριγγές τ’ ἄνω</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">τροχῶν ἐπήδων</quote> (the nave flew off) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀξόνων τ’ ἐνήλατα</quote> (the linch-pins). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀντύγων_746">, the ‘rim’ or rail, surmounting a barrier or breastwork, often of osier trellis-work, which protected the front, and both sides, of the chariot; the plur., as in <ref n="Hom. Il. 5.728"> <title>Il.</title> 5. 728</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">δοιαὶ δὲ περίδρομοι ἄντυγές εἰσι</quote> (<foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, one on each side); <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1030."> <title>Ai.</title> 1030.</ref>It did not reach higher than the driver's waist, and was sometimes lower. In going round the corner, Orestes would have been leaning a little to the left (like a bicyclist in a similar case); as Nestor says, <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτὸς δὲ κλινθῆναι ἐϋπλέκτῳ ἐνὶ δίφρῳ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἦκ’ ἐπ’ ἀριστερὰ τοῖιν</quote> ( <ref n="Hom. Il. 23.335"> <title>Il.</title> 23. 335</ref>). When the left wheel came off, he would be thrown over the left <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄντυξ</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σὺν_ἑλίσσεται_746">, tmesis ( <ref n="Soph. Ant. 432"> <title>Ant.</title> 432</ref>). Eur. uses this compound, in its Ionic form (<quote xml:lang="grc">συνειλίσσω</quote>), <ref n="Eur. Ion 1164.">Eur. <title>Ion</title> 1164.</ref>So Hippolytus ( <ref n="Eur. Hipp. 1236">Eur. <title>Hipp.</title> 1236</ref>) <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτὸς δ’ ὁ τλήμων ἡνίαισιν ἐμπλακεὶς</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">δεσμὸν δυσεξήνυστον ἕλκεται δεθείς</quote>. The charioteer sometimes passed the reins round his body,—as may been seen on two sarcophagi in the ‘Sala della Biga’ of the Vatican (Smith's <title>Dict. Ant.</title>, 3rd ed., art. <title>Circus</title>, vol. 1. p. 435). Cp. <title>G.</title> 3. 107 <title>Et proni dant lora.</title>— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τμητοῖς_746"> (863), a general epithet of reins or thongs ( <ref n="Hom. Il. 10.567"> <title>Il.</title> 10. 567</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐϋτμήτοισιν ἱμᾶσιν</quote>: <ref n="Eur. Hipp. 1245">Eur. <title>Hipp.</title> 1245</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τμητῶν ἱμάντων</quote>), suggesting neat workmanship (cp. <ref n="Verg. A. 11. 579">Verg. <title>Aen.</title> 11. 579</ref> <emph>tereti...habena</emph>). Campbell thinks that it refers to the sharp edges, as giving pain.—<quote xml:lang="grc">πέδῳ</quote>, as <ref n="Aesch. Eum. 479">Aesch. <title>Eum.</title> 479</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πέδῳ</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">πέδοι</quote> Dind.) <quote xml:lang="grc">πεσών</quote>, <ref n="Soph. Trach. 789">Soph. <title>Tr.</title> 789</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">χθονὶ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ῥίπτων ἑαυτόν</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="διεσπάρησαν_δρόμον_748">, ‘were scattered into the middle of the course’; <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, left the track in which the race was being run (the ‘course’ in the narrower sense), and rushed on to the open ground between this track and the spectators. <quote xml:lang="grc">διεσπάρησαν</quote> cannot be satisfactorily explained on the assumption that all four horses remained harnessed to the car. But we need not assume that Orestes was entangled in the reins of all four. We may suppose, then, that, when the left wheel came off, the left tracehorse, plunging wildly, broke his traces, and freed himself. No mishap to the <quote xml:lang="grc">ζυγόν</quote> is noticed, and the two yokehorses, at any rate, probably remained together. Unless they broke the pole, they would still drag the disabled car. [Assuming that the four horses continued together, I formerly rendered, ‘broke off’—<foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, from the line of the race.]— Campbell thinks that <quote xml:lang="grc">διεσπάρησαν</quote> ‘is used inaccurately to denote aimless movement,’ and renders, ‘plunged wildly about the course.’</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="στρατὸς_749"> here=<quote xml:lang="grc">λεὼς</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. Trach. 795"> <title>Tr.</title> 795</ref> n.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δίφρων_749">: the plur., as in 510: <ref n="Soph. El. 727">cp. 727</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄχοις</quote>. In the narrower sense, <quote xml:lang="grc">δίφρος</quote> is the platform of the car, usu. made of interlaced thongs (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἱμάντες</quote>), stretched on a framework of wood or iron ( <ref n="Hom. Il. 5.727"> <title>Il.</title> 5. 727</ref>).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀνωλόλυξε_749">, here of grief, as <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀλόλυξαν</quote> in <ref n="Apollon. 3.1218">Ap. Rh. 3. 1218</ref>: elsewhere, almost always a triumphant cry, esp. of women ( <ref n="Soph. Trach. 205"> <title>Tr.</title> 205</ref>).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἷ_οἷα_749">: <ref n="Soph. Aj. 923"> <title>Ai.</title> 923</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἷος ὢν οἵως ἔχεις</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φορούμενος_οὖδας_752">, ‘dashed to the ground’: cp. <title>I. T.</title> 49 <quote xml:lang="grc">βεβλημένον πρὸς οὖδας</quote>. [Not, ‘dragged <emph>upon</emph> the ground’ (Campb.), which would be <quote xml:lang="grc">πρὸς οὔδει</quote>.] These words can be taken in two ways: I prefer the first. (1) With reference to his <emph>fall from the chariot.</emph> The people speak of his mishap as a whole, not merely of what he is suffering at the moment. (2) With reference to what occurs while he is being dragged; he is dashed earthwards (<emph>after</emph> being tossed upward). But this would be most awkward, when the mention of his being tossed upward follows. Eur. , where he speaks of men dragged on the ground by their chariot-horses, naturally says, ‘tossed up and down’ (not ‘down and up’): <title>Suppl.</title> 689 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἢ τοὺς ἄνω τε καὶ κάτω φορουμένους</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἱμασιν</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄλλοτ_752">: the first <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄλλο|τε</quote> is omitted: <ref n="Eur. Hec. 28">Eur. <title>Hec.</title> 28</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κεῖμαι δ’ ἐπ’ ἀκταῖς, ἄλλοτ’ ἐν πόντου σάλῳ.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐρανῷ_προφαίνων_752">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> tossed feet uppermost to the sky. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Shak. HAM 3.3">Shakesp. <title>Hamlet</title> 3. 3. 93</bibl> <quote xml:lang="eng">‘Then trip him, that his heels may kick at heaven’</quote> </cit>: where Stevens quotes from Heywood's <title>Silver Age</title>, ‘Whose heels tript up, kick'd 'gainst the firmament.’ </gloss>

data.quote:
‘Then trip him, that his heels may kick at
                                    heaven’

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="διφρηλάται_753">: the drivers of the eight previously disabled chariots would be still on the ground.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κατασχεθόντες_753">: for these forms, cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 651"> <title>O. T.</title> 651</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κέαντες_757">. This aor. part. of <quote xml:lang="grc">καίω</quote>, found in the Ionic prose of Hippocr. (7. 422 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀποκέας</quote>), occurs also in two Attic inscrr. of 408 B.C. (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐγκέαντι</quote> <emph>bis</emph>, Meisterhans, p. 86, n. 686). Here it is used without a metrical motive, such as exists in <ref n="Aesch. Ag. 849">Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 849</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κέαντες</quote>: [Eur. ] <title>Rhes.</title> 97 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκκέαντες</quote>: <ref n="Aristoph. Peace 1133">Ar. <title>Pax</title> 1133</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκκέας</quote>. The epic form is <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔκηα</quote> (for <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔκηvα</quote>), part. <quote xml:lang="grc">κήας</quote>.— The plur. part. refers, in grammar, only to the two bearers of the ashes, but, in sense, to all who assisted at the funeral. Cp. <ref n="Hdt. 8. 124">Her. 8. 124</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">αἰνέσαντες δὲ πολλά</quote> (<foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἱ Λακεδαιμόνιοι</quote>), <quote xml:lang="grc">προέπεμψαν ἀπιόντα τριηκόσιοι Σπαρτιητέων λογάδες</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐν_χαλκῷ_757">: <ref n="Soph. El. 1113">cp. 1113</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">φέροντες αὐτοῦ σμικρὰ λείψαν’ ἐν βραχεῖ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">τεύχει θανόντος</quote>: and 54 n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μέγιστον_757">. The heroes were conceived as transcending later mortals in strength (cp., <emph>e.g. </emph> <ref n="Hom. Il. 5.303"> Il. 5. 303</ref>); and so also in stature: <ref n="Hdt. 1. 68">Her. 1. 68</ref>(the grave of Orestes at Sparta) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπέτυχον σορῷ ἑπταπήχεϊ· ὑπὸ δὲ ἀπιστίης μὴ μὲν γενέσθαι μηδαμὰ μέζονας ἀνθρώπους τῶν νῦν ἄνοιξα αὐτήν, καὶ εἶδον τὸν νεκρὸν μήκεϊ ἴσον</quote> (‘proportionate’) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐόντα τῇ σορῷ.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σῶμα_σποδοῦ_757">, a body now <emph>consisting in</emph> (reduced to) dust: <ref target="cl682">see on 682</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πρόσχημ’ ἀγῶνος</quote>. (The gen. cannot be taken with <quote xml:lang="grc">χαλκῷ</quote>, as=‘an urn filled with dust.’) The conjecture <quote xml:lang="grc">δειλαίαν σποδὸν</quote> is possible, but not probable. [A schol. in the margin of L has written <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀντίπτωσις</quote>, ‘interchange of cases’: <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, he took the phrase as=<quote xml:lang="grc">μεγίστου σώματος δειλαίαν σποδόν</quote>.] Verse 758 cannot be omitted: <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν βραχεῖ</quote> could not here stand alone, as=‘in a small compass.’</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄνδρες_Φωκέων_759"> (partitive gen.): <ref n="Thuc. 7. 43">Thuc. 7. 43</ref>§ 3 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄνδρας τῶν φυλάκων.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκλάχῃ_759">, depending on <quote xml:lang="grc">φέρουσιν</quote>, is more natural here than <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκλάχοι</quote>, which would depend on <quote xml:lang="grc">τεταγμένοι</quote>, and refer to the purpose of the senders. So in 57 (n.) <quote xml:lang="grc">φέρωμεν</quote> was corrupted to <quote xml:lang="grc">φέροιμεν</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοιαῦτά_σοι_761"> (ethic dat.) </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ταῦτ_ἐστίν_761">, as in <title>O.C.</title> 62.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡς_λόγῳ_761">, <emph>so far as</emph> mere narrative can convey an impression. For the limiting sense of <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς</quote>, cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 76"> <title>O. C.</title> 76</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς ἰδόντι</quote> (n.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῖς_εἴδομεν_761">,=<quote xml:lang="grc">ἡμῖν δὲ τοῖς ἰδοῦσιν</quote>. Cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 1237"> <title>O. T.</title> 1237</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τῶν δὲ πραχθέντων τὰ μὲν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄλγιστ’ ἄπεστιν· ἡ γὰρ ὄψις οὐ πάρα</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δεσπόταισι_761"> would usu. imply that the speaker was a slave. The women of the Chorus are free (1227 <quote xml:lang="grc">πολίτιδες</quote>), and it has been suggested that the poet forgot this. But it is simpler to suppose that, in poetry, <quote xml:lang="grc">δεσπότης</quote> could bear its primary sense, ‘master of the house,’ ‘lord,’ without necessary reference to the special relation of master and slave. Cp. <ref n="Soph. Trach. 363"> <title>Tr.</title> 363</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">δεσπόζειν θρόνων</quote>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρόρριζον_761">: <ref target="cl512">see on 512</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὦ_Ζεῦ_766">. Clytaemnestra regretted her failure to destroy Orestes in childhood (296 f.). But the poet is true to nature in blending some touch of maternal grief with her sense of gain.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λέγω_766">, pres. subjunct.: cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 651"> <title>O. T.</title> 651</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῷ_λόγῳ_769">: the same phrase occurs in <ref n="Soph. OT 90"> <title>O. T.</title> 90</ref>, <ref n="Soph. OC 801."> <title>O. C.</title> 801.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δεινὸν_770">, a mysterious power, a strangely potent tie; cp. <ref n="Aesch. PB 39">Aesch. <title>P. V.</title> 39</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ συγγενές τοι δεινὸν ἥ θ’ ὁμιλία</quote>: <ref n="Aesch. Seven 1031">Aesch. <title>Theb.</title> 1031</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">δεινὸν τὸ κοινὸν σπλάγχνον</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 355">Soph. <title>Ph.</title> 355</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">δεινὸν γυναιξὶν αἱ δἰ ὠδίνων γοναί</quote>.— Isaiah xlix. 15 ‘Can a woman forget her sucking child, that she should not have compassion on the son of her womb?’</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πάσχοντι_770">: for the masc., <ref n="Soph. El. 145">cp. 145</ref> n.— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὧν_τέκῃ_770">, without <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄν</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. OT 1231"> <title>O. T.</title> 1231</ref> n.). The <emph>v.l.</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">τέκοι</quote> is possible ( <ref n="Soph. Ant. 666"> <title>Ant.</title> 666</ref> n.), but less natural. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μάτην_κτλ_772"> The old man speaks as if disappointed and aggrieved. Thus a cue is skilfully given for the change in Clytaemnestra's tone. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡμεῖς</quote> may include the bearers of the urn (759). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μάτην_λέγοις_773">, say the word ‘<quote xml:lang="grc">μάτην</quote>’: cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 567"> <title>Ant.</title> 567</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ ἥδε μέντοι μὴ λέγ’</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ψυχῆς_775">, ‘life’; see on <ref n="Soph. OC 998"> <title>O. C.</title> 998</ref> f. The word has been suspected, and prosaically altered (cr. n.). But it is strictly correct to describe a child as ‘born from’ its mother's ‘life.’ Here the phrase has a pathetic force; his very life was her gift. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μαστῶν_κτλ_776">: <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, ‘he deserted me who had suckled and reared him’: the words do not imply that Orestes was still an infant when he left her (<ref target="cl13">see on 13</ref> f.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀποστὰς_776">: similarly a slave who left his master was said <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀφίστασθαι</quote> ( <ref n="Lys. 23.7">Lys. or. 23 § 7</ref>, etc.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀπεξενοῦτο_776">, ‘became estranged.’ Cp. <ref n="Eur. Hipp. 1084">Eur. <title>Hipp.</title> 1084</ref> f. <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ ἀκούετε</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">πάλαι ξενοῦσθαι τόνδε προὐννέποντά με</quote>; (‘that he is no longer my son’). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φόνους_779">: for the plur., 206 <quote xml:lang="grc">θανάτους</quote> (n.). So in <ref n="Soph. OC 962"> <title>O. C.</title> 962</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">φόνους</quote> alludes to the death of Laïus. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὥστ_στεγάζειν_780">. When <quote xml:lang="grc">ὥστε</quote> is followed by the infin., the negative is ordinarily <quote xml:lang="grc">μή</quote>. There are, however, many exceptions. Instances strictly like the above are these:— <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1357">Soph. <title>Ph.</title> 1357</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἶσθ)· οὐ μακρὰν γὰρ τειχέων περιπτυχαί</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">ὥστ’ οὐχ ἅπαντά σ’ εἰδέναι τὰ δρώμενα</quote>: [Dem. ] or. 53 § 1 <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδ’ αὖ ο<*>τως ἄπορος ἦν ὥστ’ οὐκ ἂν ἐξευρεῖν</quote>. But most of the examples occur under special conditions: see Appendix.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐξ_ἡμέρας_780">, <emph>interdiu.</emph> The phrase <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκ νυκτῶν</quote>, ‘in the night-watches,’ is frequent ( <ref n="Hom. Od. 12.286"> <title>Od.</title> 12. 286</ref>, Theognis 460, <ref n="Aesch. Lib. 287">Aesch. <title>Ch.</title> 287</ref>: [Eur. ] <title>Rhes.</title> 13, 17): cp. also frag. adesp. 7 (Nauck) <quote xml:lang="grc">πόνῳ πόνον</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκ νυκτὸς ἀλλάσσουσα τὸν καθ’ ἡμέραν.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="στεψάζειν_780">, lit. ‘cover,’ <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> cover the eyes.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὁ_χρόνος_780">, lit., ‘the time which stands in front (of the present),’ the ‘imminent’ or ‘coming’ time (schol. <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ ἐπιγινόμενος</quote>). From moment to moment she looked for death.—As <quote xml:lang="grc">προστατεῖν</quote> usu. means ‘to govern’ or ‘to protect,’ some understand, (1) ‘Time standing over me’ like a jailor (Campbell), ‘the tyrannous time’ (Whitelaw): or (2) generally, ‘Time that controls all events.’</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="διῆγε_780">, kept me living: <ref n="Dem. 18.89">Dem. or. 18 § 89</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ γὰρ τότε ἐνστὰς πόλεμος...ἐν πᾶσι τοῖς κατὰ τὸν βίον ἀφθονωτέροις...διήγαγεν ὑμᾶς τῆς νῦν εἰρήνης</quote> (‘caused you to live’).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀπήλλαγμαι_780">, expressing final deliverance, is better here than the aor. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπηλλάγην</quote> (cr. n.).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μείζων_780">, worse than the distant Orestes, because <quote xml:lang="grc">ξύνοικος</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοὐμὸν_785">, not <quote xml:lang="grc">τῆς ἐμῆς</quote>, since <quote xml:lang="grc">ψυχῆς αἷμα</quote> forms one notion: <ref n="Soph. El. 1390">cp. 1390</ref>: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 794"> <title>Ant.</title> 794</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκπίνους_785">: cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 531"> <title>Ant.</title> 531</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">σὺ δ’, ἣ κατ’ οἴκους ὡς ἔχιδν’ ὑφειμένη</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">λήθουσά μ’ ἐξέπινες.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄκρατον_785">, ‘sheer,’ implies the pitiless cruelty of the vampire (cp. <ref n="Xen. Anab. 4. 8. 14">Xen. <title>An.</title> 4. 8. 14</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τούτους...καὶ ὠμοὺς δεῖ καταφαγεῖν</quote>): so <ref n="Aesch. Lib. 577">Aesch. <title>Ch.</title> 577</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">φόνου δ’ Ἐρινὺς οὐχ ὑπεσπανισμένη</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄκρατον αἷμα πίεται</quote>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νῦν_δ_785">: repeated from 783, after the long parenthesis.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἕκηλα_785">, adv.: <ref n="Soph. El. 164">cp. 164</ref>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὕνεχ_785">: <ref n="Soph. El. 387">cp. 387</ref>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἡμερεύσομεν_785">: a word used in Attic prose (as=‘to pass the day’). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἴμοι_τάλαινα_788">: cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 416"> <title>Ph.</title> 416</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἴμοι τάλας</quote>, and so often: but in 674 <quote xml:lang="grc">οἲ )γὼ τάλαινα.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἰμῶξαι_788">: for the aor., <ref n="Soph. El. 285">cp. 285</ref>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἆρ_καλῶς_790">; ‘Is it not well?’ <ref n="Soph. El. 816">Cp. 816</ref>; and for <quote xml:lang="grc">ἆρα</quote>, 614. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὔτοι_σὺ_791"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">καλῶς ἔχεις</quote>. The sense is not merely, ‘you are in an evil case’; but rather, ‘you are not as I could wish’—and as Orestes is. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Νέμεσι_792">: this voc. occurs also in <ref n="Soph. Phil. 183">Soph. <title>Ph.</title> 183</ref>, where, as here, <quote xml:lang="grc">νέμεσις</quote> is a <emph>v.l.</emph> (Cp. <ref n="Aristoph. Frogs 893">Ar. <title>Ran.</title> 893</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ξύνεσι</quote>.)</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῦ_ἀρτίως_792">: for the place of the adv., cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 635"> <title>Ai.</title> 635</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ νοσῶν μάταν</quote>: <ref n="Aesch. PB 216">Aesch. <title>P. V.</title> 216</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τῶν παρεστώτων τότε</quote>: Anooc. or. 1 § 53 <quote xml:lang="grc">τῶν ἀπολωλότων ἤδη</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἤκουσεν_δεῖ_793">. Clyt. turns her retort as though <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦ θανόντος</quote> depended on <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄκουε</quote>. ‘Nemesis (the goddess of retribution generally) has heard a mother who prayed for the punishment of an unnatural son.’</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κἀπεκύρωσεν_793">: <ref n="Soph. El. 919">cp. 919</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κῦρος</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὔκουν_τάδε_795">; This verse should probably be interrogative, as Electra's answer suggests. And <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔκουν</quote> (<emph>nonne ergo</emph> ..?) seems better than <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκοῦν</quote> (<emph>ergo</emph> ..?). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐχ_παύσομεν_796">=<quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ λέγω ὅπως κ.τ.λ.</quote>: ‘not to speak’ of doing so; <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, ‘so far from’ doing so (Lat. <emph>nedum</emph>). So <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐχ ὅτι</quote>: and (with <quote xml:lang="grc">λέγε</quote> understood) <quote xml:lang="grc">μὴ ὅτι</quote>, or (more rarely) <quote xml:lang="grc">μὴ ὅπως</quote>. But these phrases usu. stand in the first clause, followed by <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλὰ καὶ</quote> in a second clause; <ref n="Dem. 6.9">Dem. or. 6 § 9</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοὺς δὲ Θηβαίους ἡγεῖτο...οὐχ ὅπως ἀντιπράξειν...ἀλλὰ καὶ συστρατεύσειν</quote>. Cp., however, <title>H.</title> 2. 3. 35 <quote xml:lang="grc">διὰ τὸν χειμῶνα οὐδὲ πλεῖν, μὴ ὅτι ἀναιρεῖσθαι τοὺς ἄνδρας, δυνατὸν ἦν</quote>. Lucian, <title>Charon</title> 8 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅταν πλέῃ μηδ’ ἐμπίδα, οὐχ ὅπως ταῦρον, ἔτι ἄρασθαι δυνάμενος</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πολλῶν_ἥκοις_797">. Clyt. refers to Electra's words, <quote xml:lang="grc">πεπαύμεθ’ ἡμεῖς</quote>: ‘<title>If</title> you have <emph>indeed</emph> silenced her, then you would deserve much,’ etc. A protasis formed by <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ</quote> and a past tense of the indic. is sometimes thus combined with an apodosis formed by the optat. and <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄν</quote>. In such cases the past tense usu. denotes an actual fact, or what is assumed to be such. Thus <ref n="Soph. OC 974"> <title>O. C.</title> 974</ref> ff. <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ δ’..ἦλθον,.. πῶς ἂν..ψέγοις</quote>; <ref n="Thuc. 3. 40">Thuc. 3. 40</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ γὰρ οὗτοι ὀρθῶς ἀπέστησαν</quote> (<emph>granting</emph> that they were right), <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑμεῖς ἂν οὐ χρεὼν ἄρχοιτε</quote>. More rarely, the protasis expresses a condition recognised as unreal; <emph>e.g. </emph> <ref n="Hom. Od. 1.236">Hom. Od. 1. 236</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπεὶ οὔ κε θανόντι περ ὧδ’ ἀκαχοίμην</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ μετὰ οἷς ἑτάροισι δάμη κ.τ.λ.</quote>— Wunder's change of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔπαυσας_797"> to </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παύσαις_797"> is not (I now think) desirable.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πολλῶν_797"> goes with </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄξιος_797">, and </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τυχεῖν_797"> is epexegetic: cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 699"> <title>Ant.</title> 699</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐχ ἥδε χρυσῆς ἀξία τιμῆς λαχεῖν</quote>; </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εὖ_κυρεῖ_799">: alluding to her words in 791.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πράξειας_799"> we supply <quote xml:lang="grc">ἂν</quote> from <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀποστείχοιμ’ ἄν</quote>. G. Wolff compares <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Lysis 208b"> <title>Lys.</title>p. 208 B</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ βούλοιο λαβὼν τὴν μάστιγα τύπτειν, ἐῷεν ἄν</quote> </cit>. <quote xml:lang="grc">Πόθεν δ’, ἦ δ’ ὅς, ἐῷεν;</quote> <ref n="Xen. Anab. 4. 6. 13">Xen. <title>An.</title> 4. 6. 13</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">δοκοῦμεν δ’ ἄν μοι...ἐρημοτέρῳ ἂν τῷ ὄρει χρῆσθαι· μένοιεν γὰρ αὐτοῦ μᾶλλον ἀθρόοι οἱ πολέμιοι</quote> (where the insertion of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἂν</quote> after <quote xml:lang="grc">γὰρ</quote> is needless). Cp. also <ref n="Soph. OT 937">Soph. <title>O. T.</title> 937</ref> n.</gloss>

data.quote:
εἰ βούλοιο λαβὼν τὴν μάστιγα τύπτειν, ἐῷεν
                                    ἄν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καταξίως_799"> into <quote xml:lang="grc">κατάξἰ ἂν</quote>, though specious, is not probable. Cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 911"> <title>O. C.</title> 911</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">δέδρακας οὔτ’ ἐμοῦ καταξίως</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔθ’ ὧν πέφυκας</quote>. <ref n="Soph. OT 133"> <title>O. T.</title> 133</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπαξίως..ἀξίως</quote>. Again, </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πράξειας_799_2"> appears sound. In the 2nd and 3rd sing. and 3rd plur. of the sigmatic aorist, classical writers of this age prefer the forms in <quote xml:lang="grc">ει</quote> to those in <quote xml:lang="grc">αι</quote>. There are exceptions, such as <title>Vesp.</title> 726 <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ ἂν δικάσαις</quote>, <ref n="Thuc. 2. 84">Thuc. 2. 84</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκπνεύσαι</quote>: and it would be unwarrantable to deny that Soph. could have written <quote xml:lang="grc">πράξαις ἄν</quote>. But the presumption is in favour of the vulgate. Still less likely is <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπεί τἂν</quote>, or <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμοῦ γ’ ἂν ἀξίως</quote> (cr. n.). If <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄν</quote> were required, Bergk's <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔτ’ ἂν</quote> in v. 801 would be best.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῦ_ξένου_799">: Phanoteus (670). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῶν_φίλων_803">, Orestes; for the plur., <ref n="Soph. El. 346">cp. 346</ref>. It is no concern of Clytaemnestra's to mourn him (<ref n="Soph. El. 776">cp. 776</ref>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἆρ_ὑμὶν_804"> <quote xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</quote>: ‘does she <emph>not</emph> seem,’ etc.,—with bitter irony: <ref n="Soph. El. 790">cp. 790</ref>, 816.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὧδ_804">, by a death so piteous; <ref n="Soph. El. 751">cp. 751</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὥς_θανών_808">: as Antigone says of her brother ( <ref n="Soph. Ant. 871"> <title>Ant.</title> 871</ref>), <quote xml:lang="grc">θανὼν ἔτ’ οῦσαν κατήναρές με</quote>. Cp. <ref n="Soph. Trach. 1163"> <title>Tr.</title> 1163</ref> n. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀποσπάσας_809"> (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκείνας</quote>) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αἵ_809"> <foreign xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</foreign>: conversely <ref n="Soph. OT 1432"> <title>O. T.</title> 1432</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐλπίδος μ’ ἀπέσπασας</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μολεῖν_812">: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1747"> <title>O. C.</title> 1747</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ποῖ μόλωμεν, ὦ Ζεῦ</quote>; (‘whither shall we turn?’). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δουλεύειν_814">: <ref n="Soph. El. 1192">cp. 1192</ref>: 597 <quote xml:lang="grc">δεσπότιν</quote>. She was treated like a slave (190 ff.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πάλιν_814"> has been suspected, but is explained by the context. In fact, her servitude had never ceased, and could not be said to recommence. But in imagination, so long as Orestes lived, she could behold a deliverer. From those bright dreams she must now go <emph>back</emph> to a slavery without hope. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἆρά_ἔχει_816">; <ref n="Soph. El. 790">cp. 790</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῦ_χρόνου_817">, a partitive gen. (478), instead of the more usual <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν λ. χρόνον</quote>, or <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ λοιπόν</quote>: so <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦ λοιποῦ</quote> in <ref n="Hdt. 1. 189">Her. 1. 189</ref>, <ref n="Aristoph. Peace 1084.">Ar. <title>Pax</title> 1084.</ref> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ξύνοικος_817">, since <quote xml:lang="grc">φονεῦσι πατρός</quote> so closely precedes. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παρεῖσ_ἐμαυτὴν_819">, allowing myself to sink to the ground: cp. <ref n="Soph. Trach. 938"> <title>Tr.</title> 938</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πλευρόθεν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">πλευρὰν παρεὶς ἔκειτο.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὐανῶ_819">: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 954"> <title>Ph.</title> 954</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ αὐανοῦμαι τῷδ’ ἐν αὐλίῳ μόνος</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χάρις_821">, gratification: <ref n="Soph. El. 1266">cp. 1266</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ποῦ_ποτε_823"> <quote xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</quote> ‘where are they?’ means here, ‘what are they doing, —if they see this, and do not punish?’ It is the part of the Sun-god to reveal guilt (cp. n. on 424 f.), and of the thunderbolts to smite it.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φαέθων_823">: <ref n="Hom. Il. 11.735"> <title>Il.</title> 11. 735</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἠέλιος φαέθων</quote>. This familiar phrase explains why <quote xml:lang="grc">ἅλιος</quote> (confirmed by the metre of 838) was changed in some MSS. to <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀέλιος.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐφορῶντες_823">, passively viewing; —just as in <ref n="Soph. Trach. 1269"> <title>Tr.</title> 1269</ref>(<quote xml:lang="grc">θεοὶ</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">τοιαῦτ’ ἐφορῶσι πάθη.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κρύπτουσιν_823">: schol. <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ ἄγουσιν εἰς φῶς</quote>, do <emph>not</emph> brand by exposure and chastisement. Cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">συγκρύπτειν τινί τι</quote>, to connive by silence at an offence ( Andoc. or. 1 § 67, etc.). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τί_δακρύεις_828">; A gentle remonstrance; ‘why dost thou thus give way to grief?’ (Not: ‘what is the cause of thy grief?’) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μηδὲν_ἀΰσῃς_830">. In saying <quote xml:lang="grc">φεῦ</quote>, Electra lifted her face and stretched forth her hands to heaven, as if accusing the gods; and the Chorus hasten to warn her against any irreverent utterance (schol.). Cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 386"> <title>Ai.</title> 386</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μηδὲν μέγ’ εἴπῃς</quote>: <ref n="Hom. Od. 22.287"> <title>Od.</title> 22. 287</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μή ποτε πάμπαν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">εἴκων ἀφραδίῃς μέγα εἰπεῖν</quote>: Plat. <title>Phaedo</title>p. 95 B <quote xml:lang="grc">μὴ μέγα λέγε</quote>: <ref n="Eur. Her. 1244">Eur. <title>H. F.</title> 1244</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἴσχε στόμ’, ὡς μὴ μέγα λέγων μεῖζον πάθῃς</quote>: Theocr. 10. 20 <quote xml:lang="grc">μηδὲν μέγα μυθεῦ</quote> (do not boast). <ref n="Verg. A. 10. 547">Verg. <title>Aen.</title> 10. 547</ref> <title>Dixerat ille aliquid magnum.</title> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀΰσῃς_830">, a loud cry ( <ref n="Soph. Trach. 565"> <title>Tr.</title> 565</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκ δ’ ἤϋσ’ ἐγώ</quote>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀπολεῖς_831">, <emph>enecabis.</emph> She takes their remonstrance as implying a doubt whether Orestes is dead. For this verb in ref. to <emph>mental</emph> pain, cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1172"> <title>Ph.</title> 1172</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τί μ’ ὤλεσας</quote>; (‘afflicted me’—by reviving a painful memory). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῶν_οἰχομένων_832">, Orestes (for the plur., <ref n="Soph. El. 145">cp. 145</ref> f.): </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φανερῶς_832">, because the mention of the ashes (757 ff.) has removed the last doubt from her mind.— </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐλπίδα_832"> with gen. of the object; <ref n="Soph. El. 1460">cp. 1460</ref> f. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κατ_τακομένας_834">. This constr., instead of the simple dat. (456), is due to the peculiar sense of </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπεμβάσει_834">: cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐγγελᾶν</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπεγγελᾶν κατά τινος</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. OC 1339"> <title>O. C.</title> 1339</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Aj. 969"> <title>Ai.</title> 969</ref>). Those who would read </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κατ_τακομένᾳ_834">, and assume a tmesis, omit to observe that <quote xml:lang="grc">κατεπεμβάσει</quote> would take a genitive. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἶδα_γὰρ_836"> <quote xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</quote>: ‘Nay, (I can still offer thee comfort;) <emph>for</emph> I know that Amphiaraüs, like Agamemnon, was betrayed to death by a false wife; and yet now he is a great spiritual force beneath the earth, and is revered among men.’ Although Orestes is dead, Agamemnon may still be <emph>honoured.</emph> The Chorus do not directly hint a belief that he can still be <emph>avenged</emph>,—as Amphiaraüs was. Electra then seizes on this point of contrast, Amphiaraüs found an avenger; her father cannot now find one.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Ἀμφιάρεων_836">, scanned <quote xml:lang="grc">¯˘˘¯</quote>, as in <ref n="Soph. OC 1313"> <title>O. C.</title> 1313</ref>(n.): </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄνακτα_836">, as the seer Teiresias is so called, <ref n="Soph. OT 284"> <title>O. T.</title> 284</ref>(n.). Amphiaraüs, the Argive seer and warrior, married Eriphylè, sister of Adrastus, king of Argos. He had sworn to abide by her decision in any difference between himself and her brother, with whom he had formerly been at feud ( <ref n="Apollod. 3.6.2">Apollod. 3. 6. 2</ref>). When Polyneices sought Argive aid against Thebes, Amphiaraüs opposed the enterprise, foreseeing a fatal issue. Polyneices then bribed Eriphylè with a golden necklace, and she persuaded her husband to join the expedition. The Argives were routed by the Thebans. In the flight, Amphiaraüs was approaching the river Ismenus, near Thebes, when the earth, riven by a thunderbolt, swallowed him up, with his chariot: cp. <emph>fr.</emph> 873. Sophocles wrote an <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἐριφύλη</quote>, and also a satyric <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἀμφιάρεως</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χρυσοδέτοις_ἕρκεσι_837">, nets, or snares, of golden links; <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, the toils of fate into which he was drawn through the necklace with which his wife was bribed. (Not, ‘the snare laid for <emph>her</emph> by the necklace’; she knew what was to come.) The epithet marks the figurative sense of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἕρκεσι</quote> (as a ship is <quote xml:lang="grc">λινόπτερον ὄχημα</quote>, <ref n="Aesch. PB 468">Aesch. <title>P. V.</title> 468</ref>): cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 60">Soph. <title>Ai.</title> 60</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰς ἕρκη κακά</quote> (the net of doom). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γυναικῶν_837">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἐριφύλης</quote>, an allusive plur. (145 n.), perhaps suggested by <ref n="Hom. Od. 15.247">Hom. <title>Od.</title> 15. 247</ref>(of Amphiaraüs), <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ ὄλετ’ ἐ<*> Θήβῃσι γυναίων εἵνεκα δώρων</quote>. (This is better than to give <quote xml:lang="grc">γυναικῶν</quote> a general sense,— ‘such as women love.’) Cp. <ref n="Plat. Rep. 590A">Plat. <title>Rep.</title> 590A</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡ Ἐριφύλη ἐπὶ τῇ τοῦ ἀνδρὸς ψυχῇ τὸν ὅρμον δεξαμένη</quote>. It was the <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅρμος</quote> which had been given to Harmonia by her husband Cadmus ( <ref n="Paus. 9. 41. 2">Paus. 9. 41. 2</ref>).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κρυφθέντα_837">, which has been suspected (cr. n.), is sound; it is the word repeatedly used with ref. to the end of Amphiaraüs: <ref n="Pind. N. 9">Pind. <title>N.</title> 9. 24</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ δ’ Ἀμφιάρῃ σχίσσεν κεραυνῷ παμβίᾳ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">Ζεὺς τὰν βαθύστερνον χθόνα, κρύψεν δ’ ἅμ’ ἵπποις</quote>: <ref n="Apollod. 3.6.6">Apollod. 3. 6. 6</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">Ζεὺς κεραυνὸν βαλὼν τὴν γῆν διέστησεν, ὁ δὲ σὺν τῷ ἅρματι...ἐκρύφθη</quote>: and especially the oracle cited in Athen. p. 232 E, commanding Alcmaeon , the seer's son, to dedicate the fatal necklace at Delphi: <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ σὺ φέρειν τιμῆεν ἐμοὶ γέρας, ᾧ ποτε μήτηρ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἀμφιάραον ἔκρυψ’ ὑπὸ γῆν αὐτοῖσι σὺν ἵπποις</quote>,—where the instrum. dat. <quote xml:lang="grc">ᾧ</quote> (‘by means of which’) gives an exact parallel to <quote xml:lang="grc">ἕρκεσι</quote> here (cp. also 549). In <ref n="Aesch. Seven 587">Aesch. <title>Theb.</title> 587</ref> f. Amphiaraüs says, <quote xml:lang="grc">τήνδε πιανῶ χθόνα</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">μάντις κεκευθὼς πολεμίας ὑπὸ χθονός</quote>. Pausanias (9. 8. 3) saw near Thebes the enclosed and sacred spot where the earth had opened.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἒ_ἰώ_840">. The words <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑπὸ γαίας</quote> remind Electra of her father and brother. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πάμψυχος_841">=<quote xml:lang="grc">πᾶσαν τὴν ψυχὴν ἔχων</quote> (cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄψυχος</quote>). The mind of Amphiaraüs acts upon men through his oracles as fully as if he were alive. In the popular belief, the ordinary shades of the departed had either no intelligence or a feeble one. Thus Teiresias is an exception ( <ref n="Hom. Od. 10.495"> <title>Od.</title> 10. 495</ref>): <quote xml:lang="grc">τῷ καὶ τεθνηῶτι νόον πόρε Περσεφόνεια</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">οἴῳ πεπνῦσθαι· τοὶ δὲ σκιαὶ ἀΐσσουσιν</quote>. It was felt difficult to conceive mind apart from its supposed physical basis, the <quote xml:lang="grc">φρένες</quote> or diaphragm: cp. Leaf on <ref n="Hom. Il. 23.104."> <title>Il.</title> 23. 104.</ref>—The scholia recognise this explanation of <quote xml:lang="grc">πάμψυχος</quote>, but suggest also two others, both untenable: (1) ‘ruling over all the shades’: or (2) ‘possessing his life <quote xml:lang="grc">διὰ παντός</quote>,’ <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> ‘immortal.’</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φεῦ_842">.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φεῦ_δῆτ_842">. Electra's cry, <quote xml:lang="grc">φεῦ</quote>, is drawn from her by the thought that, while Amphiaraüs has honour, her father's spirit is unhonoured. The Chorus suppose her to mean, ‘Alas for Eriphylè's wickedness,’ and respond <quote xml:lang="grc">φεῦ δῆτ’</quote>, ‘alas indeed’ (cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 536"> <title>O. C.</title> 536</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ΧΟ. ἰώ. ΟΙ. ἰὼ δῆτα</quote>). Then they say, </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὀλοὰ_γὰρ_842">, ‘for the murderess—’ intending to add, ‘betrayed her husband's life.’ But Electra, still thinking of the difference and not of the likeness between the case of Amphiaraüs and that of Agamemnon, quiokly gives a different turn to the unfinished sentence by interjecting </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐδάμη_842">,—‘was laid low’ (by her son Alcmaeon : <ref target="cl846">see on 846</ref>).—For </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὀλοὰ_842"> (nom. fem.) active in sense, cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">Μοῖρ’ ὀλοή</quote> ( <ref n="Hom. Il. 16.849"> <title>Il.</title> 16. 849</ref>, etc.): in <ref n="Soph. Trach. 846"> <title>Tr.</title> 846</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀλοὰ στένει</quote> it is neut. pl. (‘despairingly’).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐδάμη_842_2"> should be followed by a full stop, as in L; not by a query, as in the Aldine,—a punctuation suggested by <quote xml:lang="grc">ναί</quote>. Electra is not asking a question; she is rejecting the consolation. The Chorus admit, by their </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ναί_842">, the fact that Eriphylè was slain, and then Electra points the contrast (<quote xml:lang="grc">οἶδ’ οἶδ’, κ.τ.λ.</quote>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μελέτωρ_846"> (only here): ‘one who cared’ for the dead,—an avenger (<ref n="Soph. El. 237">cp. 237</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀμελεῖν</quote>). Suidas <emph>s.v.</emph>, <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ ἐπιμελούμενος, ὁ τιμωρούμενος τῷ πατρί</quote>. The conjecture <quote xml:lang="grc">νεμέτωρ</quote> (a word applied to the avenging Zeus in <ref n="Aesch. Seven 485">Aesch. <title>Th.</title> 485</ref>) is less fitting. For the form, cp. also <quote xml:lang="grc">γενέτωρ</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸν_πένθει_847">, ‘the mourner’ (<ref n="Soph. El. 290">cp. 290</ref>): not, ‘the lamented one.’ The shade of the dead Amphiaraüs mourned until avenged. Cp. <ref n="Aesch. Lib. 39">Aesch. <title>Ch.</title> 39</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μέμφεσθαι τοὺς γᾶς</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">νέρθεν περιθύμως</quote>: <ref n="Aesch. Eum. 95">Aesch. <title>Eum.</title> 95</ref> ff. (Clytaemnestra's shade) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐγὼ δ’ ὑφ’ ὑμῶν ὧδ’ ἀπητιμασμένη κ.τ.λ.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀναρπασθείς_848">, by death, as by a storm: cp. Lucian <title>De luctu</title> 13 <quote xml:lang="grc">τέθνηκας καὶ πρὸ ὥρας ἀνηρπάσθης</quote>: <ref n="Hom. Od. 1.241"> <title>Od.</title> 1. 241</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">νῦ<*> δέ μιν ἀκλειῶς ἅρπυιαι ἀνηρείψαντο·</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">οἴχετ’ ἄϊστος, ἄπυστος</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δειλαία_δειλαίων_849">. This adj., usu. said of persons, is applied to things in <ref n="Soph. OC 513"> <title>O. C.</title> 513</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Trach. 1026."> <title>Tr.</title> 1026.</ref>Cp. <ref n="Eur. Hec. 84">Eur. <title>Hec.</title> 84</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἥξει τι μέλος γοερὸν γοεραῖς</quote>. The antistrophic verse (860), <quote xml:lang="grc">πᾶσι θνατοῖς ἔφυ μόρος</quote>, indicates that the <quote xml:lang="grc">αι</quote> of <quote xml:lang="grc">δειλαία</quote> and of <quote xml:lang="grc">δειλαίων</quote> is short, as in <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1310">Soph. <title>Ant.</title> 1310</ref>(n.). H. Heinrich SchmidtJ. , however, makes it long in both words, reading <quote xml:lang="grc">πᾶσιν θνατοῖσιν ἔφυ μόρος</quote> in 860. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἴστωρ_850">, fem., as in <title>I. T.</title> 1431: so <quote xml:lang="grc">πράκτωρ, θέλκτωρ, σωτήρ, λωβητήρ</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1074"> <title>Ant.</title> 1074</ref> n.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πανσύρτῳ_αἰῶνι_851">. The traditional text is <quote xml:lang="grc">πανσύρτῳ παμμήνῳ πολλῶν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">δεινῶν στυγνῶν τ’ ἀχέων</quote> (or <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀχαίων</quote>). Hermann substitutes </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αἰῶνι_851"> for <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀχέων</quote>, regarding the latter as a gloss upon the adjectives. Dindorf, accepting <quote xml:lang="grc">αἰῶνι</quote>, rejects <quote xml:lang="grc">πολλῶν</quote>, reading <quote xml:lang="grc">πανσύρτῳ παμμήνῳ δεινῶν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">στυγνῶν τ’ ἀχέων αἰῶνι</quote>. But the antistrophic verse (863), <quote xml:lang="grc">τμητοῖς ὁλκοῖς ἐγκῦρσαι</quote>, makes <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀχέων</quote> less metrically probable; on Dindorf's view, we might rather suggest <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀχθῶν</quote> (<ref n="Soph. El. 204">cp. 204</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔκπαγλ’ ἄχθη</quote>). And <quote xml:lang="grc">πολλῶν</quote>, as Hermann saw, is not likely to have been interpolated (‘<quote xml:lang="grc">πολλῶν</quote>, si quidquam, sanum est’). For other conjectures, see Appendix.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παμμήνῳ_851"> has a special point. The literal <quote xml:lang="grc">χειμάρρους</quote> flows only in winter.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἁθρήνεις_853">. The MS. reading, </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἃ_θροεῖς_853">, conflicts with the metre of the antistrophe (864), where the corresponding words are <quote xml:lang="grc">ἁ λώβα</quote>. In <ref n="Soph. Aj. 582"> <title>Ai.</title> 582</ref>, where <quote xml:lang="grc">θρηνεῖν</quote> is right, <quote xml:lang="grc">θροεῖν</quote> was a <emph>v.l.</emph> (Paris A). The reasons for preferring <quote xml:lang="grc">ἁθρήνεις</quote> to </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἃ_θρηνεῖς_853"> are two. (1) A lengthening of <quote xml:lang="grc">α^</quote> before <quote xml:lang="grc">θρ</quote>, though not impossible in lyrics (cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 612"> <title>Ant.</title> 612</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">το— πρίν</quote>), would be unusual. (2) The context favours the imperfect. In 850—852 Electra refers to the woes which she has so long endured. The Chorus now reply, ‘We have seen what <emph>thou wast</emph> mourning,’—‘the course of thy sorrow,’—through all those years (<ref n="Soph. El. 140">cp. 140</ref> f.). The compromise, <quote xml:lang="grc">ᾇ θρηνεῖς</quote>, meets the first point, but not the second. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μή_νυν_854">. Here <quote xml:lang="grc">νυν</quote>, not <quote xml:lang="grc">νῦν</quote>, is clearly right: ‘If ye so well know my griefs, <emph>then</emph> desist,’ etc.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παραγάγῃς_854">, by delusive comfort: cp. <title>O.T.</title> 974 <quote xml:lang="grc">τῷ φόβῳ παρηγόμην.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἵν_854">, in a case where (<ref n="Soph. El. 21">cp. 21</ref> f.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τί_φής_856">; Her words, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἵν’ οὐ</quote>, already indicate her thought; ‘where there is no — hope’: and the Chorus interrupt her with a remonstrance. She rejoins by repeating that <emph>her brother</emph> is no more. While they are vaguely consolatory, she dwells on the loss of the only avenger to whom she could look. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πάρεισιν_ἔτι_857"> <quote xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</quote> The choice is between two readings; (1) <quote xml:lang="grc"> <emph>εὐπατριδᾶν</emph> ἀρωγαί</quote>, and (2) Neue's correction, <quote xml:lang="grc"> <emph>εὐπατρίδων τ’</emph> ἀρωγαί</quote>. The first is best. The constr. then is, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐλπίδων ἀρωγαί</quote>, aids consisting in hopes, <quote xml:lang="grc">κοινοτόκων εὐπατριδᾶν</quote>, from princes born of the same parents; <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, sustaining hopes from a princely brother. For the defining gen. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐλπίδων</quote>, <ref n="Soph. El. 682">cp. 682</ref>: for the second gen., denoting the source of the hope, <ref n="Soph. El. 1460">cp. 1460</ref> f., <quote xml:lang="grc">εἴ τις ἐλπίσιν πάρος</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξήρετ’ ἀνδρὸς τοῦδε</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔφυ_860">, with ref. to a law of nature or fate: <ref n="Soph. El. 236">cp. 236</ref>: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 440"> <title>Tr.</title> 440</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">χαίρειν πέφυκεν οὐχὶ τοῖς αὐτοῖς ἀεί</quote>: <ref n="Soph. OC 1444"> <title>O. C.</title> 1444</ref>(<quote xml:lang="grc">φῦναι</quote>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χαλαργοῖς_861"> (=<quote xml:lang="grc">χηλῶν ἀργῶν</quote>), a vivid epithet, describing the tramp and rush of the horses’ feet. Cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1062"> <title>O. C.</title> 1062</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ῥιμφαρμάτοις</quote> | ...<quote xml:lang="grc">ἁμίλλαις</quote> (n.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τμητοῖς_863">, the epithet of reins (747 n.), serves here to define the sense of <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁλκοῖς</quote>. Ordinarily <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁλκός</quote> means (1) a sort of hauling machine, or (2) a furrow.— <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐγκῦρσαι</quote>, ‘to meet with them,’ as a mode of death. Cp. <ref n="Hdt. 2. 82">Her. 2. 82</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁτέοισι ἐγκυρήσει</quote> (what fortunes he will have) <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ ὅκως τελευτήσει</quote>. (We cannot supply <quote xml:lang="grc">μόρῳ</quote> with the verb, and take <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁλκοῖς</quote> as instrumental dat.) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄσκοπος_864">, such as could not have been even imagined beforehand,—inconceivably dreadful. <ref n="Soph. El. 13">Cp. 13</ref><*> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν ἄσκοπον</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">χρόνον..ἡμερῶν ἀνήριθμον. </quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἁ_λώβα_864">, the cruel doom: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 996"> <title>Tr.</title> 996</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἵαν μ’ ἄρ’ ἔθου λώβαν</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ξένος_865">: schol. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπ’ ἀλλοδαπῆς</quote>: cp. <ref n="Soph. Trach. 65"> <title>Tr.</title> 65</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξενωμένου</quote> (n.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄτερ_χερῶν_866">: <ref n="Soph. El. 1138">cp. 1138</ref> n.: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 900"> <title>Ant.</title> 900</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">θανόντας αὐτόχειρ ὑμᾶς ἐγὼ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔλουσα κἀκόσμησα</quote> (n.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κέκευθεν_868">, lies buried: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 911"> <title>Ant.</title> 911</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν Ἅιδου...κεκευθότοιν</quote>: <ref n="Soph. OT 968"> <title>O. T.</title> 968</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κεύθει κάτω δὴ γῆς.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὔτε_του_868">: here <quote xml:lang="grc">του</quote> is equivalent to an adv., ‘at all’: cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 290"> <title>Ai.</title> 290</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔτε του κλύων</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">σάλπιγγος</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 814"> <title>Ant.</title> 814</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔτ’ ἐπινύμφειός</quote> | ..<quote xml:lang="grc">τις ὕμνος.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀντιάσας_868"> =<quote xml:lang="grc">ἀντήσας</quote> (<title>O.C.</title> 1445), ‘having obtained,’ —a rare sense for <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀντιάζω</quote>, which in Sophocles elsewhere means ‘to entreat’ (1009).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τάφου_γόων_868">: cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 29"> <title>Ant.</title> 29</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄκλαυτον, ἄταφον</quote>: <ref n="Hom. Il. 22.386"> <title>Il.</title> 22. 386</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">νέκυς ἄκλαυτος ἄθαπτος</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὑφ_τοι_871">. Here </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοι_871"> prefaces the explanation of her haste: cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 245"> <title>Ph.</title> 245</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Trach. 234."> <title>Tr.</title> 234.</ref>It is better than Brunck's </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σοι_871">, which would be an ethic dat. (‘you see me come,’ etc.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸ_κόσμιον_872">. Hurried movement in public was contrary to Athenian notions of <quote xml:lang="grc">εὐκοσμία</quote>. Cp. <title>Charmides</title> p. 159 B <quote xml:lang="grc">εἶπεν ὅτι οἱ δοκοῖ σωφροσύνη εἶναι τὸ κοσμίως πάντα ποιεῖν καὶ ἡσυχῇ, ἔν τε ταῖς ὁδοῖς βαδίζειν καὶ διαλέγεσθαι</quote>. <ref n="Dem. 37.55">Dem. or. 37 § 55</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοιοῦτος...ἐγὼ ὁ ταχὺ βαδίζων καὶ τοιοῦτος σὺ ὁ ἀτρέμας</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὦν_872"> at the end of the v., cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 298"> <title>O. T.</title> 298</ref>, <ref n="Soph. OC 14"> <title>O. C.</title> 14</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Trach. 819."> <title>Tr.</title> 819.</ref> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σὺ_875">, though emphatic, is not scornful, but rather compassionate.— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἴασιν_ἰδεῖν_875">. The <emph>v.l.</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἴασις</quote> (cr. n.) might at first sight seem preferable; but the vulgate is stronger, through the weight thrown on the impersonal <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ ἔνεστι</quote>. There was also a weak <emph>v.l.</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔτι</quote> for <quote xml:lang="grc">ἰδεῖν</quote>, but the latter balances <quote xml:lang="grc">εὔροις</quote> in 875. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐναργῶς_878">, in bodily presence: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 910"> <title>O. C.</title> 910</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐναργεῖς..στήσῃς</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 223"> <title>Tr.</title> 223</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">βλέπειν..ἐναργῆ</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλ_ἦ_879">..; In this formula, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἦ</quote> asks the question, while <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλὰ</quote> marks surprise: cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 414"> <title>Ph.</title> 414</ref>(n.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῖς_879"> at the end of the v.: <ref n="Soph. OC 351"> <title>O. C.</title> 351</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 409"> <title>Ant.</title> 409</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 263."> <title>Ph.</title> 263.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μὰ_ἑστίαν_881"> is a fitting asseveration here, since the hearth symbolises the very existence of the family.
