Similarly the disguised Odysseus, when he affirms that the lord of the house will return, swears by the <quote xml:lang="grc">ἑστία</quote> ( <ref n="Hom. Od. 14.159"> <title>Od.</title> 14. 159</ref>).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλ_ὕβρει_881">. Here <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλὰ</quote> (‘nay’) protests against Electra's words, and, as usual, follows the oath: cp. <ref n="Aristoph. Frogs 173">Ar. <title>Ran.</title> 173</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ΝΕ. δύο δραχμὰς μισθὸν τελεῖς</quote>; | <quote xml:lang="grc">ΔΙ. μὰ Δἴ, ἀλλ’ ἔλαττον</quote>: and so <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 753, <ref n="Aristoph. Frogs 759">Ar. Ran. 759</ref>, <ref n="Aristoph. Frogs 1053">Ar. Ran. 1053</ref>; <ref n="Xen. Mem. 3. 13. 3.">Xen. <title>Mem.</title> 3. 13. 3.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλ_ἐκεῖνον_881">. This second <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλά</quote> merely opposes its clause to the former: ‘I do not say this in mockery, but report him as (really) present’: cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 852"> <title>Ai.</title> 852</ref> f. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ οὐδὲν ἔργον ταῦτα θρηνεῖσθαι μάτην</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ ἀρκτέον τὸ πρᾶγμα σὺν τάχει τινί</quote>. For the </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡς_881">, which might be absent (as in 676), <ref n="Soph. El. 1341">cp. 1341</ref>: <ref n="Soph. OT 956"> <title>O. T.</title> 956</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πατέρα τὸν σὸν ἀγγελῶν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς οὐκέτ’ ὄντα Πόλυβον</quote>. Others, less well, take <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκεῖνον ὡς παρόντα νῷν</quote> not as depending on <quote xml:lang="grc">λέγω</quote> but as an acc. absol. (cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 101"> <title>O. T.</title> 101</ref> n.).—For the triple <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλά</quote> in vv. 879—882, cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 645"> <title>Ph.</title> 645</ref>—651: <title>O.C.</title> 238—248.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νῷν_881"> was suggested by such passages as <ref n="Soph. Phil. 415"> <title>Ph.</title> 415</ref>, <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς μηκέτ’ ὄντα κεῖνον ἐν φάει νόει</quote>. It implies the unexampled contraction <quote xml:lang="grc">νῶ</quote>. (In <ref n="Aesch. Pers. 1054">Aesch. <title>Pers.</title> 1054</ref>, where Dindorf corrects <quote xml:lang="grc">κἀπιβόα</quote> to <quote xml:lang="grc">κἀπιβῶ, καὶ βόα</quote> is read by Eustathius on Dionys. p. 791.) </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐξ_ἐμοῦ_885">, on my own authority: <ref n="Soph. El. 344">cp. 344</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκ σαυτῆς</quote>.—For the use of </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τε_καί_885"> in coupling opposites, cp. <title>O.T.</title> 1275 <quote xml:lang="grc">πολλάκις τε κοὐχ ἅπαξ</quote>, <ref n="Soph. OC 935"> <title>O. C.</title> 935</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">βίᾳ τε κοὐχ ἑκών</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἰδοῦσα_πίστιν_887">. Nauck substitutes <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔχουσα</quote> for <quote xml:lang="grc">ἰδοῦσα</quote>, but the latter is right, since <quote xml:lang="grc">πίστιν</quote>=‘warranty,’ ‘proof.’ Cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 1420"> <title>O. T.</title> 1420</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τίς μοι φανεῖται πίστις ἔνδικος</quote>; (n.).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰς_μοι_887">: for the ethic dat., <ref n="Soph. El. 144">cp. 144</ref>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀνηκέστῳ_887">, ‘fatal’: <ref n="Soph. Aj. 52"> <title>Ai.</title> 52</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τῆς ἀνηκέστου χαρᾶς.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πυρί_887">, a feverish hope; cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 478"> <title>Ai.</title> 478</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κεναῖσιν ἐλπίσιν θερμαίνεται</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 88"> <title>Ant.</title> 88</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">θερμὴν..καρδίαν</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σὺ_οὖν_891">: cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 114"> <title>Ai.</title> 114</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">σὺ δ’ οὖν, ἐπειδὴ τέρψις ἥδε σοι τὸ δρᾶν</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">χρῶ χειρί</quote>: <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 961 <quote xml:lang="grc">οἱ δ’ οὖν γελώντων.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῷ_λόγῳ_891">, causal dat., since <quote xml:lang="grc">εἴ σοί τις ἡδονή</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐστιν</quote>)=<quote xml:lang="grc">εἴ τι ἥδει</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_δὴ_892">: 317 n.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κατειδόμην_892">: for the midd., <ref n="Soph. El. 977">cp. 977</ref>: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 351"> <title>Ph.</title> 351</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀρχαῖον_τάφον_893">. The poet is thinking of an ancestral tomb, like those <quote xml:lang="grc">πατρῷα μνήματα</quote> at Athens in which members of the same <quote xml:lang="grc">γένος</quote> were buried, [Dem. ] or. 57 § 28. He may have conceived that the ashes of Agamemnon, after his corpse was burned (901 <quote xml:lang="grc">πυρᾶς</quote>), were deposited in a stone vault, above or beside which the tumulus (<quote xml:lang="grc">κολώνη</quote>) was raised. Pausanias saw ‘in the ruins of Mycenae’ a legendary <quote xml:lang="grc">τάφος</quote> of Agamemnon, and of others who were slain along with him (2. 16. § 6). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κολώνης_ἄκρας_894"> is best taken with <quote xml:lang="grc">νεορρύτους</quote>,—‘which had newly streamed from the top of the mound’; this suits <quote xml:lang="grc">πηγάς</quote>.—Others understand, ‘I saw <emph>on</emph> the top of the mound streams of milk which had been newly poured there,’ and compare 742 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξ ὀρθῶν δίφρων</quote>. (For <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκ</quote> in phrases describing position, cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 411"> <title>Ant.</title> 411</ref> n.)</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γάλακτος_894">: cp. <ref n="Eur. Orest. 114">Eur. <title>Or.</title> 114</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐλθοῦσα δ’ ἀμφὶ τὸν Κλυταιμνήστρας τάφον</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">μελίκρατ’ ἄφες γάλακτος οἰνωπόν τ’ ἄχνην</quote>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="περιστεφῆ_ἀνθέων_894">: cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 83"> <title>O. T.</title> 83</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πολυστεφὴς..δάφνης</quote> (n.).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔστιν_894">, not <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐστὶν</quote>, as the sense is, ‘exist.’</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θήκην_894">, here denoting the <quote xml:lang="grc">κολώνη</quote>, was a general term for a sepulchre, whatever its form might be; thus Plato says (<title>Legg.</title> 947 D) <quote xml:lang="grc">θήκην δὲ ὑπὸ γῆς αὐτοῖς εἰργασμένην εἶναι ψαλίδα</quote> (vault) <quote xml:lang="grc">προμήκη λίθων</quote>: and Thuc. applies the word in one place to movable grave-fittings or coffins (1. 8 § 1 <quote xml:lang="grc">θηκῶν ἀναιρεθεισῶν</quote>), in another to monuments (3. 58 § 4 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀποβλέψατε..ἐς πατέρων..θήκας</quote>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔσχον_θαῦμα_897">=<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐθαύμασα</quote>: <ref n="Soph. El. 214">cp. 214</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">γνώμαν ἴσχεις</quote>, 1176 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔσχες ἄλγος</quote>: conversely <ref n="Soph. Phil. 687"> <title>Ph.</title> 687</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">θαῦμά μ’ ἔχει.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐγχρίμπτῃ_897"> is here more fitting than </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐγχρίμπτει_897">, since the sense is, ‘in fear lest some one be close by,’ rather than, ‘to see whether some one is not close by’: <ref n="Soph. El. 581">cp. 581</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τιθῇς</quote> (n.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐν_γαλήνῃ_899"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄντα</quote>: <ref n="Soph. El. 61">cp. 61</ref>: <title>O.C.</title> 29 <quote xml:lang="grc">πέλας γὰρ ἄνδρα τόνδε νῷν ὁρῶ</quote>, and <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 586 (n.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐσχάτης_πυρᾶς_900">, ‘on the edge of the mound,’—the local gen., developed out of the possessive (‘belonging to,’ and so ‘in the region of’): cp. <ref n="Hom. Il. 9.219"> <title>Il.</title> 9. 219</ref>(<quote xml:lang="grc">ἷζεν</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">τοίχου τοῦ ἑτέροιο</quote>: 17. 372 <quote xml:lang="grc">νέφος δ’ οὐ φαι<*>νετο πάσης</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">γαίης οὐδ’ δρέων</quote>. The word <quote xml:lang="grc">πυρὰ</quote> can mean, not only a funeral pyre, but also the place where a corpse was formerly burned (<emph>bustum</emph>), and is here a synonym for <quote xml:lang="grc">τύμβος, κολώνη</quote>. So <ref n="Pind. I. 7">Pind. <title>I.</title> 7. 57</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πυρὰ<*> τάφον θ’</quote> (of Achilles): <ref n="Eur. Hec. 386">Eur. <title>Hec.</title> 386</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πυρὰν Ἀχιλλέως</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νεώρη_900"> (<quote xml:lang="grc">ὥρα</quote>), recent, fresh ( <ref n="Soph. OC 730"> <title>O. C.</title> 730</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">φόβον νεώρη</quote>); here equivalent to an adverb (<quote xml:lang="grc">νεωστί</quote>) qualifying <quote xml:lang="grc">τετμημένον</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐμπαίει_κτλ_902">: ‘there strikes, rushes, upon my soul a familiar image, (suggesting) that in this I see a sure token of Orestes.’ </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὄμμα_902"> is the ‘face’ or ‘form’ of her brother,</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σύνηθες_902">, because she was accustomed to think of him. The inf. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὁρᾶν_902">, explanatory of the first clause, depends on the idea of a conception or belief which is implied in the mental image of the face; <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἑμπαίει μοι ὄμμα</quote> implies <quote xml:lang="grc">παρίσταταί μοι δόξα</quote>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ψυχῇ_902"> is best taken with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμπαίει</quote>, though it could also go with <quote xml:lang="grc">σύνηθες</quote>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φάσμα_902"> (cr. n.) instead of <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄμμα</quote>,—a change which would grievously mar the passage. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βαστάσασα_905">. The aor. of this verb often denotes taking into the hand ( <ref n="Soph. Phil. 657"> <title>Ph.</title> 657</ref>, etc.), as the pres. (below, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1129"> Ph. 1129</ref>) denotes holding.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δυσφηυῶ_οὔ_905">: <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, she refrains from uttering any mournful or reproachful word, with reference to his long delay in coming. <ref n="Soph. El. 1182">Cp. 1182</ref>: <ref n="Eur. Hec. 181">Eur. <title>Hec.</title> 181</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ΕΚ. ὤμοι μοι, τέκνον</quote>. | <quote xml:lang="grc">ΠΟΛ. τί με δυσφημεῖς</quote>; —Others understand merely, ‘I utter no <emph>cry</emph> of joy.’ But such a cry would not be <quote xml:lang="grc">δύσφημος</quote> merely because she was at the tomb; in <ref n="Aesch. Lib. 235">Aesch. <title>Cho.</title> 235</ref> ff. Electra freely utters her joy there.—For the place of </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὔ_905">, cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 545">Soph. <title>Ai.</title> 545</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ταρβήσει γὰρ οὔ</quote>, and n. on <ref n="Soph. Ant. 96.">Soph. <title>Ant.</title> 96.</ref> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νῦν_τότ_907">: <ref n="Soph. El. 676">cp. 676</ref>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μή_του_907">. After a verb denoting confident belief, <quote xml:lang="grc">μή</quote>, not <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ</quote>, is usu. joined with the inf.: cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 1455"> <title>O. T.</title> 1455</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοσοῦτόν γ’ οἶδα, μήτε μ’ ἂν νόσον</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">μήτ’ ἄλλο πέρσαι μηδέν</quote> (n.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀγλάϊσμα_907">: the word used by the Electra of Aeschylus in the same context, <ref n="Aesch. Lib. 193"> <title>Cho.</title> 193</ref>, <quote xml:lang="grc">εἶναι τόδ’ ἀγλάϊσμά μοι τοῦ φιλτάτου</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">βροτῶν Ὀρέστου</quote>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πῶς_γὰρ_907"> (<foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔδρασας</quote>) is followed by the relat. pron. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ᾗ_γε_907">, with a causal force, as in <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1386"> <title>Ph.</title> 1386</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πῶς</quote> (<foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">φίλος εἶ</quote>), <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅς γε τοῖς ἐχθροῖσί μ’ ἐκδοῦναι θέλεις</quote>; This causal force is further marked here by the use of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μηδὲ_907"> instead of <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδέ</quote>,—‘one who is not allowed’ (cui ne ad deos quidem <emph>liceat</emph> egredi).— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρὸς_θεοὺς_907">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> to their shrines; cp. <ref n="Aesch. PB 530">Aesch. <title>P. V.</title> 530</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">θεοὺς ὁσίαις</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">θοίναις ποτινισσομένα</quote>. The reference is to the neighbouring Heraeum (v. 8), and to the altars or images of gods in front of the palace itself (<ref n="Soph. El. 637">637,</ref> <ref n="Soph. El. 1374">1374</ref>). So in <ref n="Eur. El. 310">Eur. <title>El.</title> 310</ref> Electra says that she is <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνέορτος ἱρῶν καὶ χορῶν τητωμένη</quote>. At Athens religious festivals were among the few occasions on which unmarried women could leave the house.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀκλαύστῳ_907">: so L here, though it supports <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄκλαυτος</quote> in the other places (<title>O. C.</title> 1708, <title>Ant.</title> 29, 847, 876). L also gives <quote xml:lang="grc">κλαυστὰ</quote> in <title>O. C.</title> 1360 (the only Sophoclean instance of that word). Cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">γνωστός</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">γνωτός</quote> (<title>O. T.</title> 361 n.).—For the sense, <emph>impune</emph>, cp. <title>O. T.</title> 401 <quote xml:lang="grc">κλαίων</quote> (n.), <title>Ph.</title> 1260 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκτὸς κλαυμάτων</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλ_δὴ_913">, rejecting an alternative, as <title>Tr.</title> 1128, <title>Ai.</title> 877. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὔτε_ἄν_914">. Elmsley (on Eur. <title>Med.</title> 416 f., p. 151) remarked that the Attic poets seldom elide the <quote xml:lang="grc">ε</quote> of the 3rd pers. sing. before <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄν</quote>: <foreign xml:lang="lat">e.g.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔγραψ’ ἄν</quote>, <emph>scripsissem</emph>, is easier to find than <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔγραψ’ ἄν</quote>, <emph>scripsisset.</emph> He does not add, however, that in respect to the weak (or ‘first’) aorists there was a reason for it, viz., that <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔγραψ’ ἄν</quote>, when meant as the 3rd pers., was liable to be confused with the 1st, unless the context was decisive; a reason which did not apply to the strong aorists or imperfects, <foreign xml:lang="lat">e.g.</foreign> to <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔλαθ’ ἄν</quote> or <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐλάνθαν’ ἄν</quote>. Yet, even in regard to the first aor., there is at least one instance where the MSS. attest the elision of <quote xml:lang="grc">ε</quote>, and the sense confirms it; Ar. <title>Plut.</title> 1011 <quote xml:lang="grc">ΓΡ...νηττάριον ἂν καὶ φάττιον ὑπεκορίζετο</quote>. | <quote xml:lang="grc">ΧΡ. ἔπειτ’ ἴσως ᾔτησ’ ἂν εἰς ὑποδήματα</quote>: where Elmsley's correction <quote xml:lang="grc">ᾔτησεν</quote> for <quote xml:lang="grc">ᾔτησ’ ἂν</quote>, though not impossible, is improbable. Take, again, Eur. <title>Ion</title> 354 <quote xml:lang="grc">σοὶ ταὐτὸν ἥβης, εἴπερ ἦν, εἶχ’ ἂν μέτρον</quote> (so the MSS.): where <quote xml:lang="grc">εἶχεν</quote> would be excessively harsh, while Elmsley's emendation, <quote xml:lang="grc">σοὶ ταὔτ’ ἂν..εἶχεν μέτρα</quote>, is clearly condemned by the plural.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐλάνθανεν_914">, the reading of the MSS., even tenable? Surely not. The sense required is: ‘Nor, if she had done it, would she have escaped notice.’ But the words <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔτε δρῶσ’ ἐλάνθανεν</quote> could mean only one of two things: (1) ‘nor, when she did it, <emph>was she escaping</emph> (or <emph>used she to escape</emph>) notice’: (2) ‘nor, when she did it, <emph>was she in the way to escape</emph> notice’; cp. Eur. <title>H. F.</title> 537 <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ τἄμ’ ἔθνῃσκε τέκν’, ἀπωλλύμην δ’ ἐγώ</quote>: ‘my children were in peril of death,’ etc. That is: whichever shade of meaning were given to <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐλάνθανεν</quote>, still </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δρῶσα_914">, in the absence of anything to mark conditionality, would imply, not <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ ἔδρα</quote>, but <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅτε ἔδρα</quote>. Before <quote xml:lang="grc">δρῶσα</quote> can mean <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ ἔδρα</quote>, we must have <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐλάνθαν’ ἄν</quote>, or some equivalent (as <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔμελλε λανθάνειν</quote>), in the apodosis. Conversely, before the simple <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐλάνθανεν</quote> could have a conditional sense, we should require as protasis, not <quote xml:lang="grc">δρῶσα</quote>, but <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ ἔδρα</quote>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐλάνθαν_ἄν_914"> were incorrect (which has not been proved), I should read </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔληθεν_ἄν_914"> (<ref n="Soph. El. 1359">cp. 1359</ref>).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τἀπιτίμια_914">, a word which elsewhere always means, ‘the price set’ upon a thing, and so, ‘the penalty’ of a deed: <ref n="Soph. El. 1382">cp. 1382</ref>. It cannot be explained as denoting ‘the dues’ paid by Orestes at the grave. Cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 901"> <title>Ant.</title> 901</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κἀπιτυμβίους</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">χοὰς ἔδωκα</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θάρσυνε_916">. The verb is not elsewhere intrans., but here follows the analogy of <quote xml:lang="grc">βραδύνω, κρατύνω, ταχύνω.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐχ_ἀεί_916">: cp. <ref n="Soph. Trach. 129"> <title>Tr.</title> 129</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπὶ πῆμα καὶ χαρὰ πᾶσι κυκλοῦσιν</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νῷν_ἧν_918">. The insertion after <quote xml:lang="grc">νῷν</quote> of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δ_918"> (omitted in L) seems no gain here.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κῦρος_918">: <ref n="Soph. OC 1779"> <title>O. C.</title> 1779</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φεῦ_ὥς_920"> <quote xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</quote> This punctuation is recommended by the order of the words: cp. <ref n="Eur. Phoen. 1425">Eur. <title>Phoen.</title> 1425</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">φεῦ φεῦ κακῶν σῶν, Οἰδίπου, σ’ ὅσον στένω</quote>. Others place a comma after <quote xml:lang="grc">φεῦ</quote>, and take <quote xml:lang="grc">τῆς ἀνοίας</quote> with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐποικτίρω</quote>: but in the passages which might seem similar the verb <emph>precedes</emph> the gen., as <ref n="Aesch. PB 397">Aesch. <title>P. V.</title> 397</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">στένω σε τᾶς οὐλομένας τύχας</quote>: <ref n="Aesch. Ag. 1321">Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 1321</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἰκτίρω σε θεσφάτου μόρου</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅποι_φέρει_922"> (cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 1309"> <title>O. T.</title> 1309</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ποῖ γᾶς φέρομαι</quote>..; ), in a figurative sense, —‘you know not into what regions your thoughts are straying,’—<foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, how far from realities: <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅποι γνώμης</quote> explains this. <ref n="Soph. El. 390">Cp. 390</ref>; and a proverb cited by G. Wolff from Apostolius <title>Paroem.</title> 14. 57 <quote xml:lang="grc">ποῦ γῆς ἢ θαλάττης ὑπῆρχες; ἐπὶ τῶν ἀνοήτων</quote> (as we say, ‘to be at sea’). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πῶς_κάτοιδ_923">; cp. n. on <ref n="Soph. Phil. 250"> <title>Ph.</title> 250</ref>, <quote xml:lang="grc">πῶς γὰρ κάτοιδ’</quote>, where <quote xml:lang="grc">κάτοιδα</quote> answers to <quote xml:lang="grc">οἶσθα</quote> (as here) in the line before. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τἀκείνου_σωτήρια_924">, such means of deliverance as he could afford, —like <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐλπίδες τινός</quote>, hopes <emph>from</emph> one (857 f., n.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_ποῦ_928">; for <quote xml:lang="grc">καί</quote> prefixed to the interrogative word, <ref n="Soph. El. 236">cp. 236</ref>: <ref n="Soph. OC 263"> <title>O. C.</title> 263</ref> n.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὑπέρχεται_928">: <ref n="Soph. El. 1112">cp. 1112</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μητρὶ_929"> goes with both adjectives; for its place, cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1399"> <title>O. C.</title> 1399</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἴμοι κελεύθου τῆς τ’ ἐμῆς δυσπραξίας</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1155"> <title>Ant.</title> 1155</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">Κάδμου πάροικοι καὶ δόμων Ἀμφίονος</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὰ_πολλὰ_931">: for the art., <ref n="Soph. El. 564">cp. 564</ref>.— </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρὸς_τάφον_931">, not <quote xml:lang="grc">πρὸς τάφῳ</quote>, since the thought is, <quote xml:lang="grc">τίς προσήνεγκε ταῦτα</quote>; </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μάλιστ_932">, as the most probable explanation: cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 617"> <title>Ph.</title> 617</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἴοιτο μὲν μάλισθ’ ἑκούσιον λαβών</quote>: and <ref n="Soph. OC 1298"> <title>O. C.</title> 1298</ref> n.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μνημεἶ_932">, predicative. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὦ_δυστυχής_934">: <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐγώ</quote>: so <ref n="Soph. Trach. 377"> <title>Tr.</title> 377</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ δύστηνος</quote> (n.).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοιούσδ_934">, so joyful. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἵν_ἄτης_936">: cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 367"> <title>O. T.</title> 367</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἵν’ εἶ κακοῦ</quote>: <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 1442 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἵν’ ἕσταμεν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">χρείας</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λύσεις_939">, do away with, remove; cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1615"> <title>O. C.</title> 1615</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ ἓν γὰρ μόνον</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ πάντα λύει ταῦτ’ ἔπος μοχθήματα</quote>. Eur. fr. 573 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλγηδόνας δὲ ταῦτα κουφίζει φρενῶν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ καρδίας ἔλυσε τοὺς ἄγαν πόνους</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐκ_εἶπον_941">, ‘It is not quite what I meant’;—said with a gentle and mournful irony, which the next words, <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ γὰρ ὧδ’ ἄφρων ἔφυν</quote>, further mark. Electra is very gradually leading up to a proposal which, as she well knows, will dismay her sister; whose question—<quote xml:lang="grc">ἦ τοὺς θανόντας κ.τ.λ.</quote>—shows how far she is from conceiving that the present situation leaves any possibilities of action. To reject this verse as being (in Nauck's phrase) ‘undoubtedly a later addition,’ would be indeed to destroy a fine dramatic touch.— The form of the first clause recalls <ref n="Soph. Phil. 442"> <title>Ph.</title> 442</ref>, <quote xml:lang="grc">ο<*> τοῦτον εῖπον</quote>: though here <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ τοῦτό γ’ εἶπον</quote> would be a crude substitute for the reading in the text. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φερέγγυος_942">, ‘able to give security,’ and so ‘competent’ for a purpose, stands with a gen. only here, the usual constr. being the inf. (as <ref n="Aesch. Eum. 87">Aesch. <title>Eum.</title> 87</ref>); but <ref n="Thuc. 8. 68">Thuc. 8. 68</ref> has <quote xml:lang="grc">πρὸς τὰ δεινὰ...φερεγγυώτατος</quote>. Cp. <title>Anthol. Pal.</title> 10. 56. 11 <quote xml:lang="grc">σωφροσύνης τρόπος οὗτος ἐχέγγυος</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τλῆναι_δρῶσαν_943">: cp. <ref n="Aesch. Seven 754">Aesch. <title>Theb.</title> 754</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">σπείρας</quote>.. | ..<quote xml:lang="grc">ἔτλα</quote>: <ref n="Aesch. Ag. 1041">Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 1041</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πραθέντα τλῆναι</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὠφέλειά_γ_944">: the particle, which has been questioned (cr. n.), implies her belief that these counsels will prove <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνωφελῆ</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅρα_945">, an impressive warning that the task about to be mentioned is a great one; cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 587"> <title>O. C.</title> 587</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅρα γε μήν· οὐ σμικρός, οὔχ, ἁγὼν ὅδε.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πόνου_εὐτυχεῖ_945">: cp. Eur. fr. 233 <quote xml:lang="grc">σοὶ δ’ εἶπον, ὦ παῖ, τὰς τύχας ἐκ τῶν πόνων</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">θηρᾶν</quote>: <emph>id.</emph> fr. 432 <quote xml:lang="grc">τῷ γὰρ πονοῦντι καὶ θεὸς συλλαμβάνει</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ξυνοίσω_946">, share the burden, help; cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 627"> <title>Ph.</title> 627</ref> n. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ποεῖν_947">. The <emph>v.l.</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">τελεῖν</quote> (cr. n.) is less fitting here than the simpler word. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παρουσίαν_948"> here implies the notion of ‘support,’ ‘aid,’ as <quote xml:lang="grc">παρεῖναι</quote> often does ( <ref n="Soph. Phil. 373"> <title>Ph.</title> 373</ref> n.).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_που_948">: <ref n="Soph. El. 55">cp. 55</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λελείμμεθον_950"> is the only classical instance of a 1st pers. dual except <quote xml:lang="grc">περιδώμεθον</quote> in <ref n="Hom. Il. 23.485"> <title>Il.</title> 23. 485</ref>, and <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁρμώμεθον</quote> in <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1079"> <title>Ph.</title> 1079</ref>, where I have discussed these forms, and given my reasons for thinking that at least the two Sophoclean examples are genuine.—Cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 58"> <title>Ant.</title> 58</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μόνα δὴ νὼ λελειμμένα</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θάλλοντ_ἔτ_952"> is Reiske's certain correction of <quote xml:lang="grc">θάλλοντά τ’</quote>. Hermann, retaining the latter, followed Triclinius in taking </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βίῳ_952"> as=<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν βίῳ ὄντα</quote>, ‘alive,’ which is clearly untenable; as is also the conjecture <quote xml:lang="grc">βιοῦν</quote>.—Cp. <ref n="Soph. Trach. 235"> <title>Tr.</title> 235</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ ζῶντα καὶ θάλλοντα</quote> (n.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πράκτοῤ_953">, one who exacts a penalty; an avenger: as the Erinyes are <quote xml:lang="grc">πράκτορες αἵματος</quote> ( <ref n="Aesch. Eum. 319">Aesch. <title>Eum.</title> 319</ref>). At Athens the <quote xml:lang="grc">πράκτορες</quote> were officials who collected fines and penalties (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπιβολαί, τιμήματα</quote>) imposed by law. We have a similar figurative use of a technical term in <ref n="Soph. Aj. 508">Soph. <title>Ai.</title> 508</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μητέρα</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">πολλῶν ἐτῶν κληροῦχον</quote>: and in <ref n="Aesch. Eum. 273">Aesch. <title>Eum.</title> 273</ref> the word for an ‘auditor’ who examined the accounts of magistrates is applied to the king of the nether world; <quote xml:lang="grc">μέγας γὰρ Ἅιδης ἐστὶν εὔθυνος βροτῶν</quote>. There are Shakespearian parallels; <foreign xml:lang="lat">e.g.</foreign>, Lear (<ref n="Shak. LR 3.2">act 3, sc. 2, 59</ref>) calls the raging elements ‘these dreadful summoners’ (officers who warned offenders to appear in court): Hamlet (<ref n="Shak. HAM 5.2">act 5, sc. 2, Aesch. Eum. 347</ref>) speaks of ‘this fell sergeant, death’: and death is ‘that fell arrest without all bail’ (<ref n="Shak. SON lxxiv.1"> <title>Sonn.</title> 74, 1</ref>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰς_βλέπω_954">: here <quote xml:lang="grc">δὴ</quote>, after <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡνίκ’ οὐκέτ’ ἔστιν</quote>, marks the next resource, just as in <ref n="Soph. Ant. 173"> <title>Ant.</title> 173</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐγὼ κράτη δὴ..ἔχω</quote> follows <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅτ’ ο<*>ν ἐκεῖνοι..ὤλοντο</quote>. Thus it is here rather an equivalent for <quote xml:lang="grc">ἤδη</quote> than merely a mode of emphasising <quote xml:lang="grc">σέ</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κατοκνήσεις_956">. The clause <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅπως κατοκνήσεις</quote> denotes the object of the appeal implied in <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰς σὲ δὴ βλέπω</quote>. Cp. <ref n="Aristoph. Kn. 1255">Ar. <title>Eq.</title> 1255</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">καί σ’ αἰτῶ βραχύ</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅπως ἔσομαί σοι Φανός</quote>. This is the normal construction. The <emph>v. l.</emph> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κατοκνήσῃς_956"> is also correct, but is less probable. Any verb can be followed by a ‘final’ clause in the <emph>subjunctive</emph>, expressing the ‘end’ or purpose of the action, as <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔρχεται <*>να ἴδῃ</quote>. But a verb of endeavouring, praying, contriving, usually takes an ‘object’ clause, expressing the object of the effort, with <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅπως</quote> (or <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς</quote>) and <emph>fut. indic.</emph>: as <quote xml:lang="grc">πειρᾶται ὅπως ὄψεται</quote>. Exceptions occur, as <ref n="Xen. Anab. 5. 6.">Xen. <title>An.</title> 5. 6.</ref>§ 21 <quote xml:lang="grc">προστατεῦσαι..ὅπως ἐκπλεύσῃ ἡ στρατιά</quote>: <ref n="Dem. 1.2">Dem. or. 1 § 2</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">παρασκευάσασθαι..ὅπως ἐνθένδε βοηθήσητε καὶ μὴ πάθητε ταὐτόν</quote>: but these are comparatively rare. After a verb of effort, the presumption is always in favour of the fut. indic. See Goodwin, <title>Moods and Tenses</title> (new ed.), § 364.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κρύπτειν_957">: cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 915"> <title>Ph.</title> 915</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδέν σε κρύψω</quote>.—Nauck follows Wunder in rejecting this verse, because (1) <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν αὐτόχειρα</quote> can mean no one but Aegisthus; (2) <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδὲν γάρ σε δεῖ κρύπτειν μ’ ἔτι</quote> would have point only if Chrysothemis did not already know the guilt of Aegisthus; (3) <quote xml:lang="grc">σε</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">με</quote> cause ambiguity. We may reply: (1) The mention of the murderer's name, which Electra has hitherto uttered only in her solitary lament (98), is forcible here; and the emphatic place given to it is in the manner of Sophocles (<ref n="Soph. El. 1507">cp. 1507</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κτείνειν</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 46"> <title>Ant.</title> 46</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀδελφόν</quote>). (2) The words <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδὲν γάρ σε κ.τ.λ.</quote> refer, of course, to the purpose which Electra now discloses—not to the guilt of Aegisthus. (3) The formal ambiguity of <quote xml:lang="grc">σε..με</quote> is only such as occurs in <ref n="Soph. Ant. 288"> <title>Ant.</title> 288</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἢ τοὺς κακοὺς τιμῶντας εἰσορᾷς θεούς</quote>;</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ποῖ_μενεῖς_958">. For <quote xml:lang="grc">ποῖ</quote> as =<quote xml:lang="grc">μέχρι τίνος, εἰς τίνα χρόνον</quote>, cp. <ref n="Aristoph. Lys. 526">Ar. <title>Lys.</title> 526</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ποῖ γὰρ καὶ χρῆν ἀναμεῖναι</quote>; It is also possible to join <quote xml:lang="grc">ποῖ</quote> with <quote xml:lang="grc">βλέψασα</quote>: ‘to what quarter—to what hope— can you look?’ But the order of the words is certainly against that.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βλέψας_958">: <ref n="Soph. El. 888">cp. 888</ref>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κτῆσιν_960">, which could depend on <quote xml:lang="grc">στένειν</quote>, is perhaps best taken with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐστερημένῃ_960">. Though the simple <quote xml:lang="grc">στερεῖσθαι</quote> (as distinguished from <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀποστερεῖσθαι</quote>) is not usually joined with an acc., there is at least one instance, <title>Helen.</title> 95 <quote xml:lang="grc">πῶς; οὔ τί που σῷ φασγάνῳ βίον στερείς</quote>; </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐς_χρόνου_961">: <ref n="Soph. El. 14">cp. 14</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοσόνδ’ ἐς ἥβης</quote>. For <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ χρόνος</quote> as=one's term of life, cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 461"> <title>Ant.</title> 461</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ δὲ τοῦ χρόνου</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">πρόσθεν θανοῦμαι</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄλεκτρα_ἀνυμέναια_962">, as <ref n="Soph. Ant. 917"> <title>Ant.</title> 917</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄλεκτρον, ἀνυμέναιον</quote>. The inverse order of words would be more natural, as the <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑμέναιος</quote> escorted the bride and bridegroom to their home. <ref n="Soph. El. 164">Cp. 164</ref> f., and 187.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γηράσκουσαν_962">, acc. with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλγεῖν</quote>, while <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐστερημένῃ</quote> (960) depends on <quote xml:lang="grc">πάρεστι</quote>. Cp. <ref n="Eur. Med. 1236">Eur. <title>Med.</title> 1236</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">δέδοκται τοὔργον ὡς τάχιστά μοι</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">παῖδας κτανούσῃ τῆσδ’ ἀφορμᾶσθαι χθονός</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ μὴ σχολὴν ἄγουσαν ἐκδοῦναι τέκνα</quote>. The word <quote xml:lang="grc">γηράσκουσαν</quote>, like Electra's phrase <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ πολὺς.. βίοτος</quote> in 185 ff., must be taken relatively to the ordinary age for marriage. Plato lays it down that a woman should not marry before sixteen or after twenty: <quote xml:lang="grc">γάμου δὲ ὅρον εἶναι κόρῃ ἀπὸ ἑκκαίδεκα ἐτῶν εἰς εἴκοσι τὸν μακρότατον χρόνον ἀφωρισμένον</quote> (<title>Legg.</title> 785 B)—a good comment on the words of Aristophanes, <quote xml:lang="grc">τῆς δὲ γυναικὸς σμικρὸς <*> καιρός</quote> ( <ref n="Aristoph. Lys. 596"> <title>Lys.</title> 596</ref>). It would suit the data to suppose that Electra was about twenty-five, and her sister a little younger. Cp. <title>Helen.</title> 283 <quote xml:lang="grc">θυγάτηρ ἄνανδρος πολιὰ παρθενεύεται</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῶνδε_963">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">λέκτρων καὶ ὑμεναίων</quote>: as in <ref n="Soph. Trach. 260"> <title>Tr.</title> 260</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τόνδε</quote> refers to the preceding phrase, <quote xml:lang="grc">πόλιν τὴν Εὐρυτείαν.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅπως_963">: cp. <ref n="Eur. Heraclid. 1051">Eur. <title>Heracl.</title> 1051</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μὴ γὰρ ἐλπίσῃς ὅπως</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">αὖθις πατρῴας ζῶν ἔμ’ ἐκβαλεῖς χθονός</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἢ_κἀμὸν_965">, ‘or mine either.’— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πημονὴν_965">, acc. in apposition with the sentence: <ref n="Soph. El. 130">cp. 130</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">παραμύθιον</quote>: 564 <quote xml:lang="grc">ποινάς</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπίσπῃ_967">: <ref n="Soph. El. 1052">cp. 1052</ref>: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 636"> <title>Ant.</title> 636</ref>(<quote xml:lang="grc">γνώμας</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">αἷς ἔγωγ’ ἐφέψομαι</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εὐσέβειαν_οἴσει_968">: cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 924"> <title>Ant.</title> 924</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὴν δυσσέβειαν εὐσεβοῦσ’ ἐκτησάμην</quote> (n.): and for <quote xml:lang="grc">οἴσει..ἐκ</quote>, <ref n="Soph. Trach. 461"> <title>Tr.</title> 461</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κοὔπω τις αὐτῶν ἔκ γ’ ἐμοῦ λόγον κακὸν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἠνέγκατ’ οὐδ’ ὄνειδος</quote>.—As </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θανόντος_968"> here denotes the state of the dead, and not the act of dying, </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κάτω_968"> can be joined to it; though it would have been clearer if the art. had been added, as in <ref n="Eur. Orest. 674">Eur. <title>Or.</title> 674</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν κατὰ χθονὸς</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">θανόντ’ ἀκούειν τάδε δόκει</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐξέφυς_968"> implies the stock from which she sprang, as in <ref n="Soph. OT 1084"> <title>O. T.</title> 1084</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοιόσδε δ’ ἐκφύς</quote>, ‘such being my lineage.’ It is usu. joined with a genitive.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καλεῖ_968">: this fut. midd. (used by <title>Nub.</title> 1221 and <ref n="Aristoph. Eccl. 864"> <title>Eccl.</title> 864</ref>) seems to occur only here in a pass. sense; the fut. pass. is usu. <quote xml:lang="grc">κεκλήσομαι</quote>, more rarely <quote xml:lang="grc">κληθήσομαι.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὰ_χρηστὰ_968">=<quote xml:lang="grc">τὰς χρηστάς</quote>: <ref n="Soph. El. 1507">cp. 1507</ref>: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 448"> <title>Ph.</title> 448</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ μὲν πανοῦργα καὶ παλιντριβῆ...τὰ δὲ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">δίκαια καὶ τὰ χρηστά</quote>. With <quote xml:lang="grc"> <emph>ὁρᾶν πρός</emph> τι</quote>, cp. <title>Alcib.</title> I. p. 134 D <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰς τὸ θεῖον καὶ λαμπρὸν ὁρῶντες</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λόγων_εὔκλειαν_973">, lit., ‘as to fame, <emph>however,</emph>’ <quote xml:lang="grc">γε μήν</quote> merely marking that the speaker turns to a new point. (Not, ‘as to fame, <emph>at all events</emph>,’—<foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, even if the other rewards are <emph>not</emph> won.) Cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 587"> <title>O. C.</title> 587</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅρα γε μήν</quote>,—the only other instance of <quote xml:lang="grc">γε μήν</quote> in Sophocles. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λόγων_973"> implies a contrast with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔργα</quote>,—<foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, the substantial gains mentioned in 971 f. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀστῶν_ξένων_975">. As in <ref n="Soph. OT 1489"> <title>O. T.</title> 1489</ref> ff., the poet is thinking of festivals or spectacles at which Athenian women could appear in public, when many visitors from other cities were present.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δεξιώσεται_975">, properly, to give the right hand to one in welcome; Aeschin. or. 3 § 87 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ νυνὶ πάντας δεξιούμενος καὶ προσγελῶν</quote>: <title>Plut.</title> 752 (<quote xml:lang="grc">οἱ γὰρ δίκαιοι</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτὸν ἠσπάζοντο καὶ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐδεξιοῦνθ’ ἅπαντες</quote>: then, generally, ‘to greet’; <ref n="Paus. 2. 16. 2">Paus. 2. 16. 2</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἰδεῖν ἤθελε τὸν γονέα τῆς μητρός, καὶ λόγοις τε χρηστοῖς καὶ ἔργοις δεξιώσασθαι</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἴδεσθε_977">: cp. Haemon's report of the popular praises called forth by Antigone's deed, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 692"> <title>Ant.</title> 692</ref>—699. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εὖ_βεβηκόσιν_979">: <ref n="Soph. El. 1057">cp. 1057</ref>: <ref n="Hdt. 7. 164">Her. 7. 164</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τυραννίδα...εὖ βεβηκυῖαν.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀφειδήσαντε_979">, fem. (<ref n="Soph. El. 1003">cp. 1003</ref>, 1006). The properly feminine form of the dual in participles of the third declension is actually rare, though it was certainly in use (thus <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐχούσα</quote> occurs in an Attic inscr. of 398 B.C.). See appendix on <ref n="Soph. OC 1676"> <title>O. C.</title> 1676</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἰδόντε καὶ παθούσα</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προὐστήτην_φόνου_979">, lit., ‘became minis ters of bloodshed.’ <quote xml:lang="grc">προστῆναί τινος</quote> is properly to place oneself ‘in front of,’ or ‘at the head of,’—a general sense which yields several shades of meaning; thus <ref n="Eur. Andr. 220">Eur. <title>Andr.</title> 220</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">χείρον’ ἀρσένων νόσον</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ταύτην νοσοῦμεν, ἀλλὰ προὔστημεν καλῶς</quote>, ‘we are wont to <emph>control</emph> it well’: <ref n="Soph. Aj. 803">Soph. <title>Ai.</title> 803</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πρόστητ’ ἀναγκαίας τύχης</quote>, ‘<emph>shelter</emph> it’: Aeschin. or. 2 § 161 <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν προστάντα τῆς εἰρήνης</quote>, ‘the champion’ of it. Here, the presence of the dat. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐχθροῖς_979"> serves to blend the sense of ‘administering’ required by </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φόνου_979"> with that of ‘standing forth’ to confront an adversary; cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1133">Soph. <title>Ai.</title> 1133</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἦ σοὶ γὰρ Αἴας πολέμιος προὔστη ποτέ</quote>;</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τούτω_τώδε_979">, cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 841"> <title>Ph.</title> 841</ref> n.—As dist. from </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἑορταῖς_979">, the phrase </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πανδήμῳ_πόλει_979"> denotes any gathering of the citizens, as in the ordinary intercourse of the agora; cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 1489"> <title>O. T.</title> 1489</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ποίας γὰρ ἀστῶν ἥξετ’ εἰς ὁμιλίας</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">ποίας δ’ ἑορτάς</quote>..; Similarly Lucian <title>Toxaris</title> 1 <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ ἑορταῖς καὶ πανηγύρεσι τιμῶμεν αὐτούς</quote>. For </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοι_979"> introducing the final comment, cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 776"> <title>Ai.</title> 776</ref>(n.). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="συμπόνει_986"> and </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σύγκαμνε_986"> are here synonymous, though the idea of effort is more prominent in the former, and that of distress in the latter. Instead of repeating a word, the poet often thus uses a synonym; <ref n="Soph. El. 1308">cp. 1308</ref> f. <quote xml:lang="grc">στέγας ..οἴκοις</quote>: <ref n="Soph. OT 54"> <title>O. T.</title> 54</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄρξεις...κρατεῖς</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 669"> <title>Ant.</title> 669</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">καλῶς...εὖ</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 457"> <title>Tr.</title> 457</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">δέδοικας...ταρβεῖς</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀδελφῇ_986"> (<foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> Electra herself), which not only eliminates all thought of Orestes, but also destroys the antithesis marked by </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐμέ_986">. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παῦσον_κακῶν_986">: <ref n="Soph. El. 231">cp. 231</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκ καμάτων ἀποπαύσομαι</quote>. For the aor. imper. combined with the pres., cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 507"> <title>Ai.</title> 507</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">αἴδεσαι</quote>, <ref n="Soph. Aj. 510"> Ai. 510</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἴκτιρε</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῖς_πεφυκόσιν_986"> both senses of ‘noble’ are involved, just as in <ref n="Soph. Trach. 721"> <title>Tr.</title> 721</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ζῆν γὰρ κακῶς κλύουσαν οὐκ ἀνασχετόν</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἥτις προτιμᾷ μὴ κακὴ πεφυκέναι</quote>. Cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 479"> <title>Ai.</title> 479</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ ἢ καλῶς ζῆν ἢ καλῶς τεθνηκέναι</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν εὐγενῆ χρή</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐν_τοιούτοις_990"> <quote xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</quote> These words of the Chorus, though neutral in tone (like their words at v. 369), imply that Electra's plan is over-bold, and Chrys. speaks as if sure of their approval; which, indeed, she receives at v. 1015.— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_κλύοντι_990">: for the omission of the art., <ref n="Soph. El. 1498">cp. 1498</ref>: <ref n="Aesch. Ag. 324">Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 324</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τῶν ἁλόντων καὶ κρατησάντων</quote>: and n. on <ref n="Soph. OC 606.">Soph. <title>O. C.</title> 606.</ref>— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σύμμαχος_990"> here merely=<quote xml:lang="grc">σύμφορος</quote>, helpful. The figurative sense of the word is usually closer to the literal; as in <ref n="Antiph. 5.43">Antiphon or. 5 § 43</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ εἰκὸς σύμμαχόν μοί ἐστιν</quote> (‘is on my side’).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μὴ_990">, cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 66"> <title>Ph.</title> 66</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ δ’ ἐργάσει</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">μὴ ταῦτα</quote> (n.).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐσῴζετ_990">, ‘remembered’: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 682"> <title>Tr.</title> 682</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐμβλέψασα_995"> is a slightly strengthened <quote xml:lang="grc">βλέψασα</quote>, implying a more intent gaze; cp. <ref n="Plat. Ion 535">Plat. <title>Ion</title> 535</ref>E <quote xml:lang="grc">δεινὸν ἐμβλέποντας</quote>, ‘with stern countenances.’— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θράσος_ὁπλίζει_995">: the acc. as with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀμφιέννυσθαι</quote>, etc.: Schneidewin cp. <title>Anthol. Pal.</title> 5. 93 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὥπλισμαι πρὸς Ἔρωτα περὶ στέρνοισι λογισμόν</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰσορᾷς_997">: <ref n="Soph. El. 584">cp. 584</ref>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γυνὴ_μὲν_997"> <foreign xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</foreign>: as Ismene, too, reminds her sister ( <ref n="Soph. Ant. 61"> <title>Ant.</title> 61</ref>).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σθένεις_χερί_997">: this is not merely an amplification of <quote xml:lang="grc">γυνὴ μὲν κ.τ.λ.</quote>, meaning that a woman's arm is weaker than a man's, but refers to the fighting forces at the disposal of the rulers: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Eur. El. 629">Eur. <title>El.</title> 629</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἰκεία..χείρ</quote> </cit>, a band of domestics. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀπορρεῖ_1000">, like water that runs off; cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 523"> <title>Ai.</title> 523</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπορρεῖ μνῆστις</quote>: <ref n="Verg. A. 2. 169">Verg. <title>Aen.</title> 2. 169</ref> <title>Ex illo fluere ac retro sublapsa referri</title> | <title>Spes Danaum.</title> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κἀπὶ_ἔρχεται_1000">: so fr. 788. 8 (the waning moon) <quote xml:lang="grc">πάλιν διαρρεῖ κἀπὶ μηδὲν ἔρχεται</quote>. More often the art. is added, as in <ref n="Eur. Hec. 622">Eur. <title>Hec.</title> 622</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς ἐς τὸ μηδὲν ἥκομεν</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοιοῦτον_1001">, so strong and so prosperous.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄλυπος_ἄτης_1001">: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 786"> <title>O. C.</title> 786</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κακῶν ἄνατος</quote>: <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 1519 <quote xml:lang="grc">γήρως ἄλυπα</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κτησώμεθ_1004">: <ref n="Soph. El. 217">cp. 217</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πολὺ γάρ τι κακῶν ὑπερεκτήσω</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λύει_1005"> here=‘set free,’ ‘extricate’ from trouble, and therefore takes the acc.: cp. <ref n="Soph. Trach. 181"> <title>Tr.</title> 181</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄκνου σε λύσω</quote>. Those who change </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἡμᾶς_1005"> to </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἡμῖν_1005"> assume that <quote xml:lang="grc">λύει</quote>=‘profits.’ The schol. gives the sense rightly: <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδὲν ἡμᾶς ἐκλύσεται</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δυσκλεῶς_θανεῖν_1005">, a death of ignominy, <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, such as is appointed for malefactors. The <quote xml:lang="grc">βάξις καλὴ</quote> from admiring citizens and foreigners (975) will poorly compensate for the doom which Aegisthus can inflict. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐ_θανεῖν_1007"> <quote xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</quote>: here </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γὰρ_1007"> refers to <quote xml:lang="grc">δυσκλεῶς</quote> in 1006: ‘(a death <emph>of ignominy</emph>, I say,) <emph>for</emph> mere death is not the worst that we have to fear; we shall suffer a lingering death, and shall long in vain to be put out of our misery.’ Cp. Creon's threat to the guard,—<quote xml:lang="grc">οὐχ ὑμὶν Ἅιδης μοῦνος ἀρκέσει, κ.τ.λ.</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. Ant. 308"> <title>Ant.</title> 308</ref>),—and the slow death in the vault which he designed for Antigone (<foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 773 ff.).—The rejection of these two verses (cr. n.) is absolutely groundless. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κἀξερημῶσαι_γένος_1010">: cp. <ref n="Dem. 43.73">Dem. or. 43 § 73</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπιμέλειαν ἐποιησάμην τοῦ οἴκου τοῦ Ἁγνίου ὅπως μὴ ἐξερημωθήσεται</quote>: and <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> § 76 <quote xml:lang="grc">πῶς ἂν μᾶλλον ἐξερημώσαιεν ἄνθρωποι οἶκον</quote>; <ref n="Antiph. 5.11">Antiphon or. 5 § 11</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξώλειαν αὑτῷ καὶ γένει καὶ οἰκίᾳ ἐπαρώμενον</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄρρητα_κἀτελῆ_1012">, she will not divulge them, nor act upon them; they will be <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀτελῆ</quote>, as finding no accomplishment, and therefore doing no harm. The dat. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σοι_1012"> implies that this is for Electra's own interest. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλὰ_ποτέ_1013">: for <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλὰ</quote> <ref n="Soph. El. 411">cp. 411</ref>. The same phrase occurs in <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1041"> <title>Ph.</title> 1041</ref>: in <ref n="Soph. Trach. 201"> <title>Tr.</title> 201</ref>, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλὰ σὺν χρόνῳ</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πείθου_1015"> and </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πιθοῦ_1015"> is simply that the pres. implies a mental process (‘be persuaded,’ ‘allow the reasoning to weigh with thee’), while the aor. denotes an act (‘obey,’ ‘comply,’ —do the thing recommended). <quote xml:lang="grc">πείθου</quote>, as more suggestive of gentle entreaty, seems a little the better here; while <quote xml:lang="grc">πιθοῦ</quote> is more fitting in v. 1207. An instance in which metre confirms <quote xml:lang="grc">πείθου</quote> is <ref n="Soph. OC 520."> <title>O. C.</title> 520.</ref>Our MSS. are precarious guides on such a point: <quote xml:lang="grc">πείθου</quote> has the better authority here, but so it has also in 1207 and in <ref n="Soph. Trach. 470"> <title>Tr.</title> 470</ref>, where <quote xml:lang="grc">πιθοῦ</quote> is preferable. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καλῶς_δ_1017">: for the elision at the end of the v., see on <ref n="Soph. OT 29."> <title>O. T.</title> 29.</ref>— <quote xml:lang="grc"> <emph>ἁπηγγελλόμην</emph>, ἃ ἐπηγγελλόμην</quote>. Sophocles uses <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀγγέλλομαι</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1376"> <title>Ai.</title> 1376</ref>) and <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξαγγέλλομαι</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. OT 148."> <title>O. T.</title> 148.</ref> etc.) in a similar sense. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὐτόχειρί_μοι_1019">. The older editions (including Brunck's) have the adverb <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτοχειρί</quote> (Lycurgus § 122). Porson restored the adj. here, and (with the Ald.) in <ref n="Eur. Orest. 1040">Eur. <title>Or.</title> 1040</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ αὐτόχειρι θνῄσχ’ ὅτῳ βούλει τρόπῳ.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μόνῃ_τε_1019">: contrast <ref n="Soph. Trach. 1194"> <title>Tr.</title> 1194</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτόχειρα καὶ ξὺν οἷς χρῄζεις φίλων.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐ_δὴ_1019"> rejects the alternative which </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γε_1019"> emphasises, as in <ref n="Soph. OC 110"> <title>O. C.</title> 110</ref>, <ref n="Soph. OC 265"> O. C. 265</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 246."> <title>Ph.</title> 246.</ref> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κενόν_1019">, ‘void,’ since <quote xml:lang="grc">τοὔργον τόδ’</quote> refers to what is still only a project. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἴθ_ὤφελες_1021">: <ref n="Soph. El. 1131">cp. 1131</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς ὤφελον</quote> (n.). The ironical wish is a way of expressing how insane she thinks Electra's present design: ‘It is a pity that thy courage was not shown in preventing, rather than avenging, our father's murder.’</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πᾶν_κατειργάσω_1021">, lit., ‘thou wouldst have achieved anything’; nothing would have been too hard for thee,—even to defeat the murderous conspiracy. The MS. reading, <quote xml:lang="grc">πάντα γὰρ</quote> <quote xml:lang="grc">κατειργάσω</quote>, is impossible: <quote xml:lang="grc">ἂν</quote> could not be omitted here. [Campbell defends that reading by <ref n="Thuc. 8. 86.">Thuc. 8. 86.</ref>§ 4, <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡρμημένων γὰρ τῶν ἐν Σάμῳ Ἀθηναίων πλεῖν ἐπὶ σφᾶς αὐτούς, ἐν ᾧ σαφέστατα Ἰωνίαν καὶ Ἑλλήσποντον εὐθὺς εἶχον οἱ πολέμιοι, κ.τ.λ.</quote> There, however, (<emph>a</emph>) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν ᾧ</quote>, ‘<emph>in which case</emph>,’ serves at once to suggest the conditional sense; and (<emph>b</emph>) <quote xml:lang="grc">εἶχον</quote> is an imperf., not an aorist (<ref n="Soph. El. 914">cp. 914</ref> n.). Stahl, indeed, reads <quote xml:lang="grc">σαφέστατ’ ἂν</quote>, with Dobree: but that does not seem necessary.] </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φύσιν_γε_1023">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, in loyalty,—as she proved by saving her brother; though she had not then the ripe intelligence to grasp the whole situation, or to form a plan for averting the crime. The retort of Chrysothemis shows that she feels the reproach to herself implied by </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τότε_1023">. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡς_συνδράσουσα_1025"> <quote xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</quote>: ‘You wish that I were still <quote xml:lang="grc">ἥσσων νοῦν</quote>, <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, incapable of forming such a plan as that on which I now propose to act. This is a hint that you will not act with me.’</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νουθετεῖς_1025"> has the same tone as <quote xml:lang="grc">νουθετήματα</quote> in 343. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰκὸς_γὰρ_1026"> <quote xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</quote>: ‘(I will not act with thee), for it is likely that one who makes the attempt should e'en (<quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ</quote>) fare ill.’ It is perhaps best to take </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐγχειροῦντα_1026"> in this absolute sense, rather than to supply <quote xml:lang="grc">κακοῖς</quote> (or <quote xml:lang="grc">κακά</quote>) from <quote xml:lang="grc">κακῶς</quote>, as if the verse were a symmetrical <quote xml:lang="grc">γνώμη</quote>, ‘Evil ventures are likely to bring evil fortunes.’ Had the poet meant this, he might rather have written <quote xml:lang="grc">πάσχειν κακά</quote>: cp. fr. 877 <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ δείν’ ἔδρασας, δεινὰ καὶ παθεῖν σε δεῖ</quote>. The participle is in the masc., since the statement is general: <ref n="Soph. El. 145">cp. 145</ref>.— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πράσσειν_1026">, not <quote xml:lang="grc">πράξειν</quote>. For the pres. inf., <ref n="Soph. El. 305">cp. 305</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μέλλων..δρᾶν</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1398"> <title>Ph.</title> 1398</ref> f. <quote xml:lang="grc">ᾔνεσας</quote>.. | <quote xml:lang="grc">πέμπειν</quote>: <ref n="Thuc. 3. 13">Thuc. 3. 13</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὥστε οὐκ εἰκὸς αὐτοὺς περιουσίαν νεῶν ἔχειν, ἢν ὑμεῖς...ἐπεσβάλητε</quote>. In such cases the notion of fut. time is sufficiently expressed by the principal verb. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ζηλῶ_1027">: cp. <title>I. A.</title> 1407 <quote xml:lang="grc">ζηλῶ δὲ σοῦ μὲν Ἑλλάδ’, Ἑλλάδος δὲ σέ</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀνέξομαι_1028"> <quote xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</quote>: ‘I will listen patiently <emph>also</emph> when you commend me (as I now listen to your taunts),’—<foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, ‘when, taught by bitter experience, you recognise the wisdom of my advice’: <ref n="Soph. El. 1044">cp. 1044</ref>. The point of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνέξομαι</quote> is that it will be a trial of patience—not less than that of being reproached with <quote xml:lang="grc">δειλία</quote>—to hear Electra's acknowledgments and regrets when her rash attempt has failed. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μὴ_τόδε_1029">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">μὴ ἐπαινεθῇς</quote>: as if the mere fact of being praised by Electra was the trial foreseen by her sister. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸ_κρῖναι_1030">, instead of the simple inf.: <ref n="Soph. El. 1079">cp. 1079</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τό τε μὴ βλέπειν ἑτοίμα</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 78."> <title>Ant.</title> 78.</ref>Since </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μακρὸς_1030"> here implies ‘long <emph>enough</emph>,’ an inf. can go with it as with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἱκανός, δυνατός</quote>, etc.: cp. <ref n="Thuc. 2. 61">Thuc. 2. 61</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ταπεινὴ</quote> (<foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀδύνατος</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑμῶν ἡ διάνοια ἐγκαρτερεῖν ἃ ἔγνωτε</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χὡ_χρόνος_1030">: <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, ‘whether I am right or not, cannot be decided by the present moment alone; there is <emph>also</emph> the future to be considered.’ That is what <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ</quote> marks here, but marks so lightly that if we say, ‘Time enough in the future to decide that,’ the slight emphasis which naturally falls on the word ‘future’ will sufficiently express it. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μητρὶ_σῇ_1033">: <ref n="Soph. El. 366">cp. 366</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">καλοῦ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">τῆς μητρός</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐδ_αὖ_1034"> <quote xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</quote> ‘I will not, indeed, act with thee; but, on the other hand (<quote xml:lang="grc">αὖ</quote>), I do not hate thee so bitterly as to report thy words’; <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, if they were reported, the consequences would be direful. For </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐδ_1034"> as=<quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ οὐ</quote>, <ref n="Soph. El. 132">cp. 132</ref>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔχθος_1034">: cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 59"> <title>Ph.</title> 59</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔχθος ἐχθήρας μέγα</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλ_γ_1035">: <ref n="Soph. El. 233">cp. 233</ref>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἷ_ἀτιμίας_1035">: <ref n="Soph. El. 404">cp. 404</ref>: <ref n="Dem. 4.9">Dem. or. 4 § 9</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἷ προελήλυθεν ἀσελγείας ἅνθρωπος</quote>. The <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀτιμία</quote> is the rejection (1018 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπορρίψουσαν</quote>) of Electra's earnest and solemn appeal. She means, ‘you disclaim hatred of me; but at least do not conceal from yourself the cruelty of the slight which you inflict.’ </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀτιμίας_οὔ_1036">: for <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔ</quote>, <ref n="Soph. El. 905">cp. 905</ref>. The genitive is adapted to the form of the preceding verse; ‘do not call it <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀτιμία</quote>: it is <quote xml:lang="grc">προμηθία</quote>.’ </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῷ_δικαίῳ_1037">: <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, ‘you dissuade me from this deed because, as you say, you are anxious for my welfare. Am I not, then (</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δῆτ_1037">), to obey my own sense of duty? Must I obey yours instead?’ The peculiarity of the phrase is that <quote xml:lang="grc">τῷ σῷ δικαίῳ</quote> means here, ‘what is right according to you,’ whereas it would normally mean, ‘the right on which you rely,’ ‘your plea, or claim.’ <ref n="Soph. El. 1110">Cp. 1110</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὴν σὴν κληδόν’</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἦ_δεινὸν_1039">: cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 323"> <title>Ant.</title> 323</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἦ δεινόν, ᾦ δοκεῖ γε, καὶ ψευδῆ δοκεῖν.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εὖ_λέγουσαν_1039">, because the sentiment expressed by Chrysothemis is sound in itself: </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐξαμαρτάνειν_1039">, because Chrysothemis assumes that true wisdom is <emph>now</emph> upon her own side, and not upon Electra's. [Not: ‘It is sad that <title>I</title> should speak aright and yet <emph>miss my aim</emph>,’—produce no result.] </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ᾧ_κακῷ_1040">: <ref n="Soph. El. 240">cp. 240</ref> n.: <quote xml:lang="grc">κακῷ</quote>, instead of acc. <quote xml:lang="grc">κακόν</quote>, the antecedent drawn into the relative clause (cp. <ref n="Soph. Trach. 1060"> <title>Tr.</title> 1060</ref> f.).—Chrysothemis means that Electra <quote xml:lang="grc">εὖ λέγει</quote>, as upholding a right principle, but <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξαμαρτάνει</quote>, in proposing a desperate scheme. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τί_σοι_1041"> <quote xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</quote>: ‘You say that I am in error. How then? Do you deny that right is on my side?’ Chrysothemis had already admitted that <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ δίκαιον</quote> was with Electra (338): she does so here also, but argues, as before, from expediency alone. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰ_ποήσεις_1044">: for the fut. indic. (‘if you are going to do this’) cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1155"> <title>Ai.</title> 1155</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ γὰρ ποήσεις, <ref n="Soph. Aj. i"> αι. ι</ref>)/σθι πημανούμενος</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 66"> <title>Ph.</title> 66</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ δ’ ἐργάσει</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">μὴ ταῦτα, λύπην πᾶσιν Ἀργείοις βαλεῖς</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_μὴν_1045">: <ref n="Soph. El. 556">cp. 556</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βουλεύσει_πάλιν_1046">=<quote xml:lang="grc">μεταβουλεύσει</quote>. Cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 961"> <title>Ph.</title> 961</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ καὶ πάλιν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">γνώμην μετοίσεις</quote> (‘change it back,’—not, ‘change a second time’): <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 1270 <quote xml:lang="grc">μεταγνῶναι πάλιν</quote>. <ref n="Aesch. Seven 1040">Aesch. <title>Theb.</title> 1040</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μηδέ τῳ δόξῃ πάλιν</quote> (‘contrariwise’). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φρονεῖν_1048">: <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, ‘you seem to share none of my sentiments’; cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 370"> <title>Ant.</title> 370</ref> f. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμοὶ</quote>.. | ..<quote xml:lang="grc">ἴσον φρονῶν</quote>: <ref n="Hom. Il. 4.361"> <title>Il.</title> 4. 361</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ γὰρ φρονέεις τά τ’ ἐγώ περ</quote>: <ref n="Hdt. 7. 102">Her. 7. 102</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἢν οἱ ἄλλοι...τὰ σὰ φρονέωσι</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ταῦτα_1049">, her own rules of conduct, as distinguished from her sister's.— </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νεωστί_1049">: for the quantity of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ι_1049"> in these adverbs, see on <ref n="Soph. OC 1251."> <title>O. C.</title> 1251.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τολμᾷς_1051">, ‘bring thyself’ to do it; cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 184"> <title>O. C.</title> 184</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τόλμα</quote>.. | ..<quote xml:lang="grc">ὅ τι καὶ πόλις</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">τέτροφεν ἄφιλον ἀποστυγεῖν</quote>: and <ref n="Soph. Phil. 82"> <title>Ph.</title> 82</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὔ_μεθέψομαι_1052">. When <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ μή</quote> stands with the fut. indic., it can express either (1) a prohibition, if joined with the 2nd pers.; or (2) a denial, as here, if joined with the 1st or 3rd pers. Cp. <ref n="Aristoph. Frogs 508">Ar. <title>Ran.</title> 508</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ μή σ’ ἐγὼ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">περιόψομἀπελθόντ’</quote>. See n. on <ref n="Soph. OC 177.">Soph. <title>O. C.</title> 177.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_κενά_1054">: cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 92"> <title>Ant.</title> 92</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀρχὴν δὲ θηρᾶν οὐ πρέπει τἀμήχανα</quote>: and for </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_1054">, Plat. <title>Prot.</title>p. 317 A <quote xml:lang="grc">πολλὴ μωρία καὶ τοῦ ἐπιχειρήματος</quote> (‘the very attempt is ridiculous’).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κενά_1054">, vain dreams that her sister could ever feel and act with her: <ref n="Soph. El. 1031">cp. 1031</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">σοὶ γὰρ ὠφέλησις οὐκ ἔνι</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅταν_γὰρ_1056"> <quote xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</quote>: like the warning with which she concludes at v. 430. Schol.: <quote xml:lang="grc">παρὰ τὸ Ὁμηρικὸν, ῥεχθὲν δέ τε νήπιος ἔγνω</quote> ( <ref n="Hom. Il. 17.32"> <title>Il.</title> 17. 32</ref>).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βεβήκῃς_1056">: 979 n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄνωθεν_1058"> here=<quote xml:lang="grc">ἄνω</quote>: so 1449 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔξωθεν</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 601"> <title>Tr.</title> 601</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔσωθεν</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 521"> <title>Ant.</title> 521</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κάτωθεν. </quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἰωνοὺς_1058"> with <quote xml:lang="grc">οι^</quote> (the only example in this word): <ref n="Soph. El. 1001">cp. 1001</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοιοῦτον</quote>: fr. 881 <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν Βοι^ώτιον νόμον</quote>: <ref n="Hom. Od. 7.312"> <title>Od.</title> 7. 312</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῖος ἐὼν οἷός ἐσσι</quote>.—The stork was especially a type of parental and filial piety: <ref n="Aristoph. Birds 1355">Ar. <title>Av.</title> 1355</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπὴν ὁ πατὴρ ὁ πελαργὸς ἐκπετησίμους</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">πάντας ποήσῃ τοὺς πελαργιδῆς τρέφων</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">δεῖ τοὺς νεοττοὺς τὸν πατέρα πάλιν τρέφειν</quote>. Suidas <emph>s.v.</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀντιπελαργεῖν</quote>: <quote xml:lang="grc">παροιμία ἐπὶ τῶν τὰς χάριτας ἀποδιδόντων</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐσορώμενοι_1059">: for the midd., cp. <ref n="Soph. Trach. 909"> <title>Tr.</title> 909</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰσορωμένη</quote>, <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 306 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁρωμένη</quote>: <ref n="Soph. OC 244"> <title>O. C.</title> 244</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">προσορωμένα</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 593"> <title>Ant.</title> 593</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁρῶμαι</quote>. —(<quote xml:lang="grc">τούτων</quote>) </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀφ_ὧν_1059">: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1388"> <title>O. C.</title> 1388</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κτανεῖν θ’ ὑφ’ οὗπερ ἐξελήλασαι</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εὕρωσι_1061">, where <quote xml:lang="grc">εὕρωνται</quote> would be more usual: <ref n="Soph. El. 1305">cp. 1305</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μέγ’ εὑρεῖν κέρδος.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπ_ἴσας_1061">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">μοίρας</quote>: cp. <ref n="Hdt. 1. 74">Her. 1. 74</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">διαφέρουσί σφι ἐπὶ ἴσης τὸν πόλεμον</quote>. For similar phrases, cp. n. on <ref n="Soph. Ant. 994">Soph. <title>Ant.</title> 994</ref>(<quote xml:lang="grc">δἰ ὀρθῆς</quote>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλ_τὰν_1063"> <quote xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</quote> Although the text in the antistrophic v., 1075, is uncertain, it seems probable that the words <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἠλέκτρα, τὸν ἀεὶ πατρὸς</quote> there represent the true metre, and that therefore Turnebus was right in deleting <quote xml:lang="grc">μὰ</quote> before <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰν</quote> here. <quote xml:lang="grc">μὰ</quote> is similarly omitted in <title>O.T.</title> 660, 1088, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 758."> <title>Ant.</title> 758.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὰν_Θέμιν_1064">. Pindar, in a <quote xml:lang="grc">ὕμνος</quote> for the Thebans (fr. 30), celebrates <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰν εὔβουλον Θέμιν οὐρανίαν</quote>, brought by the Fates from the Ocean stream to Olympus, where she was wedded to Zeus, and gave birth to the <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλαθέας Ὥρας</quote>, ‘the true Seasons,’ who come so surely in their turn. Thus there is a twofold fitness in the mention of her here. She is the goddess of just counsel, enthroned beside Zeus (<quote xml:lang="grc">Διὸς..πάρεδρος..Θέμις</quote>, <title>Ol.</title> 8. 21); and her faithful daughters will bring the time of vengeance. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀπόνητοι_1065">=<quote xml:lang="grc">ἄπονοι</quote>, free from trouble or suffering; a form found elsewhere only in the adv. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπονητότατα</quote> ( <ref n="Hdt. 2. 14">Her. 2. 14</ref>). The reference is explained by the words, <quote xml:lang="grc">τάδ’ οὐκ ἐπ’ ἴσας τελοῦμεν</quote>; ‘Those of us mortals who neglect these duties do not long escape suffering.’ A reflection suggested by the conduct of Chrysothemis is softened by being put in a general form.—Others suppose that Aegisthus and Clytaemnestra are the subjects of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπόνητοι</quote>: but the context clearly excludes this. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χθονία_1066">, preceding </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βροτοῖσι_1066">, serves to indicate that the dead are meant (<ref n="Soph. El. 462">cp. 462</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">βροτῶν</quote> n.): the dat. is ethic, denoting those who perceive the <quote xml:lang="grc">φάμα.</quote> Others understand <quote xml:lang="grc">βροτοῖσι</quote> of the living, and explain the dat. as virtually=a gen.; ‘rumour on the part of mortals,’=‘a rumour which proceeds from them.’ (For such a dat., cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 861"> <title>Ant.</title> 861</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἁμετέρου πότμου κλεινοῖς Λαβδακίδαισιν</quote>, n.) This seems less simple and less forcible.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φάμα_1066">: cp. <ref n="Pind. O. 8">Pind. <title>O.</title> 8. 81</ref>, where the news of an athlete's victory is brought to his dead father in the under-world by <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἀγγελία</quote>, daughter of Hermes; also <ref n="Pind. O. 14">Pind. <title>O.</title> 14. 20</ref> ff., where <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἀχὼ</quote> is charged with a message ‘to the dark house of Persephonè.’</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Φάμα_1066">. Aeschines mentions <quote xml:lang="grc">Φήμης θεοῦ μεγίστης βωμόν</quote> at Athens (or. 1. § 291: cp. <ref n="Hes. WD 761">Hes. <title>Op.</title> 761</ref> f.). But here, I think, <quote xml:lang="grc">φάμα</quote> rather hovers on the verge of personification than is actually personified, just as in <ref n="Hdt. 9. 100">Her. 9. 100</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">φήμη..ἐσέπτατο ἐς τὸ στρατόπεδον</quote>. So in <ref n="Hom. Od. 24.413">Hom. <title>Od.</title> 24. 413</ref>, <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄσσα δ’ ἄρ’ ἄγγελος ὦκα κατὰ πτόλιν ᾤχετο πάντῃ</quote>, we need not write <quote xml:lang="grc">Ὄσσα</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κατά_βόασον_1067">: for the tmesis, cp. <title>O.T.</title> 1198 <quote xml:lang="grc">κατὰ μὲν φθίσας</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 977"> <title>Ant.</title> 977</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κατὰ δὲ τακόμενοι.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μοι_1067">: <ref n="Soph. El. 144">cp. 144</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Ἀτρείδαις_1068">. As <quote xml:lang="grc">τέκνων</quote> in 1071 shows, the ref. is to Agamemnon only; for the plur., <ref n="Soph. El. 1419">cp. 1419</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἱ γᾶς ὑπαὶ κείμενοι</quote>: <ref n="Aesch. Lib. 49">Aesch. <title>Cho.</title> 49</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοὺς γᾶς</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">νέρθεν</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀχόρευτα_1068">: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1222"> <title>O. C.</title> 1222</ref> f. (death) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνυμέναιος</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄλυρος ἄχορος</quote>: <title>Suppl.</title> 681 (war) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄχορον ἀκίθαριν</quote>: <ref n="Aesch. Eum. 331"> <title>Eum.</title> 331</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὕμνος</quote> (of the Furies)..<quote xml:lang="grc">ἀφόρμικτος</quote>: <ref n="Eur. Tro. 121">Eur. <title>Tro.</title> 121</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄτας..ἀχορεύτους</quote>: <title>I. T.</title> 146 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλύροις ἐλέγοις</quote>.—The </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὀνείδη_1068"> are the dishonours of the house,—not reproaches to the spirit of Agamemnon for inactivity; though it is implied, of course, that now more than ever his aid is needed. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νοσεῖ_1070"> answers to <quote xml:lang="grc">οἰωνοὺς</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">˘ ¯ ¯</quote>) in 1058: it is certain, therefore, that a syllable has dropped out after it. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δή_1070"> (supplied by Triclinius) is at least tolerable, and is not precluded by <quote xml:lang="grc">ἤδη</quote>: cp. <ref n="Eur. Tro. 233">Eur. <title>Tro.</title> 233</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">δοῦλαι γὰρ δὴ</quote> (‘<emph>very</emph> slaves’) | <quote xml:lang="grc">Δωρίδος ἐσμὲν χθονὸς ἤδη</quote>. So here <quote xml:lang="grc">δή</quote> will emphasise <quote xml:lang="grc">νοσεῖ</quote>. For other conjectures, see Appendix. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὰ_τέκνων_1071">, acc.: lit., ‘as to the relations between their children.’</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="διπλῆ_φύλοπις_1071">, ‘strife between two,’ ‘strife of sister with sister.’ The use of the word in ref. to a private quarrel is like that of <quote xml:lang="grc">πολέμους</quote> in 219.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐκέτ_ἐξισοῦται_1071">, ‘is no longer equal ised’; <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, cannot be resolved into harmony, does not permit unity of feeling, </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φιλοτασίῳ_διαίτᾳ_1071">, in a friendly homelife; for the dat. seems to be modal rather than instrumental. The boldness of the phrase resides in the fact that <quote xml:lang="grc">διπλῆ φύλοπις</quote>, ‘strife between two,’ is treated as=‘two who are at strife,’ and so <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξισοῦται</quote> expresses what would more properly be said of the sisters' minds. The schol. gives the sense rightly: <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκέτι ἴσα φρονοῦσιν ὡς ἐν φιλίᾳ διαιτώμεναι, ἀλλὰ στασιάζουσι πρὸς ἀλλήλας</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σαλεύει_1074">: <title>O.T.</title> 22 <quote xml:lang="grc">πόλις</quote>.. | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἤδη σαλεύει</quote>. Plato similarly applies the word to persons, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν νόσοις ἢ γήρᾳ σαλεύοντας</quote> (<title>Legg.</title> 923 B). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Ἠλέκτρα_πατρὸς_1075">.
