The traditional interpretation, preserved in the scholia, took <quote xml:lang="grc">πατρὸς</quote> with <quote xml:lang="grc">στενάχουσ’</quote>, as=‘mourning for her sire,’ and <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν ἀεὶ</quote> as=<quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν ἀεὶ χρόνον</quote>. The gen. in this sense is quite tenable: cp. <ref n="Hom. Il. 22.424"> <title>Il.</title> 22. 424</ref>(quoted by schol.) <quote xml:lang="grc">τῶν πάντων οὐ τόσσον ὀδύρομαι, ἀχνύμενός περ</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς ἑνός</quote>: <ref n="Hom. Od. 14.40"> <title>Od.</title> 14. 40</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀντιθέου γὰρ ἄνακτος ὀδυρόμενος καὶ ἀχεύων</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἧμαι</quote>: <title>I. A.</title> 370 <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἑλλάδος μάλιστ’ ἔγωγε τῆς ταλαιπώρου στένω</quote>. The phrase <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν ἀεὶ χρόνον</quote> is also frequent ( <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1520">Soph. <title>Ph.</title> 1520</ref> f., <ref n="Plat. Gorg. 525C">Plat. <title>Gorg.</title> 525C</ref>, E): cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 342">Soph. <title>Ai.</title> 342</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν εἰσαεὶ</quote> | ...<quote xml:lang="grc">χρόνον</quote>. But there is nothing to show that <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν ἀεὶ</quote> could be used, without <quote xml:lang="grc">χρόνον</quote>, as=‘for ever’: in <ref n="Soph. OC 1584">Soph. <title>O. C.</title> 1584</ref> the words <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν ἀεὶ</quote> conceal some corruption: in <ref n="Soph. Trach. 80">Soph. <title>Tr.</title> 80</ref> we should read <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰς τό γ’</quote> (not <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">ὔστερον</quote>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀεὶ_1075"> is clearly genuine: hence <quote xml:lang="grc">θάνατον πατρὸς</quote> (Fröhlich) is improbable. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πατρὸς_1075"> must also be right: since the comparison with the <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀηδών</quote> (used by Electra herself, 147) at once indicates— as, indeed, the whole context does—that the doom which she mourns is not <emph>her own.</emph> This excludes Dindorf's conjecture, <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν ἑὸν πότμον</quote>. Schneidewin proposed <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἠλέκτρα, πότμον ἀεὶ πατρὸς</quote>, retaining <quote xml:lang="grc">μὰ</quote> after <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ οὐ</quote> in 1063: but this is metrically wrong, since <quote xml:lang="grc">μὰ</quote> is always short.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἁ_πατρὸς_1075">. A marginal gloss on <quote xml:lang="grc">ἁ παῖς</quote>, namely <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἠλέκτρα</quote>, would easily cause the corruption, especially since the words <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν ἀεὶ</quote> so often stand together. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πάνδυρτος_1077"> is the form used by <ref n="Aesch. Pers. 940">Aesch. <title>Pers.</title> 940</ref> and <ref n="Eur. Hec. 212.">Eur. <title>Hec.</title> 212.</ref>As the MSS. have <quote xml:lang="grc">πανόδυρτος</quote> here (a form not found in Tragedy), so they sometimes change <quote xml:lang="grc">δύρομαι</quote> to <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀδύρομαι</quote>, as in <ref n="Soph. OT 1218.">Soph. <title>O. T.</title> 1218.</ref>—<ref n="Soph. El. 147">Cp. 147</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἁ στονόεσσ’</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὔτε_τε_1078">: <ref n="Soph. El. 350">cp. 350</ref>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸ_βλέπειν_1078">, instead of the simple inf.: for the art., <ref n="Soph. El. 1030">cp. 1030</ref> (n.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="διδύμαν_Ἐρινύν_1080">, Aegisthus and Clytaemnestra. So the word is applied to Helen ( <ref n="Aesch. Ag. 749">Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 749</ref>, <ref n="Verg. A. 2. 573">Verg. <title>Aen.</title> 2. 573</ref>) and to Medea ( <ref n="Eur. Med. 1260">Eur. <title>Med.</title> 1260</ref>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τίς_εὔπατρις_1081"> <quote xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</quote>: ‘what woman so truly noble is likely ever to be born?’ Will the world see again a maiden so worthy of her descent? <quote xml:lang="grc">εὔπατρις</quote> is chosen so as to suggest the <emph>father</emph> to whom she was so loyal. Cp. Byron, <title>The Giaour</title>, v. 6, ‘When shall such hero live again?’ </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐδεὶς_γὰρ_1082">. This is a comment on Electra's devotion, as just described. The train of thought is;—‘Yet such devotion might be expected in one who is truly noble (in nature as well as in race); <emph>for</emph> no generous soul will stoop to baseness.’ By </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῶν_ἀγαθῶν_1082"> here are meant <quote xml:lang="grc">οἱ καλῶς πεφυκότες</quote> in the full sense (989 n.). The quality of Electra's heroism is such as belongs to them generally; though in the degree of it she is unique. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ζῶν_κακῶς_1083">, by an unworthy, a base life (<ref n="Soph. El. 989">cp. 989</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ζῆν..αἰσχρῶς</quote>); opposed to <quote xml:lang="grc">καλῶς ζῆν</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. Aj. 479"> <title>Ai.</title> 479</ref>).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νώνυμος_1083">, proleptic; <ref n="Soph. El. 18">cp. 18</ref> (<quote xml:lang="grc">σαφῆ</quote>), 242 (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκτίμους</quote>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πάγκλαυτον_κοινὸν_1085">, ‘a life of mourning, shared with thy friends,’ <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, with the unavenged father whose spirit is mourning in the world below (<ref n="Soph. El. 847">cp. 847</ref> n.). For this sense of <quote xml:lang="grc">κοινὸν</quote>, cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 265"> <title>Ai.</title> 265</ref> ff.: <quote xml:lang="grc">πότερα δ’ ἄν, εἰ νέμοι τις αἵρεσιν, λάβοις</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">φίλους ἀνιῶν αὐτὸς ἡδονὰς ἔχειν</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἢ κοινὸς ἐν κοινοῖσι λυπεῖσθαι ξυνών</quote>; ‘to pain thy friends, and have delights thyself, or to share the grief of friends who grieve?’ If it be objected that here there is nothing to define the reference of <quote xml:lang="grc">κοινόν</quote>, the answer is that the thought of her father pervades the whole context from 1074 onwards. I incline to believe, then, that the text is genuine; though I grant that <quote xml:lang="grc">κοινὸν</quote> leaves room for doubt. Conjectures will be found in the Appendix.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἵλου_1085">: the ‘choice’ is illustrated by Electra's replies to the Chorus in the Parodos (121—250), and it is to these more especially that they allude. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸ_καθοπλίσασα_1087">. I believe that <quote xml:lang="grc">καθοπλίσασα</quote> is corrupt, and has supplanted some word which meant ‘having rejected’ or ‘spurned.’ In the antistrophic verse (1095), <quote xml:lang="grc">βεβῶσαν, ἃ δὲ μέγιστ’ ἔβλαστε κ.τ.λ.</quote>, two short syllables (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἃ δὲ</quote>) correspond with the (now) long final of <quote xml:lang="grc">καλόν</quote>. The best conjecture is H. Heinrich SchmidtJ. 's </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀπολακτίσασα_1087">, which gives precisely the required sense, and also an exact metrical correspondence with the antistrophe. Cp. <ref n="Aesch. PB 651">Aesch. <title>P. V.</title> 651</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">σὺ δ’, ὦ παῖ, μὴ ἀπολακτίσῃς λέχος</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ Ζηνός</quote>: <ref n="Aesch. Eum. 141">Aesch. <title>Eum.</title> 141</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κἀπολακτίσασ’ ὕπνον</quote>. Only, if this was the original word, then we must suppose that it had been partly obliterated before <quote xml:lang="grc">καθοπλίσασα</quote> took its place. The same remark, however, applies more or less to the other conjectures, as <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀποπτύσασα</quote> (Gleditsch, prefixing <quote xml:lang="grc">δ’</quote>: Blaydes, prefixing <quote xml:lang="grc">τ’</quote>): <quote xml:lang="grc">καταπτύσασα</quote> (Paley): <quote xml:lang="grc">καθαγνίσασα</quote> (Campbell, ‘purging away as by fire’): <quote xml:lang="grc">καθιππάσασα</quote> (Hermann; but the act. form does not occur).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καθοπλίσασα_1087"> be retained, the choice is between two explanations, of which I prefer the first.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φέρειν_1088">, <emph>so as</emph> to win (<ref n="Soph. El. 872">cp. 872</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μολεῖν</quote>), = <quote xml:lang="grc">φέρεσθαι</quote>, as <ref n="Soph. OT 590"> <title>O. T.</title> 590</ref>(n.). Cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 117"> <title>Ph.</title> 117</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς τοῦτό γ’ ἔρξας δύο φέρει δωρήματα.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐν_λόγῳ_1088">, ‘in,’ or as we say, ‘on,’ one account; cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν λόγῳ εἶναι</quote>, etc. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καθύπερθεν_1090">, an epic word not elsewhere used in Tragedy: this figurative sense of it is not Homeric, but is frequent in Herodotus (as <cit> <bibl n="Hdt. 8.60">8. 60</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τῶν ἐχθρῶν κατύπερθε γενέσθαι</quote> </cit>: <ref n="Hdt. 1.67">cp. 1.67</ref>, <ref n="Hdt. 5.69">5. 69</ref>).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τεῶν_1090">, for the MSS. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῶν_1090">, is a simpler and far more probable correction than </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοσόνδ_1090"> (cr. n.). The epic and Ionic <quote xml:lang="grc">τεὸς</quote> is used in lyrics by Aesch. ( <ref n="Aesch. PB 162">Aesch. <title>P. V.</title> 162</ref>, <ref n="Aesch. Seven 105">Aesch. <title>Th.</title> 105</ref>, <ref n="Aesch. Seven 108">Aesch. Th. 108</ref>): in <ref n="Soph. Ant. 604">Soph. <title>Ant.</title> 604</ref>(lyr.) the MSS. give <quote xml:lang="grc">τεὰν</quote>, which seems right: and in <ref n="Eur. Heraclid. 911">Eur. <title>Heracl.</title> 911</ref>(lyr.) <quote xml:lang="grc">τεὸς</quote> is the best correction of <quote xml:lang="grc">θεός</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὑπόχειρ_1092"> (Musgrave's correction of <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑπὸ χεῖρα</quote>) is not elsewhere extant, but is correctly formed (cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπίχειρ, ἀντίχειρ</quote>), and is placed beyond reasonable doubt by the metre (= 1084 <quote xml:lang="grc">νώνυμος, ὦ παῖ παῖ</quote>). <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑποχείριος</quote> is frequent in this sense. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μοίρᾳ_βεβῶσαν_1094">: <ref n="Soph. El. 1056">cp. 1056</ref> f. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν κακοῖς</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">βεβήκῃς</quote>, and 979 n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μέγιστα_νόμιμα_1095">, those ‘unwritten and unfailing’ laws of the gods which prescribe natural piety in human relationships. See on <ref n="Soph. Ant. 454"> <title>Ant.</title> 454</ref> f. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄγραπτα κἀσφαλῆ θεῶν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">νόμιμα</quote>: and <ref n="Soph. OT 865"> <title>O. T.</title> 865</ref> ff. <quote xml:lang="grc">νόμοι..ὑψίποδες, οὐρανίαν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">δἰ αἰθέρα τεκνωθέντες, ὧν Ὄλυμπος</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">πατὴρ μόνος.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔβλαστε_1095">: they are the greatest that have ever ‘come into existence,’ being of divine origin, and antecedent to any human law: <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀεί ποτε</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ζῇ ταῦτα, κοὺδεὶς οἶδεν ἐξ ὅτου )φάνη</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. Ant. 456"> <title>Ant.</title> 456</ref>).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῶνδε_ἄριστα_1095">: ‘on account of these,—<foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, for observance of them,— winning excellent things,’ ‘winning an excellent reward,’—viz., praise of the noblest kind. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῶνδε_1095"> is then a causal gen.: cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 48"> <title>O. T.</title> 48</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">σωτῆρα κλῄζει τῆς πάρος προθυμίας</quote>. That this is the sense of <quote xml:lang="grc">φερομέναν</quote> here, is strongly suggested by other passages; <ref n="Soph. El. 968">cp. 968</ref> f. <quote xml:lang="grc">εὐσέβειαν</quote>.. | ..<quote xml:lang="grc">οἴσει</quote>, and esp. <ref n="Soph. OT 863"> <title>O. T.</title> 863</ref> ff. <quote xml:lang="grc">εἴ μοι ξυνείη φέροντι</quote> (=<quote xml:lang="grc">φερομένῳ</quote>) | <quote xml:lang="grc">μοῖρα τὰν εὔσεπτον ἁγνείαν λόγων</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔργων τε πάντων, ὧν νόμοι πρόκεινται</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑψίποδες</quote> (‘winning the praise of reverent purity,’ etc.). Some take <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄριστα</quote> as = <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀριστεῖα</quote> (‘winning the foremost place’ in respect to these laws): an impossible sense for it. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄριστα</quote> might, indeed, be taken with <quote xml:lang="grc">τῶνδε</quote> as possessive gen.; ‘winning the best things belonging to these laws,’— their best gifts; those, namely, which they bring to those who obey them. But this would be somewhat forced.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τᾷ_εὐσεβείᾳ_1097">, ‘by thy piety towards Zeus’: for the objective gen., cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 239"> <title>O. T.</title> 239</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">θεῶν εὐχαῖσι</quote>. The MSS. have </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Διὸς_1097">, against metre, just as in <ref n="Soph. Trach. 956"> <title>Tr.</title> 956</ref> they have <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν Διὸς ἄλκιμον γόνον</quote>. In both places, </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Ζηνὸς_1097">, the correction of Triclinius, seems the best. Here, indeed, to judge by a marginal scholium in L (cr. n.), <quote xml:lang="grc">Ζηνὸς</quote> may have been an old variant. The change of <quote xml:lang="grc">Ζηνὸς</quote> into <quote xml:lang="grc">Διὸς</quote> is such as might have been due to a slip of memory in actors, by whom the tragic texts had been much corrupted even before 330 B.C.—Recent criticism is inclined to suspect some deeper fault here (see cr. n.); but, as it seems to me, without good reason. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰσηκούσαμεν_1098">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, from some one in the neighbourhood. These envoys from Strophius (1111) are not supposed to have travelled with the messenger from Phanoteus (670). The poet has skilfully varied the dialogue from the similar one in 660 ff.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὀρθῶς_θ_1098">. The <emph>v. l.</emph> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δ_1098"> for </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θ_1098">, though it has the better authority, is improbable here. For instances in which </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τε_1098"> can fitly be followed by </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δέ_1098">, see <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1096"> <title>Ant.</title> 1096</ref>(n.), <ref n="Soph. Trach. 143"> <title>Tr.</title> 143</ref>(n.).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔνθα_1098"> here=‘whither’: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1466."> <title>Ph.</title> 1466.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Αἴγισθον_1101">: for the constr., cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 444"> <title>Ph.</title> 444</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦτον οἶσθ’ εἰ ζῶν κυρεῖ</quote>; (n.)— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ᾤκηκεν_1101">, ‘has fixed his abode,’—a light touch of dramatic irony, since his tenure of it is so nearly at an end. <title>Legg.</title> 666 E <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ ἐν ἄστεσι κατῳκηκότων</quote> (but nomads). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλ_1102">, ‘well’: cp. <ref n="Soph. Trach. 229"> <title>Tr.</title> 229</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ εὖ μὲν ἵγμεθ’.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χὡ_φράσας_1102">: cp. <ref n="Pind. P. 4">Pind. <title>P.</title> 4. 117</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">δόμους πατέρων</quote>.. | <quote xml:lang="grc">φράσσατέ μοι</quote>: <title>Suppl.</title> 492 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀπάονας δὲ φρά<*> <*>ράς τ’ ἐγχωρίων</quote>: <ref n="Xen. Cyrop. 5. 4. 40">Xen. <title>Cyr.</title> 5. 4. 40</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">δῶν φραστῆρα.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀζήμιος_1102">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ ἔνοχος ζημίᾳ</quote>: schol. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄμεμπτος</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τίς_ἄν_1103">; For the form of the request, <ref n="Soph. El. 660">cp. 660</ref> (n.); <ref n="Soph. OC 70"> <title>O. C.</title> 70</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἆρ’ ἄν τις αὐτῷ πομπὸς ἐξ ὑμῶν μόλοι</quote>; For the doubled <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄν</quote>, <ref n="Soph. OC 333"> O. C. 333</ref>(n.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ποθεινὴν_1103">, passive, ‘desired,’ as in <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1445"> <title>Ph.</title> 1445</ref>(the only other place where Soph. has the word). <ref n="Soph. El. 666">Cp. 666</ref> f., <quote xml:lang="grc">σοὶ φέρων ἥκω λόγους</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡδεῖς</quote>. The Chorus are meant to understand that the arrival of the new comers has been expected, and will prove welcome, as confirming the news from Phocis. To the ear of the spectator <quote xml:lang="grc">ποθεινὴν</quote> suggests the longing of Electra for her brother's return.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κοινόπουν_1103"> (found only here) recalls <quote xml:lang="grc">κοινόπλουν</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">ὁμιλίαν</quote>) in <ref n="Soph. Aj. 872."> <title>Ai.</title> 872.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸν_ἄγχιστον_1105">: <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, nearest of kin to Clyt. and Aegisthus; the Chorus do not surmise her relationship to the young Phocian. For the general masc., <ref n="Soph. El. 145">cp. 145</ref>, 1026. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἴθ_γύναι_1106">. As <quote xml:lang="grc">ἴθι</quote> was used in entreaty ( <ref n="Soph. OT 46"> <title>O. T.</title> 46</ref>), it is not, in itself, abrupt; but the tone of the direction implies ignorance of Electra's rank, and is thus in keeping with the part of the Phocian <quote xml:lang="grc">ξένος</quote>. Orestes,—who thought that he recognised her voice when it was heard from within (v. 80),—can, of course, be in no doubt as to her identity. Verse 1125 already indicates this. After v. 1148, at any rate, he would know the fact, if he had been so dull as not to discover it sooner. But he pretends not to know who she is until he hears her name pronounced by the Chorus (1171). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐ_ποθ_1108">: cp. <ref n="Soph. Trach. 876"> <title>Tr.</title> 876</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ δή ποθ’ ὡς θανοῦσα</quote>; (n.). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὴν_κληδόν_1110">: cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1251"> <title>Ph.</title> 1251</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν σὸν οὐ ταρβῶ φόβον.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Στρόφιος_1110">: <ref target="cl45">see on 45</ref>. The name occurs nowhere else in the play. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φέροντες_1113">, ‘carrying’ (in the urn), refers simply to the mode of conveyance; </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κομίζομεν_1113">, ‘we bring,’ expresses the care with which they perform their mission. In <quote xml:lang="grc">κομίζω</quote>, ‘care’ is indeed the primary notion (cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">κομιδή</quote>): that of ‘taking a thing to a place’ is secondary. <quote xml:lang="grc">φέρω</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">κομίζω</quote> are similarly combined in Isaeus or. 8 § 21 (referring to a funeral): <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰσελθὼν δὲ εἴσω κομίζειν οἷος ἦν, ἔχων τοὺς οἴσοντας</quote>: ‘I was disposed to <emph>remove</emph> the body, having <emph>bearers</emph> with me.’ </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῦτ_ἐκεῖν_1115"> <quote xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</quote> Three modes of punctuation are possible; the first is perhaps the best, though the second is also satisfactory. (1) To place no point either after <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκεῖν’</quote> or after <quote xml:lang="grc">σαφές</quote>. ‘There, it seems, I clearly see that sorrow (=the sorrow which I foreboded) in your hands.’ <quote xml:lang="grc">σαφὲς</quote> is then equiv. to an adv. with <quote xml:lang="grc">δέρκομαι</quote>: and the combination of <quote xml:lang="grc">σαφὲς</quote> with <quote xml:lang="grc">πρόχειρον</quote> is like that in <ref n="Soph. Trach. 223"> <title>Tr.</title> 223</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τάδ’ ἀντίπρῳρα δή σοι</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">βλέπειν πάρεστ’ ἐναργῆ</quote>. (2) To place a point after <quote xml:lang="grc">σαφές</quote>. ‘This is what I feared (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκεῖνο</quote>), now placed beyond a doubt; I see,’ etc. The only objection to this is that the words <quote xml:lang="grc">πρόχειρον κ.τ.λ.</quote> then become a little abrupt and obscure. (3) To place a point after <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκεῖν’</quote>, and none after <quote xml:lang="grc">σαφές</quote>. ‘That is it; I now see clearly,’ etc. Cp. <ref n="Aristoph. Birds 354">Ar. <title>Av.</title> 354</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦτ’ ἐκεῖνο· ποῖ φύγω δύστηνος</quote>; But this colloquialism, though used by Eur. ( <ref n="Eur. Orest. 804">Eur. <title>Or.</title> 804</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦτ’ ἐκεῖνο, κτᾶσθ’ ἑταίρους</quote>), seems too homely for the style of Sophocles. In 1178 we have merely <quote xml:lang="grc">τόδ’ ἔστ’ ἐκεῖνο</quote>: in <ref n="Soph. OC 137">Soph. <title>O. C.</title> 137</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅδ’ ἐκεῖνος ἐγώ</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρόχειρον_1115">, ‘ready in the hand’: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 747"> <title>Ph.</title> 747</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πρόχειρον εἴ τί σοι, τέκνον, πάρα</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ξίφος χεροῖν</quote> (n.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄχθος_1115">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, the urn, but with ref. to the figurative sense, ‘woe’ (<ref n="Soph. El. 120">cp. 120</ref>, 204): <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1172"> <title>Ant.</title> 1172</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τί δ’ αὖ τόδ’ ἄχθος βασιλέων ἥκεις φέρων</quote>; </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κέκευθεν_1120">, trans., as in <ref n="Hom. Il. 22.118"> <title>Il.</title> 22. 118</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅσσα πτόλις ἥδε κέκευθε</quote>, and <ref n="Hom. Od. 3.18"> <title>Od.</title> 3. 18</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μῆτιν ἐνὶ στήθεσσι κέκευθε</quote> (the only Homeric examples of this perf.). In Attic it is elsewhere intrans.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δότε_1123"> (<quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτῇ</quote>), </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἥτις_ἐστί_1123">: cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 35"> <title>Ant.</title> 35</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὃς ἂν τούτων τι δρᾷ</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">φόνον προκεισθαι</quote> (n.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπαιτεῖται_1123">: the only instance of the midd.: in <title>O.T.</title> 1416 and <title>O.C.</title> 1364 we have the ordinary act. form. In <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπαιτεῖν</quote>, too, the midd. forms are avoided.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρὸς_αἵματος_1123">: cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1305"> <title>Ai.</title> 1305</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοὺς πρὸς αἵματος</quote>: <ref n="Aristot. Pol. 2.1262b">Arist. <title>Pol.</title> 2. 3</ref>, § 7 (<quote xml:lang="grc">συγγένειαν</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἢ πρὸς αἵματος ἢ κατ’ οἰκειότητα καὶ κηδείαν.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φύσιν_1123">: <ref n="Soph. El. 325">cp. 325</ref> n. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ψυχῆς_λοιπόν_1127">, lit., ‘remaining from the life of Orestes.’</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀπ_ἐλπίδων_1127">, far away from my hopes, contrary to them: cp. <ref n="Apollon. 2.863">Apoll. Rh. 2. 863</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μάλα πολλὸν ἀπ’ ἐλπίδος ἔπλετο νόστος</quote>. So <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπὸ δόξης</quote>, contrary to expectation ( <ref n="Hom. Il. 10.324"> <title>Il.</title> 10. 324</ref>, <ref n="Hom. Od. 11.344"> <title>Od.</title> 11. 344</ref>): <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπὸ θυμοῦ</quote>, unpleasing to one ( <ref n="Hom. Il. 1.562"> <title>Il.</title> 1. 562</ref>): <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ ἀπὸ γνώμης</quote>, not unwisely ( <ref n="Soph. Trach. 389"> <title>Tr.</title> 389</ref>): and often <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ ἀπὸ τρόπου</quote>, not unreasonably: <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ ἀπὸ καιροῦ</quote>: <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ ἀπὸ σκοποῦ</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐχ_ὧνπερ_1127"> (if sound) is best explained as standing, by attraction to <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐλπίδων</quote>, for <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐχ αἷσπερ</quote>: and the sense is: —‘In a manner how contrary to my hopes—not with those hopes wherewith I sent thee forth—have I received thee back.’ The notion of contrariety is thus expressed twice over; first by <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπό</quote>, then by <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐχ</quote>. If this is awkward in grammar, yet it has a certain pathetic emphasis.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐχ_ὅνπερ_1127"> or to </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐχ_ὥσπερ_1127">. Each of these occurs in one or more of the later MSS., though probably either by conjecture or by error. Of the two, <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐχ ὥσπερ</quote> gives the simplest sense, while <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐχ ὅνπερ</quote> is perhaps superior in vigour and pathos.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νῦν_γὰρ_1129"> <quote xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</quote> The schol. on 1126 quotes <ref n="Hom. Il. 19.288"> <title>Il.</title> 19. 288</ref>(Briseïs mourning Patroclus): <quote xml:lang="grc">ζωὸν μέν σε ἔλειπον ἐγὼ κλισίηθεν ἰοῦσα</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">νῦν δέ σε τεθνηῶτα κιχάνομαι</quote> (from which Hermann infers,— surely somewhat fancifully,—that the schol. read <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐχ ὅνπερ</quote> in 1128).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐδὲν_ὄντα_1129">: <ref n="Soph. El. 1166">cp. 1166</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ μηδέν.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βαστάζω_1129">: <ref n="Soph. El. 905">cp. 905</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λαμπρὸν_1129"> refers to the bright light of life in the young face. Cp. <title>O.T.</title> 81 <quote xml:lang="grc">λαμπρὸς ὥσπερ ὄμματι</quote>: <ref n="Eur. Ion 475">Eur. <title>Ion</title> 475</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τέκνων οἷς ἂν...λάμπωσιν ἐν θαλάμοις</quote> | ... <quote xml:lang="grc">νεανίδες ἧβαι</quote>. Nauck brackets these two vv., because (1) <quote xml:lang="grc">λαμπρὸν</quote> is strange; and (2) the verses are irrelevant to the ‘hopes’ mentioned in 1127. But the fitness of <quote xml:lang="grc">λαμπρόν</quote>, as poetically used here, is precisely that it suggests the bright promise of the life on which those hopes rested. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡς_ὤφελον_1131">: cp. <ref n="Hom. Il. 3.428"> <title>Il.</title> 3. 428</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς ὤφελες αὐτόθ’ ὀλέσθαι</quote>. With Homer, <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">αἴθ’</quote> are alike frequent in this phrase: Attic usage prefers <quote xml:lang="grc">εἴθ’</quote> (1021), though <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς</quote> also occurs (as in <ref n="Aristoph. Frogs 955">Ar. <title>Ran.</title> 955</ref>, <ref n="Eur. Ion 286">Eur. <title>Ion</title> 286</ref>). The simple <quote xml:lang="grc">ὤφελον</quote> stands in <title>O.T.</title> 1157, and with <quote xml:lang="grc">μή</quote> in <ref n="Soph. Phil. 969"> <title>Ph.</title> 969</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Trach. 998."> <title>Tr.</title> 998.</ref> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὤφελες_1131"> is excluded by </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κλέψασα_1131">, since we should then require (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμὲ</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">κλέψασαν</quote>: it would also forestall v. 1134. Electra's self-reproach is that her action, without ultimately saving his life, deprived him of funeral rites at home. She goes on to lament that she herself had not rendered those rites (1138 ff.). That regret might seem inconsistent with her wish in 1131; but it represents a separate thought which rises in her mind as she pictures him perishing among strangers.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῖνδε_1131">, the fem. form in Attic inscriptions (<title>O.T.</title> 1462 cr. n.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κἀνασώσασθαι_1131">, ‘rescue’: so the act. in <title>O.T.</title> 1351 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπό τε φόνου</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔρρυτο κἀνέσωσε</quote>. The word means esp. to ‘recover’ what has been lost. <ref n="Hdt. 3. 65">Her. 3. 65</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μὴ δὲ ἀνασωσαμένοισι τὴν ἀρχήν, μηδ’ ἐπιχειρήσασι ἀνασῴζειν.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φόνου_1131">: <ref n="Soph. El. 11">cp. 11</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅπως_ἔκεισο_1134">, lit., ‘in order that thou mightest have lain’: see on <title>O.T.</title> 1319 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἵν’ ἦ τυφλός τε καὶ κλύων μηδέν</quote>, and <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 1392 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς ἔδειξα μήποτε κ.τ.λ.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τύμβου_πατρῴου_1134">: cp. n. on 893. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φυγὰς_1136">: as Clyt. says (776 f.), <quote xml:lang="grc">φυγὰς</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπεξενοῦτο</quote>: and Electra (865 ff.), <quote xml:lang="grc">ξένος</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄτερ ἐμᾶν χερῶν</quote> | ...<quote xml:lang="grc">κέκευθεν</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐν_1138">, instrumental ( <ref n="Soph. Ant. 764"> <title>Ant.</title> 764</ref> n.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φίλαισι_1138">, as opp. to <quote xml:lang="grc">ξέναισι</quote> (1141). Cp. Pope's <title>Elegy</title>, vv. 47 ff.: ‘What can atone, oh ever-injured shade! | Thy fate unpity'd, and thy rites unpaid? | No friend's complaint, no kind domestic tear | Pleas'd thy pale ghost, or grac'd thy mournful bier. | By foreign hands thy dying eyes were clos'd, | By foreign hands thy decent limbs compos'd, | By foreign hands thy humble grave adorn'd, | By strangers honour'd, and by strangers mourn'd!’ </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λουτροῖς_ἐκόσμης_1139">: not merely, ‘honoured with washings,’ but rather, ‘washed and <emph>dressed</emph>’ for the <quote xml:lang="grc">πρόθεσις</quote>. The sense is thus the same as in <ref n="Soph. Ant. 900"> <title>Ant.</title> 900</ref> f. <quote xml:lang="grc">θανόντας αὐτόχειρ ὑμᾶς ἐγὼ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔλουσα κἀκόσμησα</quote>. For <quote xml:lang="grc">κοσμεῖν</quote> in ref. to funeral rites, <ref n="Soph. El. 1401">cp. 1401</ref>: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 395"> <title>Ant.</title> 395</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τάφον</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">κοσμοῦσα</quote> (‘showing grace to the dead’— by sprinkling dust and pouring libations). Similarly <title>O.C.</title> 1602 <quote xml:lang="grc">λουτροῖς τέ νιν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐσθῆτί τ’ ἐξήσκησαν</quote>. Isaeus or. 8 § 22 <quote xml:lang="grc">λεγούσης ὅτι βούλοιτ’ ἂν αὐτὴ τὸ σῶμα τὸ ἐκείνου συμμεταχειρίζεσθαι</quote> (referring esp. to the washing of the corpse) <quote xml:lang="grc">μεθ’ ἡμῶν καὶ κοσμῆσαι</quote>. Lucian <title>De luctu</title> 11 mentions washing, anointing with perfumes, crowning with flowers and dressing. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄθλιον_βάρος_1140">, the calcined bones. So in <ref n="Hom. Il. 24.793"> <title>Il.</title> 24. 793</ref>(at Hector's funeral), when the body had been burned and the pyre quenched with wine, <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀστέα λευκὰ λέγοντο κασίγνητοί θ’ ἕταροί τε</quote>. They then place them in a <quote xml:lang="grc">λάρναξ</quote> or urn, which is laid in a grave (<quote xml:lang="grc">κάπετος</quote>), and over this a mound (<quote xml:lang="grc">σῆμα</quote>) is raised. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐν_κηδευθεὶς_1141">: cp. Demades <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑπὲρ τῆς δωδεκαετίας</quote> § 9 (in Baiter and Sauppe's <title>Oratores Attici</title>, vol. 11. p. 314) <quote xml:lang="grc">χιλίων ταφὴ Ἀθηναίων μαρτυρεῖ μοι, κηδευθεῖσα ταῖς τῶν ἐναντίων χερσίν</quote> (a reminiscence of this verse?). Kaibel <title>Epigr. Gr.</title> 604. 3 <quote xml:lang="grc">Μαρκιανὸς δέ μ’ ἔθαψε καὶ ἐκήδευσεν.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σμικρὸς_ὄγκος_1141">: <ref n="Soph. El. 758">cp. 758</ref> n. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῆς_ἀνωφελήτου_1143">: for the order of words, <ref n="Soph. El. 133">cp. 133</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παρέσχον_1143">, not <quote xml:lang="grc">παρεῖχον</quote>, because she is looking back on a closed chapter of her life: cp. VI. <ref n="Thuc. 92.">Thuc. 92.</ref>§ 3 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπολιτεύθην</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἢ_κἀμοῦ_1146">: for the redundant <quote xml:lang="grc">καί</quote>, see on <title>O.C.</title> 53 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅσ’ οἶδα κἀγώ.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μητρὸς_φίλος_1146">, her ‘dear one,’ ‘darling.’ The objections made to <quote xml:lang="grc">φίλος</quote>, on the ground that it must mean ‘friend,’ illustrate the danger of identifying a word with its conventional equivalent in another language. The conjectures are all bad (<quote xml:lang="grc">γάνος, θάλος, τέκος, φάος</quote>, and, worst of all, <quote xml:lang="grc">)φελος</quote>, <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄφελος</quote>).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἱ_οἶκον_1147">, here=<quote xml:lang="grc">οἱ οἰκέται</quote>, as in <ref n="Soph. Trach. 934"> <title>Tr.</title> 934</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τῶν κατ’ οἶκον</quote>. In <ref n="Aesch. Lib. 749">Aesch. <title>Cho.</title> 749</ref> ff. it is a domestic, the <quote xml:lang="grc">τροφός</quote>, who dwells on her care for the infancy of Orestes. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἦσαν_1147">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">τροφοί.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλ_τροφός_1147">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἦ</quote>. [Not, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐγὼ</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">μὲν</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">τροφός, ἐγὼ δ’ ἀδελφὴ..προσηυδώμην</quote>.] </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀδελφὴ_σοὶ_1148">, rather than <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀδελφή σοι</quote>, since a slight emphasis on the pron. better marks the reciprocity of affection; ‘I was thy nurse; and by thee I was ever called “sister.”’ He had other sisters, but it was she who stood in the child's mind for all that ‘sister’ means.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προσηυδώμην_1148">: <ref n="Soph. El. 274">cp. 274</ref>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκλέλοιπε_1149">: 19 n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θανόντι_1149">, in its simple pathos, is better than the <emph>v.l.</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">θανόντα</quote>, for which Brunck quotes <ref n="Eur. Her. 69">Eur. <title>H. F.</title> 69</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ νῦν ἐκεῖνα μὲν θανόντ’ ἀνέπτατο.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="συναρπάσας_1149">, like the more homely <quote xml:lang="grc">συλλαβὼν</quote> in <title>O.T.</title> 971 (n.).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θύελλ_1149">: <ref n="Hom. Il. 13.39"> <title>Il.</title> 13. 39</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">φλογὶ ἶσοι ἀολλέες ἠὲ θυέλλῃ</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τέθνηκ_σοί_1152">: ‘I am dead in relation to thee.’ For Electra, this is another way of saying, ‘I am dead, so far as any aim or joy in life is concerned’; since the only hopes which made life tolerable to her were centred in her brother. See her words in 808 ff., <quote xml:lang="grc">ὥς μ’ ἀπώλεσας θανών·</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀποσπάσας γὰρ κ.τ.λ.</quote> For the dat. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σοί_1152">, cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1030"> <title>Ph.</title> 1030</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τέθνηχ’ ὑμῖν πάλαι</quote>. These words are usu. written </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τέθνηκ_σοι_1152">: but the enclitic <quote xml:lang="grc">σοι</quote> destroys the point.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τέθνηκ_σὺ_1152"> <foreign xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</foreign> is that the unqualified <quote xml:lang="grc">τέθνηκα</quote> would be unfitting when a reference to actual death immediately follows. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μήτηρ_ἀμήτωρ_1154">: cp. <title>O.T.</title> 1214 <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν ἄγαμον γάμον</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Aj. 665"> <title>Ai.</title> 665</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄδωρα δῶρα</quote>: <title>P.V.</title> 544 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄχαρις χάρις.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἧς_1154">, ‘concerning whom,’ depends primarily on <quote xml:lang="grc">φήμας προὔπεμπες</quote> (<ref n="Soph. El. 317">cp. 317</ref> n.), but also denotes the object of <quote xml:lang="grc">τιμωρός</quote>. It must not be taken with <quote xml:lang="grc">λάθρᾳ</quote>: the messages were of course secret; the point here is their tenor and their frequency. <ref n="Soph. El. 169">Cp. 169</ref> f.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἴμοι_μοί_1162"> <quote xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</quote> These three verses are divided as above in L, and now in most editions. Hermann wrote <quote xml:lang="grc">οἴ μοι</quote>. | <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ δέμας οἰκτρόν</quote>. | <quote xml:lang="grc">φεῦ, φεῦ</quote>. | <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ δεινοτάτας</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">οἴ μοι</quote>.—For anapaestic verses inserted in dialogue, cp. <ref n="Soph. Trach. 1081"> <title>Tr.</title> 1081</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">αἰαῖ, ὦ τάλας, αἰαῖ</quote>, and <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 1085 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦναξ Ἀΐδη, δέξαι μ’</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ Διὸς ἀκτίς, παῖσον</quote>. This is one of the traits from which it may be inferred that the <title>Electra</title> was a comparatively late play. <ref n="Soph. El. 1502">Cp. 1502</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κελεύθους_1163">, from Crisa to Mycenae: <quote xml:lang="grc">δεινοτάτας</quote>, since the expected avenger returns as dust. For the poetical plur., <ref n="Soph. El. 68">cp. 68</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ταῖσδε ταῖς ὁδοῖς</quote>. The plur. might, indeed, mean the journey from Mycenae to Phocis, and the return; but this is less natural.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀπώλεσας_1163">: <ref n="Soph. El. 808">cp. 808</ref>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δῆτ_1163">: 842 n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοιγὰρ_μ_1165"> <quote xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</quote>: cp. <title>Romeo and Juliet</title>, act 5, sc. 3, 106: ...‘I still will stay with thee, | And never from this palace of dim night | Depart again: here, here will I remain | With worms that are thy chamber-maids; O, here | Will I set up my everlasting rest’...</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὴν_μηδὲν_1165">, as in <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1231"> <title>Ai.</title> 1231</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦ μηδὲν</quote>= <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦ θανόντος.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸ_μηδὲν_1165"> also can be said of a person who is dead ( Eur. fr. 522, quoted on 244 ff.), or doomed to death, <ref n="Soph. Trach. 1107">Soph. <title>Tr.</title> 1107</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κἂν τὸ μηδὲν ὦ</quote>: but here, following <quote xml:lang="grc">τὴν μηδὲν</quote>, it rather suggests the state, ‘thy nothingness.’ On these phrases generally cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1325">Soph. <title>Ant.</title> 1325</ref> n. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μετεῖχον_ἴσων_1168">: cp. <ref n="Dem. 21.96">Dem. or. 21 § 96</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τῶν ἴσων μετεῖχε τοῖς <*>λλοις ἡμῖν.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μὴ_ἀπολείπεσθαι_1168">: this mode of writing, which implies synizesis, is now more usual than <quote xml:lang="grc">μἀπο‐</quote> (crasis), or <quote xml:lang="grc">μὴ )πο‐</quote> (prodelision): see n. on <ref n="Soph. Phil. 782"> <title>Ph.</title> 782</ref>, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπολείπεσθαι</quote>=‘to be left behind by,’ and so, ‘to be parted from,’ ‘deprived of’: <ref n="Eur. Med. 35">Eur. <title>Med.</title> 35</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πατρῴας μὴ ἀπολείπεσθαι χθονός</quote>: <ref n="Eur. Orest. 216">Eur. <title>Or.</title> 216</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τῶν πρὶν ἀπολειφθεὶς φρενῶν</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοὺς_1170">..</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θανόντας_1170"> <foreign xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</foreign> This verse has been rejected (cr. n.) as a commonplace not suitable here. But it is surely one which might naturally occur in any utterance of weary despair. It closes her lament with a cadence like that which we find twice elsewhere near the end of a speech: <title>O.C.</title> 955 <quote xml:lang="grc">θανεῖν· θανόντων δ’ οὐδὲν ἄλγος ἅπτεται</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 1173"> <title>Tr.</title> 1173</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῖς γὰρ θανοῦσι μόχθος οὐ προσγίγνεται</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θνητοῦ_1171"> <quote xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</quote>: ‘as thy father was a mortal, so his son, thy brother, was but mortal also’: with </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θνητὸς_1171"> supply <quote xml:lang="grc">ἦν.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φρόνει_1171">, ‘bethink thee’ (not, ‘be patient’).—Nauck would change <quote xml:lang="grc">πέφυκας</quote> to <quote xml:lang="grc">γεγῶσα</quote>, and <quote xml:lang="grc">θνητὸς δ’</quote> to <quote xml:lang="grc">θανόντ’</quote>, because (1) it is ‘absurd’ to call a dead man <quote xml:lang="grc">θνητός</quote>: (2) it is no comfort for Electra that her father was mortal: and (3) the parenthetic <quote xml:lang="grc">φρόνει</quote> is unusual. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πᾶσιν_ἡμῖν_1173">. Bergk rejects this verse, thinking that it was probably interpolated from Euripides (cp. <ref n="Eur. Alc. 419"> <title>Alc.</title> 419</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς πᾶσιν ἡμῖν κατθανεῖν ὀφείλεται</quote>, and <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 782). Itis true that the comment of the Chorus on an actor's speech is usu. limited to two verses. But we find three above, <ref n="Eur. IT 369"> It 369</ref>—371, and in <ref n="Soph. OT 1073"> <title>O. T.</title> 1073</ref>— 1075. Few will think with Dindorf that </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῦτ_1173"> (=<quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ θανεῖν</quote>, implied in <quote xml:lang="grc">θνητὸς</quote>) is too obscure. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φεῦ_λέξω_1174">; Orestes, deeply moved, speaks to himself, though loud enough for Electra to hear. A similar ‘aside’ (if it can be called so) marks the crisis in the purpose of Neoptolemus, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 895"> <title>Ph.</title> 895</ref> f.: <quote xml:lang="grc">ΝΕ. παπαῖ· τί δῆτ’ ἂν δρῷμ’ ἐγὼ τοὐνθένδε γε; ΦΙ. τί δ’ ἔστιν, ὦ παῖ; ποῖ ποτ’ ἐξέβης λόγῳ;</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λόγων_1174"> goes with </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ποῖ_1174">, not with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀμηχανῶν_1174">. Cp. <title>O.C.</title> 310 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ Ζεῦ, τί λέξω; ποῖ φρενῶν ἔλθω, πάτερ;</quote> <ref n="Soph. Phil. 897"> <title>Ph.</title> 897</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ οἶδ’ ὅποι χρὴ τἄπορον τρέπειν ἔπος</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔσχες_ἄλγος_1176">: <ref n="Soph. El. 897">cp. 897</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔσχον θαῦμα</quote>: and for the sense of the aor., 1256, 1465. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κλεινὸν_1177">, as a daughter of the great Agamemnon, the names of whose children were widely known: so Iolè, daughter of Eurytus, is <quote xml:lang="grc">κάρτα λαμπρὰ καὶ κατ’ ὄνομα καὶ φύσιν</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. Trach. 379"> <title>Tr.</title> 379</ref>). Cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 575"> <title>Ph.</title> 575</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅδ’ ἔσθ’ ὁ κλεινός σοι Φιλοκτήτης, ξένε</quote>.—Though joined with <quote xml:lang="grc"> <emph>εἶδος</emph>, κλεινὸν</quote> should not be taken as referring to the fame of her beauty; it is equivalent to <quote xml:lang="grc">κλεινῆς</quote>, by the common idiom (785). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_μάλ_1178">: here, as in 1455, the <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ</quote>=‘and’; sometimes, however, <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ μάλα</quote>=<emph>vel maxime</emph> (cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ πολύ, καὶ λίαν</quote>), as in <ref n="Xen. Cyrop. 6. 1. 36">Xen. <title>Cyr.</title> 6. 1. 36</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνθρώπους..καὶ μάλα δοκοῦντας φρονίμους εἶναι</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ταλαίνης_1179"> is better taken with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="συμφορᾶς_1179"> than with <quote xml:lang="grc">σοῦ</quote> understood. Cp. <ref n="Aesch. Seven 695">Aesch. <title>Th.</title> 695</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τάλαιν’ ἀρά</quote>: <ref n="Aesch. Lib. 1069">Aesch. <title>Ch.</title> 1069</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μόχθοι τάλανες</quote>. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 980">Soph. <title>Ai.</title> 980</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὤμοι βαρείας ἆρα τῆς ἐμῆς τύχης</quote>: where, as here, and in <title>O.T.</title> 1395, <title>O.C.</title> 409, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἆρα</quote>=simply <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄρα</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐ_1180"> <quote xml:lang="grc">δή ποτ’</quote>, the reading of the scholiast, is clearly better than that which prevails in our MSS., <quote xml:lang="grc"> <emph>τί</emph> δήποτ’</quote>. It expresses her first feeling of surprise: she can hardly believe that his pity is for her. In 1184, on the other hand, <quote xml:lang="grc"> <emph>τί</emph> δή ποτ’</quote> is fitting: she has recognised the fact, and asks the cause. <ref n="Soph. El. 1108">Cp. 1108</ref>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀτίμως_1181">, ruthlessly: <ref n="Soph. El. 444">cp. 444</ref> n.— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κἀθέως_1181">, in the act. sense of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄθεος</quote>, ‘disregarding the gods,’ ‘impious.’ Kinsfolk have wronged their kinswoman. Cp. 124 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀθεώτατα</quote>: <ref n="Antiph. 1.21">Antiphon or. 1 § 21</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀθέως καὶ ἀκλεῶς πρὸ τῆς εἱμαρμένης ὑφ’ ὧν ἥκιστ’ ἐχρῆν τὸν βίον ἐκλιπών</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἢ_μὲ_1182">: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 83"> <title>Ant.</title> 83</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μὴ )μοῦ προτάρβει</quote>: <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 736 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἢ )μοί.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δυσφημεῖς_1182">: <ref n="Soph. El. 905">cp. 905</ref> n. The schol. gives the sense rightly, <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ δύσφημα ταῦτα ἂ λέγεις ἐμοὶ καὶ οὐκ ἄλλῳ τινὶ ἁρμόζει</quote>. For the fem. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄλλην_1182">, <ref n="Soph. El. 100">cp. 100</ref>. The words express, courteously, yet with a certain reserve and dignity, her surprise that a stranger should make the comment; </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ξένε_1182"> is significant. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀνύμφου_1183">, as her forlorn appearance shows (<ref n="Soph. El. 188">cp. 188</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">φίλος οὔτις ἀνὴρ ὑπερίσταται</quote>: also 165, 962).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τροφῆς_1183">, way of life: <ref n="Soph. Aj. 499"> <title>Ai.</title> 499</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">δουλίαν ἕξειν τροφήν</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τί_ποτ_1184">. <quote xml:lang="grc">δή</quote>, which the scribe of L appears to have regarded as the true reading (cr. n.), is slightly better here than </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μοι_1184">, which would be an ethic dat. (‘I pray thee’: <ref n="Soph. El. 144">cp. 144</ref>): <quote xml:lang="grc">μοι</quote> could not depend on </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπισκοπῶν_1184">, which would require the acc. The partic. is absolute (‘with this steadfast gaze’); it does not govern </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τί_1184"> (as=‘with what meaning?’). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐν_εἰρημένων_1186">, ‘by means of what that has been said?’: cp. Plat. <title>Prot.</title>p. 324 <quote xml:lang="grc">Ε ἐν τούτῳ..λύεται ἡ ἀπορία</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὁρῶν_σὲ_1187"> <quote xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</quote> ‘What,’ she asks, ‘has quickened this sense of <emph>thy</emph> woes?’ ‘The sight of <emph>thine</emph>,’ he answers. Clearly we must write </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σὲ_1187">, not </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σε_1187">: the antithesis with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῶν_ἐμῶν_1187"> (1185) requires it, and otherwise the point is lost. [A school ed. published by me in 1867 was the first, so far as I know, which gave </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σέ_1187">. Mr Blaydes (ed. of 1873) approved this (p. 322), and adopted it.]</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐμπρέπουσαν_1187">. Cp. <ref n="Aesch. Lib. 17">Aesch. <title>Ch.</title> 17</ref>(Electra) <quote xml:lang="grc">πένθει λυγρῷ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">πρέπουσαν</quote>: which refers to all the outward signs of grief, and not merely to dress. <title>Suppl.</title> 116 (if sound) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἰηλέμοισιν ἐμπρεπῆ</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμφερῆ</quote> Tucker) <quote xml:lang="grc">ζῶσα γόοις με τιμῶ</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πόθεν_1191">, predicate: <quote xml:lang="grc">πόθεν ἐστὶ τοῦτο τὸ κακὸν ὃ ἐξεσήμηνας</quote>; Cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 26"> <title>Ph.</title> 26</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοὔργον οὐ μακρὰν λέγεις</quote> (n.). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἶτα_1192"> marks a further aggravation of her lot. His murderers though they are, she is their slave.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δουλεύω_1192">: 190 n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀνάγκῃ_προτρέπει_1193">. Cp. <ref n="Hom. Il. 6.336"> <title>Il.</title> 6. 336</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἥμην ἐν θαλάμῳ, ἔθελον δ’ ἄχεϊ προτραπέσθαι</quote>, ‘to turn forwards towards’ grief, and so, ‘to <emph>yield</emph> myself up to it.’ It seems possible that this Homeric use of the middle was that on which Sopho cles modelled his use of the act. here. ‘Who <emph>causes</emph> thee to <emph>yield</emph> to this necessity,’—‘subjects thee’ to it? No dative occurs with this verb elsewhere; nor is any emendation probable. [I formerly understood, ‘<emph>impels</emph> thee <emph>with</emph> this necessity.’ But this is awkward: in <ref n="Soph. Ant. 269"> <title>Ant.</title> 269</ref> f. <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅς πάντας ἐς πέδον κάρα</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">νεῦσαι φόβῳ προὔτρεψεν</quote>, the sense is, indeed, ‘impelled,’ but the inf. aids it, as in <ref n="Soph. OT 358"> <title>O. T.</title> 358</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">σὺ γάρ μ’ ἄκοντα προὐτρέψω λέγειν</quote>.] </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐξισοῖ_1194">, trans., as in 738 and elsewhere in Sophocles: schol. <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ ἴσα πράττει τῷ τῆς μητρὸς ὀνόματι</quote>. Cp. <ref n="Soph. Trach. 818"> <title>Tr.</title> 818</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μηδὲν ὡς τεκοῦσα δρᾷ</quote>. It is needless to assume an intrans. use here (‘resembles’). Such an use is generally recognised, indeed, in <ref n="Thuc. 5. 71">Thuc. 5. 71</ref>§ 2, and 6. 87 § 5; though in each place it is possible to supply an acc. from the context. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χερσὶν_1195">, personal violence, hinted at in 627, 912: </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λύμη_βίου_1195">, in respect of food, lodging, dress, etc.; 189 ff. Cp. <ref n="Soph. Trach. 793"> <title>Tr.</title> 793</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">λυμαντὴν βίου</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὑπαρήξων_1197">: cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 297"> <title>O. T.</title> 297</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προὔθηκας_1198">, set before me, presented: cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1294"> <title>Ai.</title> 1294</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">προθέντ’ ἀδελφῷ δεῖπνον</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ποτέ_1200"> is right; the <emph>v.l.</emph> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐμέ_1200"> was probably due to a fancied need for the pron., which, however, can easily be understood; cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 801"> <title>Ph.</title> 801</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔμπρησον</quote> (n.). If it were required, it would be simplest to read </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νύν_μ_1200">. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῖσι_σοῖς_1201"> was the prevalent reading here, while </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῖς_ἴσοις_1201"> can claim to be the original reading of L: but, in a case where confusion was so easy, the authority of our MSS. is not great. In <ref n="Soph. OT 648"> <title>O. T.</title> 648</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πάρεισί σοι</quote> has been made in L from <quote xml:lang="grc">πάρεισ’ ἴσοι</quote>: and in <title>Vesp.</title> 747 <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῖς ἴσοις</quote> was a <emph>v.l.</emph> for the true <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῖσι σοῖς</quote>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ξυγγενὴς_1201"> in 1202 is the point which inclines me to prefer </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῖσι_σοῖς_1201_2">. If he had said </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῖς_ἴσοις_1201_2">,—‘equal,’ or ‘equivalent,’ woes,—that would have explained, indeed, why he should feel sympathy; but it would not have warranted the surmise that he was a kinsman. The objection that, with <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῖσι σοῖς</quote>, v. 1201 merely repeats 1200, is un founded. He <emph>pities</emph> her woes, he says, because he <emph>feels</emph> them (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλγῶν</quote>). Cp. <ref n="Aesch. Lib. 222">Aesch. <title>Cho.</title> 222</ref>(shortly before the <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀναγνώρισις</quote>): <quote xml:lang="grc">ΗΛ. ἀλλ’ ἐν κακοῖσι τοῖς ἐμοῖς γελᾶν θέλεις</quote>; | <quote xml:lang="grc">ΟΡ. κἀν τοῖς ἐμοῖς ἄῤ, εἴπερ ἔν γε τοῖσι σοῖς</quote>. While preferring <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῖσι σοῖς</quote>, however, I recognise that <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῖς ἴσοις</quote> is also quite tenable. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸ_τῶνδε_1203">=<quote xml:lang="grc">αἵδε</quote>. Cp. <title>Legg.</title> p. 657 <quote xml:lang="grc">Δ οἱ μὲν νέοι...τὸ δὲ τῶν πρεσβυτέρων</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νυν_1205"> refers to the assurance just given, <quote xml:lang="grc">πρὸς πιστὰς ἐρεῖς</quote>: it is clearly better than <quote xml:lang="grc">νῦν</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πιθοῦ_1207">: cp. <ref n="Soph. Trach. 470"> <title>Tr.</title> 470</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πιθοῦ λεγούσῃ</quote>. L, with most MSS., has </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πείθου_1207">: <ref target="cl1015">see on 1015</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρὸς_γενείου_1208">: a formula of solemn appeal, accompanied, perhaps. by the gesture of raising her right hand towards his face. In <ref n="Hom. Il. 1.500"> <title>Il.</title> 1. 500</ref> f. Thetis clasps the knees of Zeus with her left hand, and places her right under his chin. The same two acts of supplication are associated in <ref n="Eur. Her. 1207">Eur. <title>H. F.</title> 1207</ref>, <ref n="Eur. Andr. 573">Eur. <title>Andr.</title> 573</ref>, etc. Cp. <ref n="Eur. Hec. 344">Eur. <title>Hec.</title> 344</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μή σου προσθίγω γενειάδος</quote>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μ_ξέλη_1208">, is stronger and more pathetic than </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μ_ἐξέλῃ_1208">: and <quote xml:lang="grc">με</quote> is easily understood. For the reiterated <quote xml:lang="grc">μή</quote>, cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 210"> <title>O. C.</title> 210</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μή, μή μ’ ἀνέρῃ</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Aj. 191"> <title>Ai.</title> 191</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μή, μή μ’, ἄναξ</quote>,.. | ..<quote xml:lang="grc">κακὰν φάτιν ἄρῃ.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐξέλῃ_1208">, properly, ‘take out of my keeping,’ while <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀφέλῃ</quote> would be simply ‘take away from me.’ So <ref n="Hdt. 3. 137">Her. 3. 137</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἑξαιρεθέντες τε τὸν Δημοκήδεα καὶ τὸν γαῦλον..ἀπαιρεθέντες.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὰ_φίλτατα_1208">: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1110"> <title>O. C.</title> 1110</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔχω τὰ φίλτατ’</quote> (his daughters): <ref n="Soph. Phil. 434"> <title>Ph.</title> 434</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">σοῦ πατρὸς ἦν τὰ φίλτατα</quote> (Patroclus). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐ_ἐάσειν_1209">. Cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 816"> <title>Ph.</title> 816</ref> f. <quote xml:lang="grc">ΦΙ. μέθες, μέθες με</quote>... | <quote xml:lang="grc">ΝΕ. οὐ φήμ’ ἐάσειν</quote>. He approaches her; she clings to the urn, and at v. 1216 is still holding it; then his words, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ οὐκ Ὀρέστου</quote> (1217), reconcile her to parting with it, and he gently takes it from her hands.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τάλαιν_σέθεν_1209">: cp. <ref n="Soph. Trach. 972"> <title>Tr.</title> 972</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἴμοι ἐγὼ σοῦ μέλεος</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰ_στερήσομαι_1210">. For <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ</quote> with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὦ_φῶς_1224">. Cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 530"> <title>Ph.</title> 530</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ φίλτατον μὲν ἦμαρ, <*>διστος δ’ ἀνήρ</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὦ_φθέγμ_1225">. A beautifully natural expression of her new joy in his living presence. So the solitary Philoctetes welcomes the sound of Greek speech,—<quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ φίλτατον φώνημα</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. Phil. 234"> <title>Ph.</title> 234</ref>). Cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 14"> <title>Ai.</title> 14</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ φθέγμ’ Ἀθάνας</quote>. <ref n="Soph. OC 863"> <title>O. C.</title> 863</ref>(Oed. to Creon) <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ φθέγμ’ ἀναιδές.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μηκέτ_πύθῃ_1225">. <ref n="Soph. El. 1474">Cp. 1474</ref>: <ref n="Soph. OC 1266"> <title>O. C.</title> 1266</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τἀμὰ μὴ )ξ ἄλλων πύθῃ</quote>. For <quote xml:lang="grc">μηκέτ’</quote>, cp. <ref n="Pind. O. 1">Pind. <title>O.</title> 1. 114</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μηκέτι πάπταινε πόρσιον</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔχω_ἀεί_1226">. Cp. <ref n="Eur. El. 578">Eur. <title>El.</title> 578</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ χρόνῳ φανείς</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔχω σ’ ἀέλπτως</quote>. <quote xml:lang="grc">ΟΠ. κἀξ ἐμοῦ γ’ ἔχει χρόνῳ</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πολίτιδες_1227">: <ref n="Soph. El. 251">cp. 251</ref> n.: 1413. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μηχαναῖσι_1228">: as <ref>Hamlet (3. 4. 188)</ref> is ‘mad in craft.’</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σεσωσμένον_1228">: <ref n="Soph. El. 60">cp. 60</ref>. Here the word implies, ‘brought safely home’ ( <ref n="Soph. Trach. 610"> <title>Tr.</title> 610</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐς δόμους</quote> | ..<quote xml:lang="grc">σωθέντ’</quote>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="συμφοραῖσι_1230">. Eustathius p. 647. 37: <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ ἡ συμφορὰ δὲ οὐ μόνον ἀποτρόπαιος ἀλλὰ καὶ ἀγαθή, ὡς δηλοῖ σὺν ἄλλοις καὶ ὁ εἰπὼν ἐπ’ ἀγαθῷ τὸ ἐπὶ συμφοραῖς γεγηθὸς...ἄπο</quote>. <ref n="Aristoph. Kn. 406">Ar. <title>Eq.</title> 406</ref>(from Simonides) <quote xml:lang="grc">πῖνε πῖν’ ἐπὶ συμφοραῖς. </quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γεγηθὸς_δάκρυον_1230">: cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 527"> <title>Ant.</title> 527</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">φιλάδελφα κάτω δάκρὐ εἰβομένη</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γοναὶ_σωμάτων_1233"> <quote xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</quote> This phrase seems to gain in fulness and force if taken as meaning, ‘son of the father whom I so loved,’ rather than as a mere periphrasis for <quote xml:lang="grc">σώματα φίλτατα</quote>, ‘dearest of all men ever born’: the latter would be weaker than (<foreign xml:lang="lat">e.g.</foreign>) <quote xml:lang="grc">τέκνων σῶν..δίπτυχος γονή</quote> ( <ref n="Eur. Med. 1136">Eur. <title>Med.</title> 1136</ref>), though it is tenable. In either case the use of the plur. <quote xml:lang="grc">σωμάτων</quote> instead of <quote xml:lang="grc">σώματος</quote> (schol. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀντὶ τοῦ ἑνικοῦ</quote>) is very bold, and seemingly unique. (<ref n="Soph. El. 206">Cp. 206</ref>, n. on <quote xml:lang="grc">θανάτους</quote>.) It is confirmed, however, by the fact that Latin poetry tolerates a corresponding use of <foreign xml:lang="lat">corpora:</foreign> <cit> <bibl n="Ov. Ep. 8">Ovid <title>Her. Epist.</title> 8. 113</bibl> <quote xml:lang="lat">male corpora tacta relinquo</quote> </cit>, the (living) body of Pyrrhus. (Conversely in <ref n="Eur. Med. 1108">Eur. <title>Med.</title> 1108</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">σῶμα τέκνων</quote> = <ref n="Eur. Med. 1111">ib. 1111</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">σώματα τέκνων</quote>.) The plur. certainly cannot be explained by supposing a reference to Pylades also. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀρτίως_1234"> marks her sense of the sudden change from the despair which she was feeling but a few moments before.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὓς_1234">, meaning herself: for the plur., cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 1184"> <title>O. T.</title> 1184</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ξὺν οἷς τ’</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ χρῆν ὁμιλῶν, οὕς τέ μ’ οὐκ ἔδει κτανών.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐχρήζετε_1234">: <ref n="Soph. El. 171">cp. 171</ref>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σῖγ_ἔχουσα_1236">: so <ref n="Soph. Phil. 258"> <title>Ph.</title> 258</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc"><*>γ’ ἔχοντες</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλ_μὰ_1239"> <quote xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</quote> Far the most probable account of the text here is that an iambic trimeter was corrupted, first by an accidental transposition of words and then by attempted correction, into the form which appears in most MSS. (cr. n.), <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ οὐ τὰν Ἄρτεμιν τὰν αἰὲν ἀδμήταν</quote>. A strong argument for this is the antistrophic verse, 1260, <quote xml:lang="grc">τίς οὖν ἂν ἀξίαν γε σοῦ πεφηνότος</quote>: where <quote xml:lang="grc">ἂν</quote>, though omitted by the scribe of L, was supplied by another early hand, and appears in A, as in most other MSS., and in the Aldine. The inference is confirmed by the manner in which <emph>pairs</emph> of iambic trimeters alternate with lyrics throughout this strophe: 1235 f., 1243 f., 1251 f.—For other views, see Appendix.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὴν_ἄδμητον_1239">: cp. the prayer of the Danaïdes to Artemis, <title>Suppl.</title> 149 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀδμάτας ἀδμάτα</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ῥύσιος γενέσθω.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Ἄρτεμιν_1239">: 626 n. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τόδε_ἀεί_1240">. These words express her new exultation in the sense that she has a brother for her champion.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="περισσὸν_ἄχθος_1240">, vainly burdening the ground: cp. fr. 859 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς οὐδέν ἐσμεν πλὴν σκιαῖς ἐοικότες</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">βάρος περισσὸν γῆς ἀναστρωφώμενοι</quote>. <ref n="Hom. Od. 20.378"> <title>Od.</title> 20. 378</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδέ τι ἔργων</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔμπαιον οὐδὲ βίης, ἀλλ’ αὔτως ἄχθος ἀρούρης</quote>. <ref n="Hom. Il. 18.104"> <title>Il.</title> 18. 104</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ ἧμαι παρὰ νηυσὶν ἐτώσιον ἄχθος ἀρούρης.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔνδον_ἀεί_1240">. Cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 343"> <title>O. C.</title> 343</ref> n.: <ref n="Eur. Orest. 928">Eur. <title>Or.</title> 928</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τἄνδον οἰκουρήμαθ’</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γε_δὴ_1243">: cp. <ref n="Soph. Trach. 484"> <title>Tr.</title> 484</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπεί γε μὲν δὴ</quote> (n.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Ἄρης_1243">, the warlike spirit: cp. <ref n="Aesch. Ag. 78">Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 78</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἄρης δ’ οὐκ ἐνὶ χώρᾳ</quote>: id. <title>Suppl.</title> 749 <quote xml:lang="grc">γυνὴ μονωθεῖσ’ οὐδέν· οὐκ ἔνεστ’ Ἄρης</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εὖ_ἔξοισθα_1243"> <foreign xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</foreign> These words prove the genuineness of the verse (which Triclinius rejected, cr. n.): for it is this allusion to her sufferings at the hands of Clyt. that prompts Electra's reply. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀνέφελον_κακόν_1246">. Her sorrow—the long and bitter strife with her mother—is one over which, from its nature (<quote xml:lang="grc">οἷον ἔφυ</quote>), no veil can be drawn; it is manifest to all, and fierce, like the sun in a cloudless summer sky. It is a sorrow which can never be done away with, <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔ ποτε καταλύσιμον</quote>, because no reconciliation is possible. And it is one of which no time can efface the memory (<quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδέ ποτε λησόμενον</quote>).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐνέβαλες_1246">:—<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπείπερ ὑπέμνησεν αὐτὴν τῆς τοῦ πατρὸς ἀναιρέσεως, διὰ τῶν προειρημένων δύο στίχων φησὶν ὅτι ἐνέβαλές μοι κακὸν ἀνέφελον</quote>. This yields a good sense; ‘thou hast mentioned’ (<emph>mentionem iniecisti</emph>). Cp. <ref n="Plat. Rep. 344">Plat. <title>Rep.</title> 344</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">Δ οἷον ἐμβαλὼν λόγον ἐν νῷ ἔχεις ἀπιέναι</quote>. The traditional </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπέβαλες_1246"> has been explained in two ways. (1) ‘You have <emph>mentioned.</emph>’ For this sense there is no parallel. (2) ‘You have <emph>laid</emph> the burden of the woe <emph>upon</emph> me,’—<foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, ‘brought it to my recollection.’ This is perhaps just possible: but it is so artificial as to seem improbable. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὑπέβαλες_1246">, ‘you have suggested,’ would be an easy correction: <ref n="Soph. El. 833">cp. 833</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑποίσεις</quote>. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1170"> <title>Ph.</title> 1170</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">παλαιὸν ἄλγημ’ ὑπέμνασας</quote>. If, however, it was the original word, the corruption to <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπέβαλες</quote> is strange, since the sense would have been clear.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καταλύσιμον_1246">, fitting here, as suggestive of <quote xml:lang="grc">καταλύεσθαι ἔχθραν, πόλεμον, κ.τ.λ.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λησόμενον_1246">: ‘that never its own burden <emph>can forget</emph>,’ as Whitelaw renders. The <quote xml:lang="grc">κακόν</quote> is half-personified here, though not in the preceding clauses; precisely as in <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1167"> <title>Ph.</title> 1167</ref> the <quote xml:lang="grc">κήρ</quote> (disease) is <quote xml:lang="grc">οἰκτρὰ.. βόσκειν, ἀδαὴς δ’</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔχειν μυρίον ἄχθος ὃ ξυνοικεῖ</quote> (n.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔξοιδα_ταῦτ_1251">: ‘these things <emph>also</emph>,’ <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, the sufferings to which she has just alluded. The change of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_1251"> to <quote xml:lang="grc">παῖ</quote> (rightly made in <ref n="Soph. Phil. 79"> <title>Ph.</title> 79</ref>) is needless here.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παρουσία_1251"> may be freely rendered ‘occasion’; it seems to be a purposely vague word, intended to suggest ‘the presence’ of the murderers; a dark hint of the coming vengeance. When their presence admonishes (</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φράζῃ_1251">),—gives the signal for action,—then will be the time to recall their crimes; which are indicated, with a similar reserve, by </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔργων_τῶνδε_1251">.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παρρησία_1251">, doubtless a conjecture, would enfeeble the passage, and would further require us to alter </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φράζῃ_1251_2">. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὁ_χρόνος_1253">, ‘all time’ to come: cp. <ref n="Isoc. 1.11">Isocr. or. 1 § 11</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπιλίποι δ’ ἂν ἡμᾶς ὁ πᾶς χρόνος, εἰ πάσας τὰς ἐκείνου πράξεις καταριθμησαίμεθα</quote>. In <ref n="Pind. P. 1">Pind. <title>P.</title> 1. 46</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ πᾶς χρόνος</quote> has a like sense. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παρὼν_1253">, ‘when present,’ <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, ‘as it comes.’ There can be no moment at which she might not fitly make her just complaints. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δίκᾳ_1253"> goes with </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐννέπειν_1253">, not with </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρέποι_1253">.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μόλις_γὰρ_1253"> <foreign xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</foreign>: <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, having waited so long for freedom of utterance, she should not now be required to keep silence.— </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔσχον_1253">, ‘have obtained’: <ref n="Soph. El. 1176">cp. 1176</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σῴζου_τόδε_1257">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> the <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐλεύθερον στόμα</quote>. If she is overheard in the house, she may yet lose the newly-gained free dom. Except in the sense of ‘remembering’ (993 n.), the midd. of the simple <quote xml:lang="grc">σῴζω</quote> is somewhat rare; but cp. <ref n="Aristoph. Kn. 1017">Ar. <title>Eq.</title> 1017</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">σῴζεσθαί σ’ ἐκέλευσ’ ἱερὸν κύνα</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μακρὰν_λέγειν_1259">: <ref n="Aristoph. Thes. 382">Ar. <title>Th.</title> 382</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μακρὰν ἔοικε λέξειν</quote>. Cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1040">Soph. <title>Ai.</title> 1040</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μ<*> τεῖνε μακράν</quote>: <ref n="Aesch. Ag. 916">Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 916</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μακρὰν γὰρ ἐξέτεινας</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τίς_οὖν_1260">, a remonstrance, in which <quote xml:lang="grc">οὖν</quote> may be rendered by ‘Nay.’ </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀξίαν_1260"> is predicative, and equiv. to an adverb, ‘worthily,’ ‘fitly’; cp. the schol., <quote xml:lang="grc">τίς ἄν, φησί, σοῦ φανέντος δικαίως ἕλοιτο ἀντὶ λόγων σιωπήν</quote>; I agree with the schol. also in taking </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σοῦ_πεφηνότος_1260"> as a gen. absol., rather than as depending on <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀξίαν</quote>. For the absolute use of the latter, <ref n="Soph. El. 298">cp. 298</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀξίαν δίκην</quote>: <ref n="Soph. OT 1004"> <title>O. T.</title> 1004</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">χάριν.. ἀξίαν</quote>: <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 133 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀξίως.</quote> Join </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λόγων_1260"> with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μεταβάλοιτ_1260">, not with <quote xml:lang="grc">σιγὰν</quote>: cp. <ref n="Antiph. 5.79">Antiphon or. 5 § 79</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἠλλάξαντο..εὐδαιμονίας.. κακοδαιμονίαν.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δε_1260">, as thou biddest.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τότ_εἶδες_1260">, the MSS. have </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅτε_1260">: but the tribrach in the second foot, while there is no caesura in the third, makes an intolerably lame verse. Read </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εὖτε_1260"> instead of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅτε_1260_2">, and scan </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θεοὶ_1260"> as a monosyllable by synizesis. In the other trimeters of Sophocles where <quote xml:lang="grc">θεὸς</quote> belongs to the third foot, it is always so scanned: <ref n="Soph. OC 964"> <title>O. C.</title> 964</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1020"> <title>Ph.</title> 1020</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Aj. 489"> <title>Ai.</title> 489</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1129."> Ai. 1129.</ref> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅτε_1260_3"> arose from </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τότ_1260">.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπώτρυναν_1260">, by the oracle (35): an answer to the reproach unconsciously conveyed by <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀέλπτως</quote> (1263). He came as soon as Apollo gave the word. After this verse, a trimeter has been lost, as the strophe shows, where v. 1244 (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἔνεστιν κ.τ.λ.</quote>) is certainly genuine. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χάριτος_1266">, a grace shown to her, a matter for thankfulness.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπόρισεν_1266"> is the best correction of the corrupt <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπόρσεν</quote> or <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπῶρσεν</quote>. It is true that <quote xml:lang="grc">πορίζω</quote> has not elsewhere the special sense of <quote xml:lang="grc">πορεύω</quote>, ‘to convey,’ ‘bring’: but how easily a poet might transfer that sense to it, is suggested by the similar case of <quote xml:lang="grc">πορεῖν</quote> in <ref n="Soph. OC 1457"> <title>O. C.</title> 1457</ref>, (<quote xml:lang="grc">πῶς ἄν τις</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">δεῦρο Θησέα πόροι</quote>, ‘fetch him hither.’ </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δαιμόνιον_1269">: cp. <ref n="Xen. Mem. 1. 3.">Xen. <title>Mem.</title> 1. 3.</ref>§ 5 <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ μή τι δαιμόνιον εἴη</quote>. <ref n="Dem. 2.1">Dem. or. 2 § 1</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">δαιμονίᾳ τινὶ καὶ θείᾳ παντάπασιν ἔοικεν εὐεργεσίᾳ.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τίθημ_1269">=<quote xml:lang="grc">τίθεμαι</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1166"> <title>Ant.</title> 1166</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ τίθημ’ ἐγὼ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ζῆν τοῦτον</quote>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὰ_δὲ_1271"> (adverbial), ‘on the one hand,’ ‘on the other’: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 534"> <title>Tr.</title> 534</ref> f. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὁδὸν_φανῆναι_1274">: <ref n="Soph. El. 1318">cp. 1318</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξήκεις ὁδόν</quote>. Here, too, the acc. is ‘cognate,’ since the verb implies <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐλθεῖν</quote>. (It is not really like <ref n="Soph. Aj. 878"> <title>Ai.</title> 878</ref>, <quote xml:lang="grc">κέλευθον..φανείς</quote>, where the acc. denotes the ground which the search has traversed.) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τί_ποήσω_1276">; He interrupts her entreaty, <quote xml:lang="grc"> <emph>μή</emph> τί με</quote>—, by asking, ‘what am I not to do?’ (the negative form of the delib. subjunct.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μή_ἁδονὰν_1277">: the double acc. with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀποστερεῖν</quote>, though less frequent than acc. of person and gen. of thing, is not rare: cp. Antiphon <title>Tetral.</title> <quote xml:lang="grc">β. γ.</quote> § 2 <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ ἥμισυ τῆς κατηγορίας ἐμαυτὸν ἂν ἀπεστέρησα</quote>: Isaeus or. 8 § 43 <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ.. χρήματα ἡμᾶς ἀποστερήσων</quote>: <ref n="Xen. Anab. 7. 6. 9">Xen. <title>An.</title> 7. 6. 9</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡμᾶς..ἀποστερεῖ τὸν μισθόν.</quote> </gloss>
