<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μεθέσθαι_1277">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτῆς</quote>, epexegetic inf.: <ref target="cl543">see on 543</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">δαίσασθαι</quote>. Cp. <ref n="Eur. Med. 736">Eur. <title>Med.</title> 736</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄγουσιν οὐ μεθεἶ ἂν ἐκ γαίας ἐμέ</quote>, where the acc. depends on the part., and <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμοῦ</quote> is to be supplied with the verb. (Porson, in his note on that v., suggests <quote xml:lang="grc">ἁδονᾶν</quote> here, comparing the plur. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡδονὰς</quote> in 1302.) </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ξυναινεῖς_1279">, as in 402, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 122."> <title>Ph.</title> 122.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τί_οὔ_1279">; ‘why should I not?’ =‘of course I do.’ Cp. [Eur. ] <title>Rhes.</title> 706 <quote xml:lang="grc">ΗΜ. Α. δοκεῖς γάρ; ΗΜ. Β. τί μὴν οὔ;</quote> Similarly, without <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔ</quote>, <ref n="Aesch. Eum. 203">Aesch. <title>Eum.</title> 203</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔχρησα ποινὰς τοῦ πατρὸς πέμψαι· τί μήν</quote>; (‘what then?’ <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> ‘I was right in doing so.’) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὦ_φίλαι_1281">. It seems fitting that, towards the close of these lyrics, Electra should address some words to the sympathetic Chorus,—as she did before, just after the discovery (1227); though at v. 1285 she again speaks to Orestes. The emendation </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὦ_ὅτ_1281"> (Wunder), like others designed to alter </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φίλαι_1281">, is hardly probable.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὐδάν_1281"> is unquestionably the living voice of Orestes, which ‘she could never have hoped to hear,’ after the apparent proofs of his death. <ref n="Soph. El. 1225">Cp. 1225</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ φθέγὐ, ἀφίκου</quote>: and with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐδ_ἤλπις_1281">, <ref n="Soph. El. 1263">cp. 1263</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀέλπτως</quote>: 832 f. <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ τῶν φανερῶς οἰχομένων</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰς Ἀΐδαν ἐλπίδ’ ὑποίσεις</quote>: and 858 f. The first and best scholium on 1281 recognises that <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐδάν</quote> denotes a source of joy, not of grief, to her (<quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ γὰρ ἡ ἡδονὴ προετρέπετο αὐτὴν βοῆσαι</quote>), though wrongly explaining the word as <quote xml:lang="grc">φήμην περὶ τοῦ ἀδελφοῦ</quote> (news of his safety). If <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐδάν</quote> meant the report of his death, the words <quote xml:lang="grc">ἃν ἐγὼ οὐδ’ ἂν ἤλπισ’</quote> would lose all force.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔσχον_1281">. Arndt supplies </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐδ_ἂν_1281">, which might easily have been omitted, either through its likeness to <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐδάν</quote> just before it, or through the <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδ’ ἂν</quote> above it. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὁρμὰν_1281">, for the traditional <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀργὰν</quote>, is due to Blomfield (<title>Mus. Crit.</title> 1. 214).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὁρμὰν_1281_2"> is the ‘impulse’ or ‘emotion’ which compelled her to utter her new joy. She turns to these sympathetic women, and excuses her incaution by her happiness.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐδ_ἔσχον_1281"> is the potential indicative: see Goodwin, <title>Moods and Tenses</title>, new ed., § 244. Cp. <ref n="Dem. 37.57">Dem. or. 37 § 57</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πῶς ἂν...ἐγώ τί σε ἠδίκησα</quote>; (‘how could I possibly have wronged thee?’). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄναυδον_1281">, proleptic: <ref n="Aesch. Ag. 1247">Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 1247</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">εὔφημον... κοίμησον στόμα</quote>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τάλαινα_1285"> is a comment on her own joyous emotion, precisely as in 902 <quote xml:lang="grc">κεὐθὺς τάλαιν’ ὡς εἶδον κ.τ.λ.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὰ_λόγων_1288">: cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 24"> <title>Ph.</title> 24</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τἀπίλοιπα τῶν λόγων</quote> (n.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_μήτηρ_1289"> <quote xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</quote> These verses plainly intimate the poet's opinion that it would be a fault in art to retard the action at this point by a long narrative. A similar criticism is implied in <ref n="Soph. OC 1115"> <title>O. C.</title> 1115</ref> f., <quote xml:lang="grc">καί μοι τὰ πραχθέντ’ εἴπαθ’ ὡς βράχιστ’, ἐπεὶ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ταῖς τηλικαῖσδε σμικρὸς ἐξαρκεῖ λόγος</quote> (n.).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πατρῴαν_1290">, properly the epithet of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δόμων_1290"> (492 n.): <ref n="Soph. El. 960">cp. 960</ref>.—(<quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ μὲν</quote>) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀντλεῖ_ἐκχεῖ_1290">: cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 1228"> <title>O. T.</title> 1228</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅσα</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">κεύθει, τὰ δ’ αὐτίκ’ εἰς τὸ φῶς φανεῖ κακά</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 117"> <title>Tr.</title> 117</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">στρέφει, τὸ δ’ αὔξει</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκχεῖ_1290">, <emph>effundit:</emph> <ref n="Aesch. Pers. 826">Aesch. <title>Pers.</title> 826</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄλβον ἐκχέῃ μέγαν</quote>: <title>Anth. Pal.</title> 9. 367 <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν πατρικὸν πλοῦτον</quote>... | <quote xml:lang="grc">αἰσχρῶς εἰς ἀκρατεῖς ἐξέχεεν δαπάνας</quote>. As dist. from <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκχεῖ</quote> (profuse outlay on luxury), </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="διασπείρει_μάτην_1290"> expresses <emph>aimless</emph> waste, which obtains no return. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀντλεῖ_1290">, <emph>exhaurit</emph>, is properly the general term, while the other two verbs denote special modes of it. But, since <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ μὲν</quote> is implied in <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ δέ</quote>, the sentence is in form a rhetorical climax,— ‘spends,’ ‘spends profusely,’ ‘spends aimlessly. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χρόνου_καιρὸν_1292">, <emph>temporis modum</emph>, due limit or measure of time. Cp. <ref n="Pind. N. 7">Pind. <title>N.</title> 7. 58</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὶν δ’ ἐοικότα καιρὸν ὄλβου</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">δίδωσι</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">Μοῖρα</quote>): <ref n="Aesch. Ag. 785">Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 785</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πῶς σε σεβίζω</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">μήθ’ ὑπεράρας μήθ’ ὑποκάμψας</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">καιρὸν χάριτος</quote>; (‘the due measure of courtesy’).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐξείργοι_1292">, ‘shut out,’ ‘preclude.’</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φανέντες_1294">, if it be safe for them to enter the house (as it is, <ref n="Soph. El. 1308">cp. 1308</ref>): </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κεκρυμμένοι_1294">, if it be necessary to wait in concealment until they can safely enter.— </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γελῶντας_1294">: <ref n="Soph. El. 1153">cp. 1153</ref>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὕτω_δὲ_1296"> (<quote xml:lang="grc">σκόπει</quote>) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅπως_1296">: cp. <ref n="Aristoph. Frogs 905">Ar. <title>Ran.</title> 905</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ ὡς τάχιστα χρὴ λέγειν· οὕτω δ’ ὅπως ἐρεῖτον</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀστεῖα</quote>. Without <quote xml:lang="grc">οὕτω</quote>, <ref n="Soph. OT 1518">Soph. <title>O. T.</title> 1518</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">γῆς μ’ ὅπως πέμψεις ἄποικον</quote> (n.).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πιγνώσεται_1296">, ‘detect’: the dat. is instrumental.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νῷν_1296">, Orestes and Pylades: <ref n="Soph. El. 1372">cp. 1372</ref> f.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπελθόντοιν_δόμους_1296">: cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 152"> <title>Ant.</title> 152</ref> f. <quote xml:lang="grc">ναοὺς χοροῖς</quote> | ..<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπέλθωμεν</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μάτην_1298">, falsely: 63 n.—The conjecture </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μὴ_1298"> for </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῇ_1298"> would mean, ‘as if the calamity were not a fiction’; but the vulgate is manifestly better.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εὐτυχησωμεν_1298"> is strictly the ‘ingressive’ aorist, ‘when we shall have become prosperous.’ </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_σοὶ_1301">, ‘to thee, on thy part’ (<ref n="Soph. El. 1146">cp. 1146</ref> n.): followed by <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ τοὐμὸν</quote>, as in <ref n="Soph. OT 165"> <title>O. T.</title> 165</ref> f. <quote xml:lang="grc">εἴ ποτε καὶ προτέρας ἄτας</quote> is followed by <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔλθετε καὶ νῦν</quote>: <title>Nub.</title> 356 <quote xml:lang="grc">εἴπερ τινὶ κἄλλῳ</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐρανομήκη ῥήξατε κἀμοὶ φωνήν</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὧδ_τῇδε_1301">: <ref n="Soph. El. 643">cp. 643</ref>. <quote xml:lang="grc">τῇδε</quote> is here redundant, repeating the sense of <quote xml:lang="grc"><*>δε</quote> for emphasis.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοὐμὸν_1301">=‘my conduct’: cp. <ref n="Soph. Trach. 53"> <title>Tr.</title> 53</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">φράσαι τὸ σόν</quote> (n.).—For </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φίλον_1301">, cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1205"> <title>O. C.</title> 1205</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔστω δ’ οὖν ὅπως ὑμῖν φίλον</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κοὐκ_ἐμὰς_1303">, lit., ‘and not as my own,’—<foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, as the fruit of my own efforts. He has a right to restrict the joys which he has bestowed. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κοὐδ_1304">. The adverbial <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδὲ</quote> goes with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βραχὺ_1304">, though separated from it; cp. <ref n="Hom. Il. 1.354"> <title>Il.</title> 1. 354</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">νῦν δ’ οὐδέ με τυτθὸν ἔτισεν.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_1304"> precedes it as in <ref n="Xen. Anab. 3. 2. 4">Xen. <title>An.</title> 3. 2. 4</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ οὐδὲ Δία Ξένιον ᾐδέσθη</quote>.—The part. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λυπήσασα_1304"> expresses the condition of </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δεξαίμην_1304">: cp. Andoc. or. 1. § 3 <quote xml:lang="grc">πάντα τὰ ἀγαθὰ ἔχειν στερόμενος τῆς πατρίδος οὐκ ἂν δεξαίμην</quote>: Aeschin. or. 3. § 217 <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔτε τὰ αὐτὰ τούτῳ δημηγορήσας ἐδεξάμην ἂν ζῆν</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὑπηρετοίην_1306"> is a certain correction of the MS. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὑπηρετοίμην_1306">. The midd. is not found in classical writers, though it was frequent later. Metre proves the active form in five of the seven places where Soph. uses the verb (996; <ref n="Soph. OT 217">Soph. <title>O. T.</title> 217</ref>; <ref n="Soph. OC 283">Soph. <title>O. C.</title> 283</ref>; <ref n="Soph. Phil. 15">Soph. <title>Ph.</title> 15</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 990">Soph. Ph. 990</ref>): only here and in <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1024">Soph. <title>Ph.</title> 1024</ref> is the midd. even possible. Blaydes ingeniously suggests that <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑπηρετοίμην</quote> may have come from <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑπηρετμοίην. ὑπηρετμεῖν</quote>, however, does not occur.— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δαίμονι_1306">, the god who has brought him home (<ref n="Soph. El. 1266">cp. 1266</ref> ff.). Her brother is the <quote xml:lang="grc">καθαρτὴς πρὸς θεῶν ὡρμημένος</quote> (70). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τἀνθένδε_1307">=<quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ ἐνθάδε</quote>: cp. <ref n="Eur. Ba. 48">Eur. <title>Bacch.</title> 48</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰς δ’ ἄλλην χθόνα</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">τἀνθένδε θέμενος εὖ, μεταστήσω πόδα</quote>.—Not, ‘what should be done next’ (as in <ref n="Soph. OT 1267">Soph. <title>O. T.</title> 1267</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τἀνθένδ’</quote>=‘the sequel’). That was, indeed, the regular sense of the sing. <quote xml:lang="grc">τοὐνθένδε</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. Phil. 895">Soph. <title>Ph.</title> 895</ref>). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="στέγας_1308"> and </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἴκοις_1308"> are synonyms; <ref n="Soph. El. 986">cp. 986</ref> f. <quote xml:lang="grc">συμπόνει..σύγκαμν’</quote> (n.). —Electra here gives the information which was to have been brought by the old man (41). He, however, has been in the house since v. 803. At v. 1368 he repeats these facts.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δείσῃς_ὄψεται_1308">: <ref n="Soph. El. 1426">cp. 1426</ref> f.: <ref n="Xen. Cyrop. 5. 2. 12">Xen. <title>Cyr.</title> 5. 2. 12</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνδρὸς μὴ φοβοῦ ὡς ἀπο‐</quote> <quote xml:lang="grc">ρήσεις ἀξίου</quote>. So with <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅπως</quote>: <ref n="Eur. Heraclid. 248">Eur. <title>Heracl.</title> 248</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μὴ τρέσῃς ὅπως σέ τις</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">σὺν παισὶ βωμοῦ τοῦδ’ ἀποσπάσει βίᾳ</quote>.—Distinguish the wholly different use of <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅπως μή</quote>, instead of a simple <quote xml:lang="grc">μή</quote>, after a verb of fearing ( <ref n="Soph. OT 1074">Soph. <title>O. T.</title> 1074</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">δέδοιχ’ ὅπως</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">μὴ..ἀναρρήξει</quote>, ‘I fear that it will break forth’). Here, for example, <quote xml:lang="grc">δείσῃς ὅπως μὴ ὄψεται</quote> would mean the same thing as <quote xml:lang="grc">δείσῃς ὡς</quote> (or <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅπως</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄψετᾳι</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐντέτηκε_1311">: cp. Plat. <title>Menex.</title>p. 245 D <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅθεν καθαρὸν τὸ μῖσος ἐντέτηκε τῇ πόλει τῆς ἀλλοτρίας φύσεως</quote>. Lucian <ref n="Lucian Peregr. 22"> <title>Peregr.</title> 22</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοσοῦτος ἔρως τῆς δόξης ἐντέτηκεν αὐτῷ</quote>. The word leans to a bad sense (like ‘inveterate’): yet cp. Julian p. 130 C <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐντέτηκέ μοι δεινὸς τοῦ θεοῦ πόθος</quote>. See also <title>Tr</title> 463 (n.). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χαρᾷ_1312"> is right. The objection to the MS. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χαρᾶς_1312">, which Hermann and Paley keep, is the sense. She means that her tears of joy will be mistaken by Clyt. for tears of sorrow. But if we read <quote xml:lang="grc">χαρᾶς</quote>, too much stress is laid on that <emph>feeling</emph> of joy which she is to conceal, and </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δακρυρροοῦσα_1312"> then becomes ambiguous; since the words could mean, as Triclinius saw, <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔποτε παύσομαι τοῦ χαίρειν</quote> (=<quote xml:lang="grc">χαρᾶς</quote>), <quote xml:lang="grc">κἂν εἰς δάκρυα ἔλθω</quote> (‘even though I weep’). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἥτις_1314">, with causal force: <ref n="Soph. El. 187">cp. 187</ref>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐσεῖδον_1314"> suits <quote xml:lang="grc">θανόντα</quote> no less than <quote xml:lang="grc">ζῶντα</quote>, since she had seen the urn: <ref n="Soph. El. 1129">cp. 1129</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">νῦν μὲν γὰρ οὐδὲν ὄντα βαστάζω χεροῖν.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ᾶσκοπα_1314">, in a way which she could not have imagined beforehand; <ref n="Soph. El. 864">cp. 864</ref>. As the next verses show, the meaning is not merely, ‘thou hast given me an unlooked-for joy,’ but rather, ‘thou hast wrought upon my mind with a bewildering effect of joy,—so that, if the dead returned, I should scarcely marvel.’ </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅτε_1318">, causal: <ref n="Soph. El. 38">cp. 38</ref>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοιαύτην_1318">, so wondrous; prompted and conducted by a god (1266 ff.).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὥς_σοι_1318"> is better than </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡς_σοὶ_1318">, because, after the emphatic <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτός</quote>, the next stress should fall rather on <quote xml:lang="grc">θυμός</quote> than on <quote xml:lang="grc">σοι</quote> (‘rule me thyself, as thou <emph>wilt</emph>’). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐκ_ἥμαρτον_1320">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, would have secured one of the two things. Classical Greek idiom preferred this negative form to a positive (such as <quote xml:lang="grc">δυοῖν θατέρου ἂν ἔτυχον</quote>). The modes of stating the dilemma vary; thus we find: (1) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἢ γὰρ..ἢ</quote>, as here; Andoc. or. 1 § 4 <quote xml:lang="grc">δυοῖν.. οὐκ ἦν αὐτῷ ἁμαρτεῖν· ἢ γὰρ..μηνῦσαι.. ἢ ἀποκτεῖναι</quote>: so <ref n="Dem. 19.151">Dem. or. 19 § 151</ref>, etc. (2) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἢ..ἢ</quote>, as <ref n="Thuc. 1. 33">Thuc. 1. 33</ref>§ 3 <quote xml:lang="grc">μηδὲ δυοῖν φθάσαι ἁμάρτωσιν</quote> (not fail to be beforehand with us in one of two things), <quote xml:lang="grc">ἢ κακῶσαι..ἢ βεβαιώσασθαι</quote> n. (3) <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ μὲν γὰρ..εἰ δὲ</quote>, as <ref n="Isoc. 11.43">Isocr. or. 11 § 43</ref>.—Remark that the modification of this phrase used by <ref n="Eur. Orest. 1151">Eur. <title>Or.</title> 1151</ref>, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἑνὸς γὰρ οὐ σφαλέντες ἕξομεν κλέος</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">καλῶς θανόντες ἢ καλῶς σεσωσμένοι</quote>,—where <quote xml:lang="grc">δυοῖν</quote> is merely a bad conjecture,—is due to the fact that the principal verb, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἕξομεν</quote>, is positive. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σιγᾶν_χωροῦντος_1322">. Although it is usually the Chorus that announces a new comer, it is best to follow the MSS. in ascribing these words to Orestes, who has already so often enjoined silence (1236, etc.). The <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀντιλαβή</quote> in 1323 confirms the MSS., since a trimeter is seldom divided between the Chorus and another speaker. (The altercation between the Chorus and Creon in <ref n="Soph. OC 829"> <title>O. C.</title> 829</ref> ff. is an exception.)</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπῄνες_1322">: for the aor., <ref n="Soph. El. 668">cp. 668</ref>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπ_ἐξόδῳ_1322">: cp. <ref n="Soph. Trach. 532"> <title>Tr.</title> 532</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς ἐπ’ ἐξόδῳ.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῶν_ἔνδοθεν_1322">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">τινός</quote>: <ref n="Soph. El. 697">cp. 697</ref> (n.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄλλως_φέροντες_1324">: <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, besides the general claim of <quote xml:lang="grc">ξένοι</quote>, they have this special claim. So fr. 61 (a short speech befits <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῖς φρονοῦσι σώφρονα</quote>), <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄλλως τε καὶ κόρῃ τε κἀργείᾳ γένος</quote>. Aeschylus uses <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄλλως τε πάντως καὶ</quote> ( <ref n="Aesch. PB 636"> <title>P. V.</title> 636</ref>, <ref n="Aesch. Pers. 689"> <title>Pers.</title> 689</ref>).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἷ_ἀπώσαιτ_1324">: <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, ostensibly, the relics of a kinsman; in her secret meaning, retribution. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τητώμενοι_1326">: 265 n. The faithful old servant scolds them as if he was still their <quote xml:lang="grc">παιδαγωγός</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παρ_οὐδὲν_1326">, ‘of no account,’ is usu. joined with <quote xml:lang="grc">εἶναι</quote> (as <ref n="Soph. OT 983"> <title>O. T.</title> 983</ref>), or with verbs of ‘esteeming,’ such as <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄγειν</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. Ant. 35"> <title>Ant.</title> 35</ref>). The phrase here may be compared with <ref n="Soph. Ant. 466"> <title>Ant.</title> 466</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">παρ’ οὐδὲν ἄλγος</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐστί</quote>), n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐ_αὐτοῖς_1329"> <quote xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</quote> Here <quote xml:lang="grc">παρά</quote> has its simple locative sense, ‘beside.’ <quote xml:lang="grc"> <emph>παρὰ</emph> κακοῖς</quote> differs from <quote xml:lang="grc"> <emph>ἐν</emph> κακοῖς</quote> just as <quote xml:lang="grc">παρὰ πυρὶ</quote> ( <ref n="Hom. Od. 7.154"> <title>Od.</title> 7. 154</ref>) from <quote xml:lang="grc"> <emph>ἐν</emph> πυρί</quote>. They stand, not ‘just on the verge,’ but ‘just in the midst,’ of deadly perils. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὐτοῖς_1329"> is repeated, because it intensifies each of the prepositions. (<ref n="Soph. El. 720">Cp. 720</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑπ’ αὐτὴν.. στήλην</quote>, n.) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σταθμοῖσι_1331">, the door-posts. Cp. <ref n="Hom. Od. 22.181"> <title>Od.</title> 22. 181</ref>(with ref. to the watchers at the door of the armoury), <quote xml:lang="grc">τὼ δ’ ἔσταν ἑκάτερθε παρὰ σταθμοῖσι μένοντε</quote>. For the dat. of place, see 174 n. He stood there to intercept any inmate whom the sound of voices outside might have brought to the doors. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὑμὶν_ὑμῶν_1332">: the repeated pron. has a certain sarcastic force; ‘ye would have had your plans in the house,’ etc. If any change were needed, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡμὶν</quote> for <quote xml:lang="grc">ὗμὶν</quote> would be the best.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὰ_δρώμεν_1332">: 85 n. A listener at the doors must quickly have discovered that Orestes had returned; and Clyt. would have been warned. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὰ_σώματα_1332"> is a hint that the lives of the avengers would then have paid the penalty. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νῦν_νῦν_1334">. Cp. <ref n="Soph. Trach. 88"> <title>Tr.</title> 88</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">νῦν δ’</quote> (= ‘but as it was’), followed in 90 by <quote xml:lang="grc">νῦν δ’</quote> (= ‘but now’). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σὺν_βοῆς_1336"> has been rashly changed to <quote xml:lang="grc">σὺν βοῇ χαρᾶς</quote> (cr. n.). The latter would throw the emphasis on <quote xml:lang="grc">χαρᾶς</quote>: but it is the <quote xml:lang="grc">βοή</quote>, not the <quote xml:lang="grc">χαρά</quote>, which he deprecates. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀπηλλάχθαι_1338">: for the perf. (implying ‘at once’), <ref n="Soph. El. 64">cp. 64</ref> n.: <ref n="Soph. Aj. 479"> <title>Ai.</title> 479</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ ἢ καλῶς ζῆν ἢ καλῶς τεθνηκέναι</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν εὐγενῆ χρή</quote>. In 1335 we had <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπαλλαχθέντε</quote>: <ref n="Soph. El. 163">cp. 163</ref>, n. on <quote xml:lang="grc">γᾶν</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τἀντεῦθεν_1339">, ‘the next things,’ <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, the conditions with which he will have to deal as soon as he enters. <ref n="Soph. El. 728">Cp. 728</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κἀντεῦθεν</quote>, and 1307 n. on <quote xml:lang="grc">τἀνθένδε</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὑπάρχει_1340"> <quote xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</quote>, ‘it is secured that no one shall recognise thee’: cp. <ref n="Eur. Heraclid. 181">Eur. <title>Heracl.</title> 181</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄναξ, ὑπάρχει μὲν τόδ’ ἐν τῇ σῇ χθονί</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰπεῖν ἀκοῦσαί τ’ ἐν μέρει πάρεστί μοι.</quote> <ref n="Dem. 3.15">Dem. or. 3 § 15</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦτ’ οὖν δεῖ προσεῖναι, τὰ δ’ ἄλλα ὑπάρχει</quote>.—For the verbal ambiguity of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σε_τινά_1340">, cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 288">Soph. <title>Ant.</title> 288</ref>(n.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡς_τεθν_1341">.: for the double <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς</quote>, cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 735"> <title>Ant.</title> 735</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁρᾷς τόδ’ ὡς εἴρηκας ὡς ἄγαν νέος</quote>; and <ref n="Soph. Trach. 394."> <title>Tr.</title> 394.</ref>The pron. <quote xml:lang="grc">με</quote> is easily understood; <ref n="Soph. El. 1200">cp. 1200</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἷς_ἀνήρ_1342">, ‘one of the dead’: for this indefinite use of <quote xml:lang="grc">εἷς</quote>, cp. <ref n="Isoc. 20.11">Isocr. or. 20 § 11</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὧν οὗτος εἷς ὢν τυγχάνει</quote>. More often <quote xml:lang="grc">τις</quote> is added, as Ar. fr. 418. 2 <quote xml:lang="grc">τῶν ἀδολεσχῶν εἷς γέ τις</quote> (cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 269">Soph. <title>Ant.</title> 269</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τις εἷς</quote>, n.). Here <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνήρ</quote> virtually=<quote xml:lang="grc">τις</quote>. —Campbell renders: ‘I would have you know that you are the only dead man who is in the light of day’ (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐνθάδε</quote>, as opp. to <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν Ἅιδου</quote>). But </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐνθάδε_1342"> must surely mean, ‘in the house.’ </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χαίρουσιν_τούτοισιν_1343">..; Though </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐν_1343"> has L's support, </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὖν_1343">, the prevalent reading, is far better. With <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν</quote>, the sense would be, ‘rejoice under these circumstances’ (not, ‘<emph>in</emph> these things’); see on <ref n="Soph. Trach. 1118"> <title>Tr.</title> 1118</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν οἷς</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">χαίρειν προθυμεῖ</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τελουμένων_1344"> cannot mean ‘when the <emph>deed of vengeance</emph> is being done.’ Rather it is a purposely vague phrase; ‘when our task is being finished’; ‘towards the end’: <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, when, the vengeance having been taken, that work is being crowned by re-establishing a rightful rule in the house. Cp. <ref n="Eur. Andr. 997">Eur. <title>Andr.</title> 997</ref>(<quote xml:lang="grc">μηχανὴ</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἣν πάρος μὲν οὐκ ἐρῶ</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">τελουμένων δὲ Δελφὶς εἴσεται πέτρα</quote>: where the phrase has a similar tone of mystery and reserve. —For the neut. plur. part. in the gen. abs., without subject, cp. <ref n="Aesch. Seven 274">Aesch. <title>Th.</title> 274</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ε<*> ξυντυχόντων</quote>: <title>I. A.</title> 1022 <quote xml:lang="grc">καλῶς δὲ κρανθέντων</quote>: <ref n="Thuc. 1. 116">Thuc. 1. 116</ref>§ 3 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐσαγγελθέντων</quote>: <ref n="Xen. Cyrop. 1. 4. 18">Xen. <title>Cyr.</title> 1. 4. 18</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">σημανθέντων</quote>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡς_ἔχει_1344">, but, as things stand now, </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὰ_καλῶς_1344"> (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἔχει</quote>), all the conditions on their part (that of Clyt. and Aeg.) are good (for us), </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_καλῶς_1344"> (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἔχοντα</quote>), even those which are not morally good,—viz., Clytaemnestra's joy at the death of her son, and those insults which expressed her new sense of security (773—803). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐχὶ_ξυνίης_1347">; The <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀντιλαβή</quote> marks the interest of the moment: <ref n="Soph. El. 1209">cp. 1209</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐδέ_φέρω_1347">: lit., ‘No, I cannot even bring (a conjecture) into my mind’; ‘I cannot form an idea.’ The phrase occurs nowhere else, and is not really like <title>O.T.</title> 975, <quote xml:lang="grc">μή νυν ἔτ’ αὐτῶν μηδὲν ἐς θυμὸν βάλῃς</quote> (‘lay to heart’). As </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐδέ_1347"> shows, the Greek would not be adequately rendered by our phrase, ‘bring to mind’ (= ‘recall to memory’). When we say that an impression is ‘borne in upon’ the mind, the analogy is nearer. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ποίῳ_1349"> is conformed to </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅτῳ_1349"> in 1348, since the acc. for </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἶσθα_1349"> is suppressed; the practice being that, in a curt question such as this, <quote xml:lang="grc">ποῖος</quote> takes the case of the word to which it refers (<foreign xml:lang="lat">e.g.</foreign>, <ref n="Soph. OT 1176"> <title>O. T.</title> 1176</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ποίων</quote> <emph>; </emph> <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1322"> Ai. 1322</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ποίους</quote>; ). It implies that, for the moment, she fails not only to recognise the man but to recall the occasion: cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 1129"> <title>O. T.</title> 1129</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ποῖον ἄνδρα καὶ λέγεις</quote>;</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὗ_χεροῖν_1349">: the long space between the words is noteworthy; cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 598"> <title>Ph.</title> 598</ref> f. (<quote xml:lang="grc">τίνος..χάριν</quote>), n.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πέδον_1349">: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 643"> <title>O. C.</title> 643</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">δόμους στείχειν</quote> (n.).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὑπεξεπέμφθην_1349">: <ref n="Soph. El. 297">cp. 297</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑπεξέθου</quote> (n.).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σῇ_προμηθίᾳ_1349">: <ref n="Soph. El. 12">cp. 12</ref>, 1132 f. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προσηῦρον_πιστὸν_1352">, ‘<emph>found</emph> a true <emph>ally</emph>’: <quote xml:lang="grc">πρός</quote> denoting the acquisition. Cp. Polyb. 1. 59. 6 <quote xml:lang="grc">προσηυρέθη ἡ πρὸς τὴν συντέλειαν</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">χορηγία</quote>), ‘the funds for the completion of the enterprise were made up.’ Classical prose preferred <quote xml:lang="grc">προσεξευρίσκω</quote> (Isocr.), or <quote xml:lang="grc">προσεπεξευρίσκω</quote> (Thuc. ). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φῶς_1354">, day, as in 1224. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τόνδε_κἄμ_1356">: he saved Orestes from murder, and Electra from that bereavement.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔσωσας_1356">, not <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔσωσεν</quote>, in spite of </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κεῖνος_1356">. Cp. <ref n="Eur. Heraclid. 945">Eur. <title>Heracl.</title> 945</ref> ff. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκεῖνος εἶ σύ</quote>, followed by <quote xml:lang="grc">ὃς..ἠξίωσας</quote>. So, when a speaker has referred to himself in the 3rd person, he quickly reverts to the first, as in <ref n="Soph. OC 6">Soph. <title>O. C.</title> 6</ref>(n.) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμοί</quote> follows <quote xml:lang="grc">Οἰδίπουν</quote> in v. 3. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὦ_χεῖρες_1357">: she takes his hands in her own. This explains why the poet has not written <quote xml:lang="grc">φιλτάτας..χεῖρας</quote>: we see, too, how natural is the transition to <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔχων</quote>, as she is looking in her old friend's face. The sense is, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔχων πόδας οἳ τὰ ἥδιστα ὑπηρέτησαν</quote>, viz., in the journeys to and from Phocis. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐδ_ἔφαινες_1359">, ‘and didst not give any light.’ This absolute use of the word is sufficiently interpreted by the context; it is scarcely needful to supply (<foreign xml:lang="lat">e.g.</foreign>) <quote xml:lang="grc">τὴν ἀλήθειαν</quote>, or <quote xml:lang="grc">συνόντα σεαυτόν.—φαίνω</quote> is never really intrans. (= ‘to appear’) except in the epic aor. <quote xml:lang="grc">φάνεσκον</quote> ( <ref n="Hom. Il. 11.64"> <title>Il.</title> 11. 64</ref>, <ref n="Hom. Od. 12.241"> <title>Od.</title> 12. 241</ref>, etc.).—For </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλά_με_1359"> cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 361."> <title>Ai.</title> 361.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔργ_ἔχων_1360">, ‘possessed of them,’ ‘knowing them’: <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔργα</quote> being ‘facts’ as opp. to <quote xml:lang="grc">λόγοι</quote> (59 f. <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅταν λόγῳ θανὼν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔργοισι σωθῶ</quote>). Cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 9"> <title>Ant.</title> 9</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔχεις τι κεἰσήκουσας</quote>; —Others explain, ‘while engaged in a course of action’ (cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 300"> <title>Ant.</title> 300</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πανουργίας...ἔχειν</quote>, n.).—For </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐμοί_1360">, after </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="με_1360">, cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 811"> <title>O. C.</title> 811</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μηδέ με</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">φύλασσ’ ἐφορμῶν ἔνθα χρὴ ναίειν ἐμέ</quote> (n.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πατέρα_1361">. This is the only tragic trimeter in which the third foot is formed by a single word of three short syllables. When the third foot is a tribrach there is usu. a caesura both in the third and in the fourth foot (as <ref n="Soph. OT 248"> <title>O. T.</title> 248</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κακὸν κακῶς νιν ἄμορον ἐκτρῖψαι βίον</quote>: cp. <ref n="Eur. Tro. 497">Eur. <title>Tro.</title> 497</ref>): or at least in the third foot (as <ref n="Soph. Ant. 31">Soph. <title>Ant.</title> 31</ref>). But it should be observed that the pause after </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χαῖῤ_πάτερ_1361"> makes a vital difference. The movement of the verse begins afresh at </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πατέρα_1361_2">, and the effect of that word to the ear is like that of a tribrach in the first, rather than in the third, place of a trimeter. Hence we may defend the text here, and yet concede that no tragic poet could have written such a verse without the pause (<foreign xml:lang="lat">e.g.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἦλθ’ ἄσμενος πατέρα ποτ’ εἰσορᾶν δοκῶν</quote>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοὺς_λόγους_1364">, the story of the brother's and sister's experiences in the interval since Orestes left Mycenae. Cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 583"> <title>O. C.</title> 583</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ..ἐν</quote> <quote xml:lang="grc">μέσῳ</quote> (the needs of the interval, until death): <ref n="Eur. Med. 819">Eur. <title>Med.</title> 819</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">περισσοὶ πάντες οὑν μέσῳ λόγοι</quote> (between the present moment, and her deed): <ref n="Eur. Orest. 16">Eur. <title>Or.</title> 16</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰς γὰρ ἐν μέσῳ σιγῶ τύχας</quote> (the fortunes of the house in the interval).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοὺς_λόγους_1364_2"> is resumed in 1366 by </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ταῦτα_1364">, because the sentence <quote xml:lang="grc">πολλαὶ κ.τ.λ.</quote> has intervened. Cp. <ref n="Thuc. 2. 62">Thuc. 2. 62</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν δὲ πόνον...ἀρκείτω μὲν ὑμῖν καὶ ἐκεῖνα ἐν οἷς ἄλλοτε..ἀπέδειξα οὐκ ὀρθῶς αὐτὸν ὑποπτευόμενον</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 287">Soph. <title>Tr.</title> 287</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτὸν δ’ ἐκεῖνον</quote>, resumed in 289 by <quote xml:lang="grc">νιν</quote>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κυκλοῦνται_1364">, the form originally written by the scribe of L, is confirmed by usage, though the other reading, </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κυκλοῦσι_1364">, displaced it in most MSS. See on <ref n="Soph. Trach. 129"> <title>Tr.</title> 129</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κυκλοῦσιν</quote>, the only instance of <quote xml:lang="grc">κυκλεῖν</quote> used intransitively which occurs in Greek before Aristotle. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σφῷν_γε_1367">: ‘And further’ (</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γε_1367">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> besides counselling Electra), ‘I tell <emph>you</emph>,’ etc.: cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1150"> <title>Ai.</title> 1150</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐγὼ δέ γ’ ἄνδρ’ ὄπωπα κ.τ.λ.</quote>: <ref n="Eur. Orest. 1236">Eur. <title>Or.</title> 1236</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐγὼ δέ γ’ ἐπεκέλευσα</quote>. (This is better than to take <quote xml:lang="grc">γε</quote> as emphasising <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐννέπω</quote> only; ‘I <emph>warn</emph> you,’—whether you will heed me or not.) Hermann's conjecture, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐννέπω <emph>)γὼ</emph> </quote>, is no improvement. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τούτοις_1370"> refers to <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνδρῶν</quote> in 1369, the male domestics, who are supposed to be now busied out of doors. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄλλοισι_1370"> are the body-guards, <quote xml:lang="grc">δορυφόροι</quote>, of Aegisthus, who may be expected to return ere long from the country (313); </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σοφωτέροις_1370"> (<quote xml:lang="grc">μάχεσθαι</quote>), as being trained to arms. Since the two comparatives, <quote xml:lang="grc">σοφωτέροις</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">πλείοσιν</quote>, are not linked by a conjunction, </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τούτων_1370"> cannot be construed with both; it is perhaps best taken with <quote xml:lang="grc">πλείοσιν</quote>. ‘Ye will have to fight both with these men (the <quote xml:lang="grc">οἰκέται</quote>), and with others of greater skill, more numerous than these.’</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τούτοις_1370_2"> in 1370 means Clytaemnestra and her women-servants: this seems less natural. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐδὲν_1372"> is adv.: lit., ‘this task would no longer seem to be in any way (<quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδὲν</quote>) a case for many words, but for entering,’ etc. The two constructions of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔργον ἐστίν</quote>, as=<emph>opus est</emph>, are here combined. (1) For the gen., cp. <title>Plut.</title> 1154 <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ ἔργον ἔστ’ οὐδὲν στροφῶν</quote>: (2) for the inf., <ref n="Soph. Aj. 11"> <title>Ai.</title> 11</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">καί σ’ οὐδὲν εἵσω τῆσδε παπταίνειν πύλης</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔτ’ ἔργον ἐστίν</quote>. The peculiarity here is that, instead of the simple <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔργον</quote>, we have <quote xml:lang="grc">τόδε τοὔργον</quote>, from which <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔργον</quote> has to be supplied with the gen. and with the inf. Cp. <ref n="Eur. Andr. 551">Eur. <title>Andr.</title> 551</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ γάρ, ὡς ἔοικέ μοι</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">σχολῆς τόδ’ ἔργον</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πατρῷα_1374">, instead of <quote xml:lang="grc">πατρῴων</quote>: <ref n="Soph. El. 1290">cp. 1290</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἕδη_1374">, images of the gods ( <ref n="Soph. OT 886"> <title>O. T.</title> 886</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">δαιμόνων ἕδη</quote>, n.), placed in the </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρόπυλα_1374">, here a statelier term for <quote xml:lang="grc">πρόθυρον</quote>, the porch or vestibule of the house. Cp. <title>Vesp.</title> 875 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ δέσποτ’ ἄναξ, γεῖτον Ἀγυιεῦ τοὐμοῦ προθύρου προπύλαιε</quote>. The gods of the entrance were esp. Apollo Agyieus (<ref n="Soph. El. 637">cp. 637</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">προστατήριε</quote>), and Hermes. It is these who, with Zeus, are invoked by the returning herald in <ref n="Aesch. Ag. 509">Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 509</ref>—515: cp. <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 519 <quote xml:lang="grc">σεμνοί τε θᾶκοι δαίμονές τ’ ἀντήλιοι</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προσκύσαντε_1374">: the worshipper stretched forth his right arm towards the image, presenting to it the flat of his hand, by bending back the wrist; then kissed his hand, and wafted a salute to the god. Orestes and Pylades perform this act of reverence before each <quote xml:lang="grc">ἕδος</quote> in the vestibule. Meanwhile, turning towards the statue of Apollo Lykeios which stands in front of the palace (645), Electra makes her prayer. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀφ_ὧν_1378">: for <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπό</quote>, denoting one's resources, <ref n="Soph. El. 1127">cp. 1127</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔχοιμι_1378">: optat. of indefinite frequency in past time; cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 289"> <title>Ph.</title> 289</ref> n.: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 905"> <title>Tr.</title> 905</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κλαῖε δ’ ὀργάνων ὅτου</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ψαύσειεν</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λιπαρεῖ_1378">, earnest, devout: <ref n="Soph. El. 451">cp. 451</ref> n.— <quote xml:lang="grc"> <emph>προὔστην</emph> σε</quote>, presented myself (as a suppliant) at thy shrine. Similarly <quote xml:lang="grc">προστάτης</quote> is ‘one who presents himself before a god,’ ‘a suppliant,’ <ref n="Soph. OC 1171"> <title>O. C.</title> 1171</ref>, <ref n="Soph. OC 1278."> O. C. 1278.</ref>The only other trace of <quote xml:lang="grc">προστῆναι</quote> as = ‘to approach,’ with an acc., is in a fragment from the <quote xml:lang="grc">Τυρώ</quote> of Sophocles (fr. 599, Nauck 2nd ed.). Athenaeus, in illustrating the word <quote xml:lang="grc">καρχήσιον</quote>, quotes it as follows (p. 475 A): <quote xml:lang="grc">Σοφοκλῆς δὲ Τυροῖ</quote>: <quote xml:lang="grc">προστῆναι μέσην</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">τράπεζαν ἀμφὶ σίτια</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">σῖτα</quote> Macrobius) <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ καρχήσια</quote>: adding, <quote xml:lang="grc">πρὸς τὴν τράπεζαν φάσκων προσεληλυθέναι τοὺς δράκοντας κ.τ.λ.</quote> The meaning was, then, that the serpents ‘approached the table’; a parallel for the use here. Schweighäuser, indeed, conjectured <quote xml:lang="grc">προσστῆναι</quote> (cp. <ref n="Aesch. Pers. 203">Aesch. <title>Pers.</title> 203</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">βωμὸν προσέστην</quote>), and Bergk, less well, <quote xml:lang="grc">προσπτῆναι</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πέστην_1378">: but <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπέστην σε</quote> as = ‘approached thee’ would be unexampled (see on <ref n="Soph. Trach. 339"> <title>Tr.</title> 339</ref>). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐξ_ἔχω_1379"> | </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αἰτῶ_1379">: lit., ‘I make the prayer with such means as I have’; <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, no longer with offerings— since none are at hand—but with heartfelt vows. Schol. <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς δυνάμεως ἔχω, λόγοις ἀξιοῦν, οὐ θύειν</quote>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προπίπτω_1379">, as metre requires, not </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προπίτνω_1379">. The first syllable of <quote xml:lang="grc">πίτνω</quote> is never long. Thus, where </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ι_1379"> is needed, <ref n="Aesch. Pers. 588">Aesch. <title>Pers.</title> 588</ref> has <quote xml:lang="grc">προπίτνοντες</quote>, and <ref n="Soph. OC 1754">Soph. <title>O. C.</title> 1754</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">προσπίτνομεν</quote>: but, requiring </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ι_1379_2">, <title>Suppl.</title> 63 writes <quote xml:lang="grc">προπίπτουσα</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τἀπιτίμια_1382">. <ref n="Eur. Hec. 1086">Eur. <title>Hec.</title> 1086</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">δράσαντι δ’ αἰσχρὰ δεινὰ τἀπιτίμια</quote>. Josephus <title>Bell. Iud.</title> 1. 30 § 6 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπέχω τῆς ἀσεβείας τὸ ἐπιτίμιον</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἴδεθ_1384">, like <ref n="Soph. Trach. 821"> <title>Tr.</title> 821</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἴδ’ οἷον, ὦ παῖδες κ.τ.λ.</quote> It seems necessary to alter </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅπου_1384">, the reading of L (cr. n.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προνέμεται_1384"> expresses a gradual and regular advance. <quote xml:lang="grc">προνέμεσθαι</quote> is lit. ‘to go forward in grazing.’ The midd. occurs only here; nor is the act. found in a strictly parallel sense; in <ref n="Aesch. Eum. 313">Aesch. <title>Eum.</title> 313</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">καθαρὰς χεῖρας προνέμοντ’</quote>=‘putting forward.’ But we have the analogy of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπινέμειν</quote>, as meaning to encroach on a neighbour's pastures; <ref n="Aristot. Pol. 5.1305a">Arist. <title>Pol.</title> 5. 5. 9</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τῶν εὐπόρων τὰ κτήνη ἀποσφάξας, λαβὼν</quote> (<foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοὺς εὐπόρους</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">παρὰ τὸν ποταμὸν ἐπινέμοντας</quote>. In <ref n="Aesch. Ag. 485">Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 485</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πιθανὸς ἄγαν ὁ θῆλυς ὅρος ἐπινέμεται</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ταχύπορος</quote>, some make <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπινέμεται</quote> pass., ‘is encroached upon’; but <quote xml:lang="grc">ταχύπορος</quote> is in favour of its being midd., like <quote xml:lang="grc">προνέμεται</quote> here: ‘the limit of a woman's belief (too lightly won) quickly oversteps the border’ (between fact and fiction).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸ_αἷμα_1385">, bloodshed, deadly vengeance, against which the guilty will strive in vain. <quote xml:lang="grc">δυσέριστον</quote>= <quote xml:lang="grc">δύσμαχον</quote>: <ref n="Soph. El. 220">cp. 220</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ ἐριστά</quote>. Not, as the schol. took it, ‘bloodshed caused by unholy strife’ (between mother and children).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φυσῶν_1385">: cp. <title>I. A.</title> 125 <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ μέγα φυσῶν θυμὸν ἐπαρεῖ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">σοὶ</quote>..<emph>; I. T.</emph> 288 (the Erinys) <quote xml:lang="grc">πῦρ πνέουσα καὶ φόνον</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δωμάτων_ὑπόστεγοι_1386">: cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 796"> <title>Ai.</title> 796</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">σκηνῆς ὕπαυλον</quote>: <ref n="Aesch. Eum. 669">Aesch. <title>Eum.</title> 669</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">σῶν δόμων ἐφέστιον</quote>: <title>I. T.</title> 80 <quote xml:lang="grc"><*>ξεδροι χθονός</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μετάδρομοι_κύνες_1387">, the Erinyes. Cp. <ref n="Aesch. Lib. 1054">Aesch. <title>Ch.</title> 1054</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">σαφῶς γὰρ αἵδε μητρὸς ἔγκοτοι κύνες</quote>. <ref n="Aesch. Eum. 246">Aesch. <title>Eum.</title> 246</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς κύων νεβρὸν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">πρὸς αἷμα καὶ σταλαγμὸν ἐκμαστεύομεν</quote>. <ref n="Aristoph. Frogs 472">Ar. <title>Ran.</title> 472</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">Κωκυτοῦ τε περίδρομοι κύνες</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀμμενεῖ_1389"> was in the schol.'s text (see cr. n.), and is clearly better than <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀμμένει</quote>. For the apocopè of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνά</quote> in comp., cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1275."> <title>Ant.</title> 1275.</ref> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοὐμὸν_ὄνειρον_1390">: <ref n="Soph. El. 492">cp. 492</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αἰωρούμενον_1390">. Cp. <ref n="Hdt. 8. 100">Her. 8. 100</ref>(of Mardonius) <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑπὲρ μεγάλων αἰωρηθέντα</quote>, ‘in suspense concerning great issues’ (victory or death). <ref n="Thuc. 7. 77">Thuc. 7. 77</ref>(Nicias) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν τῷ αὐτῷ κινδύνῳ τοῖς φαυλοτάτοις αἰωροῦμαι</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐνέρων_ἀρωγός_1391">. Orestes is the champion, not only of his father's spirit, but also of Hades, who is ‘not regardless’ (184), and of the other powers invoked by Electra from the nether world (110 ff.). Cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 126"> <title>O. T.</title> 126</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">Λαΐου δ’ ὀλωλότος</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδεὶς ἀρωγὸς ἐν κακοῖς ἐγίγνετο</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀρχαιόπλουτα_1393">. He is to eject the usurper, and to recover his inheritance (<ref n="Soph. El. 72">cp. 72</ref>: 162: 1290). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νεακόνητον_ἔχων_1394">. The words, if sound, mean, ‘bearing keen-edged death in his hands.’ <quote xml:lang="grc">αἷμα</quote> is the deed of blood by which vengeance is to be taken. <quote xml:lang="grc">νεακόνητον</quote> refers primarily to the keen edge of the weapon (sword or dagger) with which the blow is to be dealt; but may suggest also the keen edge of the avengers' resolve ( <ref n="Aesch. Seven 715">Aesch. <title>Th.</title> 715</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τεθηγμένον τοί μ’ οὐκ <*>παμβλυνεῖς λόγῳ</quote>).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αἷμα_1394"> may be illustrated by <ref n="Aesch. Lib. 932">Aesch. <title>Ch.</title> 932</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πολλῶν αἱμάτων</quote>, ‘many deeds of blood’; <ref n="Eur. Orest. 284">Eur. <title>Or.</title> 284</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἴργασται δ’ ἐμοὶ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">μητρῷον αἷμα</quote>, ‘the murder of a mother.’ Eur. Itshould be remembered that Orestes does <emph>not</emph> enter the house <emph>with a weapon displayed in his hands.</emph> The sword or dagger is concealed. We may be sure, then, that, whether <quote xml:lang="grc">αἷμα</quote> is genuine or not, at least Sophocles did not use any word (such as <quote xml:lang="grc">μάχαιραν</quote>) denoting a <emph>weapon</emph>; since then the words </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χειροῖν_ἔχων_1394"> would necessarily have had their literal sense, ‘holding in the hands’; they could no longer have meant, as the context requires them to mean, simply, ‘charged with,’ ‘bearing with him.’ This fact—that some <emph>abstract</emph> word is required—is, to my mind, the strongest argument in favour of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αἷμα_1394_2">.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νεάκονητον_1394"> is right, the </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="α_1394"> must be short. The verse corresponds with 1387 <quote xml:lang="grc">με^τ <01> α^δρο^μο—ι— κα^κ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ω—ν πα^ν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ου—ργη—μα^τ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ω—ν Λ</quote> ||, a dochmiac dimeter. Now, analogy would suggest that <quote xml:lang="grc">νεακόνητον</quote> is Doric for <quote xml:lang="grc">νεηκόνητον</quote>. Cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">νεηκονής</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. Aj. 820"> <title>Ai.</title> 820</ref>): <quote xml:lang="grc">νεηκής</quote> (Hom. ): <quote xml:lang="grc">νεηλιφής</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">νέος, ἀλείφω</quote>, Arist. ). Even when the second part of the compound begins with a consonant, we find such forms as <quote xml:lang="grc">νεα—γενής</quote> ([Eur. ] <title>I. A.</title> 1623), <quote xml:lang="grc">νεηθαλής, νεήτομος, νεήφατος</quote>. Yet at least two exceptions occur. (1) <quote xml:lang="grc">νεα^λής</quote> in Nicander <title>Alexiph.</title> 358 and 364; although the <quote xml:lang="grc">α</quote> is normally long. (2) <title>Anthol. Pal.</title> 7. 13 <quote xml:lang="grc">παρθενικὴν νεάοιδον</quote>: in an epigram ascribed to Leonidas of Tarentum, <emph>c.</emph> 280 B.C. It seems possible, then, that Sophocles should have written <quote xml:lang="grc">νεα^κόνητον</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὁ_παῖς_1395">. Hermes <quote xml:lang="grc">χθόνιος</quote>, whom Electra had already invoked (111), acts here in his twofold quality as <quote xml:lang="grc">πομπαῖος</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">δόλιος</quote>. Cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 133"> <title>Ph.</title> 133</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἑρμῆς δ’ ὁ πέμπων δόλιος ἡγήσαιτο νῷν</quote>. A like part is assigned to him in the <title>Choephori</title>, 727 <quote xml:lang="grc">χθόνιον δ’ Ἑρμῆν</quote> | .. <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῖσδ’ ἐφοδεῦσαι</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ξιφοδηλήτοισιν ἀγῶσιν</quote>: <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 812 <quote xml:lang="grc">ξυλλάβοι δ’ ἐνδίκως</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">παῖς ὁ Μαίας ἐπιφορώτατος</quote> (like a favouring wind) | <quote xml:lang="grc">πρᾶξιν οὐρίαν θέλων</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Ἑρμῆς_ἄγει_1395"> is the most probable reading. The erasure in L, and the variants in later MSS., have prompted conjectures. Thus Neue, whom Nauck and Wecklein follow, writes <quote xml:lang="grc"> <emph>ἐπί</emph> σφ’ ἄγει</quote>: G. Wolff suggests <quote xml:lang="grc">ἑξῆς σφ’ ἄγει</quote>: Kvíčala, <quote xml:lang="grc">σφ’ ἐπεξάγει</quote>. But it is rash to assume that <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἑρμῆς</quote> is a gloss: cp. [Eur. ] <title>Rhes.</title> 216 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ εὖ σ’ ὁ Μαίας παῖς ἐκεῖσε καὶ πάλιν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">πέμψειεν Ἑρμῆς, ὅς γε φηλητῶν ἄναξ</quote>. If the </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ς_1395"> of </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σφ_1395"> had been lost after <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἑρμῆς, φ)ἄγει</quote> might have led to <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπάγει</quote>, and this to mistaken remedies, such as <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπεισάγει</quote> (cr. n.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τελοῦσι_1399">, fut. This ‘Attic’ fut. of <quote xml:lang="grc">τελῶ</quote> is frequent; <foreign xml:lang="lat">e.g.</foreign> 1435: <ref n="Soph. OT 232"> <title>O. T.</title> 232</ref>: <ref n="Soph. OC 630"> <title>O. C.</title> 630</ref>, <ref n="Soph. OC 881"> O. C. 881</ref>, <ref n="Soph. OC 1634"> O. C. 1634</ref>, etc. But <quote xml:lang="grc">τελέσω</quote> has also good authority: <ref n="Dem. 21.66">Dem. or. 21 § 66</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">διατελέσω</quote>: <ref n="Plat. Rep. 425E">Plat. <title>Rep.</title> 425E</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">διατελέσουσιν</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τάφον_1400"> (<ref n="Soph. El. 1140">cp. 1140</ref> n.). Nothing would be gained by reading <quote xml:lang="grc">ταφὴν</quote> (1210).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λέβητα_1400">, the urn: cp. <ref n="Aesch. Ag. 444">Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 444</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">σποδοῦ γεμίζων λέβητας εὐθέτου;</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κοσμεῖ_1400">. In <ref n="Hom. Il. 23.253"> <title>Il.</title> 23. 253</ref> f. the golden urn containing the bones of Patroclus is ‘covered with a linen veil.’ In <ref n="Hom. Il. 24.796"> <title>Il.</title> 24. 796</ref> the urn with Hector's ashes is ‘shrouded in soft purple robes.’ Leaf observes there: ‘In Etruscan graves very fine linen covers are found spread on the stools on which stand the urns containing the ashes.’ Some such ‘dressing’ is denoted by <quote xml:lang="grc">κοσμεῖ</quote> here; but wreaths of flowers may also be meant. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αἰαῖ_στέγαι_1404">. Dindorf (<title>Metra</title> p. 106) reads these words as a dochmiac (<quote xml:lang="grc">˘˘˘¯˘¯</quote>): Nauck and Wecklein take them as an iambic tripody (<quote xml:lang="grc">¯¯˘¯˘¯</quote>). The latter view seems correct. The hiatus after <quote xml:lang="grc">αἰαῖ</quote> is excused by the pause. Cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1276"> <title>Ant.</title> 1276</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">φεῦ φεῦ, ὦ πόνοι</quote>. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1106"> <title>Ph.</title> 1106</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">αἰαῖ αἰαῖ</quote> (=1086 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὤμοι μοί μοι</quote>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀνήκουστα_1407">, lit., ‘not to be heard’: then, ‘dreadful to hear’; cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄρρητα</quote>. So <ref n="Eur. Hipp. 362">Eur. <title>Hipp.</title> 362</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔκλυες, <*></quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνήκουστα τᾶς</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">τυράννου πάθη μέλεα θρεομένας</quote>. <ref n="Antiph. 1.22">Antiphon or. 1 § 22</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">δεήσεται ἀθέμιτα καὶ ἀτέλεστα καὶ ἀνήκουστα καὶ θεοις καὶ ὑμῖν</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἰδού_1410">, referring to <emph>sound</emph> (as we could say, ‘there!’): so <ref n="Soph. Aj. 870"> <title>Ai.</title> 870</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἰδού</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">δοῦπον αὖ κλύω τινά.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μάλ_αὖ_1410">: <ref n="Soph. OC 1477"> <title>O. C.</title> 1477</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔα, <ref n="Soph. OC i"> ο. ξ. ι</ref>)δοὺ μάλ’ αὖθις ἀμφίσταται</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">διαπρύσιος ὄτοβος</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκ_σέθεν_1411">: for <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκ</quote>, cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 51"> <title>O. C.</title> 51</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κοὐκ ἄτιμος ἔκ γ’ ἐμοῦ φανεῖ.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὁ_πατήρ_1411">: <ref n="Soph. El. 261">cp. 261</ref>: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 311"> <title>Tr.</title> 311</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ φιτύσας πατήρ</quote>: <ref n="Soph. OT 793"> <title>O. T.</title> 793</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦ φυτεύσαντος πατρός</quote>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φθίνει_φθίνει_1411"> is vindicated, and all difficulty is removed, by the slight change of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σε_1411"> to </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σοι_1411">. The </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μοῖρα_καθαμερία_1411"> is the fate which has afflicted the house day by day. In the only other place where <quote xml:lang="grc">καθαμέριος</quote> occurs ( <ref n="Soph. Phil. 229">Soph. <title>Ph.</title> 229</ref>), it has this sense, ‘daily’; nor is any other, indeed, tenable. (<ref n="Soph. El. 259">Cp. 259</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κατ’ ἦμαρ</quote>.) This fate is now being extinguished (</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φθίνει_1411">) by the righteous act of vengeance, which, according to the poet's view in this play, closes the misfortunes of the race (<ref n="Soph. El. 1510">cp. 1510</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τῇ νῦν ὁρμῇ τελεωθέν</quote>). For <quote xml:lang="grc">φθίνειν</quote> said of an <emph>evil</emph> which wanes or dies out, cp. fr. 718 (<quote xml:lang="grc">ὕβρις</quote>)..<quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνθεῖ τε καὶ φθίνει πάλιν</quote>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σε_1411_2"> and </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φθίνει_φθίνει_1411_2">: ‘fate is <emph>destroying</emph> thee <emph>this day.</emph>’ But the alleged evidence for a transitive use of <quote xml:lang="grc">φθίνειν</quote> is very small and doubtful: see Appendix. (2) Keeping </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σε_1411_3">, but reading </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φθίνειν_φθίνειν_1411">: ‘now it is the doom <emph>of this day</emph> that thou shouldst fade.’ Both these versions force an impossible sense on <quote xml:lang="grc">καθαμερία</quote>. That explanation of it was a mere makeshift of the scholiast; <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ γενεὰ τοῦ οἴκου τούτου, κατὰ ταύτην σε τὴν ἡμέραν ἡ Μοῖρα εἰς φθορὰν καὶ ἐλάττωσιν τοῦ γένους ἄγει</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="διπλῆν_1411">: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 544"> <title>O. C.</title> 544</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">δευτέραν ἔπαισας</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1307"> <title>Ant.</title> 1307</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τί μ’ οὐκ ἀνταίαν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔπαισέν τις</quote>..;</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰ_ὁμοῦ_1411"> (adv.). The dat., suggested by <quote xml:lang="grc">ὤμοι</quote>, depends on the notion which that exclamation implies; as if (<foreign xml:lang="lat">e.g.</foreign>) <quote xml:lang="grc">κακὸν ἥκει μοι</quote> were followed by <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ γὰρ Αἰγίσθῳ γ’ ὁμοῦ ἧκεν</quote>. This is simpler than to supply (<emph>a</emph>) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπλήγης</quote> or (<emph>b</emph>) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐβόας</quote>, taking <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁμοῦ</quote> as a prep.—The reading <quote xml:lang="grc">Αἰγίσθῳ θ’ ὁμοῦ</quote>, though not impossible, is very awkward. The sense would be: ‘Oh that thou wert crying,— ‘and woe to Aegisthus also’!’ </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τελοῦς_ἀραί_1417">, ‘are doing their work.’ <quote xml:lang="grc">τελοῦσι</quote> is transitive, though the object (<quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ ἔργον</quote>) is not expressed. Cp. <ref n="Soph. Trach. 825"> <title>Tr.</title> 825</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἱ_κείμενοι_1417">, Agamemnon: for the plur. (like <quote xml:lang="grc">οἱ..θανόντες</quote>, 1421), <ref n="Soph. El. 146">cp. 146</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">γονέων</quote>, n.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὑπαὶ_1417">: 711 n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παλίρρυτον_1419">: <ref n="Soph. El. 246">cp. 246</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πάλιν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">δώσουσ’ ἀντιφόνους δίκας</quote>: <ref n="Hom. Od. 1.379"> <title>Od.</title> 1. 379</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">παλίντιτα ἔργα</quote>, deeds of retribution. In <cit> <bibl n="Eur. El. 1155">Eur. <title>El.</title> 1155</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">παλίρρους … δίκα</quote> </cit> is ‘the turning tide of justice,’ which comes upon the guilty.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὑπεξαιροῦσι_1419">: cp. <ref n="Eur. Hipp. 633">Eur. <title>Hipp.</title> 633</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄλβον δωμάτων ὑπεξελών</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_μὴν_1422">: 78 n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="στάζει_θυηλῆς_1422">: the gen. depends on the notion of fulness; cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 16"> <title>O. C.</title> 16</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">βρύων</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">δάφνης</quote>: fr. 264 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀραχνᾶν βρίθει</quote>. (There is no other example of <quote xml:lang="grc">στάζω</quote> in this constr., for in fr. 491, <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀπὸν..στάζοντα τομῆς</quote>, the sense is, ‘sap dripping <emph>from</emph> the cleft wood.’)— The <quote xml:lang="grc">θυηλή</quote> is <quote xml:lang="grc">φόνος</quote>. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Shak. 1H4 4.1">Shakesp. <title>H. IV.</title>, pt. 1, act 4, sc. 1, 113,</bibl> <quote xml:lang="eng">‘They come like sacrifices in their trim, | And to the fire-eyed maid of smoky war | All hot and bleeding will we offer them.’</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
‘They come like sacrifices in their trim, |
                                    And to the fire-eyed maid of smoky war | All hot and bleeding
                                    will we offer them.’

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ψέγειν_1422">, Erfurdt's correction of </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λέγειν_1422">, deserves the favour which it has found with almost all editors from Hermann onwards. If <quote xml:lang="grc">λέγειν</quote> be sound, the sense must be, ‘utterance fails me,’—at a moment so terrible. But <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδ’ ἔχω λέγειν</quote> is not equivalent to <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδ’ ἔχω φωνεῖν</quote>, nor, again, to <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδ’ ἔχω τί λέγω</quote> (or <quote xml:lang="grc">τί φῶ</quote>). It ought to mean rather, ‘nor can I <emph>describe</emph>’ (what I see): which would be strange here. Hermann suggested that <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδ’ ἔχω λέγειν</quote> might be an unfinished sentence,—‘but I cannot tell..’ (whether the avengers have accomplished their deed). This, however, as he allows, would be frigid. So also would <quote xml:lang="grc">ψέγειν</quote> be, if explained in his way (‘I cannot complain that they have not well dyed their swords’). Taken, however, in its natural sense, ‘nor can I blame the deed,’ <quote xml:lang="grc">ψέγειν</quote> is forcible enough. In <ref n="Aesch. Lib. 989">Aesch. <title>Ch.</title> 989</ref> the converse corruption took place, <quote xml:lang="grc">λέγω</quote> becoming <quote xml:lang="grc">ψέγω</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κυρεῖτε_1424"> is a certain correction of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κυρεῖ_1424">. With Hermann's conjecture, </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κυρεῖ_δὲ_1424">, the sense would be, ‘now, how goes it?’—<quote xml:lang="grc">δέ</quote> giving animation to the question. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐθέσπισεν_1425">: <ref n="Soph. El. 36">cp. 36</ref> f. Contrast the calm confidence of Orestes with his words of anguish in <ref n="Aesch. Lib. 1016">Aesch. <title>Ch.</title> 1016</ref> f., <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλγῶ μὲν ἔργα καὶ πάθος γένος τε πᾶν</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc"><*>ζηλα νίκης τῆσδ’ ἔχων μιάσματα</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκφοβοῦ_ὡς_1426">: 1309 n. The sense of </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λῆμα_1426"> is not necessarily either good or bad; thus <ref n="Pind. P. 8">Pind. <title>P.</title> 8. 44</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ γενναῖον</quote>.. | ..<quote xml:lang="grc">λῆμα</quote>: <ref n="Soph. OC 960">Soph. <title>O. C.</title> 960</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">λῆμ’ ἀναιδές</quote>: though it generally implies at least a <emph>strong</emph> spirit. Cp. however <ref n="Pind. P. 3">Pind. <title>P.</title> 3. 24</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔσχε τοιαύταν μεγάλαν ἀvάταν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">καλλιπέπλου λῆμα Κορωνίδος</quote>, where, as here, <quote xml:lang="grc">λῆμα</quote> implies blame, though it has no epithet (Gildersleeve happily renders, ‘wilful Coronis’).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παύσασθε_1426"> in 1428 is sufficiently justified by the brief dialogue between Orestes and Electra which has already taken place (1424—1427). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκ_προδήλου_1429">, like <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκ τοῦ ἐμφανέος</quote> ( <ref n="Hdt. 4. 120">Her. 4. 120</ref>), <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκ τοῦ προφανοῦς</quote> ( <ref n="Thuc. 3. 43">Thuc. 3. 43</ref>§ 3), <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκ τοῦ φανεροῦ</quote>, etc.: <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκ</quote> being used as above in 455, <ref n="Thuc. 725.">Thuc. 725.</ref>In this adverbial phrase the adj. is merely a stronger <quote xml:lang="grc">δῆλος</quote>, as in <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1311">Soph. <title>Ai.</title> 1311</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">θανεῖν προδήλως</quote> (‘before all eyes’). Its other sense was ‘clear <emph>beforehand</emph>’ ( <ref n="Dem. 18.196">Dem. or. 18 § 196</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ..ἦν σοι πρόδηλα τὰ μέλλοντα</quote>).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὦ_παῖδες_1430">: <ref n="Soph. El. 1220">cp. 1220</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ παῖ</quote>, n.— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐκ_ἄψορρον_1430">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἴσιτε</quote>, into the house. Cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 369"> <title>Ai.</title> 369</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ ἐκτὸς ἄψο<*>ρον <*>κνεμεῖ πόδα</quote> <emph>; </emph> <ref n="Soph. OT 430"> O. T. 430</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐφ_ἡμῖν_1431">, ‘in our power’; cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 66"> <title>O. C.</title> 66</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἢ )πὶ τῷ πλήθει λόγος</quote> <emph>; </emph> <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1003">Ph. 1003</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μὴ )πὶ τῷδ’ ἔστω τάδε</quote>. <ref n="Xen. Anab. 3. 1. 35">Xen. <title>An.</title> 3. 1. 35</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς μήποτ’ ἐπὶ τοῖς βαρβάροις γενώμεθα, ἀλλὰ μᾶλλον...ἐκεῖνοι ἐφ’ ἡμῖν</quote>. The sense of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐφ’ ἡμῖν</quote> is the reason for giving the words to Electra, and not, as L does (cr. n.), to Orestes.—Not, ‘<emph>towards</emph> us.’ With a dat. denoting persons, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπί</quote> would mean rather ‘against,’ as in <ref n="Hom. Od. 10.214">Hom. <title>Od.</title> 10. 214</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδ’ οἵ γ’ ὡρμήθησαν ἐπ’ ἀνδράσιν</quote>. It is different when the dat. denotes a place, though even then such a sense for <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπί</quote> is rare; <ref n="Hom. Il. 5.327">Hom. <title>Il.</title> 5. 327</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">νηυσὶν ἐπὶ γλαφυρῇσιν ἐλαυνέμεν</quote> (‘towards’).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χωρεῖ_γεγηθώς_1432">. The rest of the trimeter is lost. Hermann suggests <quote xml:lang="grc">κάρτα σὺν σπουδῇ ποδός</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βᾶτε_ἀντιθύρων_1433">, ‘make for the vestibule.’ If the words are sound, <quote xml:lang="grc">κατά</quote>, lit. ‘down upon,’ denotes <emph>the point on which</emph> the rapid movement is directed. The application of <quote xml:lang="grc">κατά</quote>, with gen., to downward movement, in the literal sense, is not rare; cp. <ref n="Hom. Il. 13.504"> <title>Il.</title> 13. 504</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">αἰχμὴ..κατὰ γαίης</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc"><*>χετ’</quote>: 3. 217 <quote xml:lang="grc">κατὰ χθονὸς ὄμματα πήξας</quote>. Again, it can denote ‘descent upon’ in a purely figurative sense (<quote xml:lang="grc">λεγειν κατά τινος</quote>). The peculiarity here is that, while the movement is literal, the descent is figurative. Even <quote xml:lang="grc">κατὰ σκοποῦ τοξεύειν</quote> (Herodian 6. 7. 19) is different, since the arrow is conceived as describing a curve. I do not know any real paraliel for this use of <quote xml:lang="grc">κατά</quote>. It seems to justify some suspicion of the text.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κατ_ἀντιθυρῶν_1433">. The corrupt <emph>v. l.</emph> in one MS. (<quote xml:lang="grc">Γ</quote>), <quote xml:lang="grc">κατάντι θυρῶν</quote>, is also noteworthy. <quote xml:lang="grc">κατάντι</quote> does not occur: but in <ref n="Hom. Il. 23.116"> <title>Il.</title> 23. 116</ref> we have <quote xml:lang="grc">κάταν‐</quote> <quote xml:lang="grc">τα</quote>, ‘down hill,’ as opp. to <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄναντα</quote>. This suggests another possibility, with <quote xml:lang="grc">θυρῶνα, </quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βᾶτε_θυρῶν_1433">, where <quote xml:lang="grc">κάταντα</quote> would be explained by a gesture, ‘haste down there to the porch.’ </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εὖ_θέμενοι_1434">: cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 633"> <title>O. T.</title> 633</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">νεῖκος εὖ θέσθαι</quote>: fr. 324 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἢν παρὸν θέσθαι καλῶς</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτός τις αὑτῷ τὴν βλάβην προσθῇ φέρων</quote>: <ref n="Eur. Ba. 49">Eur. <title>Bacch.</title> 49</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τἀνθένδε θέμενος εὖ.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τάδ_πάλιν_1434">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">εὖ θῆσθε</quote>. For <quote xml:lang="grc">πάλιν</quote> as= ‘in their turn,’ <ref n="Soph. El. 371">cp. 371</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ᾗ_νοεῖς_1435">, lit., hasten ‘on the path that thou hast in view,’—<foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, to the execution of the plan within the house. Cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 868"> <title>Ai.</title> 868</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πᾷ γὰρ οὐκ ἔβαν ἐγώ</quote>;</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ᾗ_νοεῖς_1435_2"> to Electra, rather than to Orestes (cr. n.), is not merely that her share in this verse then becomes the same as in v. 1415. That ground would be inconclusive (<ref n="Soph. El. 1431">cp. 1431</ref> n.). The better reasons are these: (1) the simple </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τελοῦμεν_1435"> is thus far more forcible. (2) <quote xml:lang="grc">ᾗ νοεῖς</quote>, if said by Orestes, would be a feeble reference to the wish of the Chorus; as said by Electra, it is a natural comment on <quote xml:lang="grc">τελοῦμεν</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_βέβηκα_1436">: <ref n="Soph. El. 558">cp. 558</ref> n.: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 345"> <title>Tr.</title> 345</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ δὴ βεβᾶσι</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δἰ_ὠτὸς_1437"> implies gentle, whispering tones; cp. Theocr. 14. 27 <quote xml:lang="grc">χἁμῖν τοῦτο δἰ ὠτὸς ἔγεντο πόχ’ ἅσυχον οὕτως.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡς_ἠπίως_1437">, ‘<emph>as if</emph> kindly,’ ‘with seeming gentleness’: not like <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς ἐτητύμως</quote> in 1452.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λαθραῖον_1437">, as epith. of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀγῶνα</quote>, has a predicative force,—‘that he may rush upon his doom without foreseeing it’; it may thus be represented by an adv., ‘blindly.’—<quote xml:lang="grc"> <emph>δίκας ἀγῶνα</emph>. δίκη</quote> is the just retribution which awaits him. <quote xml:lang="grc">δίκης ἀγών</quote> is the struggle which this retribution brings upon him,—his conflict with the avenging power. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ναυαγίοις_1444">: <ref n="Soph. El. 730">cp. 730</ref>, where, however, it refers to the general wreck of chariots. Here it ought to denote the later and independent disaster, which affected the chariot of Orestes only. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σέ_τοι_1445">: cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 441"> <title>Ant.</title> 441</ref> n.— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κρίνω_1445">=<quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνακρίνω</quote>, ‘question’: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 314"> <title>Tr.</title> 314</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τί δ’ ἄν με καὶ κρίνοις</quote>; Cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 399"> <title>Ant.</title> 399</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="συμφορᾶς_1448">, which can denote either good fortune (1230) or evil, suits the purposed ambiguity. For the ear of Aegisthus, her reply means: ‘Of course I know; else I should be a stranger to the fortune of my nearest <emph>kinswoman</emph>,’— viz., her mother. She leaves him to decide whether she means that Clytaemnestra is afflicted or rejoiced by the news. And meanwhile <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡ συμφορὰ τῆς φιλτάτης</quote> has a further meaning—Clytaemnestra<*>s death—which he cannot yet surmise.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φίλης_κατήνυσαν_1451">. The ostensible meaning is that they have <emph>reached her house</emph>, <quote xml:lang="grc">οῖκον</quote> being understood. Cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 607"> <title>Ai.</title> 607</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνύσειν</quote> | ..<quote xml:lang="grc">Ἅιδαν</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 804"> <title>Ant.</title> 804</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν παγκοίτην..θάλαμον</quote> | ..<quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνύτουσαν</quote>. <ref n="Soph. OC 1562"> <title>O. C.</title> 1562</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξανύσαι</quote> | ..<quote xml:lang="grc">τὰν</quote>.. | <quote xml:lang="grc">νεκρῶν πλάκα</quote>. The hidden meaning is, <quote xml:lang="grc">φίλης προξένου κατήνυσαν φόνον</quote>: they have <emph>accomplished her murder.</emph> To the ear of the audience, the nature of the ellipse would be plain enough.—This is Whitelaw's view, and I am now satisfied that it is the best. The English rendering given above is borrowed from his verse translation, ‘To the heart of their hostess they have found their way.’ I formerly took the inner meaning to be, <quote xml:lang="grc">φίλην.. πρόξενον κατήνυσαν</quote> (<emph>confecerunt</emph>). But this is hardly warranted by (<foreign xml:lang="lat">e.g.</foreign>) <ref n="Eur. Orest. 89">Eur. <title>Or.</title> 89</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">αἷμα γενέθλιον κατήνυσεν</quote>: and the genitive ought to suffice for both meanings. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἦ_καὶ_1452">: 314 n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡς_ἐτητύμως_1452">, like <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς ἀληθῶς</quote>, etc. This emphasising use of <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς</quote> with adverbs of the positive degree was probably developed out of its use with superlatives, as <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς μάλιστα</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὔκ_κἀπέδειξαν_1453">. The added words, </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐ_μόνον_1453">, represent the sense of the clause suppressed after </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὔκ_1453">: <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ μόνον ἤγγειλαν, ἀλλὰ κ.τ.λ.</quote> Cp. <ref n="Aristoph. Frogs 103">Ar. <title>Ran.</title> 103</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ΗΡ. σὲ δὲ ταῦτ’ ἀρέσκει; ΔΙ. μὴ ἀλλὰ πλεῖν ἢ μαίνομαι</quote>: <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, <quote xml:lang="grc">μὴ</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">λέξῃς ὅτι</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀρέσκει, ἀλλὰ κ.τ.λ.</quote>—Cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 813">Soph. <title>Ai.</title> 813</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">χωρεῖν ἕτοιμος, κοὐ λόγῳ δείξω μόνον</quote>. </gloss>
