<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πάρεστ_1454"> is meant by Aegisthus to be impersonal; but suggests to the spectators another meaning (‘is he here?’).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὥστε_1454">: cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 656"> <title>Ph.</title> 656</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἆρ’ ἔστιν ὥστε κἀγγύθεν θέαν λαβεῖν;</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κἀμφανῆ_μαθεῖν_1454">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτόν</quote>, Orestes. The ambiguous gender of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμφανῆ</quote> suits the situation. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμφανῆ</quote> might, indeed, be plur., meaning the relics (<quote xml:lang="grc">λείψανα</quote>, 1113): but this is less simple. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πάρεστι_δῆτα_1455">. The nom. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄζηλος θέα</quote> renders it better to understand <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ νεκρός</quote> with <quote xml:lang="grc">πάρεστι</quote> than to take the verb impersonally. For </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δῆτα_1455">, <ref n="Soph. El. 843">cp. 843</ref>: </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_μάλ_1455">, 1178. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἦ_εἶπας_1456">, lit. ‘thou hast bidden me rejoice much,’ <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, greeted me with most joyful news. The words would more usually mean ‘a long farewell,’ as in <ref n="Eur. Hipp. 113">Eur. <title>Hipp.</title> 113</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὴν σὴν δὲ Κύπριν πόλλ’ ἐγὼ χαίρειν λέγω</quote>. Cp. however <ref n="Soph. Aj. 112">Soph. <title>Ai.</title> 112</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">χαίρειν, Ἀθάνα, τἄλλ’ ἐγώ σ’ ἐφίεμαι</quote>, <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, ‘be happy in all else.’</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τυγχάνοι_1456"> has the support of L, the more prevalent reading </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τυγχάνει_1456"> is the better here. She means, ‘Rejoice, if this <emph>is</emph> matter for joy,’ rather than, ‘You might (or would) rejoice, if it <emph>were</emph>’ such. <ref n="Soph. El. 891">Cp. 891</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">σὺ δ’ οὖν λέγ’, εἴ σοι τῷ λόγῳ τις ἡδονή</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κἀναδεικνύναι_πύλας_1458">, if right, is a pregnant phrase, ‘open the gates and show the interior.’ <title>Nub.</title> 302 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἵνα</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">μυστοδόκος δόμος</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν τελέταις ἁγίαις ἀναδείκνυται</quote>. Bold as it is, I incline to believe that it is sound. No probable emendation has yet been made: see Appendix. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Μυκηναίοισιν_1459">, the townsfolk: </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Ἀργείοις_1459">, the people of the neighbouring district: see 4 n., and <ref n="Soph. El. 160">cp. 160</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἁ κλεινὰ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">γᾶ..Μυκηναίων.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πᾶσιν_1459">: cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 1287"> <title>O. T.</title> 1287</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">βοᾷ διοίγειν κλῇθρα καὶ δηλοῦν τινα</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῖς πᾶσι Καδμείοισι τὸν πατροκτόνον</quote>. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1183"> <title>Ant.</title> 1183</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ πάντες ἀστοί</quote> (n.).—I do not think that the verse is spurious: see Appendix on 1458. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐλπίσιν_ἀνδρὸς_1460">: for the gen., <ref n="Soph. El. 857">cp. 857</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="στόμια_1462">, poet. plur.; so <ref n="Aesch. Ag. 237">Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 237</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">χαλινῶν</quote>. Cp. <title>Compar. Pericl. et Fabii</title> 1 <quote xml:lang="grc">σπαργῶντι τῷ δήμῳ χαλινὸν ἐμβαλεῖν ὕβρεως.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φύσῃ_φρένας_1462">: <ref n="Soph. OC 804"> <title>O. C.</title> 804</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδὲ τῷ χρόνῳ φύσας φανεῖ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">φρένας ποτ’</quote> (n.). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_δὴ_1464"> <quote xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</quote>: lit. ‘already my part is being performed.’ For </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τἀπ_ἐμοῦ_1464"> cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1682"> <title>O. C.</title> 1682</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τἀπὸ σοῦ βραδύνεται</quote>. She refers ostensibly to what he has just said: ‘I, at least, am already resolved to be loyal and docile.’ Her secret meaning is, ‘My part in the plan of vengeance is being accomplished.’ She is luring him to his fate: <ref n="Soph. El. 1438">cp. 1438</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τἀνθάδ’ ἂν μέλοιτ’ ἑμοί.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τελεῖται_1464"> is pres. There is no certain instance in Attic of <quote xml:lang="grc">τελοῦμαι</quote> as fut. pass. ( <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1381"> <title>Ph.</title> 1381</ref> n.).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῷ_χρόνῳ_1464">: for the art., <ref n="Soph. El. 1013">cp. 1013</ref>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="συμφέρειν_κρείσσοσιν_1464">, to agree with them, to live in concord with them. Cp. <ref n="Eur. Med. 13">Eur. <title>Med.</title> 13</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτή τε πἀντα συμφέρουσ’ Ἰάσονι</quote>. <ref n="Aristoph. Lys. 166">Ar. <title>Lys.</title> 166</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνήρ, ἐὰν μὴ τῇ γυναικὶ συμφέρῃ</quote>. This sense comes from that of ‘sharing a burden’ (946), so that it resembles our phrase, ‘pull together.’ Here it suits her inner meaning,—that she is working with those who now are the stronger (<foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, the avengers).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δέδορκα_φάσμ_1466"> <quote xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</quote>: ‘I behold that which has not fallen without the jealousy of the gods.’ Aegisthus is not openly exulting here; he veils his joy in specious language, for he is in public, and speaks before hearers whom he distrusts. He affects to think that the gods have struck down his enemy. The meaning implied by <quote xml:lang="grc">φθόνου</quote> is that Orestes had incurred the divine displeasure by unnatural threats against his mother and his step-father (779 <quote xml:lang="grc">δείν’ ἐπηπείλει τελεῖν</quote>).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὦ_Ζεῦ_1466">, at once indicates the sense of </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φθόνου_1466"> as = the <emph>divine</emph> jealousy. For that sense, see on <ref n="Soph. Phil. 776"> <title>Ph.</title> 776</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν φθόνον δὲ πρόσκυσον</quote>.—The word </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φάσμα_1466"> is chosen on account of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δέδορκα_1466">, in place of a word like <quote xml:lang="grc">σῶμα</quote>, or <quote xml:lang="grc">πτῶμα</quote>, adapted to </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πεπτωκός_1466">. In <ref n="Soph. Trach. 693"> <title>Tr.</title> 693</ref>, on the other hand, <quote xml:lang="grc">δέρκομαι φάτιν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄφραστον</quote>, the subst. is adapted to the adj.— For other views, see Appendix.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔπεστι_1466">: cp. <ref n="Aesch. Eum. 542">Aesch. <title>Eum.</title> 542</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ποινὰ γὰρ ἐπέσται</quote>: <ref n="Xen. Cyrop. 6. 2. 33">Xen. <title>Cyr.</title> 6. 2. 33</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔπεστι γάρ τις αἰσχύνη.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νέμεσις_1466">, the divine resentment; <ref n="Soph. OC 1753"> <title>O. C.</title> 1753</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πενθεῖν οὐ χρή· νέμεσις γάρ</quote>. Nemesis is not here so definitely a person as above in 792.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐ_λέγω_1466">, <emph>indictum volo.</emph> <ref n="Aesch. Eum. 866">Aesch. <title>Eum.</title> 866</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐνοικίου δ’ ὄρνιθος οὐ λέγω μάχην</quote>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸ_συγγενές_1469">: the neuter gender suits the intended ambiguity.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοι_1469"> emphasises <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ συγγενές</quote>, as <quote xml:lang="grc">γε</quote> or <quote xml:lang="grc">γοῦν</quote> could do, but has also a sententious force, implying that such a tribute to kinship is a duty. Thus it suits the pious tone of the speaker: ‘If he was my enemy, still he was my kinsman.’ Cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 278"> <title>Ant.</title> 278</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμοί τοι</quote>: <ref n="Xen. Mem. 1. 6. 11">Xen. <title>Mem.</title> 1. 6. 11</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐγώ τοί σε δίκαιον μὲν νομίζω</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κἀπ_ἐμοῦ_1469">=<quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ ἀπ’ ἐμοῦ</quote>, from my side, on my part: <ref n="Soph. El. 433">cp. 433</ref>: <ref n="Soph. OC 1289"> <title>O. C.</title> 1289</ref> f. <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ ταῦτ’ ἀφ’ ὑμῶν..βουλήσομαι</quote> | ..<quote xml:lang="grc">κυρεῖν ἐμοί</quote>. [I formerly understood, <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ ἐπ’ ἐμοῦ</quote>, ‘in my case’ also: but now prefer the other view.]</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θρήνων_τύψῃ_1469">: <ref n="Soph. Aj. 924"> <title>Ai.</title> 924</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς καὶ παρ’ ἐχθροῖς ἄξιος θρήνων τυχεῖν</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βάσταζ_1470">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ κάλυμμα</quote>, ‘handle it,’ <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, ‘lift it’: <ref n="Soph. El. 905">cp. 905</ref> n.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐκ_ἐμὸν_1470">, since Orestes is supposed to be a Phocian stranger.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ταῦθ_1470">, ‘these relics’; he avoids saying either <quote xml:lang="grc">τήνδ’</quote> or <quote xml:lang="grc">τόνδ’</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προσηγορεῖν_φίλως_1470">. Lucian (<title>De luctu</title>, c. 13) describes the sorrowful farewells addressed by relatives to the corpse, when laid out for burial: <quote xml:lang="grc">φήσει γὰρ ὁ πατήρ, γοερόν τι φθεγγόμενος καὶ παρατείνων</quote> (‘drawing out’) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἕκαστον τῶν ὀνομάτων· τέκνον ἥδιστον, οἴχῃ μοι καὶ τέθνηκας καὶ πρὸ ὥρας ἀνηρπάσθης, μόνον ἐμὲ τὸν ἄθλιον καταλιπών κ.τ.λ.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σὺ_δέ_1472">, Electra.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἴ_μοι_1472">. The words mean properly, ‘if she is anywhere in the house <emph>for</emph> me,’ <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, ‘so that I can see her.’ The enclitic </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μοι_1472">, thus placed, could not go with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κάλει_1472">, as it does in <ref n="Soph. Trach. 1147"> <title>Tr.</title> 1147</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κάλει τὸ πᾶν μοι σπέρμα σῶν ὁμαιμόνων</quote>. A translation, however, need not mark this. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μηκέτ_σκόπει_1474">: <ref n="Soph. El. 1225">cp. 1225</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τίνα_φοβεῖ_1475"> <quote xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</quote> As Aegisthus gazes in terror and horror on Clytaemnestra's face, Orestes says, ‘Whom dost thou fear? Who is it that thou dost not know?’ <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, ‘Why should <emph>that</emph> face terrify thee? Is it not familiar?’</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀρκυστάτοις_1476">. The <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄρκυς</quote> (<emph>cassis</emph>) was a hunting tunnel-net, ending in a pouch (<quote xml:lang="grc">κεκρύφαλος</quote>, <title>Cyneg.</title> 6 § 7). It was meant to receive the game when driven to the extremity of the enclosed ground. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀρκύστατα</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἄρκυς, ἵστημι</quote>) meant properly such nets <emph>when set up</emph>; and <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀρκυστάσιον</quote>, or <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀρκυστασία</quote>, is the enclosure formed by them ( <title>Cyneg.</title> 6 § 6). When used figuratively, as here, the word suggests, not merely the capture of the victim, but also the act of decoying or driving him into the toils. It is thus more expressive than <quote xml:lang="grc">δίκτυον</quote> (the general word for ‘net’), <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀμφίβληστρον</quote> (‘castingnet’), or <quote xml:lang="grc">γάγγαμον</quote> (a circular fishing-net), —which are also used metaphorically ( <ref n="Aesch. Ag. 358">Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 358</ref>, <ref n="Aesch. Ag. 1382">Aesch. Ag. 1382</ref>, <ref n="Aesch. Ag. 361">Aesch. Ag. 361</ref>). Cp. <ref n="Aesch. Ag. 1374">Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 1374</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πῶς γάρ τις ἐχθροῖς ἐχθρὰ πορσύνων, φίλοις</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">δοκοῦσιν εἶναι, πημονῆς ἀρκύστατ’ ἂν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">φράξειεν ὕψος κρεῖσσον ἐκπηδήματος</quote>;</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μέσοις_1476">, cp. <ref n="Aesch. Eum. 112">Aesch. <title>Eum.</title> 112</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκ μέσων ἀρκυστάτων</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ὤρουσεν</quote>: <cit> <bibl n="Eur. El. 965">Eur. <title>El.</title> 965</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">καλῶς ἄρ’ ἄρκυν ἐς μέσην πορεύεται.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote: καλῶς ἄρ’ ἄρκυν ἐς μέσην πορεύεται.

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πέπτωκα_1476"> with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐν_1476"> (instead of <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰς</quote> with acc.), as <ref n="Eur. Her. 1091">Eur. <title>H. F.</title> 1091</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς ἐν κλύδωνι καὶ φρενῶν ταράγματι</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">πέπτωκα δεινῷ</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πάλαι_1477"> goes with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αἰσθάνει_1477"> (lit., ‘hast thou not long since been aware?’): not with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνταυδᾷς</quote>. For <quote xml:lang="grc">πάλαι</quote> referring to a recent moment, <ref n="Soph. El. 676">cp. 676</ref>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀνταυδᾷς_1477">, a compound found only here, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀντί</quote> has the same force as in <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀντονομάζω</quote>, <ref n="Thuc. 6. 4.">Thuc. 6. 4.</ref>§ 6 <quote xml:lang="grc">τὴν πόλιν</quote> (Rhegium)..<quote xml:lang="grc">οἰκίσας Μεσσήνην...ἀντωνόμασε</quote>, ‘changed its name’ to Messene. Aegisthus ‘changes the designation’ of living men, and speaks of them </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἴσα_θανοῦσιν_1477">, in the same terms which would properly be applied to the dead. Thus the strict sense of the words is, ‘Thou perversely (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἀντ‐</quote>) speakest of the living as if they were dead.’ The acc. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ζῶντας_1477"> stands with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνταυδᾷς</quote> as with the simple <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐδᾶν</quote> (<foreign xml:lang="lat">e.g.</foreign>, <ref n="Eur. Hipp. 582">Eur. <title>Hipp.</title> 582</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐδῶν δεινὰ πρόσπολον κακά</quote>). If <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνταυδᾷς</quote> meant ‘reply to,’ or ‘speak face to face with,’ it would require the dative.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ζῶντας_1477_2">, Tyrwhitt's correction of the MS. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ζῶν_τοῖς_1477">. Campbell, who retains <quote xml:lang="grc">ζῶν τοῖς</quote>, explains thus: ‘that thou, a living man, hast been replying to the dead (Orestes), in tones like theirs (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἴσα</quote>)’: <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, ‘with a tongue that is already doomed to death.’ </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ξυνῆκα_1479">: for the aor., <ref n="Soph. El. 668">cp. 668</ref> n.—Cp. <ref n="Aesch. Lib. 886">Aesch. <title>Ch.</title> 886</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ΟΙ. τὸν ζῶντα καίνειν τοὺς τεθνηκότας λέγω</quote>. | <quote xml:lang="grc">ΚΛ. οἲ )γώ, ξυνῆκα τοὔπος ἐξ αἰνιγμάτων.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐ_οὐκ_1479">: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 97"> <title>O. C.</title> 97</ref>: <ref n="Soph. OT 1058."> <title>O. T.</title> 1058.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_μάντις_1481">: ‘and, though so good a prophet, (yet) thou wast deceived so long?’ The interrogative <quote xml:lang="grc">καί</quote> (928, 1046) is here nearly=<quote xml:lang="grc">κᾆτα</quote>. The antithesis between <quote xml:lang="grc">μάντις</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐσφάλλου</quote> makes this better than to take <quote xml:lang="grc">καί</quote> as=‘also’ (‘so good a seer <emph>too</emph>, and yet deceived so long?’). Others take <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ</quote> as=<quote xml:lang="grc">καίπερ</quote>, with <quote xml:lang="grc">ὤν</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πάρες_εἰπεῖν_1482">. Here <quote xml:lang="grc">κἂν</quote> is most simply explained as <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ ἐὰν</quote>, <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">παρῇς</quote>. Cp. <ref n="Aristoph. Ach. 1021">Ar. <title>Ach.</title> 1021</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μέτρησον εἰρήνης τί μοι, κἂν πέντ’ ἔτη</quote>, <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">μετρήσῃς</quote>. See Appendix. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βροτῶν_1485"> is a partitive gen., on which <quote xml:lang="grc">θνῄσκειν ὁ μέλλων</quote> depends (cp. <ref n="Thuc. 4. 102">Thuc. 4. 102</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τῶν ἄλλων τὸν βουλόμενον</quote>, etc.); though the sense is not affected in a translation if it be rendered as a gen. absol.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σὺν_μεμιγμένων_1485">, ‘involved in miseries,’ implying here, ‘crimes, and their consequences.’ Cp. <ref n="Hom. Od. 20.203"> <title>Od.</title> 20. 203</ref>(<quote xml:lang="grc">ἄνδρας</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">μισγέμεναι κακότητι</quote>. For <quote xml:lang="grc">σὺν</quote> (which need not be taken as a case of tmesis), cp. <ref n="Pind. N. 3">Pind. <title>N.</title> 3. 77</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μεμιγμένον μέλι λευκῷ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">σὺν γάλακτι</quote>. So <ref n="Pind. I. 3">Pind. <title>I.</title> 3. 3</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄξιος εὐλογίας ἀστῶν μεμῖχθαι</quote>. The converse phrase (like <quote xml:lang="grc">πρόσκειται κακόν μοι</quote> relatively to <quote xml:lang="grc">πρόσκειμαι κακῷ</quote>, <ref n="Pind. I. 240">Pind. I. 240</ref> n.) occurs in <ref n="Hdt. 7. 203">Her. 7. 203</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἶναι δὲ θνητὸν οὐδένα οὐδὲ ἔσεσθαι τῷ κακὸν ὲξ ἀρχῆς γινομένῳ οὐ συνεμίχθη</quote>. We may also compare <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1311">Soph. <title>Ant.</title> 1311</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">δειλαίᾳ δὲ συγκέραμαι δύᾳ</quote> (‘steeped in it’): <title>Plut.</title> 853 <quote xml:lang="grc">οὕτω πολυφόρῳ συγκέκραμαι δαίμονι</quote>. Shelley, <title>The Cenci</title>, act 5, sc. 4: ‘Be constant to the love | Thou bearest us; and to the faith that I, | <title>Though wrapt in a strange cloud of crime and shame</title>, | Lived ever holy and unstained.’</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θνῄσκειν_1485">: for the pres. inf. with <quote xml:lang="grc">μέλλω</quote>, cp. n. on <ref n="Soph. OT 967."> <title>O. T.</title> 967.</ref> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῦ_χρόνου_1485">, the time implied in <quote xml:lang="grc">μηκύνειν</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ταφεῦσιν_1488">, birds and dogs: <ref n="Aesch. Seven 1020">Aesch. <title>Th.</title> 1020</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑπ’ οἰωνῶν</quote>... | <quote xml:lang="grc">ταφέντ’ ἀτίμως</quote>: see on <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1081.">Soph. <title>Ant.</title> 1081.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄποπτον_ἡμῶν_1488">, far from our sight; the gen. as after words of ‘distance from’ ( <ref n="Soph. OT 762"> <title>O. T.</title> 762</ref> n.). Cp. <ref n="Hom. Od. 3.258"> <title>Od.</title> 3. 258</ref>, where Nestor says that, if Menelaüs on his return had found Aegisthus still living,—<quote xml:lang="grc">τῷ κέ οἱ οὐδ<*> θανόντι χυτὴν ἐπὶ γαῖαν ἔχευαν</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ ἄρα τόν γε κύνες τε καὶ οἰωνοὶ κατ‐</quote> <quote xml:lang="grc">έδαψαν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">κείμενον ἐν πεδίῳ ἑκὰς ἄστεος</quote>. Pausan. 2. 16 § 7 <quote xml:lang="grc">Κλυταιμνήστρα δὲ ἐτάφη καὶ Αἴγισθος ὀλίγον ἀπωτέρω τοῦ τείχους, ἐντὸς δὲ ἀπηξιώθησαν, ἔνθα Ἀγαμέμνων τε αὐτὸς ἔκειτο καὶ οἱ σὺν αὐτῷ φονευθέντες</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἁγών_1492">, <emph>discrimen</emph>, the issue: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 587"> <title>O. C.</title> 587</ref>: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 588">Soph. <title>Ph.</title> 588</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μῆτερ, οὐ λόγων ἔθ’ ἁγών</quote>: <ref n="Eur. Orest. 1291">Eur. <title>Or.</title> 1291</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐχ ἕδρας ἀγών</quote>: <ref n="Thuc. 3. 44">Thuc. 3. 44</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ γὰρ περὶ τῆς ἐκείνων ἀδικίας ἡμῖν ὁ ἀγών, εἰ σωφρονοῦμεν, ἀλλὰ περὶ τῆς ἡμετέρας εὐβουλίας</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρόχειρος_κτανεῖν_1494">. In its primary and usual sense, <quote xml:lang="grc">πρόχειρος</quote> denotes what is ready in the hand (1116). Here it passes into a wholly different sense, applied to a person whose hand is ‘forward’ or ready, as <quote xml:lang="grc">πρόθυμος</quote> is one whose spirit is forward. Cp. <ref n="Eur. Her. 161">Eur. <title>H. F.</title> 161</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τῇ φυγῇ πρόχειρος ἦν</quote>, ‘prompt <emph>for</emph> flight’ (=<quote xml:lang="grc">φεύγειν</quote>), where Wilamowitz illustrates the dat. by <ref n="Pind. O. 4">Pind. <title>O.</title> 4. 14</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τροφαῖς ἕτοιμον ἵππων</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μὴ_τάσσε_1495">: cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 664"> <title>Ant.</title> 664</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοὐπιτάσσειν τοῖς κρατύνουσιν</quote>: Eur. fr. 690 <quote xml:lang="grc">τάσσειν δὲ μᾶλλον ἢ )πιτάσσεσθαι θέλοις. </quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔνθαπερ_1495">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκεῖσε</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔνθαπερ</quote>: <ref n="Soph. El. 1099">cp. 1099</ref>. The place meant is the <quote xml:lang="grc">μέγαρον</quote> of the house: <ref target="cl268">see on 268</ref> f. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πᾶσ_ἀνάγκη_1497">, as <ref n="Soph. OT 986"> <title>O. T.</title> 986</ref>, <ref n="Hdt. 2. 22">Her. 2. 22</ref>, Plat. <title>Phaedo</title>p. 67 A, etc.— </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τά_μέλλοντα_1497">: for the omission of the art. with the second partic., <ref n="Soph. El. 991">cp. 991</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Πελοπιδῶν_1497">, he appeals, as a last hope, to family sympathies. Thyestidae, like himself, and Atreidae, like Orestes, are alike ‘children of Pelops.’ According to the legend, the calamities of the house were closed by the righteous vengeance of Orestes, whom Athena's judgment vindicated from the Furies. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὰ_ς_1499">: <quote xml:lang="grc">σά</quote>, though em phatic, is elided; see on <ref n="Soph. OT 64"> <title>O. T.</title> 64</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πόλιν τε κἀμὲ καὶ σ’ ὁμοῦ στένει.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄκρος_1499">: cp. <ref n="Aesch. Ag. 1130">Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 1130</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ κομπάσαιμ’ ἂν θεσφάτων γνώμων ἄκρος</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">εἶναι</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὴν_τέχνην_1500">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, <quote xml:lang="grc">τὴν μαντικήν</quote>. Agamemnon fell into the snare laid for him: <ref n="Aesch. Ag. 911"> <title>Ag.</title> 911</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐς δῶμ’ ἄελπτον ὡς ἂν ἡγῆται Δίκη</quote>.—Cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1121"> <title>Ai.</title> 1121</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ γὰρ βάναυσον τὴν τέχνην ἐκτησάμην</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἕρφ_1500">. The word is always so written here, as if Orestes could foresee that Aegisthus would utter an aspirated word. Similar instances occur elsewhere: see Appendix. In the theatre, we must suppose, the actor said <quote xml:lang="grc">ἕρπε</quote> at full length: and possibly the poet so wrote it. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἦ_σε_1503">; ‘(dost thou do this), lest..?’ Cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 1012"> <title>O. T.</title> 1012</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἦ μὴ μίασμα τῶν φυτευσάντων λάβῃς;</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μὲν_οὖν_1503">: <ref n="Soph. OT 705."> <title>O. T.</title> 705.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καθ_θάνῃς_1503">; <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, with such comfort as would be given by permission to choose the place or mode of death. <ref n="Soph. El. 1493">Cp. 1493</ref>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χρῆν_1505">, with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἶναι_1505">, implies that, though it ought to be so, it is not. The sense is, in substance, what might be expressed by a conditional sentence, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἥδε δίκη ἦν ἂν τοῖς πᾶσιν, εἰ τὸ εἰκὸς ἔπαθον.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εὐθὺς_1505">, immediately after the crime in each case; Aegisthus has enjoyed too long an impunity. <ref n="Soph. El. 13">Cp. 13</ref> f.n.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θέλει_1505"> is better here than </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θέλοι_1505">, since it suggests more clearly the reference to the actual case of Aegisthus. The optative is, however, also tenable. It would mark the generality of the statement, ‘any one who should wish’; and could follow a present, <quote xml:lang="grc">χρή</quote>, no less than <quote xml:lang="grc">χρῆν</quote> (cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 666"> <title>Ant.</title> 666</ref>).— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῖς_ὅστις_1505">: cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 760"> <title>Ai.</title> 760</ref>, where <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅστις</quote> refers to <quote xml:lang="grc">σώματα</quote> in 758: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 709"> <title>Ant.</title> 709</ref>, where <quote xml:lang="grc">οὗτοι</quote> follows <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅστις</quote> in 707: <ref n="Eur. El. 933">Eur. <title>El.</title> 933</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κἀκείνους στυγῶ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">τοὺς παῖδας, ὅστις κ.τ.λ.</quote>: <ref n="Plat. Rep. 566d">Plat. <title>Rep.</title> 566 D</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀσπάζεται πάντας ᾧ ἂν περιτυγχάνῃ</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πράσσειν_γε_1505">: <quote xml:lang="grc">γε</quote> emphasises, not <quote xml:lang="grc">πράσσειν</quote>, but rather the whole sentence, and might have immediately followed <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅστις</quote>, if metre had allowed: cp. <ref n="Hom. Il. 3.279"> <title>Il.</title> 3. 279</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνθρώπους τίνυσθον, ὅτις γ’ ἐπίορκον ὀμόσσῃ</quote>. Certainly <quote xml:lang="grc">πράσσειν <emph>τι</emph> </quote> is no improvement.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κτείνειν_1505">, rather than <quote xml:lang="grc">θνῄσκειν</quote>, because the speaker is himself the executioner. For the emphatic place of the word, <ref n="Soph. El. 957">cp. 957</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">Αἴγισθον.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸ_πανοῦργον_1505">, equiv. in sense to <quote xml:lang="grc">οἱ πανοῦργοι</quote>: <ref n="Soph. El. 972">cp. 972</ref> n.: <ref n="Thuc. 1. 13">Thuc. 1. 13</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ λῃστικὸν καθῄρουν</quote>.—<cit> <bibl n="Shak. MM 2.2.91">Shakesp. <title>Meas. for Meas.</title> act 2, sc. 2, 91:</bibl> <quote xml:lang="eng">‘Those many had not dared to do that evil, | If the first that did the edict infringe | Had answer'd for his deed.’</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
‘Those many had not dared to do that evil, |
                                    If the first that did the edict infringe | Had answer'd for his
                                    deed.’

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὦ_Ἀτρέως_1508">. The dynasty of the Atreidae (<quote xml:lang="grc">δεσπόται οἱ πάλαι</quote>, 764) is about to be restored in the person of the rightful heir, Orestes (162), who displaces the usurper Aegisthus, the representative of the Thyestidae. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δἰ_ἐξῆλθες_1509">, come forth <emph>in</emph> freedom. For <quote xml:lang="grc">διά</quote> denoting the state, cp. <ref n="Thuc. 6. 34">Thuc. 6. 34</ref>§ 2 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀεὶ διὰ φόβου εἰσί</quote>. The phrase here is in one respect peculiar. When the verb denotes <emph>motion</emph>, <quote xml:lang="grc">διά</quote> in this idiom usu. denotes a course of action, and not a state; <foreign xml:lang="lat">e.g.</foreign>, <ref n="Thuc. 6. 60">Thuc. 6. 60</ref>§ 3 <quote xml:lang="grc">διὰ δίκης ἐλθεῖν</quote>, <ref n="Hdt. 6. 9">Her. 6. 9</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">διὰ μάχης ἐλεύσονται</quote>: see on <ref n="Soph. Ant. 742.">Soph. <title>Ant.</title> 742.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὁρμῇ_1510">, the enterprise of the avengers against the tyrants. Cp. <ref n="Xen. Anab. 3. 1. 10">Xen. <title>An.</title> 3. 1. 10</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ γὰρ ᾕδει τὴν ἐπὶ βασιλέα ὁρμήν</quote> (‘the purpose to attack him’).— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τελεωθέν_1510">, ‘consummated,’ ‘perfected’; <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, ‘made completely prosperous.’ The word is applied to those who attain maturity in body and mind; <ref n="Plat. Rep. 487A">Plat. <title>Rep.</title> 487A</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τελειωθεῖσι..παιδείᾳ τε καὶ ἡλικιᾳ</quote>. Herodotus uses it in a sense akin to that which it bears here, <cit> <bibl n="Hdt. 3.86">3. 86</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπιγενὀμενα δὲ ταῦτα τῷ Δαρείῳ ἐτελέωσέ μιν</quote> </cit>, ‘when these omens came to the aid of Dareius, they made him completely acceptable.’—In <ref n="Soph. OC 1089"> <title>O. C.</title> 1089</ref>Sophocles uses the form <quote xml:lang="grc">τελειῶσαι</quote>. Both <quote xml:lang="grc">τελειοῦν</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">τελεοῦν</quote> (as <quote xml:lang="grc">τέλειος</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">τέλεος</quote>) were Attic, while the forms without <quote xml:lang="grc">ι</quote> are alone used by Herodotus.</gloss>

data.quote:
ἐπιγενὀμενα δὲ ταῦτα τῷ Δαρείῳ ἐτελέωσέ
                                    μιν
